## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart — The Shell Balance Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Flow Geometry] --> B[Draw System and Choose Coordinates]
    B --> C[Select a Thin Shell of Finite Thickness]
    C --> D[Apply Conservation of Momentum: Rate In - Rate Out + Forces = 0]
    D --> E[Substitute Newton's Law of Viscosity: tau = -mu dvz/dr]
    E --> F[Divide by Shell Thickness and Take Limit as dr -> 0]
    F --> G[Obtain a Second-Order ODE]
    G --> H[Integrate Twice to Get General Solution]
    H --> I[Apply No-Slip BC at Wall: v = 0]
    I --> J[Apply Symmetry or Zero-Shear BC]
    J --> K[Solve for Integration Constants]
    K --> L[Final Velocity Profile vz of r or y]
    L --> M[Derive Secondary Quantities: Q, vmax, tau_w]
    M --> N[End: Validate with Physical Limits]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interrelations of Simple Flow Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYS[Simple Flow Systems] --> GEOM1[Circular Tube]
    SYS --> GEOM2[Parallel Plates]
    SYS --> GEOM3[Falling Film]
    SYS --> GEOM4[Annulus]

    GEOM1 --> DRV1[Driving Force: Pressure Gradient]
    GEOM2 --> DRV1
    GEOM3 --> DRV2[Driving Force: Gravity]
    GEOM4 --> DRV1

    DRV1 --> ODE1[Second-Order Linear ODE in r or y]
    DRV2 --> ODE1

    ODE1 --> INT[Integrate Twice]
    INT --> BC[Apply BCs: No-Slip + Symmetry]
    BC --> PROF[Parabolic Velocity Profile]

    PROF --> BIOAPP[Bio-Applications]
    BIOAPP --> APP1[Blood Flow in Arteries]
    BIOAPP --> APP2[Dialysis Membrane Flow]
    BIOAPP --> APP3[Microfluidic Biochips]
    BIOAPP --> APP4[Bioreactor Feed Lines]
    BIOAPP --> APP5[Tablet Film Coating]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Velocity Profile for Hagen–Poiseuille Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Z[Flow Direction z] --> P1[At r = 0: Maximum Velocity vmax]
    P1 --> P2[Parabolic Decay: vz = dP/4muL times R squared minus r squared]
    P2 --> P3[At r = R: vz = 0 No-Slip Condition]
    P3 --> P4[Wall Shear Stress: tau_w = dP times R over 2L]
    P4 --> P5[Volumetric Flow Rate: Q = pi R to power 4 dP over 8 mu L]
```

### 4.4 Mapped Relationship Between Geometry, ODE, and Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Tube: Shell at radius r] --> ODE1[d/dr of r dvz/dr equals dP r over mu L]
    G2[Plates: Shell at height y] --> ODE2[d2vz over dy2 equals dP over mu L]
    G3[Film: Shell at distance x] --> ODE3[d2vz over dx2 equals minus rho g sin theta over mu]

    ODE1 --> S1[Parabolic: vz = dP over 4 mu L times R squared minus r squared]
    ODE2 --> S2[Parabolic: vz = dP over 2 mu L times h squared minus y squared]
    ODE3 --> S3[Half-Parabolic: vz = rho g sin theta over 2 mu times 2 h x minus x squared]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how all three geometries ultimately produce a **parabolic (or half-parabolic)** velocity profile, but the **ODE forms** differ because the geometry dictates the divergence of the momentum flux.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** State Newton's law of viscosity. What is the physical significance of the negative sign in the expression $\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$?

**Model Answer:**
Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress between adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the **rate of shear deformation** (velocity gradient), with the constant of proportionality being the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that **momentum is transported in the direction of decreasing velocity** — i.e., from faster-moving layers to slower-moving layers. Physically, fluid layers moving faster "drag" slower layers forward, and vice versa. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** For steady, fully-developed, laminar flow in a circular tube, what is the ratio of the maximum velocity to the mean velocity? State two physical situations in bioprocess engineering where this flow is observed.

**Model Answer:**
For Hagen–Poiseuille flow:

$$v_{z,\max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4\mu L} \quad ; \quad \langle v_z \rangle = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8\mu L}$$

$$\therefore \frac{v_{z,\max}}{\langle v_z \rangle} = 2$$

**Bio-applications:** (1) Blood flow in capillaries and arterioles; (2) Laminar feed flow in tubular bioreactors and hollow-fiber dialysis modules. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note to Students:** KTU ESE convention requires an *internal choice* — solve either Option A or Option B. Both are provided below.

---

#### **Option A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]*

**Q. (a)** Derive the velocity profile for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$ under a pressure drop $\Delta P$ over length $L$. Use the shell balance approach. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state flow.
2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid.
3. Laminar, fully-developed flow ($\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$).
4. Axisymmetric flow: $v_z = v_z(r)$ only.
5. No end effects (ignoring entrance length).
6. Constant viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Consider a cylindrical shell at radius $r$ with thickness $\Delta r$, length $L$. The flow direction is $z$.

Step 2: The momentum balance (rate in − rate out + forces = 0) gives:

$$\left.\tau_{rz}\right|_r (2\pi r L) - \left.\tau_{rz}\right|_{r+\Delta r} 2\pi (r+\Delta r) L + \Delta P \cdot 2\pi r \Delta r = 0$$

Step 3: Substitute $\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$ and divide by $2\pi L \Delta r$:

$$\frac{1}{\Delta r}\left[\left.r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{r+\Delta r} - \left.r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_r\right] = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{\mu L}$$

Step 4: Take the limit $\Delta r \to 0$:

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{\mu L}$$

Step 5: Integrate twice with BCs:
- Symmetry at $r=0$: $C_1 = 0$.
- No-slip at $r=R$: $v_z(R) = 0 \implies C_2 = -\frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$.

**Final result:**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2) \quad \text{(parabolic profile)} \tag{7 marks}$$

---

**Q. (b)** For the flow profile derived in (a), obtain expressions for: (i) the maximum velocity, (ii) the volumetric flow rate, and (iii) the wall shear stress. Show all integration steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum velocity** — occurs at centerline $r = 0$:

$$v_{z,\max} = v_z(0) = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4\mu L} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate** — integrate velocity over the annular ring of area $2\pi r \, dr$:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2\pi \Delta P}{4\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}} \tag{3 marks}$$

**(iii) Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R}$$

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot 2r}{4\mu L} = -\frac{\Delta P \, r}{2\mu L}$$

At $r = R$:

$$\tau_w = -\mu \left(-\frac{\Delta P \, R}{2\mu L}\right) = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2L} \tag{2 marks}$$

---

#### **Option B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**Q. (a)** With a clear sketch, derive the velocity profile for a Newtonian fluid falling as a film of thickness $h$ down a vertical wall. Use the shell momentum balance. State the boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:** Steady, laminar, incompressible Newtonian flow. Film thickness $h$ is constant. Atmospheric pressure acts on the free surface (no pressure gradient). The flow is in the $z$-direction and the velocity varies only in the $x$-direction (normal to the wall), with $x = 0$ at the wall and $x = h$ at the free surface.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Choose a slab of fluid at distance $x$ from the wall, thickness $\Delta x$, width $w$, length $L$. The driving force per unit volume in the $z$-direction is the gravity component $\rho g$ (vertical wall, so $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\sin\theta = 1$).

Step 2: Momentum balance on the slab:

$$\left[-\mu \frac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg|_x + \mu \frac{dv_z}{dx}\bigg|_{x+\Delta x}\right] (w L) + \rho g (w L \Delta x) = 0$$

Step 3: Divide by $w L \Delta x$ and take $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$\frac{d^2 v_z}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho g}{\mu}$$

Step 4: Integrate twice:

$$v_z(x) = -\frac{\rho g}{2\mu} x^2 + C_1 x + C_2$$

Step 5: Apply BCs:
- **No-slip at wall** ($x = 0$): $v_z(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$.
- **Zero shear at free surface** ($x = h$): $\left.\frac{dv_z}{dx}\right|_{x=h} = 0 \implies C_1 = \frac{\rho g h}{\mu}$.

**Final result:**

$$v_z(x) = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu}(2 h x - x^2) \quad \text{(half-parabolic profile)} \tag{7 marks}$$

---

**Q. (b)** For the falling film in (a), determine the (i) maximum velocity, (ii) volumetric flow rate per unit width, and (iii) the mean velocity. Comment on how the mean velocity scales with film thickness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum velocity** — at free surface $x = h$:

$$v_{z,\max} = v_z(h) = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu}(2h^2 - h^2) = \frac{\rho g h^2}{2\mu} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate per unit width** ($w = 1$):

$$Q = \int_0^h v_z(x) \, dx = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu} \int_0^h (2 h x - x^2) \, dx$$

$$Q = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu} \left[h x^2 - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^h = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu}\left[h^3 - \frac{h^3}{3}\right] = \frac{\rho g}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{2 h^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\rho g h^3}{3\mu}} \tag{3 marks}$$

**(iii) Mean velocity:**

$$\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{Q}{h} = \frac{\rho g h^2}{3\mu} = \frac{2}{3} v_{z,\max} \tag{2 marks}$$

**Scaling Comment:** The mean velocity scales as $\langle v_z \rangle \propto h^2$. This means that **doubling the film thickness quadruples the mean velocity** — a critical design consideration in pharmaceutical film-coating operations and in tear-film dynamics on the human eye. **(Bonus comment for full marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in Newton's law of viscosity.** This propagates through the entire derivation and can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in the momentum balance.** Students often write "rate in − rate out" as "rate out − rate in." Remember: *momentum in* (at $r$) is **positive** and *momentum out* (at $r + \Delta r$) is **negative** in the convention used here.
> 3. **Omitting the symmetric boundary condition.** Both BCs (no-slip and symmetry) must be stated and applied.
> 4. **Writing $\ln(0)$ instead of recognizing that $C_1 = 0$ for finiteness.** This is a classic trap in the circular tube derivation.
> 5. **Missing units in the final answer.** Always state units explicitly (e.g., $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\tau_w$ in $\text{Pa}$).
> 6. **In falling-film problems, forgetting that $\theta = 90^\circ$ for a vertical wall, hence $\sin\theta = 1$.** For inclined walls, the component is $\rho g \sin\theta$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shell Balance for Simple Flow Systems**

- **The shell balance method is a 6-step recipe:** (1) draw system, (2) select shell, (3) momentum balance, (4) substitute Newton's law, (5) take $\Delta r \to 0$, (6) integrate with BCs.
- **Newton's law of viscosity** is the cornerstone: $\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$. Always remember the **negative sign**.
- **Two universal boundary conditions** for internal flows: no-slip at walls ($v = 0$) and symmetry at centerline ($\frac{dv}{dr} = 0$).
- **Circular tube flow (Hagen–Poiseuille):** Parabolic profile, $v_{z,\max} : \langle v \rangle = 2:1$, and $Q \propto R^4$ — the famous **Poiseuille's Law**.
- **Parallel plates:** Parabolic profile, $v_{z,\max} : \langle v \rangle = 3:2$, $Q = \frac{2 h^3 w \Delta P}{3\mu L}$.
- **Falling film:** Half-parabolic profile, $v_{z,\max} = \frac{\rho g h^2 \sin\theta}{2\mu}$, $Q = \frac{\rho g h^3 \sin\theta}{3\mu}$, $\langle v \rangle = \frac{2}{3} v_{\max}$.
- **Wall shear stress** is always evaluated at the wall: $\tau_w = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}\big|_{\text{wall}}$.
- **Biomedical relevance:** Hagen–Poiseuille governs blood flow; falling-film models tear-film and lung surfactant behavior; parallel-plate models dialysis and microfluidics.
- **Critical scaling insight:** Tube flow rate is extremely sensitive to radius ($R^4$); film flow rate is sensitive to thickness ($h^3$).
- **Always state assumptions** clearly at the start of any derivation — KTU examiners award full marks only when assumptions are explicit.
- **For KTU Part B (14-mark) questions:** Derive the profile (7 marks) + compute derived quantities (7 marks). The two halves of the question are **always** structured this way.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow of a Newtonian Fluid in the Space Between Two Parallel Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newtonian Fluid:** A fluid is termed **Newtonian** when the shear stress at any point is **directly proportional** to the local shear rate (velocity gradient) at that point, and the constant of proportionality is **independent of the shear rate** itself. Mathematically, $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$, where $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity** $[\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}]$.

The classical problem of **"Flow of a Newtonian Fluid in the Space Between Two Parallel Plates"** (often called **Plane Poiseuille Flow**, **Plane Couette Flow**, or a superposition of the two) describes the steady, laminar, fully-developed momentum transport of an incompressible Newtonian fluid confined between two infinitely wide, parallel, flat plates separated by a small gap $h$. The flow is driven either by an applied pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ in the axial ($x$) direction, by the relative motion of the bounding plates, or by a combination of both.

### Assumptions Embedded in the KTU Standard Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical KTU Assumptions:**
> 1. Steady state: $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\cdot) = 0$
> 2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid: $\rho = \text{const}$, $\mu = \text{const}$
> 3. Laminar, fully-developed flow: $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} = 0$
> 4. Infinite plate width $\Rightarrow$ no edge effects ($v_z = 0$, no $z$-dependence)
> 5. Unidirectional flow: $v_y = 0$, $v_z = 0$, only $v_x \neq 0$
> 6. Gravity acts only in the $y$-direction, hence no body force in $x$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Honey Squeeze 🍯**
> Imagine two large, flat glass plates with a thin film of honey sandwiched between them. If you press one plate sideways (Couette flow), the honey layer touching the moving plate is dragged along, while the layer touching the stationary plate refuses to move (this is the **no-slip condition**). Between these two extremes, each infinitesimally thin layer drags the one beneath it — viscous momentum transport. If instead you squeeze the honey out by pushing it with a syringe (pressure-driven Poiseuille flow), the honey in the middle moves fastest, while the honey stuck to the walls barely creeps. **Both scenarios coexist** when you push the syringe *while* sliding the top plate.

### Biological & Engineering Analogies

| System | Analogous Flow Scenario |
|---|---|
| **Blood in large arteries** | Plane Poiseuille (pressure-driven by heart) |
| **Synovial fluid in joint cavities** | Combined Couette–Poiseuille (joint motion + load) |
| **Microfluidic lab-on-chip channels** | Plane Poiseuille at low Re |
| **Lubricant film in artificial joints/implants** | Couette flow (rotational motion dominates) |
| **Interstitial flow in cartilage** | Plane Poiseuille through porous matrix |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$: **[Pa·s]** = **[kg/(m·s)]**
> - **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu/\rho$: **[m²/s]**
> - **Pressure gradient** $dP/dx$: **[Pa/m]**
> - **Maximum velocity** $v_{\max}$: **[m/s]**
> - **Volumetric flow rate per unit width** $Q/w$: **[m²/s]**
> - **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v_{avg}(2h)/\mu < 1400$ ensures laminar regime
> - Standard biological Newtonian analogue: **water, plasma (dilute), saline**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Starting Point

The governing equation for momentum transport is the **Navier–Stokes equation** in the $x$-direction:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x$$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reduction of the Navier–Stokes Equation

**Step 1 — Steady state:** Drop the local acceleration term $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Continuity for incompressible flow:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Unidirectional flow:** $v_y = 0$ and $v_z = 0$, so $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} = 0$. Thus $v_x = v_x(y)$ **only**.

**Step 4 — Infinite plate width:** No variation in $z$, so $\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} = 0$.

**Step 5 — Fully-developed:** $\dfrac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} = 0$.

**Step 6 — No body force in $x$:** $g_x = 0$.

The equation **collapses** to the simple **ODE**:

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Repository — Examiners Expect These Identities**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Governing ODE | $\dfrac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ | Steady, fully-developed |
| 2 | General integrated form | $v_x(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$ | m/s | Two integrations |
| 3 | **Plane Poiseuille** (both plates stationary, plates at $y = \pm h$) | $v_x(y) = -\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}(h^2 - y^2)$ | m/s | $dP/dx < 0$ for forward flow |
| 4 | Plane Poiseuille (plates at $y=0$ and $y=h$) | $v_x(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}(y^2 - hy)$ | m/s | $dP/dx < 0$ |
| 5 | **Pure Couette flow** (top plate at $V_0$) | $v_x(y) = V_0 \dfrac{y}{h}$ | m/s | $dP/dx = 0$ |
| 6 | **Combined Couette–Poiseuille** | $v_x(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}(y^2 - hy) + V_0\dfrac{y}{h}$ | m/s | Plates at $y=0,\, h$ |
| 7 | Max velocity (Poiseuille, $y=h/2$ or $y=0$) | $v_{\max} = -\dfrac{h^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | m/s | Symmetric geometry |
| 8 | Average velocity (Poiseuille) | $\langle v \rangle = -\dfrac{h^2}{3\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | m/s | Symmetric geometry |
| 9 | **Volumetric flow rate per unit width** (Poiseuille) | $\dfrac{Q}{w} = -\dfrac{2h^3}{3\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | m²/s | Plane channel |
| 10 | Shear stress distribution | $\tau_{yx} = \mu\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = \dfrac{dP}{dx}y - C_1\mu$ | Pa | General |
| 11 | Wall shear stress (Poiseuille) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{h}{2}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dx}\right)$ | Pa | At $y = \pm h$ |
| 12 | Friction factor (Fanning) | $f = \dfrac{2\tau_w}{\rho \langle v \rangle^2}$ | dimensionless | Laminar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** In KTU convention, **positive flow** is in the $+x$ direction. A **negative** pressure gradient ($dP/dx < 0$) is required to drive fluid forward. This convention must be strictly followed in board examinations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Model in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Biomedical devices:** Designing microfluidic biochips for cell sorting and drug delivery requires the velocity and shear distributions between parallel plates.
> - **Hemodynamics:** The Hagen–Poiseuille law (cylindrical analogue) is the foundation for diagnosing arterial stenosis via the pressure-drop–flow relationship.
> - **Lubrication theory:** Artificial hip and knee joints operate in the Couette regime; bearing design depends on the linear shear-stress distribution.
> - **Membrane filtration:** Cross-flow filtration in dialysis uses plane-channel flow; wall shear rate governs fouling.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Plane Poiseuille Flow (Both Plates Stationary)

### 3.1.1 Geometry & Boundary Conditions

Two stationary, infinite plates at $y = 0$ (bottom) and $y = h$ (top). The pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ is constant.

**No-slip boundary conditions:**
$$v_x(y=0) = 0 \qquad v_x(y=h) = 0$$

### 3.1.2 First Integration

Starting from:
$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

Integrate once with respect to $y$:
$$\frac{dv_x}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y + C_1$$

**Reasoning:** Since the right-hand side is independent of $y$, the antiderivative is linear in $y$ plus a constant $C_1$.

### 3.1.3 Second Integration

Integrate a second time:
$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Reasoning:** Each integration introduces a constant of integration; two integrations yield $C_1, C_2$.

### 3.1.4 Apply Boundary Condition 1 — $v_x(0) = 0$

$$v_x(0) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(0)^2 + C_1(0) + C_2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; C_2 = 0$$

### 3.1.5 Apply Boundary Condition 2 — $v_x(h) = 0$

$$v_x(h) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}h^2 + C_1 h + 0 = 0$$

Solve for $C_1$:
$$C_1 = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}h$$

### 3.1.6 Final Velocity Profile

$$\boxed{\;v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,(y^2 - hy)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For flow in the $+x$ direction, the pressure must **decrease**, i.e., $\dfrac{dP}{dx} < 0$, making $v_x > 0$.

### 3.1.7 Maximum Velocity (Symmetric Geometry)

If we relocate the plates at $y = -h$ to $y = +h$ (more common textbook form), the symmetric solution is:
$$v_x(y) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(h^2 - y^2)$$

Maximum at $y = 0$:
$$v_{\max} = -\frac{h^2}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

### 3.1.8 Shear-Stress Distribution

$$\tau_{yx}(y) = \mu\frac{dv_x}{dy} = \mu\left[\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(2y - h)\right] = \frac{dP}{dx}\left(y - \frac{h}{2}\right)$$

At the walls ($y = 0$ and $y = h$):
$$\tau_w = -\frac{h}{2}\frac{dP}{dx} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_w(h) = +\frac{h}{2}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

(equal magnitude, opposite sign — Newton's third law).

### 3.1.9 Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Width

$$Q/w = \int_0^h v_x(y)\,dy = \int_0^h \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(y^2 - hy)\,dy$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left[\frac{y^3}{3} - \frac{hy^2}{2}\right]_0^h$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\frac{h^3}{3} - \frac{h^3}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(-\frac{h^3}{6}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q}{w} = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\;}$$

### 3.1.10 Average Velocity

$$\langle v \rangle = \frac{Q/w}{h} = -\frac{h^2}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the factor difference between plane and cylindrical cases: plane channel gives $h^2/12$, cylindrical tube (Hagen–Poiseuille) gives $R^2/8$. This is a **favourite KTU viva question**.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Pure Couette Flow (Upper Plate Moving)

### 3.2.1 Setup

Bottom plate at $y = 0$ stationary; top plate at $y = h$ moving at velocity $V_0$ in the $+x$ direction; no pressure gradient.

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = 0$$

Boundary conditions:
$$v_x(0) = 0 \qquad v_x(h) = V_0$$

### 3.2.2 Reduced ODE and Solution

$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = 0$$

Integrate twice:
$$v_x(y) = C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply BCs:
$$v_x(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$$
$$v_x(h) = V_0 \Rightarrow C_1 = V_0/h$$

$$\boxed{\;v_x(y) = V_0 \frac{y}{h}\;}$$

### 3.2.3 Shear Stress

$$\tau_{yx} = \mu\frac{dv_x}{dy} = \mu\frac{V_0}{h} = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Couette flow, the **shear stress is uniform** across the gap — the defining feature that distinguishes it from Poiseuille flow.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Combined Couette–Poiseuille (General Case)

### 3.3.1 Setup

Bottom plate at $y = 0$ stationary; top plate at $y = h$ moving at $V_0$; pressure gradient $dP/dx \neq 0$.

Boundary conditions:
$$v_x(0) = 0 \qquad v_x(h) = V_0$$

### 3.3.2 General Solution and Integration

$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply BC1: $C_2 = 0$.

Apply BC2:
$$V_0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}h^2 + C_1 h \;\Longrightarrow\; C_1 = \frac{V_0}{h} - \frac{h}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

### 3.3.3 Final Profile

$$\boxed{\;v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(y^2 - hy) + V_0\frac{y}{h}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The total velocity profile is the **linear superposition** of Poiseuille (parabolic) and Couette (linear) components — a direct consequence of the linearity of the governing ODE.

### 3.3.4 Position of Maximum Velocity

Set $\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = 0$:
$$\frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}y + C_1 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; y_{\max} = -\frac{\mu C_1}{(dP/dx)} = -\mu\left[\frac{V_0/h}{dP/dx} - \frac{h}{2\mu}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\;y_{\max} = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{\mu V_0}{h(dP/dx)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The point of maximum velocity is **displaced off-centre** whenever $V_0 \neq 0$. This is a hallmark feature of combined Couette–Poiseuille flow.

### 3.3.5 Force Balance on the Plates

Force per unit area on bottom plate (fluid exerts on wall):
$$F/A\big|_{y=0} = -\tau_{yx}(0) = -\mu\frac{dv_x}{dy}\bigg|_{y=0} = -\mu C_1$$

Force per unit area on top plate:
$$F/A\big|_{y=h} = \tau_{yx}(h) = \mu\left[\frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}h + C_1\right] = \frac{dP}{dx}h + \mu C_1$$

Newton's third law demands equal and opposite forces on the two plates for the entire control volume:
$$F_{\text{bottom}} + F_{\text{top}} = 0$$
$$-\mu C_1 + \left(\frac{dP}{dx}h + \mu C_1\right) = \frac{dP}{dx}h \neq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **non-zero net** is balanced by the **pressure force** acting on the inlet/outlet cross-sections of the control volume — confirming global force equilibrium.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Pressure-Gradient / Flow-Rate Relation for Combined Flow

Integrate the combined profile from $0$ to $h$:

$$Q/w = \int_0^h\left[\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(y^2 - hy) + V_0\frac{y}{h}\right]dy$$

Split:
$$Q/w = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\int_0^h(y^2 - hy)dy + \frac{V_0}{h}\int_0^h y\,dy$$

Evaluate integrals:
$$\int_0^h(y^2 - hy)dy = \left[\frac{y^3}{3} - \frac{hy^2}{2}\right]_0^h = -\frac{h^3}{6}$$

$$\int_0^h y\,dy = \frac{h^2}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q}{w} = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} + \frac{V_0 h}{2}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** This equation is the **design equation** for parallel-plate membrane modules, lubrication bearings, and microfluidic channels where both pressure-driven and drag-driven flows coexist.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A Newtonian fluid ($\mu = 0.001 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) flows between two stationary parallel plates separated by $h = 2\,\text{mm}$ under a pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx} = -2000 \, \text{Pa/m}$. Compute (a) the maximum velocity, (b) the volumetric flow rate per unit width, (c) the wall shear stress.

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum velocity** (occurs at centreline $y = h/2 = 1\,\text{mm} = 0.001\,\text{m}$):

$$v_{\max} = -\frac{h^2}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{(0.002)^2}{2(0.001)}(-2000)$$

$$= -\frac{4\times 10^{-6}}{2\times 10^{-3}}(-2000) = -(2\times 10^{-3})(-2000) = 4\,\text{m/s}$$

**(b) Volumetric flow rate per unit width:**

$$\frac{Q}{w} = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{(0.002)^3}{12(0.001)}(-2000)$$

$$= -\frac{8\times 10^{-9}}{1.2\times 10^{-2}}(-2000) = -(6.67\times 10^{-7})(-2000) = 1.333\times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**(c) Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = -\frac{h}{2}\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{0.002}{2}(-2000) = 2\,\text{Pa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> All numerical answers should be expressed in **SI units with three significant figures** for KTU valuation.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Expect crisp, definition-based or formula-derivation-based answers of **3 to 5 lines**.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define a **Newtonian fluid**. State the constitutive relation between shear stress and shear rate. Give **two biological examples** of fluids that approximate Newtonian behaviour.

**Model Answer:**

> A Newtonian fluid obeys the linear constitutive equation:
> $$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}$$
> where $\mu$ is a constant dynamic viscosity independent of shear rate. The shear stress is directly proportional to the local velocity gradient.
>
> **Biological examples** that approximate Newtonian behaviour at physiological shear rates: **water, blood plasma, dilute aqueous humour, synovial fluid (at high shear rates)**, and **cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)**.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for constitutive relation + 1 mark for examples)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is the **no-slip boundary condition**? Why is it essential in the derivation of plane Poiseuille flow?

**Model Answer:**

> The **no-slip boundary condition** states that the velocity of a viscous fluid at a solid surface equals the velocity of the surface itself. For a stationary plate, $v_x\big|_{\text{wall}} = 0$.
>
> It is essential because:
> 1. It provides the **two boundary conditions** ($v_x = 0$ at $y = 0$ and $y = h$) required to evaluate the two integration constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ in the second-order ODE solution.
> 2. Without it, the velocity profile and the resulting flow rate remain undetermined.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for statement + 2 marks for justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions carry **14 marks** each, with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically allocated **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equation, derive the **velocity profile** for the steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid between **two stationary parallel plates** separated by a distance $2h$, the flow being driven by a constant pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Hence obtain expressions for the **(i) maximum velocity**, **(ii) average velocity**, and **(iii) volumetric flow rate per unit width** of the channel. Show that $\langle v \rangle = \dfrac{2}{3}v_{\max}$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Derivation of velocity profile**

**Step 1:** Governing equation (Navier–Stokes in $x$):
$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate twice:
$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

**Step 3:** Boundary conditions (plates at $y = -h$ and $y = +h$):
$$v_x(-h) = 0, \quad v_x(h) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and solve:
- BC1: $\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}h^2 - C_1 h + C_2 = 0$
- BC2: $\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}h^2 + C_1 h + C_2 = 0$

Subtracting: $2C_1 h = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$

Then: $C_2 = -\dfrac{h^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$

$$\boxed{\;v_x(y) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(h^2 - y^2)\;}$$

This is a **parabolic profile**, symmetric about the centreline $y = 0$.

**Part (b): Derived Quantities**

**(i) Maximum velocity** — occurs at $y = 0$:
$$v_{\max} = v_x(0) = -\frac{h^2}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate per unit width**:
$$Q/w = \int_{-h}^{+h} v_x(y)\,dy = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\int_{-h}^{+h}(h^2 - y^2)dy$$

$$= -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left[h^2 y - \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-h}^{+h} = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(2h^3 - \frac{2h^3}{3}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q}{w} = -\frac{2h^3}{3\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\;}$$

**(iii) Average velocity:**
$$\langle v \rangle = \frac{Q/w}{2h} = -\frac{h^2}{3\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Verification:** $\dfrac{\langle v \rangle}{v_{\max}} = \dfrac{-h^2/(3\mu)(dP/dx)}{-h^2/(2\mu)(dP/dx)} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ ✓

$$\therefore \quad \boxed{\langle v \rangle = \frac{2}{3}v_{\max}}$$

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks for derivation + 4 marks for sub-parts (b)(i)–(b)(iii))*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **combined Couette–Poiseuille velocity profile** for a Newtonian fluid between two parallel plates, the bottom plate being stationary and the top plate moving at velocity $V_0$, with an imposed constant pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dx}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the combined flow, deduce the **(i) position of maximum velocity**, **(ii) condition under which the maximum velocity occurs at the centre**, and **(iii) wall shear stresses**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Derivation**

From the reduced ODE:
$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

Integrating twice:
$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply boundary conditions (plates at $y = 0$ stationary, $y = h$ moving at $V_0$):

- $v_x(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
- $v_x(h) = V_0 \Rightarrow V_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}h^2 + C_1 h$

Solving:
$$C_1 = \frac{V_0}{h} - \frac{h}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

$$\boxed{\;v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}(y^2 - hy) + V_0\frac{y}{h}\;}$$

**Part (b): Deductions**

**(i) Position of maximum velocity:**

Set $\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = 0$:
$$\frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}y + C_1 = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;y_{\max} = -\frac{\mu C_1}{(dP/dx)} = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{\mu V_0}{h(dP/dx)}\;}$$

**(ii) Condition for maximum at centre ($y_{\max} = h/2$):**

Set $y_{\max} = h/2$:
$$\frac{h}{2} = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{\mu V_0}{h(dP/dx)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\mu V_0}{h(dP/dx)} = 0$$

Therefore: **either $V_0 = 0$** (no Couette component — pure Poiseuille) **or $dP/dx \to \infty$** (pure Couette dominates).

**(iii) Wall shear stresses:**

$$\tau_{yx}(y) = \mu\frac{dv_x}{dy} = \frac{dP}{dx}y + \mu C_1$$

At bottom wall ($y = 0$):
$$\boxed{\;\tau_w(\text{bottom}) = \mu C_1 = \mu\left[\frac{V_0}{h} - \frac{h}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\right] = \frac{\mu V_0}{h} - \frac{h}{2}\frac{dP}{dx}\;}$$

At top wall ($y = h$):
$$\boxed{\;\tau_w(\text{top}) = \frac{dP}{dx}h + \mu C_1 = \mu\frac{V_0}{h} + \frac{h}{2}\frac{dP}{dx}\;}$$

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks for derivation + 4 marks for sub-parts (b)(i)–(b)(iii))*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Sign error on $dP/dx$:** A *positive* velocity in the $+x$ direction requires $dP/dx < 0$. Mixing up signs leads to an unphysical negative velocity. Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for correct sign handling.
> 2. **Missing factor of 2 in flow rate:** When plates are at $y = \pm h$ (symmetric) versus $y = 0, h$ (asymmetric), the flow rate per unit width is $2h^3/(3\mu)$ versus $h^3/(12\mu)$. A factor-of-8 discrepancy is **immediately penalised**.
> 3. **Forgetting no-slip BC at both walls:** Students often write only one boundary condition, leaving an integration constant undetermined — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Conflating Couette and Poiseuille:** State explicitly which case applies. Mixing the two cases in a single answer is a **structural error**.
> 5. **Skipping the ODE reduction:** Examiners award **2 marks** for reducing the Navier–Stokes equation to the ODE; do not jump directly to the final profile.
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Always quote final numerical answers in **SI units (m, s, Pa, m²/s)**. Mixing CGS units is a soft penalty of 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Coupling terms in non-linear regime:** Plane Poiseuille/Couette is **linear**; do not invoke inertia terms ($\rho v \nabla v$). This is an **advanced trap** for high-scoring students.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam**

- [ ] **Newtonian definition:** $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$, $\mu = $ const.
- [ ] **Reduced ODE** (the master equation): $\dfrac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$
- [ ] **Plane Poiseuille profile** (plates at $\pm h$): $v_x = -\dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}(h^2 - y^2)$ — **parabolic**
- [ ] **Couette profile** (top plate at $V_0$): $v_x = V_0\dfrac{y}{h}$ — **linear**
- [ ] **Combined profile:** parabolic + linear superposition
- [ ] **Maximum velocity (Poiseuille):** $v_{\max} = -\dfrac{h^2}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$
- [ ] **Average velocity (Poiseuille):** $\langle v \rangle = -\dfrac{h^2}{3\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx} = \dfrac{2}{3}v_{\max}$
- [ ] **Volumetric flow rate per unit width (Poiseuille):** $\dfrac{Q}{w} = -\dfrac{2h^3}{3\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ (symmetric) or $-\dfrac{h^3}{12\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ (asymmetric)
- [ ] **Wall shear stress (Poiseuille):** $\tau_w = -\dfrac{h}{2}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$
- [ ] **Couette wall shear:** $\tau = \dfrac{\mu V_0}{h}$ (uniform across gap)
- [ ] **Position of $v_{\max}$ in combined flow:** $y_{\max} = \dfrac{h}{2} - \dfrac{\mu V_0}{h(dP/dx)}$
- [ ] **Re criterion for laminar:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v_{\text{avg}}(2h)}{\mu} < 1400$
- [ ] **Biological analogues:** blood in arteries (Poiseuille), synovial fluid in joints (Couette-Poiseuille), microfluidic biochips (Poiseuille)
- [ ] **Linear superposition principle:** Because the governing ODE is linear, $v_{\text{combined}} = v_{\text{Poiseuille}} + v_{\text{Couette}}$ — **the most important conceptual takeaway**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU Board Exams:** Always begin your derivation with the full Navier–Stokes equation, *list each assumption*, *reduce systematically*, *integrate with constants*, and *substitute boundary conditions explicitly*. Examiners reward the **methodology** as much as the final answer. A clean, well-justified derivation typically fetches **80–90% marks** even if the final expression contains a minor algebraic slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Through Annulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Flow Through Annulus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition **Flow through an annulus** refers to the fully-developed, steady, incompressible laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through the geometric region bounded by two concentric cylindrical surfaces — an **outer cylinder of radius `R_o`** and an **inner cylinder (or solid core) of radius `R_i`**. The "fluid-carrying" cross-section is therefore a hollow ring of width `R_o − R_i`, and the ratio `κ = R_i / R_o` is called the **radius ratio** (curvature parameter). > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEBBT635), this topic sits at the intersection of **fluid mechanics of biological conduits** and **transport phenomena**. Biological examples include: (i) blood flow in the region between a **catheter and an artery wall**, (ii) flow in a **double-lumen tube**, (iii) urine flow in the **ureteral catheter–ureter** gap, and (iv) airflow in the **bronchial annulus** between a bronchoscope and the airway wall. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Straw Inside a Straw" Imagine two drinking straws — one large and one small — placed concentrically. The space **between** the wall of the small straw and the wall of the large straw is the **annulus**. Now pump a viscous fluid (think honey, or blood) through that ring-shaped gap. > [!NOTE] > **Key insight for a first-time learner:** Unlike flow in a single circular pipe where fluid experiences shear only at the outer wall, in an annulus the fluid experiences **two opposing shear surfaces** — the **inner wall drags fluid forward (no-slip)** and the **outer wall also drags fluid forward**. This double-no-slip condition dramatically changes the velocity profile shape compared to the familiar parabolic Hagen-Poiseuille profile. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics (Standardized) - **Dynamic viscosity `μ`** — in SI units: **Pa·s** (kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹); for blood: **μ ≈ 3–4 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. - **Density `ρ`** — blood ≈ **1060 kg/m³**. - **Wall shear stress `τ_w`** — physiologically critical; endothelial cells respond to shear in the range **τ_w ≈ 1–6 Pa** (arterial level). - **Reynolds number for annulus** is conventionally based on the **hydraulic diameter**: `D_h = 2(R_o − R_i) = 2R_o(1 − κ)`. - **Laminar–turbulent transition** for an annulus: `Re_crit ≈ 2000` (lower than a circular pipe because the secondary flow is suppressed by the inner core). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Governing Assumptions (For Fully-Developed Laminar Flow) The classical derivation rests on the following six idealizations: 1. **Steady-state** — no time dependence: `∂/∂t = 0`. 2. **Incompressible Newtonian fluid** — constant `ρ` and `μ`. 3. **Laminar, axisymmetric flow** — only `v_z(r)` is non-zero; `v_r = v_θ = 0`. 4. **Fully-developed** — `∂v_z/∂z = 0`; no entry-region effects. 5. **Concentric cylinders** — no eccentricity; only `r` matters. 6. **Negligible body forces** — gravity acts purely in `z`-direction (or is absorbed into `dp/dz`). Under these, the **z-component of the Navier-Stokes equation** in cylindrical coordinates reduces to: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$ ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — Why the Profile is NOT a Simple Parabola - **Step 1:** Integration once with respect to `r` gives `r·dv_z/dr = (r²/2μ)(dp/dz) + C₁`. - **Step 2:** Second integration yields `v_z(r) = (r²/4μ)(dp/dz) + C₁ ln r + C₂`. - **Step 3:** Because the **inner cylinder is solid**, the **logarithmic term** `C₁ ln r` survives — this is the hallmark of annular flow. For a simple pipe (κ→0), the inner wall vanishes and `C₁` is forced to zero by the requirement of finite velocity at `r = 0`. - **Step 4:** Two no-slip boundary conditions at `r = R_i` and `r = R_o` produce two linear equations in `C₁` and `C₂`. - **Step 5:** The resulting velocity profile is a **parabola + logarithm** — a "skewed" parabolic profile that is **not symmetric** about the mid-radius. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **RADIUS RATIO CONVENTION:** Throughout this note, `κ = R_i / R_o` with `0 ≤ κ < 1`. `κ = 0` recovers the circular pipe limit. `κ → 1` recovers the parallel-plates limit. | # | Quantity | Expression | Units (SI) | |---|----------|------------|------------| | 1 | Hydraulic diameter | `D_h = 2R_o(1 − κ)` | m | | 2 | Mean velocity | ` = (R_o²/8μ)(−dp/dz) · Ψ(κ) / (1 − κ²)` | m/s | | 3 | **Volumetric flow rate `Q`** | `Q = (π R_o⁴/8μ)(−dp/dz) · Ψ(κ)` | m³/s | | 4 | `Ψ(κ)` (flow-function) | `Ψ(κ) = 1 − κ⁴ + (1 − κ²)² / ln(1/κ)` | dimensionless | | 5 | Location of max velocity | `r_m / R_o = √[(1 − κ²)/(−2 ln κ)]` | dimensionless | | 6 | Max velocity | `v_max = (R_o²/4μ)(−dp/dz) · Λ(κ)` | m/s | | 7 | `Λ(κ)` | `Λ(κ) = 1 − κ² + (1 − κ²)/ln(1/κ) − 2√[(1 − κ²)/−2 ln κ] · … ` (see derivation) | dimensionless | | 8 | Inner-wall shear stress | `τ_i = (R_o/2)(−dp/dz) · [ (1 + κ²)/(1 − κ²) − 2/ln(1/κ) ]⁻¹ …` | Pa | | 9 | Outer-wall shear stress | `τ_o = (R_o/2)(−dp/dz) · [ (1 + κ²)/(1 − κ²) − 2κ²/(1 − κ²)·… ]` | Pa | | 10 | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | `f_D = 64/Re_Dh · 1/G(κ)` | dimensionless | | 11 | `G(κ)` | `G(κ) = [1 − κ⁴ − (1 − κ²)²/ln(1/κ)] / [1 − κ²]² · (1 − κ²)²` | dimensionless | | 12 | Reynolds number | `Re_Dh = ρ D_h / μ` | dimensionless | | 13 | Limiting case `κ → 0` | Recovers Hagen-Poiseuille pipe | — | | 14 | Limiting case `κ → 1` | Recovers plane Poiseuille (parallel plates) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The flow-function `Ψ(κ)` is the single most important expression** in this topic. It is **always positive** for `0 < κ < 1`, varies from `1` (κ→0) to `8/3·(1−κ)³` (κ→1), and is non-monotonic. Memorize its definition — it appears in **every** numerical problem in the KTU exam. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Catheter design**: clinicians size the gap `(R_o − R_i)` to keep wall shear stress `τ_o > 1 Pa` to prevent thrombus formation on the artery wall. - **Heat exchangers (double-pipe)**: annulus flow is the canonical configuration; the `Ψ(κ)` factor dictates pumping power. - **Polymer extrusion**: coaxial dies extrude a sheath around a core; the inner-core velocity profile is the "annulus problem in reverse". - **BioMEMS / Lab-on-chip**: many microfluidic devices have an annular channel for buffer flow around a sensing fiber. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Velocity Profile for Laminar Annular Flow **Start from the z-momentum equation (cylindrical, axisymmetric, fully-developed):** $$0 = -\frac{dp}{dz} + \mu\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)\right]$$ This is mathematically equivalent to stating that **viscous diffusion balances the pressure gradient**. **First integration** (multiply both sides by `r/μ` and integrate): $$\int \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) r\,dr = \int \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,r\,dr$$ $$r\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,r^2 + C_1$$ **Second integration** (divide by `r` and integrate again): $$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,r^2 + C_1 \ln r + C_2$$ **Apply no-slip BCs** at `r = κR_o` and `r = R_o`: $$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,\kappa^2 R_o^2 + C_1 \ln(\kappa R_o) + C_2$$ $$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,R_o^2 + C_1 \ln(R_o) + C_2$$ **Subtracting** the first from the second eliminates `C_2`: $$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,R_o^2(1 - \kappa^2) + C_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{R_o}{\kappa R_o}\right)$$ $$0 = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\,R_o^2(1 - \kappa^2) - C_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{\kappa}\right)$$ $$C_1 = -\frac{R_o^2(1 - \kappa^2)}{4\mu\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\frac{dp}{dz}$$ > Note: `dp/dz` is **negative** for flow in the `+z` direction; we typically report results in terms of `(−dp/dz) > 0`. **Solving for `C_2`** from BC at `r = R_o`: $$C_2 = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}R_o^2 - C_1 \ln R_o = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}R_o^2 + \frac{R_o^2(1-\kappa^2)}{4\mu\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\frac{dp}{dz}\ln R_o$$ **Assemble the velocity profile** by substituting back: $$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}\!\left[r^2 - R_o^2\right] - \frac{R_o^2(1-\kappa^2)}{4\mu\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\frac{dp}{dz}\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)$$ Factor out `−(R_o²/4μ)(dp/dz)` and write with the **pressure-gradient magnitude** `Δ = −dp/dz > 0`: $$\boxed{\;v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta\, R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)^2 + \frac{1 - \kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)\,\right]\;}$$ This is the **canonical annular velocity profile**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Velocity and its Location For maximum velocity, set `dv_z/dr = 0`: $$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta\, R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[-\frac{2r}{R_o^2} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{R_o\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\right] = 0$$ Solve for `r = r_m`: $$-\frac{2 r_m}{R_o^2} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{R_o\,\ln(1/\kappa)} = 0$$ $$\frac{2 r_m}{R_o} = \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{r_m}{R_o} = \sqrt{\frac{1-\kappa^2}{-2\ln\kappa}}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** As `κ → 0`, `r_m/R_o → 0` (center of pipe, parabolic). As `κ → 1`, `r_m/R_o → 1/√2` (the **mid-gap** location for parallel plates). For any `κ ∈ (0,1)`, the peak is **shifted outward from the geometric centroid** of the annulus — because the inner wall has less fluid to "drag" and the no-slip at `R_i` damps the velocity more strongly nearby. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Mean Velocity and Volumetric Flow Rate The **mean velocity** is defined as: $$\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1}{\pi R_o^2(1-\kappa^2)} \int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} v_z(r)\,2\pi r\,dr$$ Split the integral into three parts corresponding to the three terms in the velocity profile: $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta R_o^2}{2\mu} \int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} r\!\left[\,1 - \frac{r^2}{R_o^2} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\ln\!\frac{r}{R_o}\,\right] dr$$ **Integral I₁** (constant term): $$\int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} r\,dr = \frac{R_o^2}{2}(1 - \kappa^2)$$ **Integral I₂** (parabolic term): $$\int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} \frac{r^3}{R_o^2}\,dr = \frac{R_o^2}{4}(1 - \kappa^4)$$ **Integral I₃** (logarithmic term) — use `∫ r ln(r/R_o) dr = (r²/2)ln(r/R_o) − r²/4`: $$\int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} r\,\ln\!\frac{r}{R_o}\,dr = \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\ln\!\frac{r}{R_o} - \frac{r^2}{4}\right]_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} = -\frac{R_o^2}{4}(1-\kappa^2)$$ since at `r = R_o` the log vanishes and at `r = κR_o`: `(κ²R_o²/2)ln κ − κ²R_o²/4`. **Combine:** $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta R_o^4}{2\mu}\!\left[\frac{1-\kappa^2}{2} - \frac{1-\kappa^4}{4} - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{4\,\ln(1/\kappa)}(1-\kappa^2)\right]$$ After algebraic simplification (combine the first two bracket terms): $$\frac{1-\kappa^2}{2} - \frac{1-\kappa^4}{4} = \frac{2(1-\kappa^2) - (1-\kappa^4)}{4} = \frac{(1-\kappa^2)(2 - (1+\kappa^2))}{4} = \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{4}$$ Therefore: $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta R_o^4}{8\mu}\!\left[(1-\kappa^2)^2 - \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{\pi\,(-\Delta p/L)\,R_o^4}{8\mu}\!\left[\,1 - \kappa^4 + \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\right]\;}$$ > with `Δp/L = −dp/dz` for a flow length `L`. **The bracketed factor is the flow-function:** $$\boxed{\;\Psi(\kappa) = 1 - \kappa^4 + \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\;}$$ The **mean velocity** is therefore: $$\langle v_z \rangle = \frac{Q}{\pi R_o^2(1-\kappa^2)} = \frac{(-\Delta p/L)\,R_o^2}{8\mu}\cdot\frac{\Psi(\kappa)}{1-\kappa^2}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation — Wall Shear Stresses The wall shear stress is `τ = −μ (dv_z/dr)` evaluated at each wall. **At the outer wall, `r = R_o`:** $$\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{R_o} = \frac{\Delta R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[-\frac{2}{R_o} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{R_o\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\right] = -\frac{\Delta R_o}{2\mu}\!\left[1 - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\;\tau_o = -\mu\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{R_o} = \frac{\Delta R_o}{2}\!\left[1 - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]\;}$$ **At the inner wall, `r = κR_o`:** $$\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{\kappa R_o} = -\frac{\Delta R_o}{2\mu}\!\left[2\kappa - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\;\tau_i = \frac{\Delta R_o}{2}\!\left[\frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)} - 2\kappa\right]\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Both `τ_o` and `τ_i` are **positive** (in the direction of flow on the wall) when `Δ > 0`. For a thin annulus (`κ → 1`), `τ_i ≈ τ_o` — symmetry. For a thick annulus (`κ → 0`), `τ_i → 0` (no inner wall) and `τ_o → ΔR_o/2`, recovering the Hagen-Poiseuille pipe value. --- ### 3.5 Derivation — Friction Factor for Laminar Flow Define the **Darcy friction factor** in terms of the wall shear stress (using the outer wall as the reference): $$f_D = \frac{2\,\tau_o}{\rho \langle v_z \rangle^2}$$ Substitute `τ_o` and `⟨v_z⟩`: $$f_D = \frac{2}{\rho\langle v_z\rangle^2}\cdot\frac{\Delta R_o}{2}\!\left[1 - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right] = \frac{\Delta R_o\left[1 - \dfrac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]}{\rho\langle v_z\rangle^2}$$ Express `Δ` in terms of `⟨v_z⟩` using the mean velocity equation: $$\Delta = \frac{8\mu\langle v_z\rangle\,(1-\kappa^2)}{R_o^2\,\Psi(\kappa)}$$ Insert: $$f_D = \frac{8\mu\langle v_z\rangle\,(1-\kappa^2)\,R_o\left[1 - \dfrac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]}{R_o^2\,\Psi(\kappa)\,\rho\langle v_z\rangle^2}$$ $$\boxed{\;f_D = \frac{64}{Re_{D_h}}\cdot\frac{(1-\kappa)\!\left[1 - \dfrac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]}{\Psi(\kappa)}\;}$$ with `Re_{D_h} = ρ⟨v_z⟩·2R_o(1-κ)/μ`. > When `κ → 0`: `(1-κ) → 1`, the bracket → `1/2`, and `Ψ(κ) → 1`, giving `f_D·Re_{D_h} → 32`. When expressed as `f_D·Re_D = 64` (pipe convention using `D = 2R`), the formulation is **fully consistent** with Hagen-Poiseuille. ✓ --- ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** Blood (μ = 3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, ρ = 1060 kg/m³) flows through an annular region between a catheter of radius `R_i = 1.0 mm` and an artery of radius `R_o = 2.5 mm`. The pressure gradient is `Δp/L = 250 Pa/m`. Compute: (a) the radius ratio `κ`; (b) the flow function `Ψ(κ)`; (c) the volumetric flow rate `Q`; (d) the mean velocity `⟨v_z⟩`; (e) the location and magnitude of maximum velocity; (f) the outer-wall shear stress; (g) the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime. **Solution:** - (a) `κ = R_i/R_o = 1.0/2.5 = 0.4`. - (b) `Ψ(0.4) = 1 − (0.4)⁴ + (1 − 0.16)² / ln(2.5) = 1 − 0.0256 + (0.84)²/0.9163 ≈ 1 − 0.0256 + 0.7702 = 1.7446`. - (c) `Q = π·250·(2.5×10⁻³)⁴ / (8·3.5×10⁻³) · 1.7446` Numerator: `π·250·(2.5×10⁻³)⁴ ≈ π·250·3.906×10⁻¹¹ ≈ 3.067×10⁻⁸` Denominator: `8·3.5×10⁻³ = 2.8×10⁻²` `Q ≈ (3.067×10⁻⁸/2.8×10⁻²)·1.7446 ≈ 1.911×10⁻⁶ m³/s ≈ 1.91 mL/s`. - (d) `A = π R_o²(1 − κ²) = π(6.25×10⁻⁶)(0.84) = 1.649×10⁻⁵ m²`; `⟨v_z⟩ = Q/A ≈ 0.116 m/s`. - (e) `r_m/R_o = √[(1 − 0.16)/(2·0.9163)] = √[0.84/1.8326] = √0.4584 ≈ 0.6771` ⇒ `r_m ≈ 1.693 mm`. Plug into profile: `v_max ≈ 0.205 m/s` (≈ 1.77 × ⟨v_z⟩). - (f) `τ_o = (Δ R_o/2)·[1 − (1 − κ²)/(2 ln(1/κ))] = (250·2.5×10⁻³/2)·[1 − 0.84/1.8326] = 0.3125·0.5416 ≈ 0.169 Pa`. This is **below** the arterial homeostatic shear window — clinically, this catheter would need to be **larger** to raise `τ_o`. - (g) `D_h = 2·2.5×10⁻³·0.6 = 3.0×10⁻³ m`; `Re = 1060·0.116·3×10⁻³ / 3.5×10⁻³ ≈ 105`. **Highly laminar.** ✓ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Annulus Flow Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: Geometry R_i, R_o, dp/dz, mu, rho] --> B[Compute kappa = R_i / R_o] B --> C[Compute Psi kappa = 1 - kappa^4 + (1 - kappa^2)^2 / ln(1/kappa)] C --> D[Compute Q from Hagen-Poiseuille-annulus formula] D --> E[Compute mean velocity u_avg = Q / A_annulus] E --> F[Compute r_m location of max velocity] F --> G[Compute v_max from velocity profile] G --> H[Compute wall shear stresses tau_i and tau_o] H --> I[Compute D_h and Reynolds number] I --> J{Flow regime?} J -->|Re < 2000| K[Laminar regime confirmed] J -->|Re >= 2000| L[Turbulent regime - use empirical f_D correlations] K --> M[Output: Complete flow characterization] L --> M ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Velocity Profile Shape Evolution with `κ` ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["kappa = 0.0
Pipe limit
Symmetric parabola
v_max at center"] P2["kappa = 0.3
Slight outward shift
of velocity peak"] P3["kappa = 0.6
Distinct outward peak
asymmetric profile"] P4["kappa = 0.9
Near-parallel-plates
peak at mid-gap"] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 Q1["psi(kappa) ~ 1.000"] -.-> P1 Q2["psi(kappa) ~ 0.879"] -.-> P2 Q3["psi(kappa) ~ 0.523"] -.-> P3 Q4["psi(kappa) ~ 0.064"] -.-> P4 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Annulus Problem Decomposition ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph IN[Inputs] I1[R_o] I2[R_i] I3[dp/dz] I4[mu] I5[rho] end subgraph GEOM[Geometric Processing] G1[kappa = R_i / R_o] G2[D_h = 2 R_o (1 - kappa)] G3[A = pi R_o^2 (1 - kappa^2)] end subgraph FLOW[Flow Field] F1[Velocity profile v_z of r] F2[Q volumetric flow rate] F3[Mean velocity u_avg] end subgraph SHEAR[Shear Analysis] S1[tau_o at outer wall] S2[tau_i at inner wall] end subgraph RE[Regime & Friction] R1[Re_Dh = rho u_avg D_h / mu] R2[f_D Darcy friction factor] R3{Laminar or Turbulent?} end I1 --> G1 I2 --> G1 I1 --> G2 I2 --> G2 G1 --> G3 I3 --> F1 I4 --> F1 G1 --> F1 F1 --> F2 F2 --> F3 G3 --> F3 F1 --> S1 F1 --> S2 F3 --> R1 G2 --> R1 I5 --> R1 I4 --> R1 F3 --> R2 R1 --> R2 R1 --> R3 ``` ### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Text-Based) ``` ↑ +z (flow direction) │ ╔══════════╪══════════╗ ← Outer cylinder wall │ v_z(r) ║ ░░░░░░░│░░░░░░░░ ║ ║ ░░░░ │ ░░░░ ║ ← Maximum velocity ║ ░░░ ──► │ ──► ░░░ ║ occurs at r = r_m ║░░░ ───► │ ───► ░░░░ ║ ║ ░░░ ──► │ ──► ░░░ ║ ║ ░░░ │ ░░░ ║ ║ ░░░░░░░│░░░░░░░░ ║ │ ╚══════╤═════════════╝ │ ← Inner cylinder (solid core) ▼ ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define "flow through an annulus" and explain its relevance in a **catheterized artery** in the human body. **Model Answer:** Flow through an annulus is the laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid through the geometric region enclosed between two concentric cylinders of radii `R_i` (inner) and `R_o` (outer), characterized by the radius ratio `κ = R_i/R_o`. In a catheterized artery, the catheter occupies the center of the vessel leaving a ring-shaped region between the catheter surface and the arterial wall through which blood flows. This annular geometry is critical because: (i) it governs the **wall shear stress** on the arterial endothelium, which regulates nitric-oxide release and prevents atherogenesis; (ii) it determines **pumping power** requirements for assisted-circulation devices; and (iii) it controls **thrombus deposition** on the catheter surface. The velocity profile is a "skewed parabola + logarithm" — distinct from the symmetric Hagen-Poiseuille parabola — and is described by the canonical annular velocity profile containing the flow-function `Ψ(κ) = 1 − κ⁴ + (1−κ²)²/ln(1/κ)`. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between the velocity profile of **laminar flow in a circular pipe** and **laminar flow in an annulus**. Why does the annular profile contain a **logarithmic term**? **Model Answer:** | Feature | Circular Pipe | Annulus | |---|---|---| | Geometry | Single wall at `r = R` | Two walls at `r = R_i` and `r = R_o` | | Profile shape | Symmetric parabola | Parabola + logarithm (skewed) | | Peak location | Center (`r = 0`) | Outward of geometric centroid | | Math form | `v_z = v_max(1 − (r/R)²)` | `v_z ∝ 1 − (r/R_o)² + (1−κ²)/ln(1/κ) · ln(r/R_o)` | **Reason for the logarithmic term:** The differential equation `d/dr(r·dv_z/dr) = (r/μ)(dp/dz)` admits a general solution containing `r²` and `ln r`. In a **single pipe**, regularity at the centerline forces `C₁ = 0` because `ln r → −∞` as `r → 0`. In an **annulus**, however, the inner wall is at `r = κR_o > 0`, so `ln r` remains finite and the integration constant `C₁` survives. Physically, the logarithmic term is the *mathematical signature* of the **two-sided no-slip constraint** — the inner cylinder "drags" the velocity profile and breaks the central symmetry. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper]** Starting from the Navier-Stokes equation for steady, fully-developed, laminar, axisymmetric Newtonian flow in cylindrical coordinates, **derive the velocity profile** for flow through an annulus of inner radius `R_i` and outer radius `R_o`. From this, obtain: **(a)** the location and magnitude of the **maximum velocity**; **(b)** an expression for the **volumetric flow rate** `Q` in terms of the pressure gradient, fluid viscosity, geometry, and the **flow-function** `Ψ(κ) = 1 − κ⁴ + (1−κ²)²/ln(1/κ). **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) Velocity profile derivation and maximum velocity:** Starting from the z-component of the Navier-Stokes equation for steady, fully-developed, laminar, axisymmetric flow (with `v_r = v_θ = 0` and `∂v_z/∂z = 0`): $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dz}$$ Integrating once: `r·dv_z/dr = (r²/2μ)(dp/dz) + C₁`. Integrating twice: `v_z = (r²/4μ)(dp/dz) + C₁ ln r + C₂`. Applying no-slip at `r = R_i = κR_o`: `0 = (κ²R_o²/4μ)(dp/dz) + C₁ ln(κR_o) + C₂`. Applying no-slip at `r = R_o`: `0 = (R_o²/4μ)(dp/dz) + C₁ ln R_o + C₂`. Solving for `C₁` and `C₂` and writing `Δ = −dp/dz > 0`: $$\boxed{\;v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[\,1 - \left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)^2 + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r}{R_o}\right)\right]\;} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ To find `r_m`, set `dv_z/dr = 0`: $$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[-\frac{2r}{R_o^2} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{R_o\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\right] = 0$$ $$\boxed{\;r_m = R_o\sqrt{\frac{1-\kappa^2}{-2\ln\kappa}}\;} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ Substituting `r_m` back into Eq.(1): $$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[1 - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{-2\ln\kappa} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\ln\!\sqrt{\frac{1-\kappa^2}{-2\ln\kappa}}\right]$$ This simplifies (after algebraic manipulation using `ln(1/κ) = −ln κ`) to: $$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[\frac{1-\kappa^2}{-2\ln\kappa} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\!\left(1 - \ln\!\frac{1-\kappa^2}{-2\ln\kappa}\right)\right]$$ (Acceptable in KTU exams to leave in terms of `κ` after substituting `r_m`.) **(7 marks)** **(b) Volumetric flow rate:** `Q = ∫_{κR_o}^{R_o} v_z(r)·2πr dr` → Expand using Eq.(1): $$Q = \frac{\pi\Delta R_o^2}{2\mu}\!\left[\int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} r\,dr - \frac{1}{R_o^2}\int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} r^3 dr + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\int_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} r\,\ln(r/R_o)\,dr\right]$$ Evaluating each integral: $$\int r\,dr\big|_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} = \frac{R_o^2}{2}(1-\kappa^2)$$ $$\int r^3\,dr\big|_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} = \frac{R_o^2}{4}(1-\kappa^4)$$ $$\int r\,\ln(r/R_o)\,dr\big|_{\kappa R_o}^{R_o} = \frac{R_o^2}{4}(1-\kappa^2)\big[\text{after applying limits}\big] = -\frac{R_o^2}{4}(1-\kappa^2)$$ Combining: $$Q = \frac{\pi\Delta R_o^4}{2\mu}\!\left[\frac{1-\kappa^2}{2} - \frac{1-\kappa^4}{4} - \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{4\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ Using `(1−κ²)/2 − (1−κ⁴)/4 = (1−κ²)²/4`: $$Q = \frac{\pi\Delta R_o^4}{8\mu}\!\left[(1-\kappa^2)^2 - \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{\pi\,(-\Delta p/L)\,R_o^4}{8\mu}\!\left[\,1 - \kappa^4 + \frac{(1-\kappa^2)^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\,\right] = \frac{\pi\,(-\Delta p/L)\,R_o^4}{8\mu}\,\Psi(\kappa)\;}$$ where `Ψ(κ) = 1 − κ⁴ + (1−κ²)²/ln(1/κ)` is the **annular flow-function**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Sample Paper]** For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid through an annulus: **(a)** Derive expressions for the **wall shear stresses** at the **inner wall** `τ_i` and the **outer wall** `τ_o` in terms of the pressure gradient, geometry, and `κ`. **(b)** Define the **Darcy friction factor** for the annulus and show that it reduces to `f_D·Re = 64` in the **circular-pipe limit** (`κ → 0`). **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Wall shear stresses:** From the velocity profile (1): `v_z = (ΔR_o²/4μ)[1 − (r/R_o)² + (1−κ²)/ln(1/κ)·ln(r/R_o)]`. Differentiate: $$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta R_o^2}{4\mu}\!\left[-\frac{2r}{R_o^2} + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{R_o\,\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ **Outer wall, r = R_o:** $$\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{R_o} = \frac{\Delta R_o}{4\mu}\!\left[-2 + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\;\tau_o = -\mu\!\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{R_o} = \frac{\Delta R_o}{2}\!\left[\,1 - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]\;} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Inner wall, r = κR_o:** $$\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{\kappa R_o} = \frac{\Delta R_o}{4\mu}\!\left[-2\kappa + \frac{1-\kappa^2}{\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\;\tau_i = \frac{\Delta R_o}{2}\!\left[\,\frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)} - 2\kappa\right]\;} \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Sanity checks:** - `κ → 0`: `τ_o → ΔR_o/2`, `τ_i → Δ·0/2 → 0` ✓ (recovers pipe) - `κ → 1`: using `ln(1/κ) ≈ (1−κ)`: `τ_o ≈ τ_i ≈ Δ(1−κ)/2` ✓ (parallel plates symmetric) **(7 marks)** **(b) Darcy friction factor:** Define using outer-wall shear (the standard convention for annulus): $$f_D = \frac{2\tau_o}{\rho\langle v_z\rangle^2}$$ Substitute `τ_o` from Eq.(3) and `⟨v_z⟩ = (ΔR_o²/8μ)·Ψ(κ)/(1−κ²)`: $$f_D = \frac{2\,\Delta R_o\!\left[\,1 - \dfrac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]/2}{\rho\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta R_o^2\,\Psi(\kappa)}{8\mu(1-\kappa^2)}\right]^2} = \frac{64\mu^2(1-\kappa^2)^2\left[\,1 - \dfrac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]}{\rho\,\Delta R_o^3\,\Psi(\kappa)^2}$$ Introduce `Re_{D_h} = ρ⟨v_z⟩D_h/μ = ρ⟨v_z⟩·2R_o(1−κ)/μ` and eliminate `Δ/μ` using the mean-velocity relation `Δ/μ = 8⟨v_z⟩(1−κ²)/(R_o²Ψ(κ))`: $$f_D\,Re_{D_h} = \frac{64(1-\kappa)}{\Psi(\kappa)}\!\left[\,1 - \frac{1-\kappa^2}{2\ln(1/\kappa)}\right]$$ **Now examine the pipe limit `κ → 0`:** - `ln(1/κ) → ∞` → the bracket `1 − (1−κ²)/(2 ln(1/κ)) → 1`. - `(1−κ) → 1`. - `Ψ(κ) = 1 − κ⁴ + (1−κ²)²/ln(1/κ) → 1 − 0 + 0 = 1`. Therefore: $$f_D\,Re_{D_h}\bigg|_{\kappa=0} = \frac{64\cdot 1\cdot 1}{1} = 64 \quad \text{✓}$$ This is precisely the Hagen-Poiseuille friction factor for a circular pipe. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in `−dp/dz`.** Many students write `dp/dz` instead of `Δ = −dp/dz` and end up with a *negative* velocity. Always define `Δ > 0` explicitly. > 2. **Confusing `ln(1/κ)` with `ln(κ)`.** These differ by a sign. Since `0 < κ < 1`, `ln(κ) < 0` but `ln(1/κ) > 0` — the **flow-function `Ψ(κ)` is positive** ONLY if you consistently use `ln(1/κ)`. Mis-signed logs give negative `Ψ` and physically meaningless flow rates. > 3. **Using `R_i` as outer radius by mistake.** The derivation defines `R_o` as the OUTER radius. Mixing up leads to `κ > 1` and `Ψ < 0`. > 4. **Omitting the logarithmic term in the velocity profile.** This is the most common error — students write only `1 − (r/R_o)²`, which is the pipe profile. KTU explicitly tests the **annulus-specific** term. > 5. **Not reducing to the pipe limit** when asked — KTU loves sanity-check questions. Always verify `Ψ(0) = 1` and `f·Re = 64` at `κ = 0`. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Geometry:** Annulus = region between two concentric cylinders; characterized by **`κ = R_i/R_o`**. - ✅ **Governing ODE:** `(1/r)·d/dr(r·dv_z/dr) = (1/μ)·dp/dz`. - ✅ **Canonical velocity profile:** `v_z(r) = (ΔR_o²/4μ)[1 − (r/R_o)² + (1−κ²)/ln(1/κ)·ln(r/R_o)]`. - ✅ **Flow-function:** `Ψ(κ) = 1 − κ⁴ + (1−κ²)²/ln(1/κ)` — **always positive**, equals `1` at `κ = 0`. - ✅ **Volumetric flow rate:** `Q = (πΔR_o⁴/8μ)·Ψ(κ)` — **single most-tested formula**. - ✅ **Mean velocity:** `⟨v_z⟩ = Q/[πR_o²(1−κ²)]`. - ✅ **Max-velocity location:** `r_m/R_o = √[(1−κ²)/(−2 ln κ)]` — outward of geometric centroid. - ✅ **Outer-wall shear:** `τ_o = (ΔR_o/2)[1 − (1−κ²)/(2 ln(1/κ))]`. - ✅ **Inner-wall shear:** `τ_i = (ΔR_o/2)[(1−κ²)/(2 ln(1/κ)) − 2κ]`. - ✅ **Darcy friction:** `f_D = 64/Re_{D_h} · (1−κ)·[1 − (1−κ²)/(2 ln(1/κ))]/Ψ(κ)`. - ✅ **Limiting cases:** `κ → 0` → pipe (Hagen-Poiseuille, `fD·Re = 64`); `κ → 1` → parallel plates (`fD·Re = 96`). - ✅ **Biological relevance:** Catheterized artery (most common), double-lumen tubes, ureteral flow, bronchoscope–airway annulus. - ✅ **Threshold for laminar flow:** `Re_Dh < ~2000`; biological flows are almost always **laminar**. - ✅ **Engineering utility:** Shear-stress regulation, pumping-power estimation, thrombus-risk assessment. > *End of Module 1 Notes — Flow Through Annulus | PEBBT635 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Flow of Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids. Shell Momentum Balance for Blood Flow in Capillaries and Porous Tissues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow of Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids. Shell Momentum Balance for Blood Flow in Capillaries and Porous Tissues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flow of Two Adjacent Immiscible Fluids

### Formal Definition
When two **immiscible fluids** (fluids that do not mix to form a homogeneous solution, e.g., oil and water, or blood plasma and an immiscible carrier fluid) flow next to each other in a channel or conduit, the system is described as the **flow of two adjacent immiscible fluids**. The boundary between them is called an **interface**, and across this interface, the **velocity must be continuous** (no-slip at the molecular level) and the **shear stress must be continuous** (momentum flux is balanced).

In biological transport, this concept is critical for understanding multi-phase systems such as **lipid bilayers adjacent to cytosol**, **tear film flow over the cornea**, **perfusion of multiple immiscible phases in tissue engineering scaffolds**, and **drug delivery using emulsion carriers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two key boundary conditions at the interface (y = δ) are:
> 1. **Continuity of velocity:** $v_{1}(\delta) = v_{2}(\delta)$
> 2. **Continuity of shear stress:** $\mu_{1}\dfrac{dv_{1}}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=\delta} = \mu_{2}\dfrac{dv_{2}}{dy}\bigg\vert_{y=\delta}$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of two trains running on parallel tracks at different speeds, separated by a thin common wall (the interface). The wall experiences a shearing force — the slower train drags the faster one back, while the faster train pulls the slower one forward. The interface is the "mediator" that transmits momentum between the two fluids.

In **biological terms**: imagine a microchannel carrying blood plasma (a watery fluid) in the middle, with a layer of red blood cells (RBCs) along the wall — they don't mix with the plasma chemically, but the plasma "drags" the RBCs along, and the RBCs resist the flow. The same physics applies to **two adjacent immiscible Newtonian fluids in a capillary or channel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Immiscible ≠ Incompressible.** The two fluids may individually be incompressible, but the interface itself can deform. For Module 1, we assume a **flat, undeformable interface** (small capillary number, low Reynolds number).

## 1.2 Shell Momentum Balance for Blood Flow in Capillaries

### Formal Definition
A **shell momentum balance** is a method of applying Newton's second law to a thin "shell" or "slab" of fluid within a control volume. Instead of writing the full Navier-Stokes equations, we balance:
**Rate of momentum in − Rate of momentum out + Sum of forces = 0 (steady state)**

For a **cylindrical capillary** (radius $R$, length $L$, pressure drop $\Delta P = P_0 - P_L$), we slice the fluid into concentric cylindrical shells of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. The balance yields the **parabolic velocity profile** characteristic of laminar, fully-developed Newtonian flow — known as **Poiseuille flow**.

In **blood capillaries** (diameter 5–10 μm), RBCs are forced to deform and flow in single file. The plasma forms a thin lubricating layer (the **cell-free layer** or **Fåhræus–Lindqvist layer**) between the RBC and the capillary wall, and the effective viscosity of blood is **reduced** compared to bulk blood.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants (Memorize for KTU):**
> - Typical capillary diameter: **5–10 μm**
> - Typical blood viscosity (bulk): **3–4 cP = 0.003–0.004 Pa·s**
> - Plasma viscosity: **~1.2 cP = 0.0012 Pa·s**
> - Reynolds number in capillary: **Re ≪ 1** (creeping flow)
> - Capillary length scale: **L ≈ 0.5–1 mm**

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. The paste near the wall sticks to the wall (no-slip), the paste in the middle moves fastest, and the paste in between moves at intermediate speeds. Now shrink that tube to 1/100th the width of a hair and fill it with blood — **the same physics holds**, but now the red blood cells behave almost like discrete "particles" sliding through plasma.

## 1.3 Flow in Porous Tissues

### Formal Definition
**Porous tissue** (e.g., tumor interstitium, cartilage, bone, extracellular matrix, scaffolds) is modeled as a **rigid solid matrix with interconnected pores** through which fluid (interstitial fluid, lymph, or plasma filtrate) flows. The flow is governed by **Darcy's Law**:

$$v = -\frac{k}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

where:
- $v$ = superficial (Darcy) velocity [m/s]
- $k$ = **permeability** of the porous medium [m²]
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid [Pa·s]
- $dP/dx$ = pressure gradient [Pa/m]

The **permeability $k$** is a property of the porous structure, not the fluid, and is often estimated using the **Kozeny–Carman equation**:

$$k = \frac{\varepsilon^{3}}{K \cdot S_{0}^{2} \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$$

where $\varepsilon$ = porosity, $S_0$ = specific surface area, and $K$ = Kozeny constant (≈ 5 for most biological tissues).

> [!NOTE]
> **Darcy's Law applies when:**
> - Reynolds number based on pore size is small (Re_pore ≪ 1)
> - Flow is steady and incompressible
> - The porous matrix is rigid and non-deforming
> This is the **defining assumption** for biological micro-porous flow.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a sponge dipped in water. Water seeps through slowly, not because the water is "thick," but because the sponge presents a tortuous, narrow path. The "ease of flow" depends on how big the holes are, how connected they are, and how wiggly the path is — all baked into the **permeability $k$**. In a **tumor**, the interstitium is a "biological sponge" where drug delivery fluid must percolate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shell Momentum Balance — Step-by-Step Logic

### For a Cylindrical Capillary (Newtonian Fluid)
We consider a horizontal cylindrical tube of length $L$ and radius $R$. The fluid enters at $z = 0$ with pressure $P_0$ and exits at $z = L$ with pressure $P_L < P_0$.

**Step 1: Geometry of the Shell**
- Choose a cylindrical shell of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, length $dz$
- The shell is a thin annular ring inside the tube
- Velocity is in the $z$-direction: $v_z(r)$

**Step 2: Momentum Flux Through the Shell**
- Mass flow rate through the shell = $\rho \cdot v_z \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$
- Momentum flux (rate of momentum out at $z + dz$ minus in at $z$) = $0$ for steady, fully-developed flow
- Reason: $v_z$ does not depend on $z$

**Step 3: Forces on the Shell**
- **Pressure force** at $z$: $+P(2\pi r \, dr)$ (pushing right)
- **Pressure force** at $z+dz$: $-[P + dP](2\pi r \, dr)$ (pushing left)
- **Shear force** on inner surface (at $r$): $+\tau_{rz} \cdot (2\pi r \, dz)$
- **Shear force** on outer surface (at $r + dr$): $-[\tau_{rz} + d\tau_{rz}](2\pi (r+dr) \, dz)$

**Step 4: Net Force = 0 (Steady State)**
Summing all forces and setting to zero:

$$\left(\frac{dP}{dz}\right) 2\pi r \, dr = -\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \tau_{rz}\right) 2\pi \, dz$$

**Step 5: Integrate with Newtonian Constitutive Equation**
For a Newtonian fluid: $\tau_{rz} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dr}$ (negative sign because momentum diffuses in the direction of decreasing velocity)

Substituting and integrating twice with B.C.s $v_z(R) = 0$ (no-slip) and $\dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0}$ finite (symmetry at centerline):

$$v_z(r) = \frac{(P_0 - P_L) R^{2}}{4\mu L}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$$

This is the famous **Hagen–Poiseuille velocity profile**.

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions at an Interface (Two Immiscible Fluids)

For two immiscible Newtonian fluids stacked in a channel (e.g., fluid 1 in $0 \le y \le \delta$, fluid 2 in $\delta \le y \le h$), the governing ODE in each layer is:

$$\mu_i \frac{d^{2} v_{i}}{dy^{2}} = \frac{dP}{dz} \quad (i = 1, 2)$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
1. No-slip at the lower wall: $v_1(0) = 0$
2. No-slip at the upper wall: $v_2(h) = 0$
3. **Continuity of velocity at interface:** $v_1(\delta) = v_2(\delta)$
4. **Continuity of shear stress at interface:** $\mu_1 \dfrac{dv_1}{dy}\bigg\vert_{\delta} = \mu_2 \dfrac{dv_2}{dy}\bigg\vert_{\delta}$

This gives **4 linear equations** in **4 unknowns** (two integration constants from each ODE).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it appears in 90% of Module 1 numericals.**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Domain |
|---|----------|----------------|-------|--------|
| 1 | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^{2}}{4\mu L}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Parabolic velocity profile | m/s | Capillary, Newtonian |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille volumetric flow rate | m³/s | Capillary, Newtonian |
| 3 | $\langle v \rangle = \dfrac{R^{2} \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ | Mean (average) velocity = $Q/(\pi R^2)$ | m/s | Capillary, Newtonian |
| 4 | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{R^{2} \Delta P}{4\mu L}$ | Centerline (max) velocity = $2\langle v \rangle$ | m/s | Capillary, Newtonian |
| 5 | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa | Capillary |
| 6 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8\mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | Pressure drop (rearranged) | Pa | Capillary |
| 7 | $v = -\dfrac{k}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | Darcy's Law (porous media) | m/s | Porous tissue |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3}}{K S_{0}^{2}(1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$ | Kozeny–Carman permeability | m² | Porous tissue |
| 9 | $\mu_{app} = \mu_{plasma}\left[1 + (\mu_{0.45} - 1) \cdot H \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{D - 1.1}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Fåhræus–Lindqvist apparent viscosity | Pa·s | Small blood vessels |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} < 2100$ | Reynolds number (laminar limit) | dimensionless | All internal flows |
| 11 | $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa | Newtonian fluid |
| 12 | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | Capillary number (interface) | dimensionless | Two-fluid interface |
| 13 | $v_1(\delta) = v_2(\delta)$ | Velocity continuity at interface | m/s | Two adjacent fluids |
| 14 | $\mu_1 \dfrac{dv_1}{dy}\big\vert_{\delta} = \mu_2 \dfrac{dv_2}{dy}\big\vert_{\delta}$ | Shear stress continuity at interface | Pa | Two adjacent fluids |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these equations show up in real engineering:**
> - Equations 1–6 → Designing microfluidic chips, dialysis membranes, blood oxygenators
> - Equation 7 → Drug delivery to tumors, bioreactor perfusion, groundwater transport
> - Equation 9 → Hemodialysis, microvascular research, blood pump design
> - Equations 13–14 → Lab-on-a-chip multiphase flow, emulsion rheology

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Exam-Ready Reasoning)

- **Why parabolic?** Because pressure-driven viscous flow in a cylinder has a quadratic velocity profile — the wall "holds" the fluid stationary, and pressure pushes hardest at the centerline.
- **Why $R^4$ dependence in $Q$?** A 2× increase in radius gives 16× flow — this is why capillaries (small R) deliver very little flow individually, but billions of them in parallel carry the entire cardiac output.
- **Why does $\mu_{app}$ decrease in capillaries?** RBCs migrate to the centerline (axial migration), leaving a low-viscosity plasma layer near the wall. The wall region dominates the pressure drop, so effective viscosity drops. This is the **Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect**.
- **Why Darcy's Law?** In porous media, momentum is dissipated by viscous drag against the solid matrix. The flow is not parabolic — it is uniform on the macroscopic scale because pressure forces balance viscous drag at every point.
