- **Proteins** separating across membranes (often tracked in mass concentration or mass fraction).
- **Enzymes** catalyzing reactions (conventionally in molarity or molality).

Each context demands a *specific* concentration variable, and KTU examiners expect students to choose correctly and convert fluently between them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rigorous Definitions (Component *A* in a Multicomponent Mixture)

Consider a mixture of $N$ chemical species. Let species *A* be the component of interest. Define:

- $m_A$ = mass of species *A* (in kg or g)
- $n_A$ = number of moles of species *A* (in kmol or mol)
- $\mathcal{N}_A$ = number of molecules of species *A*
- $V$ = total volume of the mixture
- $M_A$ = molecular weight (molar mass) of species *A*, units **kg/kmol** or **g/mol**

### 2.1.1 Mass Concentration ($\rho_A$)

$$\rho_A \equiv \frac{m_A}{V} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}\right]$$

This is essentially the **partial density** of *A*. For a pure substance, $\rho_A = \rho$ (the bulk density).

### 2.1.2 Molar Concentration ($c_A$)

$$c_A \equiv \frac{n_A}{V} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^3}\right]$$

Also called **molarity** (symbol **M**, but KTU prefers $c_A$ in transport equations to avoid confusion with molar mass).

### 2.1.3 Mole Fraction ($x_A$)

$$x_A \equiv \frac{n_A}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

The sum $\sum x_i = 1$ for all species.

### 2.1.4 Mass Fraction ($\omega_A$)

$$\omega_A \equiv \frac{m_A}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i} = \frac{m_A}{m_{\text{total}}} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

The sum $\sum \omega_i = 1$ for all species.

### 2.1.5 Number Density ($n_A$)

$$n_A \equiv \frac{\mathcal{N}_A}{V} \quad \left[\frac{\text{molecules}}{\text{m}^3}\right]$$

Related to molar concentration via **Avogadro's number**, $N_{Av} = 6.022 \times 10^{26}$ molecules/kmol:

$$n_A = N_{Av} \cdot c_A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Link:** Mass concentration and molar concentration are connected through the molecular weight: $\rho_A = M_A \cdot c_A$ (where $M_A$ is in **kg/kmol** to keep SI consistency).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **definitive cheat sheet** for Module 3 definitions. Master the units and conversion factors — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

| **Concentration Variable** | **Symbol** | **Definition** | **SI Units** | **Common Field of Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass concentration | $\rho_A$ | $m_A / V$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | Solute mass tracking, protein solutions |
| Molar concentration | $c_A$ | $n_A / V$ | $\text{kmol/m}^3$ | Diffusion equations, reaction kinetics |
| Mole fraction | $x_A$ | $n_A / n_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Vapor–liquid equilibrium, ideal solutions |
| Mass fraction | $\omega_A$ | $m_A / m_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion, biomass analysis |
| Number density | $n_A$ | $\mathcal{N}_A / V$ | $\text{molecules/m}^3$ | Kinetic theory, gas-phase molecular flux |
| Molality | $b_A$ | $n_A / m_{\text{solvent}}$ | $\text{mol/kg}$ | Colligative properties, electrolyte solutions |
| Partial pressure | $p_A$ | $y_A \cdot P_{\text{total}}$ | Pa or bar | Gas-phase diffusion (O₂, CO₂ in lungs) |

---

## 2.3 Conversion Logic (How to Move Between Definitions)

The conversion graph is **bidirectional** and **closed** — any concentration can be reached from any other.

**Core conversion identities:**

$$\rho_A = M_A \cdot c_A \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad c_A = \frac{\rho_A}{M_A}$$

$$x_A = \frac{c_A}{c_{\text{total}}} = \frac{c_A}{\sum_i c_i} \quad \text{(for liquids)}$$

$$\omega_A = \frac{\rho_A}{\rho_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\rho_A}{\sum_i \rho_i}$$

$$x_A = \frac{\omega_A / M_A}{\sum_i \omega_i / M_i} \quad \text{(mass fraction } \to \text{ mole fraction)}$$

$$\omega_A = \frac{x_A \cdot M_A}{\sum_i x_i \cdot M_i} \quad \text{(mole fraction } \to \text{ mass fraction)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a **hemodialysis module**, the dialysate's glucose $c_A$ must be converted to $\omega_A$ to compare with membrane manufacturer's specifications given in mass fraction. The membrane flux equation, however, requires $c_A$ — so two conversions are needed in a single problem!

---

## 2.4 Average Molecular Weight of a Mixture

In multicomponent transport, the **mixture-averaged molecular weight** $M_{\text{avg}}$ is critical:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i M_i = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \omega_i / M_i}$$

This is used to relate the **total mass density** and **total molar density**:

$$\rho_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{avg}} \cdot c_{\text{total}}$$

where $c_{\text{total}} = c_A + c_B + c_C + \ldots$ in kmol/m³.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relating Mass Concentration to Molar Concentration

**Starting point:** A quantity $n_A$ moles of species *A* occupies volume $V$ of a mixture.

**Step 1 — Express the mass of species A.**

The mass of $n_A$ moles of species *A*, each of molar mass $M_A$, is:

$$m_A = n_A \cdot M_A$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by volume V.**

$$\frac{m_A}{V} = \frac{n_A \cdot M_A}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the concentration variables.**

By definition, the left-hand side is $\rho_A$ (mass concentration), and the right-hand side contains $n_A / V = c_A$. So:

$$\rho_A = M_A \cdot c_A$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the molar concentration.**

$$c_A = \frac{\rho_A}{M_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** If you dissolve 180 g of glucose ($M_{\text{glucose}} = 180 \text{ g/mol}$) in 1 L of water, the *mass concentration* is 180 g/L, and the *molar concentration* is exactly 1 mol/L (assuming volume is unchanged by solute — a dilute solution approximation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Converting Mass Fraction to Mole Fraction

**Goal:** Given $\omega_A$, $\omega_B$, ... and $M_A$, $M_B$, ..., derive $x_A$.

**Step 1 — Choose a basis.**
Take **1 kg of mixture** as the basis. Then the mass of species *i* is simply $\omega_i$ (in kg).

**Step 2 — Convert each mass to moles.**

$$n_i = \frac{m_i}{M_i} = \frac{\omega_i}{M_i}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total moles.**

$$n_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\omega_i}{M_i}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of mole fraction.**

$$x_A = \frac{n_A}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\omega_A / M_A}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \omega_i / M_i}$$

This is the canonical KTU 2024 formula. The analogous reverse conversion is:

$$\omega_A = \frac{x_A \cdot M_A}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot M_i}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relating Number Density to Molar Concentration

**Step 1 — Express Avogadro's number.**
Avogadro's number in SI-consistent form: $N_{Av} = 6.022 \times 10^{26}$ molecules per **kmol**.

**Step 2 — Start with molar concentration.**
A molar concentration of $c_A$ kmol/m³ means there are $c_A$ "Avogadro bundles" of *A* per m³.

**Step 3 — Multiply by Avogadro's number.**

$$n_A = N_{Av} \cdot c_A$$

$$\boxed{n_A = 6.022 \times 10^{26} \cdot c_A \;\; \text{molecules/m}^3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** In kinetic theory of gases, the **mean free path** and **collision frequency** are derived from $n_A$, not $c_A$. KTU problems on gas diffusion frequently require this conversion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Conversions

### Example A (KTU Style): Aqueous NaCl Solution

A saline solution contains **58.5 g of NaCl** dissolved in water to make a **total volume of 1.0 L**. The total mass of solution is 1.0585 kg. Molecular weights: $M_{\text{NaCl}} = 58.5 \text{ kg/kmol}$, $M_{\text{water}} = 18.0 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

**Compute (a) $c_{\text{NaCl}}$, (b) $\rho_{\text{NaCl}}$, (c) $\omega_{\text{NaCl}}$, (d) $x_{\text{NaCl}}$.**

**Solution:**

(a) Moles of NaCl:

$$n_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{58.5 \text{ g}}{58.5 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.0 \text{ mol} = 0.001 \text{ kmol}$$

Molar concentration:

$$c_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{n_{\text{NaCl}}}{V} = \frac{0.001 \text{ kmol}}{0.001 \text{ m}^3} = 1.0 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

(b) Mass concentration:

$$\rho_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{m_{\text{NaCl}}}{V} = \frac{0.0585 \text{ kg}}{0.001 \text{ m}^3} = 58.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

(c) Mass fraction:

$$\omega_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{0.0585 \text{ kg}}{1.0585 \text{ kg}} = 0.0553 \;\; (\text{about } 5.53\%)$$

(d) Moles of water:

$$n_{\text{water}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g} - 58.5 \text{ g}}{18.0 \text{ g/mol}} = \frac{941.5}{18.0} = 52.31 \text{ mol}$$

Total moles:

$$n_{\text{total}} = 1.0 + 52.31 = 53.31 \text{ mol}$$

Mole fraction:

$$x_{\text{NaCl}} = \frac{1.0}{53.31} = 0.01876 \;\; (\text{about } 1.88\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $\omega_{\text{NaCl}} = 5.53\%$ but $x_{\text{NaCl}} = 1.88\%$. Mole fraction is *smaller* because water is much lighter per mole — there are many more moles of water than of NaCl.

---

### Example B: Gas-Phase Concentration in the Alveoli

Inspired air in alveoli is approximately: $y_{O_2} = 0.14$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.055$, $y_{N_2} = 0.805$ (mole fractions). Total pressure $P = 101325$ Pa, $T = 310$ K. $M_{O_2} = 32$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol. $R = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$.

**Compute (a) $c_{O_2}$, (b) $c_{\text{total}}$, (c) $\omega_{O_2}$.**

**Solution:**

(a) Using ideal gas law in molar form: $c = P/(RT)$:

$$c_{\text{total}} = \frac{101325}{8314 \times 310} = \frac{101325}{2{,}577{,}340} = 0.03931 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

$$c_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot c_{\text{total}} = 0.14 \times 0.03931 = 0.005503 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

(b) Already computed: $c_{\text{total}} = 0.03931$ kmol/m³.

(c) First, the average molecular weight of the alveolar mixture:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.14 \times 32) + (0.055 \times 44) + (0.805 \times 28) = 4.48 + 2.42 + 22.54 = 29.44 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

Total mass density:

$$\rho_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{avg}} \cdot c_{\text{total}} = 29.44 \times 0.03931 = 1.157 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass density of O₂:

$$\rho_{O_2} = M_{O_2} \cdot c_{O_2} = 32 \times 0.005503 = 0.1761 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass fraction of O₂:

$$\omega_{O_2} = \frac{0.1761}{1.157} = 0.1522 \;\; (\text{about } 15.2\%)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Concentration Conversion Network

The diagram below maps the **interconversion graph** between the five major concentration variables. It is a "switchboard" the student must memorize.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM["Mass Concentration rho_A"]:::core
    nodeC["Molar Concentration c_A"]:::core
    nodeX["Mole Fraction x_A"]:::core
    nodeW["Mass Fraction omega_A"]:::core
    nodeN["Number Density n_A"]:::core
    nodeP["Partial Pressure p_A"]:::extra
    nodeB["Molality b_A"]:::extra
    nodeMAvg["Mixture Avg Molar Mass M_avg"]:::support
    nodeCAvogadro["Avogadro Number N_Av"]:::support

    nodeM -- "rho_A = M_A x c_A" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "c_A = rho_A / M_A" --> nodeM
    nodeC -- "x_A = c_A / c_total" --> nodeX
    nodeX -- "c_A = x_A x c_total" --> nodeC
    nodeC -- "n_A = N_Av x c_A" --> nodeN
    nodeN -- "c_A = n_A / N_Av" --> nodeC
    nodeX -- "omega_A = x_A M_A / sum x_i M_i" --> nodeW
    nodeW -- "x_A = omega_A / M_A / sum omega_i / M_i" --> nodeX
    nodeM -- "omega_A = rho_A / rho_total" --> nodeW
    nodeW -- "rho_A = omega_A x rho_total" --> nodeM
    nodeC -- "p_A = c_A R T" --> nodeP
    nodeP -- "c_A = p_A / R T" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "b_A = n_A / m_solvent" --> nodeC
    nodeX --> nodeMAvg
    nodeW --> nodeMAvg

    classDef core fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef extra fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef support fill:#475569,stroke:#475569,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Choice of Concentration Variable by Application

In engineering practice, the choice of concentration variable is dictated by the *governing physical law*. The following block diagram maps engineering scenarios to the appropriate concentration variable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Transport Problem in Biological System"]:::head
    nodeDecide{"What physical law governs?"}:::branch
    nodeDiff["Ficks Law of Diffusion"]:::app
    nodeReact["Chemical Reaction Kinetics"]:::app
    nodePhase["Phase Equilibrium VLE"]:::app
    nodeCombus["Combustion / Mass Tracking"]:::app
    nodeGas["Gas-Phase Kinetic Theory"]:::app
    nodeCollig["Colligative Properties"]:::app

    nodeC1["Use c_A (Molar Concentration)"]:::rec
    nodeC2["Use c_A or p_A"]:::rec
    nodeC3["Use x_A (Mole Fraction)"]:::rec
    nodeC4["Use omega_A (Mass Fraction)"]:::rec
    nodeC5["Use n_A (Number Density)"]:::rec
    nodeC6["Use b_A (Molality)"]:::rec

    nodeStart --> nodeDecide
    nodeDecide -- "Molecular diffusion" --> nodeDiff --> nodeC1
    nodeDecide -- "Reaction rate" --> nodeReact --> nodeC2
    nodeDecide -- "Raoults Law, Henrys Law" --> nodePhase --> nodeC3
    nodeDecide -- "Total mass balance" --> nodeCombus --> nodeC4
    nodeDecide -- "Mean free path, collisions" --> nodeGas --> nodeC5
    nodeDecide -- "Boiling point elevation" --> nodeCollig --> nodeC6

    classDef head fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef app fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rec fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Visual Schematic — Concentrations in a Binary Liquid Solution

The following block schematic illustrates how the five concentration variables **coexist** for the same physical solution, each describing a different *aspect* of the same physical reality.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOLUTION["Aqueous Glucose Solution at 25C"]
        direction TB
        nodeSol["V = 1.0 L total"]:::sys
        nodeSolDesc["Contains 18.0 g glucose in 1.0 L water"]:::sys
    end

    nodeSol --> nodeM2["rho_glucose = 18.0 kg/m^3"]:::core
    nodeSol --> nodeC2["c_glucose = 0.10 kmol/m^3"]:::core
    nodeSol --> nodeW2["omega_glucose = 0.018"]:::core
    nodeSol --> nodeX2["x_glucose = 0.00180"]:::core
    nodeSol --> nodeN2["n_glucose = 6.022 x 10^25 /m^3"]:::core

    classDef sys fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following concentration terms used in transport processes, giving their SI units:**
(a) Molar concentration
(b) Mass fraction
(c) Mole fraction

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Molar concentration ($c_A$):** The amount of substance (in moles) of component *A* per unit volume of the mixture.

$$c_A = \frac{n_A}{V}, \quad \text{Units: kmol/m}^3$$

**(b) Mass fraction ($\omega_A$):** The ratio of the mass of component *A* to the total mass of the mixture.

$$\omega_A = \frac{m_A}{m_{\text{total}}}, \quad \text{Units: dimensionless}$$

**(c) Mole fraction ($x_A$):** The ratio of the number of moles of component *A* to the total number of moles of all components in the mixture.

$$x_A = \frac{n_A}{\sum_i n_i}, \quad \text{Units: dimensionless}$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the relationship between mass concentration and molar concentration. Why is this relationship important in biological transport equations?**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is:

$$\rho_A = M_A \cdot c_A$$

where $M_A$ is the molecular weight of species *A* in kg/kmol.

**Importance in biological transport:**
- Fick's First Law of Diffusion in molar form requires $c_A$ (because reactions are stoichiometric in moles).
- Hemodialysis and membrane transport problems often report *mass* concentrations (e.g., glucose in g/dL) in clinical settings, but the *transport equation* uses $c_A$. The conversion via $M_A$ is therefore essential.
- The mass density of a species is a direct input to Navier–Stokes continuity equations for mass transport in biological fluids.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### `Question A — Module 3 Choice 1 (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define mass concentration, molar concentration, and mole fraction for a multicomponent mixture. Show the derivation of the conversion formula from **mass fraction to mole fraction** in an *N*-component system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fermentation broth contains three species in aqueous solution. The mass fractions are: $\omega_{\text{glucose}} = 0.10$, $\omega_{\text{enzyme}} = 0.02$, $\omega_{\text{water}} = 0.88$. Molecular weights: $M_{\text{glucose}} = 180$ kg/kmol, $M_{\text{enzyme}} = 50{,}000$ kg/kmol, $M_{\text{water}} = 18$ kg/kmol. If the total volume of broth is 2.0 m³ and the broth density is 1040 kg/m³, compute:
- (i) the total mass of each species
- (ii) the mole fraction of glucose
- (iii) the molar concentration of glucose **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Mass concentration $\rho_A$:** $\rho_A = m_A / V$ with units kg/m³.
- **Molar concentration $c_A$:** $c_A = n_A / V$ with units kmol/m³.
- **Mole fraction $x_A$:** $x_A = n_A / \sum_i n_i$, dimensionless.

**Derivation of mass fraction → mole fraction:**

Basis: 1 kg of mixture.

Mass of species $i$ = $\omega_i$ kg.

Moles of species $i$ = $\omega_i / M_i$ kmol.

Total moles = $\sum_i (\omega_i / M_i)$.

Therefore:

$$x_A = \frac{\omega_A / M_A}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \omega_i / M_i}$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Total mass of broth = $\rho V = 1040 \times 2.0 = 2080$ kg.

(i) Mass of each species:
- $m_{\text{glucose}} = 0.10 \times 2080 = 208$ kg
- $m_{\text{enzyme}} = 0.02 \times 2080 = 41.6$ kg
- $m_{\text{water}} = 0.88 \times 2080 = 1830.4$ kg

(ii) Moles of each:
- $n_{\text{glucose}} = 208 / 180 = 1.1556$ kmol
- $n_{\text{enzyme}} = 41.6 / 50000 = 0.000832$ kmol
- $n_{\text{water}} = 1830.4 / 18 = 101.689$ kmol

Total moles = $1.1556 + 0.000832 + 101.689 = 102.846$ kmol.

$$x_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{1.1556}{102.846} = 0.01124 \;\; (1.124\%)$$

(iii) Molar concentration of glucose:

$$c_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{n_{\text{glucose}}}{V} = \frac{1.1556}{2.0} = 0.5778 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

---

### `Question B — Module 3 Choice 2 (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of suitable equations, explain the difference between **molarity**, **molality**, and **mole fraction** as concentration measures. State one engineering situation where each is preferred, justifying the choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial bioreactor operates at 1.2 atm total pressure and 37 °C. The gas-phase mole fractions are: $y_{O_2} = 0.21$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.04$, $y_{N_2} = 0.75$. Compute:
- (i) the total molar concentration
- (ii) the partial pressure of O₂
- (iii) the number density of O₂ molecules
- (iv) the mass density of the gas mixture, given $M_{O_2} = 32$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ kg/kmol. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Quantity | Definition | Units | Preferred Situation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molarity ($c_A$) | $n_A / V$ of solution | kmol/m³ | Laboratory bio-reactions at constant volume |
| Molality ($b_A$) | $n_A / m_{\text{solvent}}$ | mol/kg | Temperature-varying systems, electrolyte solutions (colligative properties) |
| Mole fraction ($x_A$) | $n_A / n_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Phase equilibrium (Raoult's, Henry's, Dalton's laws) |

**Justifications:**
- *Molarity* is convenient in clinical labs where solution volume is fixed.
- *Molality* is preferred when the solution is heated or cooled, because mass of solvent is invariant to temperature, unlike volume.
- *Mole fraction* is used in vapor–liquid equilibrium because Raoult's and Henry's laws are expressed naturally in mole fractions.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Given: $P = 1.2 \text{ atm} = 1.2 \times 101325 = 121590$ Pa; $T = 37 + 273 = 310$ K; $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K).

(i) Total molar concentration (ideal gas law: $PV = nRT \Rightarrow c = P/RT$):

$$c_{\text{total}} = \frac{121590}{8314 \times 310} = \frac{121590}{2{,}577{,}340} = 0.04718 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

(ii) Partial pressure of O₂ (Dalton's law):

$$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P = 0.21 \times 121590 = 25534 \text{ Pa} \;(\approx 0.252 \text{ atm})$$

(iii) Number density of O₂:

$$n_{O_2} = N_{Av} \cdot c_{O_2} = N_{Av} \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot c_{\text{total}}$$

$$c_{O_2} = 0.21 \times 0.04718 = 0.009908 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$

$$n_{O_2} = 6.022 \times 10^{26} \times 0.009908 = 5.967 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules/m}^3$$

(iv) Mass density of the gas mixture — first, average molecular weight:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.21 \times 32) + (0.04 \times 44) + (0.75 \times 28) = 6.72 + 1.76 + 21.0 = 29.48 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

$$\rho_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{avg}} \cdot c_{\text{total}} = 29.48 \times 0.04718 = 1.391 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $M_A$ in g/mol but using $c_A$ in kmol/m³. The product $\rho_A = M_A c_A$ will be off by a factor of 1000. **Always write $M_A$ in kg/kmol when working in SI.**
> 2. **Confusing molarity with molality:** Molarity = moles per *liter of solution*; molality = moles per *kg of solvent*. In dilute aqueous solutions the numerical values are similar but the *physical meaning* is different.
> 3. **Forgetting $N_{Av}$ consistency:** Avogadro's number is **$6.022 \times 10^{26}$** molecules per **kmol** in SI, **not** $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ per mol. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mole fraction vs. mass fraction conversion shortcut:** Students often write $x_A \approx \omega_A \cdot (M_B / M_A)$ for binary mixtures. This is a *rough approximation* and is *only valid* when component B is in huge excess. KTU requires the full summation formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Definition of Concentrations**

- ☐ **Five core concentration variables** to memorize: $\rho_A$, $c_A$, $x_A$, $\omega_A$, $n_A$.
- ☐ **SI units to remember cold:** kg/m³ (mass conc.), kmol/m³ (molar conc.), dimensionless (fractions), molecules/m³ (number density).
- ☐ **Master identity:** $\rho_A = M_A \cdot c_A$ — this single equation unlocks half of Module 3.
- ☐ **Avogadro link:** $n_A = N_{Av} \cdot c_A$ with $N_{Av} = 6.022 \times 10^{26}$ /kmol.
- ☐ **Ideal gas link:** $c_A = p_A / (RT)$ — connects gas-phase concentration to partial pressure.
- ☐ **Mole-fraction closure:** $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum \omega_i = 1$. Always use this to *check* a numerical answer.
- ☐ **Conversion to/from mass fraction:**

$$x_A = \frac{\omega_A / M_A}{\sum_i \omega_i / M_i} \quad ; \quad \omega_A = \frac{x_A M_A}{\sum_i x_i M_i}$$

- ☐ **Mixture-averaged molecular weight:** $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum x_i M_i = \left( \sum \omega_i / M_i \right)^{-1}$.
- ☐ **Molarity vs. Molality:** Molarity depends on *volume* (temperature-sensitive); molality depends on *mass of solvent* (temperature-invariant). Pick molality for non-isothermal systems.
- ☐ **Biological context:** Use $c_A$ for diffusion/reaction problems; use $\omega_A$ for combustion and biomass tracking; use $x_A$ for gas–liquid equilibrium in lungs and gills.
- ☐ **Common exam trap:** KTU often gives a problem where you must do *two* conversions to reach the final answer. Always identify the **starting variable** and **target variable** in the conversion graph (Section 4.1).
- ☐ **Sanity checks before submitting:** Mole fractions must be in $[0,1]$, sum to 1, and be dimensionless. Mass concentration must be ≤ total density. These two checks catch ~80% of arithmetic errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocities and Mass/Molar Fluxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Velocities and Mass/Molar Fluxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Velocities and Fluxes in Biological Systems

In any biological transport process (oxygen transport across alveolar membrane, nutrient absorption in the intestine, drug diffusion through tissue, glucose transport in blood capillaries), we deal with **multicomponent mixtures**. These mixtures contain several chemical species moving at different velocities simultaneously.

To describe the transport of these species rigorously, we need **two key concepts**:
1. A **reference velocity** to describe the average motion of the mixture
2. A **flux** to describe the rate at which a particular species is transported

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In biological systems, the standard reference frames are the **mass-average velocity (v)** and the **molar-average velocity (v\*)**. Choosing the wrong reference frame for a given problem is one of the most common sources of error in transport calculations.

---

## 1.2 Core Definitions (KTU Terminology)

### A. Concentrations

**Mass concentration (ρᵢ):** Mass of species *i* per unit volume of mixture
$$\rho_i = \frac{m_i}{V} \quad \text{[units: kg/m}^3\text{]}$$

**Total mass density (ρ):**
$$\rho = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i$$

**Molar concentration of species i (Cᵢ):** Moles of species *i* per unit volume of mixture
$$C_i = \frac{n_i}{V} \quad \text{[units: kmol/m}^3\text{ or mol/L]}$$

**Total molar concentration (C):**
$$C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$$

**Mass fraction (ωᵢ):** Fraction of total mass contributed by species *i*
$$\omega_i = \frac{\rho_i}{\rho}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i = 1$$

**Mole fraction (xᵢ):** Fraction of total moles contributed by species *i*
$$x_i = \frac{C_i}{C}, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 1$$

### B. Reference Velocities

**Mass-average velocity (v):** The velocity of the center of mass of the fluid
$$\mathbf{v} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i \mathbf{v_i}}{\rho} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i \mathbf{v_i}$$

**Molar-average velocity (v\*):** The velocity defined on a molar basis
$$\mathbf{v^*} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \mathbf{v_i}}{C} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \mathbf{v_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of v vs v\*:**
> - **v** is the velocity "felt" by a kilogram of mixture.
> - **v\*** is the velocity "felt" by a mole of mixture.
> - For **dilute solutions** (e.g., oxygen in blood, drugs in plasma), v ≈ v\* because the solvent dominates.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Highway Analogy 🚗

Imagine a multi-lane highway with cars (species A) and motorcycles (species B) moving simultaneously.

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---------|-------------------|
| **Species velocity (vᵢ)** | Speed of an individual vehicle type |
| **Mass-average velocity (v)** | Average speed weighted by vehicle weight (a truck counts more than a bike) |
| **Molar-average velocity (v\*)** | Average speed weighted by number of vehicles (a bike counts the same as a truck) |
| **Mass flux (nᵢ)** | Kilograms of vehicle *i* passing a checkpoint per second |
| **Molar flux (Nᵢ)** | Number of vehicles *i* passing a checkpoint per second |
| **Diffusive flux (jᵢ, Jᵢ\*)** | The "spreading out" of vehicles from a crowded lane to an empty one |
| **Convective flux** | The flow of vehicles with the overall traffic stream |

> [!TIP]
> **Why two velocities?** When a chemical reaction occurs (e.g., O₂ binding to hemoglobin), mass is conserved but moles are NOT (1 mole O₂ + Hb → 1 mole HbO₂). This is why we need both frameworks in biological transport.

---

## 1.4 Types of Fluxes

| Flux Type | Symbol | Frame | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------------|
| **Mass flux** | **nᵢ** | Laboratory (fixed) | kg of *i*/(m²·s) crossing a stationary plane |
| **Molar flux** | **Nᵢ** | Laboratory (fixed) | mol of *i*/(m²·s) crossing a stationary plane |
| **Diffusive mass flux** | **jᵢ** | Mass-average | Mass flux relative to v |
| **Diffusive molar flux** | **Jᵢ\*** | Molar-average | Molar flux relative to v\* |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Classical Flux Equations

These are the **workhorse equations** of mass transfer. You will use them in nearly every problem.

### Flux Equation 1: Mass Flux of Species i
$$\mathbf{n_i} = \rho_i \mathbf{v_i} = \mathbf{j_i} + \rho_i \mathbf{v}$$

where the **diffusive mass flux** is defined as
$$\mathbf{j_i} = \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v})$$

### Flux Equation 2: Molar Flux of Species i
$$\mathbf{N_i} = C_i \mathbf{v_i} = \mathbf{J_i^*} + C_i \mathbf{v^*}$$

where the **diffusive molar flux** is defined as
$$\mathbf{J_i^*} = C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Split:**
> Total Flux = **Convective Flux** (movement of bulk) + **Diffusive Flux** (movement relative to bulk)
> This decomposition is at the heart of the **Stefan-Maxwell equations** and **Fick's law for multicomponent systems**.

---

## 2.2 Why Two Frameworks? (Biological Examples)

| Biological Scenario | Preferred Reference | Reason |
|--------------------|--------------------|---------| 
| **Oxygen diffusion through tissue** | Molar (v\*) | Concentration in mol/L, reaction stoichiometry |
| **Blood flow in arteries (Newtonian)** | Mass (v) | Fluid mechanics of incompressible flow |
| **Hemodialysis (urea removal)** | Molar (v\*) | Solute transport, clearance defined in mol |
| **Heat/mass analogy in bio-heat** | Mass (v) | Energy balances are mass-based |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Velocities and Mass/Molar Fluxes

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table.** These are the equations that will appear in at least one question in every KTU ESE paper.

| # | Quantity | Equation | SI Units | Frame |
|---|----------|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | Total mass density | $\rho = \sum_i \rho_i$ | kg/m³ | — |
| 2 | Total molar concentration | $C = \sum_i C_i$ | kmol/m³ | — |
| 3 | Mass fraction | $\omega_i = \rho_i / \rho$ | — | — |
| 4 | Mole fraction | $x_i = C_i / C$ | — | — |
| 5 | Mass-average velocity | $\mathbf{v} = \sum_i \omega_i \mathbf{v_i}$ | m/s | Mass |
| 6 | Molar-average velocity | $\mathbf{v^*} = \sum_i x_i \mathbf{v_i}$ | m/s | Molar |
| 7 | Mass flux | $\mathbf{n_i} = \rho_i \mathbf{v_i}$ | kg/(m²·s) | Lab |
| 8 | Molar flux | $\mathbf{N_i} = C_i \mathbf{v_i}$ | kmol/(m²·s) | Lab |
| 9 | Diffusive mass flux | $\mathbf{j_i} = \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v})$ | kg/(m²·s) | Mass |
| 10 | Diffusive molar flux | $\mathbf{J_i^*} = C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*})$ | kmol/(m²·s) | Molar |
| 11 | Diffusive flux sum (mass) | $\sum_i \mathbf{j_i} = 0$ | — | — |
| 12 | Diffusive flux sum (molar) | $\sum_i \mathbf{J_i^*} \neq 0$ in general | — | — |
| 13 | Fick's first law (binary) | $\mathbf{J_A^*} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A$ | kmol/(m²·s) | Molar |
| 14 | Fick's law (mass basis) | $\mathbf{j_A} = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla \omega_A$ | kg/(m²·s) | Mass |
| 15 | Convective molar flux | $C_i \mathbf{v^*} = x_i \sum_i \mathbf{N_i}$ | kmol/(m²·s) | Molar |

---

## 2.4 Useful Conversions and Constraints

**Constraint 1:** Sum of all mass fractions equals unity.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \nabla \omega_i = 0$$

**Constraint 2:** Sum of all mole fractions equals unity.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \nabla x_i = 0$$

**Constraint 3:** Diffusive mass fluxes always sum to zero.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{j_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v}) = \rho \mathbf{v} - \rho \mathbf{v} = 0$$

**Constraint 4:** Diffusive molar fluxes sum to **M_v ΣNᵢ**, where M_v is the mean molar mass.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{J_i^*} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*}) = C\mathbf{v} - C\mathbf{v^*}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This is the reason why **mass-average** diffusive fluxes add to zero, but **molar-average** diffusive fluxes do not — a favourite conceptual question!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Drug Delivery Modeling:** Predicting the molar flux of an anti-cancer drug through tumor tissue requires **v\*** as the reference because the drug concentration is in mol/L.
2. **Artificial Kidney Design:** Hemodialysis modules are designed using molar flux equations for urea and creatinine removal.
3. **Oxygenators (Heart-Lung Machine):** Oxygen transfer rates in extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) use molar flux with v\* framework.
4. **Tissue Engineering Scaffolds:** Mass transport of nutrients into 3D-printed scaffolds uses both frameworks depending on whether the scaffold is being treated as a solute (molar) or polymer (mass).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Flux Decomposition (nᵢ = jᵢ + ρᵢv)

This is the foundational derivation. Every other flux equation in this module builds on it.

### Step 1: Start with the definition of mass flux relative to a fixed laboratory frame.

By definition, the absolute mass flux of species *i* across a stationary plane is its mass density multiplied by its absolute velocity:

$$\mathbf{n_i} = \rho_i \mathbf{v_i}$$

This is the flux a stationary observer (e.g., a probe on the alveolar wall) would measure.

### Step 2: Introduce a "trick" — add and subtract the mass-average velocity.

We write the absolute velocity of species *i* as:

$$\mathbf{v_i} = (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{v}$$

where:
- $(\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v})$ = velocity of species *i* relative to the center-of-mass motion
- $\mathbf{v}$ = velocity of the center of mass itself

### Step 3: Multiply by ρᵢ to get mass flux.

Multiplying both sides by $\rho_i$:

$$\rho_i \mathbf{v_i} = \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v}) + \rho_i \mathbf{v}$$

### Step 4: Recognize the left side as the absolute mass flux and define jᵢ.

The left side is exactly $\mathbf{n_i}$, and we define the **diffusive mass flux** as:

$$\mathbf{j_i} \equiv \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v})$$

### Step 5: Write the final decomposed form.

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n_i} = \mathbf{j_i} + \rho_i \mathbf{v}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The total mass flux of species *i* is the sum of (a) the flux due to bulk motion of the mixture (convective) and (b) the flux of *i* relative to the bulk (diffusive). ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Molar Flux Decomposition (Nᵢ = Jᵢ\* + Cᵢv\*)

The derivation follows an identical logic but in the molar domain.

### Step 1: Absolute molar flux of species i.

By definition, the absolute molar flux is:

$$\mathbf{N_i} = C_i \mathbf{v_i}$$

### Step 2: Decompose vᵢ around v\*.

Add and subtract the molar-average velocity:

$$\mathbf{v_i} = (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*}) + \mathbf{v^*}$$

### Step 3: Multiply by Cᵢ.

$$C_i \mathbf{v_i} = C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*}) + C_i \mathbf{v^*}$$

### Step 4: Define the diffusive molar flux and write the result.

With $\mathbf{J_i^*} \equiv C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*})$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{N_i} = \mathbf{J_i^*} + C_i \mathbf{v^*}}$$

> **Key Difference from the Mass Case:** The convective term is $C_i \mathbf{v^*}$, not $C_i \mathbf{v}$. This is what makes molar-flux problems harder than mass-flux problems. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Relationship Between v and v\*

This is an **important bridge** between the two reference frames.

### Step 1: Start from definitions.

$$\mathbf{v} = \frac{\sum_i \rho_i \mathbf{v_i}}{\rho}, \qquad \mathbf{v^*} = \frac{\sum_i C_i \mathbf{v_i}}{C}$$

### Step 2: Convert $\mathbf{N_i} = C_i \mathbf{v_i}$ to mass terms.

Using $C_i = \rho_i / M_i$ (where $M_i$ is the molar mass of species *i* in kg/kmol):

$$C_i \mathbf{v_i} = \frac{\rho_i}{M_i} \mathbf{v_i}$$

### Step 3: Sum over all species to get an expression linking v and v\*.

$$C \mathbf{v^*} = \sum_i C_i \mathbf{v_i} = \sum_i \frac{\rho_i}{M_i} \mathbf{v_i}$$

Now, we also know:
$$C \mathbf{v^*} = \sum_i \frac{\rho_i}{M_i} \mathbf{v_i} = \sum_i \frac{\rho_i}{M_i} \left[\frac{\mathbf{j_i}}{\rho_i} + \mathbf{v}\right]$$

### Step 4: Simplify.

$$C \mathbf{v^*} = \sum_i \frac{\mathbf{j_i}}{M_i} + \mathbf{v} \sum_i \frac{\rho_i}{M_i}$$

### Step 5: Recognize the rightmost sum as C.

$$\sum_i \frac{\rho_i}{M_i} = \sum_i C_i = C$$

So the equation becomes:
$$C \mathbf{v^*} = \sum_i \frac{\mathbf{j_i}}{M_i} + C \mathbf{v}$$

### Step 6: Final relationship.

$$\boxed{\mathbf{v^*} - \mathbf{v} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\mathbf{j_i}}{M_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The two reference frames coincide when diffusion is negligible ($\mathbf{j_i} \to 0$). They differ only when there is net mass diffusion — which is precisely when multicomponent effects matter. This is the mathematical reason why **dilute solutions** allow us to use either frame interchangeably.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Binary System — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMC)

Consider a binary mixture of A and B where for every mole of A moving one way, one mole of B moves the opposite way. This is the classical **Stefan diffusion** problem, common in **lung alveoli** (O₂ in, CO₂ out) and **catalytic reactions**.

### Condition:
$$\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{N_B} = -\mathbf{N_A}$$

### Step 1: Start with the molar flux equation.

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A + x_A (\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B})$$

### Step 2: Apply the EMC condition.

Since $\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B} = 0$:

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A + x_A (0)$$

### Step 3: Result.

$$\boxed{\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}}$$

> **KTU Insight:** Under EMC, there is no net molar flow, and the convective term vanishes. This is why EMC is the *simplest case* in mass transfer — and a guaranteed exam problem.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Binary System — Unimolar Diffusion (A through stagnant B)

This applies when species B is **non-diffusing** (e.g., water vapor evaporating into stagnant air, oxygen diffusing through stagnant tissue). A classic biological example: **oxygen transport from alveoli to capillary blood** where nitrogen does not cross the membrane.

### Condition:
$$\mathbf{N_B} = 0$$

### Step 1: Write the general flux equation.

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A + x_A (\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B})$$

### Step 2: Apply $\mathbf{N_B} = 0$.

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A + x_A \mathbf{N_A}$$

### Step 3: Collect N_A terms on the left side.

$$\mathbf{N_A} - x_A \mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A$$

$$\mathbf{N_A} (1 - x_A) = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A$$

### Step 4: Recognize $1 - x_A = x_B$.

$$\mathbf{N_A} x_B = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A$$

### Step 5: Final result.

$$\boxed{\mathbf{N_A} = -\frac{C D_{AB}}{x_B} \frac{dx_A}{dz}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the extra $1/x_B$ factor?** Because while A is being transported, it must "push through" the stagnant B. The smaller $x_B$ is (i.e., dilute A), the less the resistance — and the higher the flux for a given gradient.

---

## 3.6 Summary: Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix to choose the right equation in the exam:**

| Physical Situation | Key Condition | Resulting Equation | Example |
|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|---------|
| Both A and B diffuse independently | None (general) | $N_A = -CD_{AB}\nabla x_A + x_A(N_A+N_B)$ | Evaporation of a binary liquid |
| Equimolar counter-diffusion | $N_A + N_B = 0$ | $N_A = -CD_{AB}\nabla x_A$ | Catalytic surface, alveolar gas exchange |
| A through stagnant B | $N_B = 0$ | $N_A = -\frac{CD_{AB}}{1-x_A}\nabla x_A$ | O₂ into stagnant tissue, water into air |
| Dilute solution | $x_A \ll 1$ | $N_A = -CD_{AB}\nabla x_A$ | Drug in plasma, O₂ in blood |
| Pure diffusion, no bulk flow | $\mathbf{v} = 0$ | $N_A = J_A^*$ | Stop-flow diffusion experiment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Velocities and Fluxes

This block diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of velocities and fluxes in a multicomponent system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MULTICOMPONENT MIXTURE with n species"] --> B["Individual Species Velocities v1, v2, ..., vn"]
    A --> C["Concentrations: rho_i and C_i"]
    
    B --> D["Mass-Average Velocity v = sum omega_i v_i"]
    B --> E["Molar-Average Velocity v* = sum x_i v_i"]
    
    D --> F["Relation: v* - v = (1/C) sum ji / Mi"]
    E --> F
    
    C --> G["Mass Flux ni = rho_i v_i"]
    C --> H["Molar Flux Ni = Ci v_i"]
    
    G --> I["Decomposition: ni = ji + rho_i v"]
    H --> J["Decomposition: Ni = Ji* + Ci v*"]
    
    I --> K["Diffusive Mass Flux ji = rho_i vi - v"]
    J --> L["Diffusive Molar Flux Ji* = Ci vi - v*"]
    
    K --> M["Fick Law Mass: ji = -rho D_AB grad omega_i"]
    L --> N["Fick Law Molar: Ji* = -C D_AB grad x_i"]
    
    I --> O["Special Case 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion N_A + N_B = 0"]
    J --> O
    
    I --> P["Special Case 2: Unimolar Diffusion N_B = 0"]
    J --> P
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Choosing the Correct Reference Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["GIVEN: A Mass Transfer Problem in Biology"] --> R{"Is the system DILUTE? (solvent dominates)"}
    
    R -- "Yes, x_solvent > 0.95" --> S["USE v approx v*: either frame works"]
    R -- "No, concentrated mixture" --> T{"Is a CHEMICAL REACTION occurring?"}
    
    T -- "Yes, moles change" --> U["USE MOLAR frame v*: reactions are stoichiometric"]
    T -- "No, only physical transport" --> V{"Is the problem FLUID MECHANICS / flow?"}
    
    V -- "Yes, Navier-Stokes type" --> W["USE MASS frame v: continuity is mass-conserving"]
    V -- "No, pure diffusion" --> X["USE MOLAR frame v*: concentration gradients in mol/L"]
    
    S --> Y["Solve with N_i = -CD_AB grad x_i + x_i sum N_j"]
    U --> Y
    X --> Y
    W --> Z["Solve with n_i = -rho D_AB grad omega_i + rho_i v"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Binary Mass Transfer Decision Tree (EMC vs Unimolar)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Binary System: A diffusing in B"] --> B0{"What is the boundary condition on B?"}
    
    B0 -- "N_B = -N_A (equal and opposite)" --> C0["EQUIMOLAR COUNTER-DIFFUSION"]
    B0 -- "N_B = 0 (stagnant B)" --> D0["UNIMOLAR DIFFUSION A through B"]
    B0 -- "N_B is prescribed" --> E0["GENERAL CASE: N_A = -CD_AB dx_A + x_A sum N"]
    
    C0 --> F0["Equation: N_A = -CD_AB dx_A/dz"]
    D0 --> G0["Equation: N_A = -CD_AB/(1-x_A) dx_A/dz"]
    E0 --> H0["Equation: solve coupled ODE with both fluxes"]
```

---

## 4.4 Physical Schematic (Adapted): Mass Transfer Through a Tissue Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["ALVEOLUS: x_A = 0.21, x_B = 0.79"] -- "Membrane" --> M["CAPILLARY: x_A = 0.14, x_B = 0.86"]
    
    L -- "N_A: O2 flux" --> M
    M -- "N_B: N2 flux near 0" --> L
    
    N["Boundary Layer: stagnant B near membrane"] -.-> L
    N -.-> M
    
    O["Ficks Law: J_A* = -C D_AB dx_A/dz"] --> N
```

> **Reading the schematic:** The high oxygen mole fraction in the alveolus drives a molar flux **N_A** into the capillary. Because nitrogen is essentially stagnant in the membrane (N_B ≈ 0), the **unimolar diffusion** formula applies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Mass-Average Velocity and Molar-Average Velocity. Under what condition do they become equal? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Mass-Average Velocity (v):** It is defined as the velocity of the center of mass of a fluid mixture, expressed as a mass-fraction-weighted sum of the individual species velocities.

$$\mathbf{v} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i \mathbf{v_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i \mathbf{v_i}$$

**Molar-Average Velocity (v\*):** It is the velocity defined on a molar basis, expressed as a mole-fraction-weighted sum of the species velocities.

$$\mathbf{v^*} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \mathbf{v_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \mathbf{v_i}$$

**Condition for Equality:** From the derived relation:

$$\mathbf{v^*} - \mathbf{v} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\mathbf{j_i}}{M_i}$$

The two velocities become equal when all **diffusive fluxes** $\mathbf{j_i} \to 0$. This occurs when:
- The mixture is **dilute** (one species dominates, e.g., oxygen in blood plasma)
- There is **no net diffusion** (pure convection, well-mixed)
- All species move with the **same velocity** (homogeneous flow)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Diffusive Mass Flux (jᵢ) and Diffusive Molar Flux (Jᵢ\*). Why is Σjᵢ = 0 but ΣJᵢ\* ≠ 0 in general? [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Diffusive Mass Flux (jᵢ):** The flux of species *i* relative to the **mass-average velocity**.

$$\mathbf{j_i} = \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v})$$

**Diffusive Molar Flux (Jᵢ\*):** The flux of species *i* relative to the **molar-average velocity**.

$$\mathbf{J_i^*} = C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*})$$

**Why Σjᵢ = 0:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{j_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v}) = \sum_i \rho_i \mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v} \sum_i \rho_i = \rho \mathbf{v} - \rho \mathbf{v} = 0$$

**Why ΣJᵢ\* ≠ 0 (in general):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{J_i^*} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*}) = \sum_i C_i \mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*} \sum_i C_i = C\mathbf{v} - C\mathbf{v^*}$$

Since $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{v^*}$ in general (only equal for dilute mixtures), the sum is non-zero. This is because **diffusion redistributes mass but not necessarily moles equally** — and the molar frame picks up this asymmetry.

---

## 📝 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks): Unimolar Diffusion Through Stagnant Tissue

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from the general molar flux equation, derive the equation for the steady-state molar flux of species A diffusing through stagnant species B. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Oxygen (A) is diffusing through a stagnant layer of nitrogen (B) in a biological tissue membrane. The total pressure is 101.325 kPa and the temperature is 310 K. At the membrane surface (z = 0), the partial pressure of O₂ is 21.0 kPa; at z = 2.0 mm, it is 13.3 kPa. The binary diffusion coefficient D_AB = 2.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state molar flux of O₂ through the membrane. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Starting equation (general molar flux):**

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A + x_A (\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B}) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state: $\partial / \partial t = 0$
2. One-dimensional transport in the z-direction
3. Stagnant B: $\mathbf{N_B} = 0$
4. Ideal gas behavior
5. Constant total molar concentration C and constant T, P
6. Constant diffusivity D_AB

**Applying the stagnant-B condition to equation (1):**

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A (\mathbf{N_A} + 0)$$

**Rearranging:**

$$N_A - x_A N_A = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A (1 - x_A) = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$

**Recognizing $1 - x_A = x_B$:**

$$\boxed{N_A = -\frac{C D_{AB}}{x_B} \frac{dx_A}{dz}}$$

This is the **Stefan's law** for diffusion of A through stagnant B. ∎

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $P = 101.325$ kPa
- $T = 310$ K
- $p_{A,1} = 21.0$ kPa at $z_1 = 0$
- $p_{A,2} = 13.3$ kPa at $z_2 = 2.0$ mm = $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $D_{AB} = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- Ideal gas

**Step 1: Compute total molar concentration C using ideal gas law.**

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101{,}325 \text{ Pa}}{(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(310 \text{ K})}$$

$$C = \frac{101{,}325}{2577.34} = 39.31 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute mole fractions at the two boundaries.**

At z = 0:
$$x_{A,1} = \frac{p_{A,1}}{P} = \frac{21.0}{101.325} = 0.2073$$
$$x_{B,1} = 1 - x_{A,1} = 0.7927$$

At z = 2 mm:
$$x_{A,2} = \frac{p_{A,2}}{P} = \frac{13.3}{101.325} = 0.1313$$
$$x_{B,2} = 1 - x_{A,2} = 0.8687$$

**Step 3: Recognize the equation cannot be integrated directly because $x_B$ depends on z.**

Since $x_B$ varies along z, we take the **log-mean** of $x_B$:

$$x_{B,lm} = \frac{x_{B,2} - x_{B,1}}{\ln(x_{B,2}/x_{B,1})} = \frac{0.8687 - 0.7927}{\ln(0.8687/0.7927)} = \frac{0.0760}{\ln(1.0959)}$$

$$x_{B,lm} = \frac{0.0760}{0.09170} = 0.8288$$

**Step 4: Compute the molar flux using the integrated form.**

$$N_A = \frac{C D_{AB}}{x_{B,lm}} \cdot \frac{(x_{A,1} - x_{A,2})}{(z_2 - z_1)}$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$N_A = \frac{(39.31)(2.1 \times 10^{-5})}{0.8288} \cdot \frac{(0.2073 - 0.1313)}{(2.0 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$N_A = \frac{8.255 \times 10^{-4}}{0.8288} \cdot \frac{0.0760}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$N_A = 9.961 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 38.0$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 3.785 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

**Verification of units:**
$\frac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \cdot \frac{1}{-} \cdot \frac{\text{m}}{\text{m}} = \frac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}$ ✓

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks): Equimolar Counter-Diffusion in a Binary System

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Define equimolar counter-diffusion (EMC). Show that under EMC conditions, the molar flux of A in a binary system reduces to Fick's first law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a tubular artificial kidney dialyzer, urea (A) and an isotonic buffer (B) undergo equimolar counter-diffusion across a membrane of thickness 0.5 mm. At the blood side, the mole fraction of urea is $x_{A,1} = 0.012$, and on the dialysate side, $x_{A,2} = 0.001$. The total concentration C = 55.5 kmol/m³ and the binary diffusivity D_AB = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s. Determine: (i) the steady-state molar flux of urea, and (ii) the mass flux of urea, given the molar mass of urea M_A = 60 kg/kmol. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Definition of EMC:** Equimolar counter-diffusion is a special case of binary mass transfer in which species A and B move in opposite directions at equal molar rates, so that the net molar flux is zero:

$$\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B} = 0$$

This commonly occurs in **catalytic surface reactions** (e.g., ½ O₂ + H₂ ⇌ H₂O at a Pt surface) and in **gas-phase counter-diffusion** in distillation.

**Derivation:**

Starting from the general molar flux equation:
$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A (\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B})$$

Apply the EMC condition $\mathbf{N_A} + \mathbf{N_B} = 0$:

$$\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A (0)$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{N_A} = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz}}$$

This is **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** in molar form, showing that under EMC, the convective term vanishes and only diffusion contributes to the flux. ∎

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta z = 0.5$ mm = $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $x_{A,1} = 0.012$ (blood side)
- $x_{A,2} = 0.001$ (dialysate side)
- $C = 55.5$ kmol/m³
- $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s
- $M_A = 60$ kg/kmol

**Part (i) — Molar flux of urea:**

Since this is an **equimolar counter-diffusion** problem, use the simplified Fick's law:

$$N_A = -C D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dz} = -C D_{AB} \cdot \frac{x_{A,2} - x_{A,1}}{z_2 - z_1}$$

**Compute the gradient:**

$$\frac{dx_A}{dz} \approx \frac{x_{A,2} - x_{A,1}}{\Delta z} = \frac{0.001 - 0.012}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{-0.011}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = -22.0 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Substitute:**

$$N_A = -(55.5)(1.5 \times 10^{-9})(-22.0)$$

$$N_A = (55.5)(1.5 \times 10^{-9})(22.0)$$

$$N_A = 1.8315 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 1.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Mass flux of urea:**

Mass flux and molar flux are related by:
$$n_A = M_A \cdot N_A = (60 \text{ kg/kmol})(1.8315 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)})$$

$$\boxed{n_A = 1.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

**Verification of units:**
$\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kmol}} \cdot \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the log-mean of $x_B$** in the stagnant-B (unimolar) case. Many students write $x_B = 1 - x_A$ and evaluate it at one end — this gives a wrong answer by up to 10%.
> 2. **Mixing up units of C.** In SI, $C$ is in **kmol/m³**, not mol/L. Always convert: 1 mol/L = 1 kmol/m³.
> 3. **Sign convention:** A positive $N_A$ means A moves in the +z direction. If your $x_{A,1} > x_{A,2}$ at $z_1 = 0$, then $N_A$ is positive (from blood to dialysate) — confirm this is physically reasonable.
> 4. **Confusing mass flux and molar flux:** The relation $n_A = M_A N_A$ is valid only when $M_A$ is constant and in consistent units.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- ☐ **Mass-average velocity** $\mathbf{v} = \sum_i \omega_i \mathbf{v_i}$ (mass-weighted)
- ☐ **Molar-average velocity** $\mathbf{v^*} = \sum_i x_i \mathbf{v_i}$ (mole-weighted)
- ☐ **Mass flux** $\mathbf{n_i} = \rho_i \mathbf{v_i}$ (lab frame, kg basis)
- ☐ **Molar flux** $\mathbf{N_i} = C_i \mathbf{v_i}$ (lab frame, mol basis)
- ☐ **Diffusive mass flux** $\mathbf{j_i} = \rho_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v})$
- ☐ **Diffusive molar flux** $\mathbf{J_i^*} = C_i (\mathbf{v_i} - \mathbf{v^*})$

### 🔑 The Decomposition Identity (MEMORIZE)
- ☐ $\mathbf{n_i} = \mathbf{j_i} + \rho_i \mathbf{v}$
- ☐ $\mathbf{N_i} = \mathbf{J_i^*} + C_i \mathbf{v^*}$

### 🔑 Fick's First Law (BOTH Forms)
- ☐ **Molar form:** $\mathbf{J_A^*} = -C D_{AB} \nabla x_A$
- ☐ **Mass form:** $\mathbf{j_A} = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla \omega_A$

### 🔑 Sum Constraints (Frequently Asked)
- ☐ $\sum_i \omega_i = 1$ and $\sum_i x_i = 1$
- ☐ $\sum_i \mathbf{j_i} = 0$ (always, mass frame)
- ☐ $\sum_i \mathbf{J_i^*} \neq 0$ in general (molar frame)

### 🔑 Special Binary Cases (MEMORIZE FOR NUMERICALS)
- ☐ **Equimolar counter-diffusion (EMC):** $N_A + N_B = 0 \Rightarrow N_A = -CD_{AB}\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$
- ☐ **Unimolar (A through stagnant B):** $N_B = 0 \Rightarrow N_A = -\dfrac{CD_{AB}}{x_B}\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$ (use log-mean $x_{B,lm}$ for varying $x_B$)
- ☐ **Dilute solution:** $x_A \ll 1 \Rightarrow N_A \approx -CD_{AB}\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$ (same as EMC)

### 🔑 Conversions
- ☐ $n_A = M_A N_A$ (mass flux = molar mass × molar flux)
- ☐ $C = P / RT$ for ideal gases
- ☐ $C_i = \rho_i / M_i$ (molar concentration = mass concentration / molar mass)

### 🔑 When to Use Which Frame (Decision Heuristic)
- ☐ **Reactions occurring?** → Use **molar (v\*)** frame
- ☐ **Pure fluid flow (Navier-Stokes)?** → Use **mass (v)** frame
- ☐ **Diffusion through tissue/membrane?** → Use **molar (v\*)** frame
- ☐ **Dilute biological solution?** → Both frames are equivalent

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Don't confuse $N_i$ (absolute molar flux) with $J_i^*$ (diffusive molar flux)
- ☐ Don't forget the $x_A$ convective term in the general flux equation
- ☐ Don't drop the $1/x_B$ factor in unimolar diffusion
- ☐ Don't use $C$ in mol/L with $R$ in J/(mol·K) — keep units consistent

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrelationship Between Fluxes. Fick's Law of Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interrelationship Between Fluxes. Fick's Law of Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Flux" in Biological Transport?

In transport phenomena, **flux** is the rate of transport of a quantity (mass, moles, volume, energy, momentum) per unit **cross-sectional area** perpendicular to the direction of flow. Flux is a vector quantity having both **magnitude and direction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A clear understanding of flux interrelationships is mandatory before deriving the governing differential equations for diffusion. This topic is frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions and forms the theoretical foundation for problems on membrane transport and hemodialysis.

### 1.1.1 The Three Principal Fluxes Used in Bio-Transport

| Flux Type | Symbol | Units (SI) | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molar Flux** | $N_A$ | $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | Moles of species $A$ crossing unit area per unit time |
| **Mass Flux** | $n_A$ | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ | Mass of species $A$ crossing unit area per unit time |
| **Volumetric Flux** | $N$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Total volume of mixture crossing unit area per unit time |

The mathematical interrelationship is:

$$n_A = M_A \cdot N_A$$

where $M_A$ is the **molecular weight** of species $A$ in **kg/kmol**. The volumetric flux $N$ is related to molar flux through the **molar density** ($C$, in kmol/m³) of the mixture:

$$N = \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **binary mixture** (A + B), if the total molar concentration $C$ is constant (typical for ideal gases and dilute liquid solutions), this relation is exact.

## 1.2 Fick's Law of Diffusion — Formal Definitions

### 1.2.1 Fick's First Law (1855)

Fick's First Law states that **the diffusive flux of a species is proportional to the negative gradient of its concentration** (i.e., species diffuse from high to low concentration).

For a binary mixture (A diffusing through stagnant B or in equimolar counter-diffusion):

$$\boxed{\,J_A^* = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}\,}$$

where:
- $J_A^*$ = **molar diffusive flux** of species $A$ relative to the **molar-average velocity** (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = **binary diffusion coefficient** (or diffusivity) of A in B (m²/s)
- $dC_A/dz$ = concentration gradient of $A$ in the $z$-direction (kmol/m⁴)
- The negative sign indicates flux flows from high to low concentration.

### 1.2.2 Fick's Second Law (Unsteady State)

When concentration changes with **time** (non-steady state), Fick's Second Law applies:

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\,}$$

This is the **one-dimensional form** for constant $D_{AB}$ and constant total concentration.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Crowded Hallway"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Crowded Hallway:**
> Imagine a narrow hallway (representing a tissue membrane or blood vessel wall) packed with students. Some students are standing at one end (high concentration), and the hallway is empty at the other end (low concentration). When the doors open, students naturally walk from the crowded end to the empty end. The **rate** at which students cross any imaginary line in the hallway per unit length of the line is the **flux**. The **steeper the crowd gradient** (more crowded on one side), the **faster** they move through the line — that is **Fick's First Law**. Now imagine the door stays open and you count students entering and leaving the hallway every minute: the count keeps changing until an equilibrium crowd distribution is reached. That **time-varying change in crowd density** is described by **Fick's Second Law**.

In **biological systems**, the "students" are **oxygen molecules**, **glucose**, **drug molecules**, **ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺)**, or **urea** crossing membranes, capillary walls, or tissue layers.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Biological Diffusivities (at 37 °C in dilute aqueous media):**
> - $O_2$ in water: $D_{O_2,H_2O} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-9}$ **m²/s**
> - Glucose in water: $D \approx 0.67 \times 10^{-9}$ **m²/s**
> - Urea in water: $D \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-9}$ **m²/s**
> - Albumin in water: $D \approx 0.06 \times 10^{-9}$ **m²/s**
> - $O_2$ in tissue: $D \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ **m²/s** (≈ 100× smaller than in air, but ~30% of value in water)

The **Stokes–Einstein equation** provides a first-order estimate of $D_{AB}$ for spherical molecules:

$$D_{AB} = \dfrac{k_B\,T}{6\pi\,\mu\,R_A}$$

where $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant ($1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K), $T$ = absolute temperature, $\mu$ = viscosity of the medium, $R_A$ = radius of solute.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Flux Interrelationship

To master this topic, follow this **five-step reasoning chain** (high-yield for KTU):

**Step 1 — Define the Reference Frame**
Transport equations are written relative to a chosen velocity:
- **Molar-average velocity** $\nu_M$ → gives $J_A^*$ (diffusive flux, Fick's Law)
- **Mass-average velocity** $\nu$ → gives $j_A$ (mass diffusive flux)
- **Stationary (fixed) coordinates** → gives $N_A$ (total molar flux)

**Step 2 — Define Molar Concentration**
The molar concentration of species $A$ in a mixture:
$$C_A = \dfrac{n_A}{V} \quad \text{[kmol/m}^3\text{]}$$
Total concentration: $C = C_A + C_B$ (binary).

**Step 3 — Express the Mass/Molar Flux**
$$N_A = C_A \cdot \nu_{M} + J_A^*$$
The first term is **convective** (bulk flow) contribution; the second is **diffusive** contribution.

**Step 4 — Apply Fick's Law for the Diffusive Term**
$$J_A^* = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$$
where $x_A$ is the **mole fraction** of $A$. (For constant $C$, $C\,dx_A = dC_A$.)

**Step 5 — Combine into the Working Equation**
$$\boxed{\,N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A\,(N_A + N_B)\,}$$

This is the **master equation** for binary diffusion. Two important special cases arise from this:

### 2.1.1 Special Case A: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion ($N_A = -N_B$)
The total molar flux is zero, so the bulk-flow term vanishes:
$$N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$
This occurs, e.g., in **distillation** where 1 mole vaporizing equals 1 mole condensing.

### 2.1.2 Special Case B: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B ($N_B = 0$)
Used for **gas absorption**, **evaporation**, and many **biological membrane systems**:
$$N_A = \dfrac{-C\,D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} = \dfrac{-D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Term

- **Negative sign**: The Second Law of Thermodynamics — matter flows down the chemical potential gradient.
- **Concentration gradient as the driving force**: For isothermal, isobaric, dilute systems, the chemical potential gradient reduces to a concentration gradient.
- **Proportionality constant $D_{AB}$**: Depends on molecular size, temperature, pressure, and medium viscosity — a kinetic property.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Hemodialysis** | Urea and creatinine diffuse out of blood across a semi-permeable membrane |
| **Drug delivery** | Transdermal patches rely on Fickian diffusion through skin layers |
| **Oxygen transport** | $O_2$ diffuses from alveoli → blood → tissue cells (governed by Fick's Law) |
| **Biofilm growth** | Nutrient (e.g., glucose) diffusion into biofilms limits microbial kinetics |
| **Artificial organs** | Hollow-fiber membrane oxygenators and bioartificial livers |
| **Tumor modeling** | Drug penetration into solid tumors is diffusion-limited |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the exact formulas asked in KTU ESE questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | **Fick's First Law** (equimolar counter-diffusion) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | **Fick's Second Law** (1-D, constant $D_{AB}$, constant $C$) | kmol/(m³·s) |
| 3 | $N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ | **General binary flux** | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $N_A = \dfrac{-C\,D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$ | **A through stagnant B** ($N_B = 0$) | kmol/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $n_A = M_A \cdot N_A$ | **Mass flux** ↔ Molar flux | kg/(m²·s) |
| 6 | $N = (N_A + N_B)/C$ | **Volumetric flux** in binary system | m/s |
| 7 | $D_{AB} = k_B T / (6\pi\mu R_A)$ | **Stokes–Einstein equation** (spherical solute) | m²/s |
| 8 | $J_A^* = -C\,D_{AB}\,(dx_A/dz)$ | **Fick's First Law in mole-fraction form** (constant $C$) | kmol/(m²·s) |

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Problems

For 1-D diffusion between $z = 0$ and $z = L$:

| BC Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Specified concentration** | $C_A(0,t) = C_{A0}$ | Constant surface concentration |
| **Specified flux** | $-D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = J_0$ | Constant surface flux |
| **Symmetry** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = 0$ | Centerline / symmetric plane |
| **Impermeable wall** | $J_A = 0$ at wall | No flux through boundary |
| **Continuity at interface** | $C_A^{(1)}(L^-,t) = K\,C_A^{(2)}(L^+,t)$ | Partition coefficient at membrane |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Always check whether the problem says *"equimolar counter-diffusion"*, *"diffusion through stagnant B"*, or *"constant total concentration"* — the formula simplifies dramatically for each case.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From the Master Flux Equation to Fick's First Law (Equimolar Case)

**Statement to Prove:** $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ under the condition of equimolar counter-diffusion.

**Starting Point — General Binary Flux Equation:**
$$N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A\,(N_A + N_B)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the equimolar counter-diffusion condition.**
By definition, equimolar counter-diffusion means: $N_A = -N_B$, so the total molar flux is zero:
$$N_A + N_B = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the master equation.**
The convective term vanishes:
$$N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A\,(0)$$
$$N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mole fraction gradient to concentration gradient.**
For an incompressible liquid or constant-pressure ideal gas, total molar concentration $C$ is constant, so:
$$C_A = x_A \cdot C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = C\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Final substitution.**
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = J_A^*$$

This is Fick's First Law in its most common engineering form. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From Fick's First Law to Fick's Second Law

**Statement to Prove:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass (molar) to a differential control volume.**
Consider a slab of thickness $dz$ and unit cross-section. The molar balance on species $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of accumulation} &= \text{Input flux} - \text{Output flux} + \text{Generation} \\
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\,dz &= N_A\big\vert_{z} - N_A\big\vert_{z+dz} + r_A\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Taylor-expand the output flux about $z$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A\big\vert_{z+dz} &= N_A\big\vert_{z} + \dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial z}\,dz + \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial^2 N_A}{\partial z^2}\,(dz)^2 + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

Retaining only first-order terms (since $dz$ is infinitesimal):
$$N_A\big\vert_{z+dz} \approx N_A\big\vert_{z} + \dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial z}\,dz$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\,dz &= N_A\big\vert_{z} - \left[N_A\big\vert_{z} + \dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial z}\,dz\right] + r_A\,dz \\
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\,dz &= -\dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial z}\,dz + r_A\,dz \\
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} &= -\dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial z} + r_A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert Fick's First Law for $N_A$.**
For a non-reacting system ($r_A = 0$) and constant $D_{AB}$:
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate $N_A$ with respect to $z$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial N_A}{\partial z} &= \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right) \\
&= -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \quad \text{(since } D_{AB} \text{ is constant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine into the final form.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} &= -\left(-D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\right) \\
\boxed{\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}} &= \boxed{D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Flux Through a Stagnant Film

**Problem:** Oxygen (A) diffuses through a stagnant layer of water (B) of thickness $\delta = 0.5$ mm. At $z = 0$, $C_{A,0} = 0.25$ kmol/m³; at $z = \delta$, $C_{A,\delta} = 0.05$ kmol/m³. Take $D_{AB} = 3.0 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Compute $N_A$.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
For A diffusing through stagnant B (no reaction):
$$N_A = \dfrac{-C\,D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} = \text{constant at steady state}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that for dilute systems, $x_A \ll 1$, so $1 - x_A \approx 1$.**
This simplification is **valid for $O_2$ in water** (typical $x_A < 0.01$). Then:
$$N_A \approx -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the film thickness.**
At steady state, $N_A$ is constant, so:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \int_0^{\delta} dz &= -D_{AB}\int_{C_{A,0}}^{C_{A,\delta}} dC_A \\
N_A \cdot \delta &= -D_{AB}\,(C_{A,\delta} - C_{A,0}) \\
N_A &= \dfrac{D_{AB}\,(C_{A,0} - C_{A,\delta})}{\delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \dfrac{(3.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s})\,(0.25 - 0.05)\ \text{kmol/m}^3}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}} \\
N_A &= \dfrac{(3.0 \times 10^{-9})\,(0.20)}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} \\
N_A &= \dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-10}}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} \\
\boxed{N_A} &= \boxed{1.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to more intuitive units.**
$$N_A = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** This value is consistent with measured oxygen flux through tissue layers and across alveolar membranes, confirming the order of magnitude of the result.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fick's Law Regimes

| Regime | Governing Equation | Independent Variables | KTU Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state, equimolar counter-diffusion** | $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Spatial ($z$) | Distillation, simultaneous absorption/desorption |
| **Steady-state, A through stagnant B** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A,0} - C_{A,\delta})}{\delta}$ | Spatial ($z$) | Gas absorption, evaporation, oxygen transport across alveolar membrane |
| **Transient (unsteady), 1-D slab** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Time ($t$) and space ($z$) | Drug release from polymer matrix, oxygen diffusion into tissue |
| **Transient, spherical coordinates** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{r^2}\,\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2 \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)$ | Time and radius ($r$) | Drug release from microsphere, oxygen diffusion into cell |
| **Transient, cylindrical coordinates** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{r}\,\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)$ | Time and radius ($r$) | Diffusion through hollow fiber, blood vessel wall |


## 4.4 Schematic — Concentration Profile Across a Stagnant Film

**Visualization (text-based, professional schematic):**

```
   High conc.                                      Low conc.
   C_{A,0}                                         C_{A,δ}
   (e.g., blood)                                   (e.g., tissue)
      |                                                |
      |  z=0                              z=δ          |
      |  ●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━●     |
      |   \                                      /    |
      |    \                                    /     |
      |     \  Linear (constant D, steady)    /      |
      |      \                            ___/       |
      |       \                       ___/           |
      |        \______________  _____/               |
      |                                                 |
      v                                                 v
   -------------------------------------------------------------> z
                  Concentration C_A
```

**Mathematical description of the profile** (steady-state, stagnant B, dilute system):

$$C_A(z) = C_{A,0} - \left(\dfrac{C_{A,0} - C_{A,\delta}}{\delta}\right)z$$

A **linear profile** is the hallmark of **steady-state diffusion at constant $D_{AB}$** — a direct consequence of $N_A = \text{constant}$ implying $d^2C_A/dz^2 = 0$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the significance of the negative sign in the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fick's First Law states that the molar diffusive flux of a species is directly proportional to the concentration gradient and is given by:

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that diffusion occurs in the direction of **decreasing concentration** (i.e., species move from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration). This is consistent with the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, which requires spontaneous mass transfer to occur down the chemical potential gradient, reducing the system's free energy.

The proportionality constant $D_{AB}$ is the **binary diffusion coefficient** with units of m²/s, which is a property of the (A, B) pair and depends on temperature, pressure, and molecular size.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between molar flux ($N_A$) and molar diffusive flux ($J_A^*$). State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Molar Flux ($N_A$) | Molar Diffusive Flux ($J_A^*$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference frame** | Fixed (stationary) coordinates | Molar-average velocity frame |
| **Includes convection** | Yes (convection + diffusion) | Only diffusion |
| **Symbol** | $N_A$ | $J_A^*$ |

The mathematical relationship is:
$$N_A = J_A^* + C_A\,\nu_M$$
where $\nu_M$ is the **molar-average velocity** of the mixture. Equivalently, using Fick's Law:
$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A\,(N_A + N_B)$$

When there is **no bulk flow** (equimolar counter-diffusion, $N_A = -N_B$), then $N_A = J_A^*$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from the general binary flux equation, derive Fick's First Law of Diffusion for the case of **equimolar counter-diffusion** of a binary gas mixture. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A membrane of thickness 1.5 mm separates two aqueous solutions of urea. The concentration on side 1 is 0.40 kmol/m³ and on side 2 is 0.10 kmol/m³. The diffusion coefficient of urea in water at 37 °C is $1.4 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Calculate:
   (i) The steady-state molar flux of urea.
   (ii) The mass flux of urea.
   (iii) The time taken for 1.0 kmol of urea to diffuse through an area of 0.05 m² of membrane.
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Binary mixture (A and B only).
2. Isothermal, isobaric system.
3. Constant total molar concentration $C$.
4. Steady state; no chemical reaction.
5. One-dimensional diffusion along $z$.

**Starting equation (general binary flux):**
$$N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A\,(N_A + N_B)$$

**Step 1 — Equimolar counter-diffusion condition:**
$$N_A + N_B = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_B = -N_A$$

**Step 2 — Substituting:**
$$N_A = -C\,D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz} + 0$$

**Step 3 — Convert to concentration gradient** (since $C$ is constant, $C_A = x_A\,C$):
$$\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = C\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**
$$\boxed{N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = J_A^*}$$

This is **Fick's First Law** for equimolar counter-diffusion. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\delta = L = 1.5\ \text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $C_{A,1} = 0.40\ \text{kmol/m}^3$
- $C_{A,2} = 0.10\ \text{kmol/m}^3$
- $D_{AB} = 1.4 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $M_A$ (urea, $\text{NH}_2\text{CONH}_2$) = 60.06 kg/kmol
- Area $A = 0.05\ \text{m}^2$

**(i) Molar flux:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \dfrac{D_{AB}\,(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{L} \\
&= \dfrac{(1.4 \times 10^{-9})\,(0.40 - 0.10)}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \dfrac{(1.4 \times 10^{-9})\,(0.30)}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
N_A &= 2.8 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mass flux:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_A &= M_A \cdot N_A = (60.06)\,(2.8 \times 10^{-7}) \\
\boxed{n_A} &= \boxed{1.682 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Time for 1.0 kmol through 0.05 m²:**

Total molar rate:
$$\dot{n} = N_A \cdot A = (2.8 \times 10^{-7})\,(0.05) = 1.4 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{kmol/s}$$

Time:
$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{n_{A,\text{total}}}{\dot{n}} = \dfrac{1.0}{1.4 \times 10^{-8}} \\
\boxed{t} &= \boxed{7.14 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s} \approx 826\ \text{days}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Failing to convert mm → m for membrane thickness. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting $M_A$ in part (ii):** Mass flux is NOT equal to molar flux; you must multiply by molecular weight. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 3. **Not stating assumptions in the derivation part:** KTU requires explicit listing of assumptions before the derivation. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 4. **Using the wrong form of Fick's Law:** For equimolar counter-diffusion, use $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz)$. For A through stagnant B, use the modified form with $(1-x_A)$. Mixing them up loses **3 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion from the principles of molar conservation and Fick's First Law. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Urea diffuses through a gel slab of thickness 2.0 mm initially containing 0.80 kmol/m³ of urea. At time $t > 0$, the surface concentration is suddenly reduced to 0.10 kmol/m³ and maintained. The diffusion coefficient is $D_{AB} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s. Using the one-term approximation of the infinite-series solution, calculate the **time required for the concentration at the center of the slab to drop to 0.40 kmol/m³**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

[Follow the derivation given in **Section 3.2** of these notes — Fick's Second Law derivation, step-by-step. The final answer is $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$.]

**Assumptions:**
1. Constant $D_{AB}$ (independent of concentration).
2. Constant total concentration $C$ (incompressible medium).
3. One-dimensional diffusion along $z$.
4. No chemical reaction.
5. Isothermal conditions.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Half-thickness $L = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m (slab of total thickness 2 mm)
- $C_0 = 0.80$ kmol/m³ (initial uniform concentration)
- $C_s = 0.10$ kmol/m³ (surface concentration at $t > 0$)
- $C_{\text{center}}$ at $t = t$: $0.40$ kmol/m³
- $D_{AB} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s

**Standard solution for slab with sudden surface concentration change:**

$$\dfrac{C_{\text{center}} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2\pi^2 D_{AB}\,t}{4L^2}\right]$$
