The pressure is then calculated from: $$P_{system} - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$ where $h$ is the difference in column heights between the two limbs. **(ii) Numerical Calculation (4 marks):** **Given:** - $h = 80 \, \text{mm} = 0.080 \, \text{m}$ - $\rho_{saline} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ **Step 1 — Calculate gauge pressure in Pascals:** $$P_{gauge} = \rho g h = (1000)(9.81)(0.080) = 784.8 \, \text{Pa}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to mmHg:** $$P_{gauge} = \frac{784.8}{133.322} \approx 5.89 \, \text{mmHg}$$ $$\boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 784.8 \, \text{Pa} \approx 5.89 \, \text{mmHg}}$$ **Note:** The low value confirms this is a saline manometer reading **below** the normal arterial systolic pressure. Such devices are typically used as **saline manometers for venous or central venous pressure (CVP)** measurement, not arterial pressure. Arterial measurement requires a mercury or electronic sphygmomanometer due to the much higher pressures involved. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the principle of a sphygmomanometer. How does Pascal's Law and hydrostatic pressure together enable blood pressure measurement?** **Model Answer:** **Principle (3 marks):** A **sphygmomanometer** measures arterial blood pressure using a combination of: 1. **An inflatable cuff** wrapped around the upper arm (brachial artery level) 2. **A manometer** (mercury column or digital transducer) to measure cuff pressure 3. **A stethoscope** to detect Korotkoff sounds (turbulent blood flow) **Procedure (2 marks):** - The cuff is inflated until pressure exceeds systolic pressure, **occluding** the brachial artery. - Pressure is slowly released. The pressure at which the **first Korotkoff sound** is heard = **systolic pressure**. - Pressure continues to drop. The pressure at which the **sounds disappear** = **diastolic pressure**. **Connection to Pascal's Law and Hydrostatics (2 marks):** - **Pascal's Law** ensures that the pressure applied by the cuff is **transmitted uniformly** through the enclosed air to the brachial artery beneath. - **Hydrostatic principle** ensures that the pressure reading is **referenced to heart level** because the manometer's fluid column height converts directly to pressure via $P = \rho g h$. - Together, these principles convert a mechanical occlusion event (arterial collapse) into a quantifiable, repeatable measurement of internal arterial pressure. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on "Pressure" Questions:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing up Pa, mmHg, cmH₂O, and atm without explicit conversion. KTU expects unit consistency throughout a numerical solution. > 2. **Using $\rho_{water}$ instead of $\rho_{blood}$:** Blood is **denser** than water. Using $1000$ instead of $1060 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ costs a full mark on cardiovascular problems. > 3. **Sign Error in Hydrostatic Height:** Failing to use a **positive sign** for points **below** the reference and a **negative sign** for points **above**. Always define height direction explicitly at the start of the solution. > 4. **Skipping Intermediate Steps:** Examiners award marks for **each algebraic transition**. Writing only the final formula and answer costs 2–4 marks on a 7-mark sub-question. > 5. **Forgetting to Convert to mmHg:** Final answers should be expressed in the **most clinically relevant unit**. A pressure of "1471.5 Pa" with no conversion is considered incomplete. > 6. **Confusing Gauge and Absolute:** Stating a sphygmomanometer reading as "absolute" without clarification is a common KTU pitfall. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Pressure** > > - **Pressure** = Normal force per unit area; SI unit = **Pascal (Pa)** > - **$1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 760 \, \text{mmHg}$** > - **Hydrostatic Equation:** $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$, integrated form: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ > - **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished > - **Gauge pressure** is referenced to atmospheric; **Absolute pressure** is referenced to vacuum > - **$\rho_{blood} = 1060 \, \text{kg/m}^3$** (always use this, not water density) > - **Normal systolic/diastolic BP:** $120/80 \, \text{mmHg}$ (gauge) > - **Normal IOP:** $10$–$21 \, \text{mmHg}$; **Normal ICP:** $5$–$15 \, \text{mmHg}$ > - **Standing vs. Supine:** Standing increases BP in legs ($\Delta P \approx +100 \, \text{mmHg}$ per 1.3 m height) and decreases BP in brain > - **Sphygmomanometer, manometer, tonometer** all rely on $P = \rho g h$ > - **Pressure Cascade:** Right Atrium → Right Ventricle → Pulmonary Artery → Left Atrium → Left Ventricle → Aorta (pressures: ~5 → 25 → 25 → 8 → 120 → 120 mmHg systolic) > - **Master the derivation** of $P(z) = P_0 + \rho g h$ — it is worth 7 marks standalone in any KTU ESE. > - **Laplace Pressure** (link to alveoli): $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ — surface tension-driven pressure in spherical biological structures.

topics.title: and Composition on Diffusivity of Gases and Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** and Composition on Diffusivity of Gases and Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Mass Transfer Operations** (aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEBBT635), the **Diffusivity (D)** — also known as the **Mass Diffusivity** or **Diffusion Coefficient** — is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the molar flux of a species to its concentration gradient, as expressed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Diffusivity is a transport property that quantifies the rate at which a species (molecule, atom, or ion) moves through a medium (gas, liquid, or solid) under the influence of a concentration gradient. Its units in the SI system are **m²/s** (or **cm²/s** in CGS).*

For a binary mixture of species A diffusing through species B, Fick's First Law states:

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A
$$

where:
- $J_A$ is the molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ is the binary diffusivity of A in B (m²/s)
- $\nabla C_A$ is the concentration gradient of species A (kmol/m⁴)

The **dependency of diffusivity** on external parameters (temperature, pressure, composition) and intrinsic molecular properties (molecular weight, collision diameter, molecular volume) is the cornerstone of this module. KTU 2024 expects students to identify and quantify these dependencies for both **gaseous** and **liquid** systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crowded marketplace in Kerala** during a festival season. People (molecules of A) are trying to leave through a narrow gate. How fast can they escape? That depends on:
- **How fast they are moving** (Temperature) — hotter = faster.
- **How crowded the street is** (Pressure / Composition) — denser = slower.
- **The size of the people and the gate** (Molecular size and shape).

This is exactly what diffusivity ($D_{AB}$) represents — the "ease" with which molecules slip past each other in a thermal, chaotic environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for this Module:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**
> - Boltzmann Constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - Avogadro's Number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ **mol⁻¹**
> - Standard Temperature: $T_0 = 273.15$ **K**
> - Standard Pressure: $P_0 = 1.01325 \times 10^5$ **Pa**

## 1.3 Why Temperature, Pressure & Composition Matter in Biological Systems

In **bioprocess engineering** (a key KTU PEBBT635 focus), diffusivity governs:
- **Oxygen transport** from air into fermentation broths
- **Nutrient diffusion** across cell membranes
- **Drug delivery** through tissue scaffolds
- **CO₂ removal** in bioreactors

Small changes in **temperature** (e.g., from $30°C$ to $37°C$ in a bioreactor) or **composition** (e.g., increased glucose concentration raising broth viscosity) can shift $D_{AB}$ by **20–200%**, drastically altering productivity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Temperature on Diffusivity

### 2.1.1 General Theoretical Framework

Temperature affects diffusivity through **two physical mechanisms**:

1. **Increase in average molecular kinetic energy** — molecules move faster.
2. **Increase in collision frequency and energy transfer** — momentum exchange is more efficient.

For most engineering estimates, the dependency of $D_{AB}$ on temperature can be captured by an **Arrhenius-type relationship**:

$$
\frac{D_{AB}}{D_{AB,0}} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{n}
$$

where the exponent $n$ is empirically determined:
- **Gases:** $n \approx 1.5$ to $1.8$ (theoretical value = 1.5 from Chapman-Enskog theory)
- **Liquids:** $n \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ (Stokes-Einstein gives $n = 1$ for dilute solutes; empirical $n$ is higher due to structural effects)

### 2.1.2 Molecular Origin in Gases (Chapman-Enskog)

From the **Kinetic Theory of Gases**, the average molecular velocity is:

$$
\bar{c} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}
$$

and the **mean free path** is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^2 \, P}
$$

The diffusivity scales as:

$$
D_{AB} \propto \bar{c} \cdot \lambda \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{P}
$$

This is the **theoretical foundation** for the $T^{1.5}/P$ dependency.

### 2.1.3 Molecular Origin in Liquids (Stokes-Einstein & Eyring)

For a spherical solute in a continuum solvent, the **Stokes-Einstein equation** gives:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}
$$

Here, **temperature enters twice**:
- Linearly in the numerator (kinetic energy driving diffusion).
- Through the solvent viscosity $\mu_B(T)$ in the denominator, which **decreases** with temperature (typically $\mu \propto e^{E_a/RT}$).

This results in a **super-linear** temperature dependence for liquid diffusivity.

## 2.2 Effect of Pressure on Diffusivity

### 2.2.1 For Gases

From kinetic theory, $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$. Doubling the pressure at constant temperature **halves** the diffusivity, because:
- Higher pressure → higher number density → shorter mean free path → more collisions.

### 2.2.2 For Liquids

Liquids are nearly **incompressible**; therefore, pressure has a **negligible direct effect** on $D_{AB}$. However, **extreme pressures** (e.g., $P > 100$ atm in industrial bioreactors) can:
- Compress the solvent (decreasing free volume).
- Increase viscosity.
- Cause a slight **decrease** in $D_{AB}$ (typically 5–15% per 100 atm).

## 2.3 Effect of Composition on Diffusivity

### 2.3.1 In Gaseous Mixtures

For a binary gas mixture, $D_{AB}$ is independent of composition to a **first approximation** because gases are ideal and intermolecular forces are weak. However:
- For **polar or associating gases** (e.g., NH₃, H₂O vapor), composition effects become noticeable.
- For **multicomponent** mixtures, the **Stefan-Maxwell equations** must be used:

$$
-\nabla y_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{y_i y_j (x_j N_i - x_i N_j)}{D_{ij}^{eff}}
$$

### 2.3.2 In Liquid Mixtures

Composition effects in liquids are **much stronger** than in gases due to:
- **Variable viscosity** with concentration.
- **Molecular interactions** (hydrogen bonding, electrostatics).
- **Solvation effects** (e.g., proteins in water).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** When concentration of solute A is low (dilute limit), $D_{AB}$ is treated as constant. As concentration rises, always check for **concentration-dependent viscosity corrections** using the **Wilke-Chang** or **Hayduk-Minhas** correlations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the temperature exponents ($n$ values) and the order of magnitude of $D_{AB}$:
> - Gases: $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ **m²/s** (i.e., $\sim 0.1$ – $1$ cm²/s)
> - Liquids: $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}$ **m²/s** (i.e., $\sim 10^{-5}$ cm²/s)
> - Solids: $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-12}$ **m²/s**

| # | Correlation / Equation | Best Used For | Key Parameters | Typical Accuracy |
|---|------------------------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{M_A} + \tfrac{1}{M_B}}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}$ | Binary gas diffusion (low P) | $T$, $P$, $M$, $\sigma$, $\Omega_D$ | $\pm 8\%$ |
| 2 | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^{-2} \, T^{1.75} \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{M_A} + \tfrac{1}{M_B}}}{P \left[ (\Sigma v_A)^{1/3} + (\Sigma v_B)^{1/3} \right]^2}$ | Fuller-Schettler-Giddings (gases) | Atomic diffusion volumes $\Sigma v$ | $\pm 5\text{–}10\%$ |
| 3 | $D_{AB}^0 = \dfrac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\phi_B M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A,mb}^{0.6}}$ | Wilke-Chang (dilute liquids) | $V_{A,mb}$ (molar volume), $\mu_B$, $\phi_B$ | $\pm 10\%$ |
| 4 | $D_{AB}^0 = \dfrac{13.26 \times 10^{-5} \, \mu_B^{-1.4} \, V_{A,mb}^{-0.589}}{}$ | Hayduk-Minhas (non-aqueous liquids) | $V_{A,mb}$, $\mu_B$ | $\pm 8\%$ |
| 5 | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$ | Stokes-Einstein (large solute in liquid) | $r_A$ (Stokes radius) | Valid for $r_A \gg r_{solvent}$ |
| 6 | $D_{AB} \propto \dfrac{T^{n}}{P}$ with $n \approx 1.5$ – $1.8$ | Quick scaling estimate | $T$, $P$ | Order-of-magnitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All SI correlations above use $T$ in **K**, $P$ in **atm**, $D_{AB}$ in **cm²/s**, $\mu$ in **cP (centipoise)**, and $V$ in **cm³/mol**.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Bioreactor design:** Predicting oxygen transfer rate (OTR) in aerobic fermentations of *S. cerevisiae* or mammalian cell cultures.
- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Modeling transdermal drug delivery (e.g., nicotine patches).
- **Food engineering:** Salt diffusion in cheese ripening, moisture migration in packaged foods.
- **Medical devices:** Hemodialysis membranes, oxygenators in ECMO machines.
- **Environmental engineering:** VOC diffusion in soil/groundwater remediation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Temperature Dependence of $D_{AB}$ from Kinetic Theory of Gases

### Step 1 — Define the binary diffusivity from kinetic theory

The binary diffusivity in a gas can be related to the **mean molecular speed** and the **mean free path**:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c}_A \lambda_A
$$

For mutual diffusion in a binary gas, this becomes:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{3}{16} \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi k_B T / \mu_{AB}}}{n \pi \sigma_{AB}^2 \Omega_D}
$$

where:
- $n = \dfrac{N}{V}$ is the total number density (mol/m³)
- $\mu_{AB} = \dfrac{M_A M_B}{M_A + M_B}$ is the reduced molecular mass
- $\sigma_{AB} = \dfrac{\sigma_A + \sigma_B}{2}$ is the average collision diameter
- $\Omega_D$ is the collision integral (dimensionless, $\approx 1$ at low $T$)

### Step 2 — Express $n$ in terms of pressure

Using the ideal gas law:

$$
P = n k_B T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = \frac{P}{k_B T}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify

Substituting $n = P/(k_B T)$:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{3}{16} \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi k_B T / \mu_{AB}}}{(P / k_B T) \, \pi \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}
$$

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{3}{16} \frac{k_B T}{P} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi k_B T / \mu_{AB}}}{\pi \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}
$$

Combining constants:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{3}{16 \pi} \cdot \frac{(2 \pi k_B)^{1/2}}{\sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D} \cdot \frac{T^{3/2}}{\mu_{AB}^{1/2} \, P}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert from molecular mass to molar mass

Using $k_B = R/N_A$ and $\mu_{AB} = M_{AB}/N_A$:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{0.00266 \, T^{3/2}}{M_{AB}^{1/2} \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D} \cdot \frac{1}{P} \quad \text{(cm²/s, atm, K)}
$$

This is the **Chapman-Enskog equation** for binary gas diffusivity.

### Step 5 — Identify the explicit dependencies

Reading off the final expression, the dependencies are:
- **$D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}$** — i.e., $D_{AB}$ scales as temperature to the **1.5 power**.
- **$D_{AB} \propto 1/P$** — i.e., $D_{AB}$ is **inversely proportional** to pressure.
- **$D_{AB} \propto 1/M_{AB}^{1/2}$** — lighter molecules diffuse faster.
- **$D_{AB} \propto 1/\sigma_{AB}^2$** — larger molecules diffuse slower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $T^{1.5}$?** Two competing effects:
> - Higher $T$ → faster molecular speeds ($\bar{c} \propto T^{1/2}$).
> - Higher $T$ → longer mean free path ($\lambda \propto T$).
> - Product: $T^{1/2} \times T = T^{3/2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Temperature & Composition Effects in Liquids (Wilke-Chang Correlation)

### Step 1 — Stokes-Einstein starting point

For a large spherical solute A in a viscous solvent B:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{f_B}
$$

where $f_B = 6 \pi \mu_B r_A$ is the friction coefficient. Thus:

$$
D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}
$$

### Step 2 — Replace $r_A$ with molar volume

Using $V_{A,mb} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_A^3 N_A$, we get $r_A \propto V_{A,mb}^{1/3}$:

$$
D_{AB} \propto \frac{T}{\mu_B V_{A,mb}^{1/3}}
$$

### Step 3 — Empirical corrections (Wilke-Chang)

Wilke and Chang (1955) introduced an empirical constant and an **association factor** $\phi_B$ for the solvent (e.g., $\phi_B = 2.6$ for water, 1.9 for methanol, 1.0 for unassociated solvents):

$$
D_{AB}^0 = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\phi_B M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A,mb}^{0.6}}
$$

Here $D_{AB}^0$ is the **infinite dilution** diffusivity (composition $x_A \to 0$).

### Step 4 — Composition correction (Vignes / Leffler-Cullinan)

For finite concentrations, the Vignes equation relates the diffusivity at any composition to the infinite dilution values:

$$
D_{AB}(x_A) = \left( D_{AB}^{\infty} \right)^{x_B} \cdot \left( D_{BA}^{\infty} \right)^{x_A}
$$

Taking the natural log:

$$
\ln D_{AB}(x_A) = x_B \ln D_{AB}^{\infty} + x_A \ln D_{BA}^{\infty}
$$

where $x_A + x_B = 1$.

### Step 5 — Physical interpretation

- The **exponent on $T$** is **1.0** in Stokes-Einstein, but real data show $n \approx 1.5$ to 2 because $\mu_B$ also depends on $T$.
- The **exponent on $V_{A,mb}$** is empirically **0.6** (not 1/3) because small molecules do not behave as rigid spheres.
- **Composition** enters through $\mu_B(x)$ and through the Vignes interpolation.

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical Problem)

**Problem:**
Estimate the diffusivity of **CO₂ (A)** in **air (B)** at **320 K** and **1.5 atm**, given that at **273 K** and **1$~$atm**, $D_{AB} = 0.138$ cm²/s. Use the kinetic theory scaling law.

### Step 1 — Write the scaling law

$$
D_{AB,2} = D_{AB,1} \cdot \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{1.5} \cdot \left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Plug in values

$$
D_{AB,2} = 0.138 \times \left( \frac{320}{273} \right)^{1.5} \times \left( \frac{1}{1.5} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate term-by-term

$$
\left( \frac{320}{273} \right) = 1.1722
$$

$$
1.1722^{1.5} = 1.1722 \times \sqrt{1.1722} = 1.1722 \times 1.0827 = 1.2691
$$

$$
0.138 \times 1.2691 = 0.1751
$$

$$
0.1751 \times \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.1167 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}
$$

### Step 4 — Final answer with units

$$
\boxed{D_{AB,2} \approx 0.117 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.17 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Increasing $T$ by ~17% should increase $D$ by ~27% (since $1.17^{1.5} \approx 1.27$). Increasing $P$ by 50% should decrease $D$ by 33%. Net effect: $D$ should be slightly **lower** than at STP. Result: $0.117 < 0.138$ — consistent with physics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: How Parameters Influence $D_{AB}$

The block diagram below maps the **causal pathways** through which temperature, pressure, and composition alter the binary diffusivity in gases and liquids. This is a **molecular-level process flow**, replacing the physical drawing of molecular collisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([External Operating Conditions]) --> B1[Temperature T]
    A0 --> B2[Pressure P]
    A0 --> B3[Composition xA, xB]
    A0 --> B4[Solvent Properties muB, phiB]

    B1 --> C1G[Mean Molecular Speed c_bar increases]
    B1 --> C1L[Kinetic Energy kT increases]
    B1 --> C1V[Solvent Viscosity muB decreases]

    B2 --> C2G[Number Density n increases]
    B2 --> C2L[Negligible direct effect on liquids]
    B2 --> C2E[Indirect via free volume]

    B3 --> C3G[Slight effect for ideal gases]
    B3 --> C3L[Solute Solvent interactions]
    B3 --> C3V[Viscosity muB changes with x]

    B4 --> C4[Association factor phiB and molar volume VA]

    C1G --> D1[Gas Diffusivity DAB proportional to T1.5]
    C1L --> D2[Liquid Diffusivity numerator boost]
    C1V --> D3[Liquid Diffusivity denominator reduction]
    C1L --> D2
    C1V --> D3

    C2G --> D1
    C2E --> D3

    C3G --> D1
    C3L --> D2
    C3V --> D3

    C4 --> D2

    D1 --> E1((Final DAB in Gas Phase))
    D2 --> E2((Final DAB in Liquid Phase))
    D3 --> E2

    E1 --> F1[Chapman Enskog Equation]
    E1 --> F2[Fuller Schettler Giddings]
    E2 --> F3[Wilke Chang Equation]
    E2 --> F4[Hayduk Minhas Equation]
    E2 --> F5[Stokes Einstein Equation]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Phase Behavior of $D_{AB}$ vs $T$ and $P$

The figure below illustrates the **trend comparison** of $D_{AB}$ as a function of $T$ and $P$ for gas and liquid systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG[Gas Phase Trends]
        G1[T increases] -->|DAB rises| G2[T to the 1.5 power]
        G3[P increases] -->|DAB falls| G4[1 divided by P]
    end

    subgraph SL[Liquid Phase Trends]
        L1[T increases] -->|DAB rises strongly| L2[T to the 1.5 to 2 power]
        L3[P increases] -->|DAB slight decrease| L4[~5 to 15 percent per 100 atm]
        L5[xA increases] -->|DAB may rise or fall| L6[Vignes interpolation]
    end

    SG --> Compare([Combined engineering design: choose T and P to maximize mass transfer])
    SL --> Compare
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting a Diffusivity Correlation

Use the flowchart below to select the most appropriate correlation for a given KTU 2024 problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the phase?} -->|Gas| Q2{Is it a polar or non-ideal pair?}
    Q1 -->|Liquid| Q3{Is the solute large?}

    Q2 -->|No, non-polar| R1[Chapman Enskog]
    Q2 -->|Yes, polar| R2[Fuller Schettler Giddings]

    Q3 -->|Yes, large biomolecule| R3[Stokes Einstein]
    Q3 -->|No, small molecule| Q4{Aqueous solvent?}

    Q4 -->|Yes, water rich| R4[Wilke Chang with phiB equal 2.6]
    Q4 -->|No, organic solvent| R5[Hayduk Minhas]

    R1 --> X1[Use sigma and OmegaD]
    R2 --> X2[Use atomic diffusion volumes]
    R3 --> X3[Use Stokes radius rA]
    R4 --> X4[Use VA_mb and muB]
    R5 --> X5[Use VA_mb and muB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block in the diagrams above is a **functional node**, not a physical object. The arrows indicate **causal influence** of a parameter on the diffusivity, ending at the predictive correlation. This addresses topics where physical drawings (e.g., molecular collisions) are impractical to render in Mermaid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Explain the effect of temperature on the diffusivity of gases. How does the theoretical $T^{1.5}$ dependence arise from kinetic theory?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Statement:** The diffusivity of a gas increases with temperature. For most gases, $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.5}$ to $T^{1.8}$.
- (1 Mark) **Origin:** From kinetic theory, $D_{AB} \propto \bar{c} \cdot \lambda$, where the mean speed $\bar{c} \propto T^{1/2}$ and the mean free path $\lambda \propto T$ (at constant $P$). The product gives $T^{3/2}$.
- (1 Mark) **Practical Note:** In real (non-ideal) gases, the exponent deviates to 1.6–1.8 due to the **collision integral** $\Omega_D(T)$ decreasing with $T$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*Why is the effect of pressure on liquid diffusivity considered negligible compared to gases? Mention one exception.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) Liquids are **incompressible**; their number density does not change appreciably with pressure, so $D_{AB}$ is essentially independent of $P$ in the low-to-moderate pressure range.
- (1 Mark) In gases, $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$ because $P$ directly changes the mean free path via the number density $n = P/(k_BT)$.
- (1 Mark) **Exception:** At very high pressures (e.g., $P > 100$ atm, as in supercritical bioreactors), the solvent compresses, free volume decreases, and $D_{AB}$ can drop by 5–15% per 100 atm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]**
*(a)* Derive the **Chapman-Enskog equation** for binary gas diffusivity. Clearly state the assumptions. — **7 Marks**

*(b)* Oxygen (A) diffuses through nitrogen (B). At $T_1 = 273$ K and $P_1 = 1$ atm, $D_{AB} = 0.181$ cm²/s. Estimate $D_{AB}$ at $T_2 = 310$ K and $P_2 = 2$ atm. Use the $T^{1.5}/P$ scaling. — **7 Marks**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

1. **(1 Mark) Assumptions:**
   - Spherical, non-interacting molecules
   - Elastic collisions
   - Low-to-moderate pressure (ideal gas behavior)
   - Binary, isothermal, isobaric system

2. **(2 Marks) Start with kinetic theory expressions:**
   - Mean molecular speed: $\bar{c}_A = \sqrt{8 k_B T / (\pi m_A)}$
   - Mean free path: $\lambda_A = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \, \pi d_{AB}^2 \, P)$

3. **(2 Marks) Combine:** $D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{c}_A \lambda_A$

   Substituting and using $\mu_{AB} = m_A m_B / (m_A + m_B)$:

   $$
   D_{AB} = \frac{3}{16} \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi k_B T / \mu_{AB}}}{n \, \pi \, d_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}
   $$

4. **(2 Marks) Final substitution** ($n = P/k_BT$ and conversion to molar mass $M_{AB}$):

   $$
   D_{AB} = \frac{0.00266 \, T^{3/2}}{M_{AB}^{1/2} \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D \, P} \quad \text{(cm²/s, K, atm, g/mol)}
   $$

   This is the **Chapman-Enskog equation**. **Q.E.D.**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

1. **(1 Mark) Write the scaling equation:**

   $$
   D_2 = D_1 \cdot \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{1.5} \cdot \left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} \right)
   $$

2. **(2 Marks) Temperature correction:**

   $$
   \left( \frac{310}{273} \right)^{1.5} = 1.1355^{1.5} = 1.1355 \times \sqrt{1.1355} = 1.1355 \times 1.0656 = 1.2100
   $$

3. **(2 Marks) Pressure correction:**

   $$
   \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
   $$

4. **(2 Marks) Final calculation:**

   $$
   D_2 = 0.181 \times 1.2100 \times 0.5 = 0.1095 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}
   $$

   $$
   \boxed{D_{AB}(310 \text{ K}, 2 \text{ atm}) \approx 0.110 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.10 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}
   $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the $P_1/P_2$ ratio** and only applying the $T^{1.5}$ correction.
> 2. **Confusing $D \propto T^n$** with $D \propto T^{1/n}$.
> 3. **Mixing up units** (e.g., $T$ in °C instead of K). Always use Kelvin!
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — KTU 2024 strictly allocates 1 mark for the assumptions block.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the effect of **composition** on the diffusivity of liquid mixtures. Derive the **Vignes equation** for composition-dependent liquid diffusivity. — **7 Marks**

*(b)* Acetone (A) diffuses in water (B) at 298 K. Given: $V_{A,mb} = 74$ cm³/mol, $\mu_B = 0.89$ cP, $M_B = 18$ g/mol, $\phi_B = 2.6$ (water). Calculate $D_{AB}^0$ using the **Wilke-Chang equation**. — **7 Marks**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

1. **(2 Marks) Effect of composition on liquid diffusivity:**
   - As $x_A$ increases, solvent viscosity $\mu_B$ may rise or fall, changing $D_{AB}$ nonlinearly.
   - Specific solute-solvent interactions (H-bonding, polarity) cause non-ideal behavior.
   - At infinite dilution ($x_A \to 0$), $D_{AB}$ approaches a constant $D_{AB}^{\infty}$; similarly for $D_{BA}^{\infty}$ at $x_B \to 0$.

2. **(3 Marks) Derivation of Vignes equation:**

   Start with the empirical logarithmic interpolation:

   $$
   \ln D_{AB}(x_A) = x_B \ln D_{AB}^{\infty} + x_A \ln D_{BA}^{\infty}
   $$

   Exponentiating:

   $$
   D_{AB}(x_A) = \exp\left( x_B \ln D_{AB}^{\infty} + x_A \ln D_{BA}^{\infty} \right)
   $$

   Using the identity $a^x b^y = \exp(x \ln a + y \ln b)$:

   $$
   \boxed{D_{AB}(x_A) = \left( D_{AB}^{\infty} \right)^{x_B} \cdot \left( D_{BA}^{\infty} \right)^{x_A}}
   $$

   This is the **Vignes equation** (1966). **Q.E.D.**

3. **(2 Marks) Physical checks:**
   - At $x_A = 0$: $D_{AB} = D_{AB}^{\infty}$ ✓
   - At $x_A = 1$: $D_{AB} = D_{BA}^{\infty}$ ✓
   - The interpolation is **geometric** (multiplicative), not arithmetic, consistent with thermodynamic activity coefficients.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

1. **(1 Mark) State the Wilke-Chang equation:**

   $$
   D_{AB}^0 = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\phi_B M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A,mb}^{0.6}}
   $$

2. **(2 Marks) Compute the numerator term** $(\phi_B M_B)^{1/2}$:

   $$
   \phi_B M_B = 2.6 \times 18 = 46.8
   $$

   $$
   \sqrt{46.8} = 6.841
   $$

3. **(2 Marks) Compute $V_{A,mb}^{0.6}$:**

   $$
   74^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 74} = e^{0.6 \times 4.304} = e^{2.582} = 13.22
   $$

4. **(1 Mark) Assemble the full numerator:**

   $$
   7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.841 \times 298 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2038.6 = 1.5086 \times 10^{-4}
   $$

5. **(1 Mark) Denominator:**

   $$
   \mu_B \times V_{A,mb}^{0.6} = 0.89 \times 13.22 = 11.77
   $$

6. **(Final Answer):**

   $$
   D_{AB}^0 = \frac{1.5086 \times 10^{-4}}{11.77} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}
   $$

   $$
   \boxed{D_{AB}^0 \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.28 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}
   $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfalls in this question:
> 1. **Forgetting the association factor** $\phi_B = 2.6$ for water. Without it, the answer is **2.6× too low**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $\mu_B$ MUST be in **cP** and $V_{A,mb}$ in **cm³/mol** for the constant $7.4 \times 10^{-8}$ to yield $D$ in cm²/s.
> 3. **Power on molar volume is 0.6**, not 1/3 (which is the theoretical Stokes-Einstein value). Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 4. **Wilke-Chang is for INFINITE DILUTION** ($x_A \to 0$). For finite concentrations, you must apply the **Vignes correction** from part (a).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEBBT635 Module 3:**

- **Temperature Effect (Gases):** $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.5}$ to $T^{1.8}$ (kinetic theory + collision integral correction).
- **Temperature Effect (Liquids):** $D_{AB} \propto T / \mu_B(T)$; stronger than $T^1$ because $\mu_B$ drops exponentially with $T$.
- **Pressure Effect (Gases):** $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$ — always inversely proportional.
- **Pressure Effect (Liquids):** Essentially independent; weak decrease at $P > 100$ atm.
- **Composition Effect (Gases):** Negligible for ideal gases; use Stefan-Maxwell for polar/associating systems.
- **Composition Effect (Liquids):** Use the **Vignes equation** to interpolate between $D_{AB}^{\infty}$ and $D_{BA}^{\infty}$.
- **Chapman-Enskog (gases):** $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.00266 \, T^{3/2}}{M_{AB}^{1/2} \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D \, P}$
- **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings (gases):** Use atomic diffusion volumes $\Sigma v$; easier to apply in practice.
- **Wilke-Chang (liquids, dilute):** $D_{AB}^0 = \dfrac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, (\phi_B M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A,mb}^{0.6}}$
- **Stokes-Einstein (liquids, large solute):** $D_{AB} = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$
- **Order-of-magnitude benchmarks:** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s (gas); $\sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s (liquid); $\sim 10^{-12}$ m²/s (solid).
- **Always** state assumptions before derivations (KTU 2024: 1 mark reserved).
- **Always** use SI/CGS units consistently — KTU valuation is strict.
- **Biological relevance:** $D_{O_2 \text{ in water}} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s at 25°C — this is the value most fermentation calculations hinge on.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analogies Between Heat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analogies Between Heat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transport Analogies** are mathematical and physical correspondences that relate the rate equations of **momentum transfer**, **heat (energy) transfer**, and **mass transfer** when these processes occur simultaneously across a phase interface. In biological systems, these analogies allow prediction of an unknown transport rate (e.g., heat loss from tissue) using a known transport coefficient (e.g., friction factor for blood flow).

In classical chemical/bio-transport engineering, the three primary transport phenomena are governed by analogous differential equations of the form:

$$\text{Rate} = -\text{(Diffusivity)} \cdot \frac{d(\text{Intensive Property})}{dz}$$

where the intensive property is **velocity** (momentum), **temperature** (heat), or **concentration** (mass). Because the governing differential equations and their boundary conditions are mathematically isomorphic, the dimensionless groups (Nusselt, Sherwood, Prandtl, Schmidt) and the empirical correlations for the three processes can be interchanged using the analogies.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Three Runners on a Track:**
> Imagine three runners on an identical track, each carrying a different "load":
> - Runner 1 carries **momentum** (their speed/momentum is being shed to the track surface — analogous to fluid friction).
> - Runner 2 carries **heat** (their body heat is being lost to the surrounding air — analogous to convection).
> - Runner 3 carries **solute mass** (they are "sweating out" a dissolved gas — analogous to mass transfer).
>
> Although the loads differ, the *resistance* each runner experiences against the track (the boundary layer) follows the same shape. The "track" is the **boundary layer**, and the analogies simply state: *"If you know the friction for Runner 1, you can predict the heat loss for Runner 2 and the sweat rate for Runner 3."*

In **biological systems**, the same logic applies:
- Heat dissipation from the skin surface to moving air → analogous to momentum loss from blood flowing past an arterial wall.
- Oxygen diffusion across an alveolar membrane → analogous to heat transfer across a tissue interface.

### 1.3 Why Analogies Matter in Bio-Transport

- **Standard Constants / Metrics used in this topic (bolded for KTU emphasis):**
  - **Prandtl Number, Pr** — ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
  - **Schmidt Number, Sc** — ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity.
  - **Lewis Number, Le** — ratio of thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity.
  - **Stanton Number, St** — dimensionless heat-transfer coefficient.
  - **Chilton–Colburn j-factors** — empirical bridge between the three transfer modes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The Governing Flux Equations

All three transport mechanisms in 1-D, steady state share a common flux form:

$$J_{\text{momentum}} = -\nu \frac{d(\rho u)}{dz}$$

$$J_{\text{heat}} = -\alpha \frac{d(\rho C_p T)}{dz}$$

$$J_{\text{mass}} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (momentum diffusivity), $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, and $D_{AB}$ = mass diffusivity.

The **analogy** arises because, in each case, the flux is driven by the gradient of a conserved quantity, and the resistance to transfer is concentrated in a thin **boundary layer** near the interface.

### 2.2 The Major Analogies (in Logical Order)

1. **Reynolds Analogy (1874)** — links heat transfer and momentum transfer (skin friction) for **Pr = 1**:
   $$St = \frac{C_f}{2}$$
2. **Prandtl Analogy (1910)** — extends Reynolds by adding a *laminar sub-layer correction*:
   $$St = \frac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(Pr-1)}$$
3. **Von Kármán Analogy (1939)** — further refines Prandtl by accounting for buffer and turbulent core layers.
4. **Chilton–Colburn Analogy (1934)** — the most general and widely used; introduces the **j-factor** for both heat and mass transfer:
   $$j_H = j_D = \frac{C_f}{2}$$
   $$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} \quad ; \quad j_D = \frac{k_c}{u} Sc^{2/3}$$
5. **Mass–Heat Analogy (Lewis Relation)** — connects $j_H$ and $j_D$ via the **Lewis Number**:
   $$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \frac{Sc}{Pr}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cheat Sheet — Memorize the table below for the ESE. Do NOT use the `|` character; all absolute values are written with `\vert`.**

| # | Analogy / Quantity | Formula | Valid Range / Limitation | Typical Use in Bio-Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds | $St = C_f / 2$ | $Pr = 1$, $Sc = 1$ only | Approximate skin heat loss |
| 2 | Prandtl | $St = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1+5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(Pr-1)}$ | $0.5 \le Pr \le 10$ | Blood–wall heat exchange |
| 3 | Von Kármán | $St\,\Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1+5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,\{\Pr-1+\ln[(5\Pr+1)/6]\}}$ | Wider Pr range | Tissue cooling models |
| 4 | Chilton–Colburn $j_H$ | $j_H = St\,\Pr^{2/3}$ | $0.6 \le Pr \le 60$ | Drug-coated stent heat maps |
| 5 | Chilton–Colburn $j_D$ | $j_D = \dfrac{k_c}{u}\,Sc^{2/3}$ | $0.6 \le Sc \le 1600$ | Oxygen transfer in lungs |
| 6 | $j_H = j_D$ bridge | $\dfrac{St\,\Pr^{2/3}}{k_c/u\,Sc^{2/3}} = 1$ | When Le = 1 | Cross-prediction |
| 7 | Lewis Number | $Le = \alpha / D_{AB} = Sc / Pr$ | Dimensionless | Drying of biological tissue |
| 8 | Skin friction | $C_f = 2\,\tau_w / (\rho u^2)$ | — | Wall shear in arteries |

**Units Check (mandatory for KTU 5-mark derivations):** $St$, $C_f$, $j_H$, $j_D$ are all **dimensionless**. $k_c$ has units of m/s; $u$ has units of m/s; $\alpha$, $D_{AB}$ have units of m²/s.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical device design:** Predicting heat removal from an implanted artificial organ (e.g., ventricular assist device) using the known drag coefficient of blood flow.
- **Pharmaceutical drying:** Using air-side heat-transfer correlations to predict moisture (mass) removal from a lyophilized (freeze-dried) drug.
- **Tissue engineering scaffolds:** Using the $j_H = j_D$ analogy to design scaffolds where nutrient (mass) supply and waste-heat removal are coupled.
- **Respiratory therapy:** Estimating oxygen mass-transfer rates in membrane oxygenators from heat-transfer experiments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Start the derivation from the integral momentum and energy boundary-layer equations** — this is the standard KTU-expected starting point.

**Step 1 — Integral Momentum Equation (von Kármán):**

The total drag (skin friction) on a flat plate of length $x$ per unit width is:

$$\tau_w = \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta} \rho u (u_\infty - u)\, dy$$

**Step 2 — Integral Energy Equation (same control volume):**

The convective heat loss from the plate per unit width is:

$$q'' = \rho C_p \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta_T} u (T_\infty - T)\, dy$$

**Step 3 — Assume identical velocity and temperature profiles (the *Reynolds* assumption):**

This is the *crucial* assumption. Reynolds postulated that the **dimensionless velocity profile** equals the **dimensionless temperature profile**:

$$\frac{u}{u_\infty} = \frac{T - T_s}{T_\infty - T_s}$$

This effectively forces $\delta = \delta_T$ and makes the Prandtl number **unity** (i.e., $Pr = \nu/\alpha = 1$).

**Step 4 — Equate the two fluxes through the wall:**

The wall heat flux by conduction is $q''_w = k (T_\infty - T_s)/\delta_T$ and the wall shear stress is $\tau_w = \mu u_\infty / \delta$.

**Step 5 — Form the ratio $q''_w / (\tau_w \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T)$:**

$$\frac{q''_w}{\tau_w C_p (T_\infty - T_s)} = \frac{k/\delta_T}{(\mu u_\infty/\delta)\,C_p} \cdot \frac{\delta_T}{1} = \frac{k}{\mu C_p u_\infty}$$

Using the definition $St = q''_w / [\rho u_\infty C_p (T_\infty - T_s)]$ and $C_f = 2\tau_w/(\rho u_\infty^2)$:

$$\boxed{St = \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

This is the **Reynolds Analogy** — derived for a fluid with **$Pr = 1$** and no form drag (no pressure drag term).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Prandtl Analogy (Two-Layer Model)

Prandtl recognized that the Reynolds analogy is only valid for $Pr=1$. He proposed a **two-zone boundary layer**:
- A **viscous (laminar) sub-layer** of thickness $\delta_v$, where conduction/diffusion dominates.
- A **fully turbulent core** of thickness $(\delta - \delta_v)$, where eddy transport dominates.

**Step 1 — Heat flux through the viscous sub-layer (conduction):**

$$q''_v = k \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{\delta_v}$$

**Step 2 — Total thermal resistance (sub-layer + turbulent core) in series:**

$$\frac{T_\infty - T_s}{q''_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\delta_v}{k} + \frac{\delta - \delta_v}{\rho C_p u_\infty \varepsilon_H}$$

where $\varepsilon_H$ is the **eddy thermal diffusivity** in the turbulent core.

**Step 3 — By the Reynolds analogy applied to the turbulent core alone:**

$$\frac{q''_{\text{core}}}{\tau_{\text{core}}} = C_p (T_\infty - T_s)$$

**Step 4 — Combine and eliminate $\delta_v$** using the fact that in the viscous sub-layer $\tau_w = \mu u_\infty/\delta_v$ and $q''_v = k (T_\infty - T_s)/\delta_v$. Taking the ratio $q''/\tau_w$:

$$\frac{q''}{\tau_w} = \frac{C_p (T_\infty - T_s)}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(Pr-1)}$$

(The factor 5 arises from $\delta_v/\delta \approx 5\sqrt{C_f/2}$ — a standard Prandtl mixing-length result.)

**Step 5 — Re-write in terms of Stanton number:**

$$\boxed{St = \frac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(Pr-1)}}$$

This is the **Prandtl Analogy**, valid for $0.5 \le Pr \le 10$ (covers most gases and many liquids, including blood plasma with $Pr \approx 7$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Chilton–Colburn j-Factor Analogy

Chilton and Colburn (1934) observed empirically that the Prandtl-type correction could be written in a more universal form by using a **2/3-power exponent on Pr and Sc** rather than a linear term.

**Step 1 — Start from Prandtl analogy and fit exponent:**

From a large experimental dataset covering $Pr = 0.6$ to $Pr = 60$:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} \approx \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply analogous reasoning to mass transfer** (replace Pr with Sc and St with mass-transfer Stanton number $k_c/u$):

$$j_D = \frac{k_c}{u} \cdot Sc^{2/3} \approx \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two j-factors** (the empirical "bridge"):

$$\boxed{j_H = j_D = \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

This is the **Chilton–Colburn Analogy**, the most-used form in chemical and bio-engineering practice.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Analogy

| Fluid / Bio-System | Pr (approx) | Sc (approx) | Best Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air over skin | 0.7 | 0.6 | Reynolds (good) |
| Water in dialysis | 7.0 | 350 | Chilton–Colburn |
| Blood (plasma) | 7.0 | ~2000 | Chilton–Colburn |
| Oil in bioreactor | 50–100 | 100–1000 | Von Kármán |
| Steam in sterilizer | 1.0 | 1.0 | Reynolds (exact) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hierarchical Map of the Three Analogies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transport Phenomena Family] --> B[Momentum Transfer]
    A --> C[Heat Transfer]
    A --> D[Mass Transfer]
    B --> E[Driving Force: d rho u over dz]
    C --> F[Driving Force: dT over dz]
    D --> G[Driving Force: dC over dz]
    E --> H[Reynolds Analogy Pr equals 1]
    F --> H
    E --> I[Prandtl Analogy 0.5 to 10]
    C --> I
    E --> J[Von Karman Analogy Wider Pr]
    C --> J
    E --> K[Chilton Colburn jH equals jD]
    C --> K
    D --> K
    K --> L[Bio Applications: Lung Oxygenator]
    K --> M[Bio Applications: Artificial Organ Cooling]
    K --> N[Bio Applications: Tissue Scaffold Design]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of the j-Factor Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Measure Drag Coefficient Cf] --> stepB[Step 2 Compute jH equals Cf over 2]
    stepB --> stepC{Choose Pr Range}
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3a jH equals St Pr to power 2 over 3]
    stepC --> stepE[Step 3b jH equals kc over u Sc to power 2 over 3]
    stepD --> stepF[Step 4 Predict Unknown St or kc]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Step 5 Apply to Bio System e.g. oxygenator]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Adaptation Note)

For the topic of *Analogies Between Heat* (and momentum/mass), a physical schematic would be a side-by-side depiction of three parallel boundary layers. Since pure Mermaid cannot accurately render the curved profiles of velocity, temperature, and concentration, the **Functional Architecture Flow** in the diagrams above maps the conceptual inputs, analogy selection logic, and biological outputs — which is the academically accepted substitute for a hand-drawn figure in KTU-evaluated answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the **Reynolds Analogy** between heat transfer and momentum transfer. Under what condition is it strictly valid?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Reynolds Analogy states that for flow over a flat surface,

$$St = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

where $St$ is the Stanton number for heat transfer and $C_f$ is the skin-friction coefficient for momentum transfer. It is **strictly valid only when the Prandtl number of the fluid equals unity (Pr = 1)**, i.e., when the thermal diffusivity equals the momentum (kinematic) diffusivity. For air under moderate conditions (Pr ≈ 0.7), it serves as a reasonable first-order approximation.

*Marking split: 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for definition of $St$ and $C_f$, 1 mark for the Pr = 1 condition.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **Chilton–Colburn j-factors** $j_H$ and $j_D$. What fundamental relationship do they obey?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Chilton–Colburn j-factors are empirical dimensionless groups defined as:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

$$j_D = \frac{k_c}{u} \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

They obey the **fundamental analogy relationship**:

$$j_H = j_D \approx \frac{C_f}{2}$$

This relationship allows the engineer to estimate an unknown heat- or mass-transfer coefficient from a known friction factor (or vice versa) in turbulent flow.

*Marking split: 1 mark each for $j_H$ and $j_D$ definitions, 1 mark for the analogy relation.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Heat ↔ Momentum Analogy Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Starting from the integral momentum and energy equations for flow over a flat plate, **derive the Reynolds Analogy** $St = C_f/2$. Clearly state the assumption that restricts its validity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a bio-reactor, a thin tissue culture plate is exposed to air at $u_\infty = 5$ m/s, $T_\infty = 37$ °C. The plate surface is maintained at $T_s = 32$ °C. The air properties are $k = 0.026$ W/m·K, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1.16$ kg/m³, $C_p = 1005$ J/kg·K. The experimentally measured skin-friction coefficient is $C_f = 0.0045$.

   Using the **Reynolds Analogy**, determine:
   - (i) The Stanton number.
   - (ii) The convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$.
   - (iii) The heat lost per unit area, $q''$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)** — See Section 3.1 of these notes. The key steps are:
- Write the integral momentum equation: $\tau_w = \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^\delta \rho u (u_\infty - u)\,dy$.
- Write the integral energy equation: $q'' = \rho C_p \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta_T} u(T_\infty - T)\,dy$.
- Assume $u/u_\infty = (T-T_s)/(T_\infty - T_s)$ ⇒ $\delta = \delta_T$ ⇒ $Pr = 1$.
- Take the ratio of wall fluxes to obtain $St = C_f/2$.
- State that validity is restricted to **Pr = 1** and to flows with no form drag (flat plate, no pressure gradient).

*Marking split: 2 marks for momentum eq, 2 marks for energy eq, 2 marks for the assumption and ratio, 1 mark for the boxed result and validity condition.*

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Stanton number:**
By Reynolds analogy,
$$St = \frac{C_f}{2} = \frac{0.0045}{2} = 0.00225$$
*(1 mark)*

**(ii) Convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$:**
Definition of $St$:
$$St = \frac{h}{\rho u_\infty C_p}$$
Solve for $h$:
$$h = St \cdot \rho u_\infty C_p = 0.00225 \times 1.16 \times 5 \times 1005$$
$$h = 0.00225 \times 5829 \approx 13.12 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$
*(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for calculation)*

**(iii) Heat flux:**
$$q'' = h (T_\infty - T_s) = 13.12 \times (37 - 32) = 13.12 \times 5$$
$$\boxed{q'' = 65.6 \text{ W/m}^2}$$
*(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for calculation)*

**Discussion (2 marks):** Since $Pr_{\text{air}} \approx 0.7 \ne 1$, the Reynolds analogy introduces a small error (~15–20%). The Prandtl or Chilton–Colburn analogy would yield a more accurate answer; the calculated value of $h \approx 13.1$ W/m²·K is consistent with literature for forced convection over a small biological plate.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Chilton–Colburn / Mass-Heat Focus (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the **Chilton–Colburn j-factors** for heat and mass transfer. Show that $j_H = j_D$ leads to a direct relation between the **heat-transfer coefficient $h$** and the **mass-transfer coefficient $k_c$**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A membrane oxygenator is being designed. In a pilot test with air at $u = 2$ m/s, the heat-transfer coefficient measured on the membrane surface is $h = 45$ W/m²·K. The fluid properties are $Pr = 0.71$ and $Sc = 0.61$ for the gas mixture. The membrane temperature is $T_s = 35$ °C and the bulk gas is at $T_\infty = 25$ °C.

   Using the **Chilton–Colburn analogy**, predict the **mass-transfer coefficient $k_c$** for oxygen transport across the membrane. Comment on whether the **Lewis Number assumption (Le = 1)** is justified here. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- Define $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{h}{\rho u C_p}\cdot Pr^{2/3}$ — *(1 mark)*
- Define $j_D = \dfrac{k_c}{u} \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ — *(1 mark)*
- Empirical Chilton–Colburn result: $j_H = j_D$ — *(1 mark)*
- Equate:
  $$\frac{h}{\rho u C_p}\, Pr^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{u}\, Sc^{2/3}$$
- Cancel $u$:
  $$\boxed{k_c = \frac{h}{\rho C_p}\left(\frac{Pr}{Sc}\right)^{2/3}}$$
  *(3 marks — algebra and boxed result)*

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Substitute $h = 45$ W/m²·K, $\rho \approx 1.16$ kg/m³, $C_p = 1005$ J/kg·K, $Pr = 0.71$, $Sc = 0.61$:

$$k_c = \frac{45}{1.16 \times 1005} \times \left(\frac{0.71}{0.61}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$= \frac{45}{1165.8} \times (1.1639)^{2/3}$$

$$= 0.03861 \times 1.1086$$

$$\boxed{k_c \approx 0.0428 \text{ m/s}}$$
*(4 marks — 2 for substitution, 1 for the power-law term, 1 for final value)*

**Lewis Number check (2 marks):**
$$Le = \frac{Sc}{Pr} = \frac{0.61}{0.71} \approx 0.86$$
Since $Le \approx 1$ (within 15%), the **Chilton–Colburn analogy is well-justified** for this gas-phase system. If $Le$ were far from 1 (e.g., in a liquid-phase system), the j-factor bridge would need a Lewis-number correction: $j_D = j_H / Le^{2/3}$.

**Biological significance (1 mark):** A $k_c$ of ~0.04 m/s is in the correct order of magnitude for oxygen transport across a microporous membrane oxygenator in clinical use, validating the analogy method for medical-device design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the Pr = 1 condition** when stating the Reynolds analogy: this is an instant 1-mark loss in Part A and 2-mark loss in Part B.
> 2. **Unit confusion on $k_c$** — students often write $k_c$ in m/s² or m²/s. It is **m/s** (a mass-transfer *velocity*).
> 3. **Mixing up $j_H$ and $j_D$ definitions** — write them out explicitly in your answer; do not just quote the analogy.
> 4. **Forgetting the 2/3 exponent** in the Chilton–Colburn definitions — it is empirical, *not* 1.
> 5. **In numerical problems, not showing the substitution** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for the substituted expression and 1 mark for the final number separately.
> 6. **Using the Reynolds analogy for liquids** (e.g., blood) — *never* do this; always go to Prandtl or Chilton–Colburn for $Pr \ne 1$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reynolds Analogy:** $St = C_f/2$ — valid only for **Pr = 1**.
- **Prandtl Analogy:** adds the viscous sub-layer correction factor $5\sqrt{C_f/2}(Pr-1)$ in the denominator.
- **Von Kármán Analogy:** adds a logarithmic buffer-layer term; valid for wider Pr.
- **Chilton–Colburn j-factors:** $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ and $j_D = (k_c/u)\cdot Sc^{2/3}$; bridge: $j_H = j_D = C_f/2$.
- **Lewis Number:** $Le = \alpha/D_{AB} = Sc/Pr$; if $Le = 1$, heat and mass boundary layers are identical in thickness.
- **Stanton Number:** $St = h/(\rho u C_p)$ — always define before using.
- **Skin-friction coefficient:** $C_f = 2\tau_w/(\rho u^2)$.
- **Biological relevance:** lung oxygenators, artificial organ cooling, tissue scaffolds, pharmaceutical drying.
- **Validity hierarchy (in order of increasing accuracy):** Reynolds → Prandtl → Von Kármán → Chilton–Colburn (j-factor).
- **In the exam:** always state the assumption, then write the equation, then substitute, then compute, then comment on the answer.
- **Remember:** $St$, $C_f$, $j_H$, $j_D$ are all **dimensionless**; $h$ is W/m²·K; $k_c$ is m/s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MASS TRANSFER FUNDAMENTALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Mass transfer** is the net movement of a chemical species from a region of higher chemical potential (concentration) to a region of lower chemical potential, occurring through molecular diffusion and/or convective transport mechanisms. In biological systems, mass transfer governs the transport of **oxygen (O₂)**, **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, **nutrients**, **metabolites**, **drugs**, and **hormones** across cellular membranes, tissues, and organ systems.

### 1.1 The Two Driving Mechanisms of Mass Transfer

| Mechanism | Description | Dominant Scale |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Molecular Diffusion** | Random Brownian motion of molecules from high to low concentration | Microscopic (μm–mm) |
| **Convective Mass Transfer** | Bulk fluid motion carrying species along | Macroscopic (mm–m) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Heat Transfer:**
> Unlike heat transfer, mass transfer involves the movement of *actual physical matter* (molecules, ions) across a system. This makes it fundamentally more complex because we must track individual chemical species, their interactions, and their boundary fluxes simultaneously.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Creamer Experiment

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Drop a sugar cube into a still cup of coffee without stirring. You will notice the sugar slowly diffuses from the bottom (high concentration) to the top (low concentration) over several minutes — this is **molecular diffusion**. Now stir the coffee with a spoon. The sugar distributes almost instantly — this bulk mixing is **convective mass transfer**.
>
> **Biological Analogy:** The same process governs how **oxygen** moves from the alveoli of your lungs (high O₂ concentration) into the bloodstream (lower O₂ concentration), and how **CO₂** moves in the opposite direction to be exhaled.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants for Mass Transfer

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** **Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ molecules/mol**
- **Boltzmann Constant (k_B):** **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Standard Temperature (T):** **T = 298.15 K (25 °C)**
- **Standard Pressure (P):** **P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fick's Laws of Diffusion — The Foundation of Mass Transfer

### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law (Steady-State Diffusion)

Fick's First Law describes mass transfer when the concentration profile is **time-invariant** (steady-state). It states that the molar flux of a species is proportional to the negative concentration gradient.

**Mathematical Form (1-D Cartesian):**

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Where:
- $J_A$ = molar flux of species A [mol/(m²·s)]
- $D_{AB}$ = binary diffusion coefficient of A in B [m²/s]
- $\frac{dC_A}{dx}$ = concentration gradient of A in the x-direction [mol/m⁴]
- Negative sign indicates flow from **high → low** concentration

### 2.1.2 Fick's Second Law (Unsteady-State Diffusion)

When the concentration profile evolves with time, Fick's Second Law governs the transient behavior.

**Mathematical Form (1-D Cartesian):**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

### 2.1.3 Why the Negative Sign? — The "Why" Behind Fick's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Origin:**
> The negative sign arises from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — systems spontaneously evolve toward maximum entropy (uniform mixing). Molecules naturally move from regions of high chemical potential to low chemical potential, so flux is always **opposite** to the concentration gradient direction.

## 2.2 Modes of Mass Transfer in Biological Systems

| Mode | Governing Equation | Biological Example |
|------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Diffusion through stagnant film** | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{z_2-z_1} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | O₂ transport across alveolar wall |
| **Diffusion with convection** | $N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dx} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ | Drug delivery in bloodstream |
| **Diffusion through membrane** | $N_A = \frac{P_M}{z_2-z_1}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | Glucose uptake by intestinal cells |
| **Permeation through polymer** | $N_A = \frac{D_S K}{L}(p_1 - p_2)$ | Controlled drug release from patch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeability (P_M) Relationship:**
> The membrane permeability is defined as:
> $$P_M = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot K}{L}$$
> Where **K** is the partition coefficient (dimensionless) and **L** is the membrane thickness [m].

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (molar flux) | mol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (transient) | mol/(m³·s) |
| 3 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1}-C_{A2})}{z_2-z_1}$ | Steady-state flux through slab | mol/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $Sh = \frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$ | Sherwood Number (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $P_M = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot K}{L}$ | Membrane permeability | m/s |
| 6 | $N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Convective mass transfer (film theory) | mol/(m²·s) |
| 7 | $J_A^* = -c D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dx}$ | Flux in terms of mole fraction | mol/(m²·s) |
| 8 | $Pe_M = \frac{vL}{D_{AB}}$ | Mass transfer Peclet Number | — |
| 9 | $\frac{D_{AB} \mu}{\rho} = \text{const}$ | Schmidt number relation | m²/s |
| 10 | $\delta_M = \frac{D_{AB}}{k_c}$ | Mass transfer boundary layer thickness | m |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:**
> Mass transfer principles are used to design **artificial kidneys (hemodialyzers)**, **oxygenators for cardiopulmonary bypass**, **controlled-release drug delivery systems**, **biosensors**, and **tissue engineering scaffolds**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Diffusion Through a Planar Membrane

### Problem Setup

A biological membrane of thickness **L = 2 × 10⁻⁵ m** separates two aqueous solutions. The concentration of species A on side 1 is **C_{A1} = 0.1 mol/m³** and on side 2 is **C_{A2} = 0.02 mol/m³**. The diffusion coefficient is **D_{AB} = 1.5 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s**. Calculate the steady-state molar flux and the mass transfer rate per unit area.

### Step 1: State the Governing Equation (Fick's First Law)

We begin with Fick's First Law in one dimension:

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

This equation states that flux is proportional to the concentration gradient.

### Step 2: Apply Steady-State Condition

At steady state, $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = 0$, which means the flux $J_A$ must be **constant** at every cross-section through the membrane (by conservation of mass — what flows in must flow out).

Therefore:

$$\frac{dJ_A}{dx} = 0 \implies J_A = \text{constant}$$

### Step 3: Separate Variables and Integrate

Starting from:

$$J_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \, dC_A$$

We integrate from side 1 to side 2:

$$\int_{0}^{L} J_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals

Performing the integration:

$$J_A \int_{0}^{L} dx = -D_{AB} [C_A]_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}}$$

$$J_A \cdot L = -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1})$$

$$J_A \cdot L = D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Flux

$$J_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Numerical Values

Given:
- $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s
- $C_{A1} = 0.1$ mol/m³
- $C_{A2} = 0.02$ mol/m³
- $L = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m

Compute the concentration difference:

$$\Delta C_A = C_{A1} - C_{A2} = 0.1 - 0.02 = 0.08 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

Substitute:

$$J_A = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-9})(0.08)}{2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$J_A = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-10}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{J_A = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

### Step 7: Verify the Result Using the Analytical Matrix

| Step | Action | Justification |
|------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Identified Fick's First Law as governing equation | Steady-state, no convection, planar geometry |
| 2 | Applied $J_A = \text{constant}$ | Mass conservation in absence of reaction |
| 3 | Separated variables | Enables direct integration |
| 4 | Integrated across membrane thickness L | Boundary conditions: $C_A(0) = C_{A1}$, $C_A(L) = C_{A2}$ |
| 5 | Solved algebraically for $J_A$ | Standard 1-D membrane flux equation |
| 6 | Substituted numerical data | Evaluated physical answer |
| 7 | Result: $J_A = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/(m²·s) | Biologically reasonable for small molecules |

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Transfer Coefficient from Film Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Film Theory Concept:**
> Near any interface (membrane, vessel wall, cell surface), a thin **stagnant film** of thickness $\delta_M$ exists where transport occurs purely by diffusion. Beyond this film, the bulk concentration is uniform due to convection.

Starting from the integrated form of Fick's Law across the film:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_M}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

We define the **mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ as:

$$k_c = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_M}$$

This gives the **fundamental convective mass transfer equation**:

$$\boxed{N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}$$

This simple form is the **single most important equation** in applied mass transfer engineering.

### 3.3 Dimensionless Analysis — Sherwood Number Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:**
> Analogous to the Nusselt number in heat transfer, the **Sherwood number (Sh)** is the dimensionless mass transfer coefficient. It is the ratio of convective to diffusive mass transport.

$$\boxed{Sh = \frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}}$$

Where **L** is the characteristic length [m]. High Sherwood numbers indicate convection dominates; low values indicate diffusion dominates.

**Empirical correlation for flow over a flat plate (laminar):**

$$Sh_x = 0.664 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$$

Where $Re_x = \frac{\rho v x}{\mu}$ and $Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mass Transfer Across a Biological Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bulk1["BULK FLUID SIDE 1\nHigh Concentration CA1\nConvective Region"]
    
    Film1["STAGNANT FILM 1\nThickness delta M1\nDiffusion Dominant"]
    
    Membrane["BIOLOGICAL MEMBRANE\nPhospholipid Bilayer\nThickness L\nPartition Coefficient K"]
    
    Film2["STAGNANT FILM 2\nThickness delta M2\nDiffusion Dominant"]
    
    Bulk2["BULK FLUID SIDE 2\nLow Concentration CA2\nConvective Region"]
    
    Cell["BIOLOGICAL CELL\nReceptor Binding\nMetabolic Uptake"]
    
    Flux1["Flux NA1\nConvective Diffusion\nk_c1 coefficient"]
    
    Flux2["Flux NA2\nMembrane Permeation\nP_M permeability"]
    
    Flux3["Flux NA3\nConvective Diffusion\nk_c2 coefficient"]
    
    Flux4["Flux NA4\nActive Uptake\nCarrier Mediated"]
    
    Bulk1 -->|"CA1"| Flux1
    Flux1 -->|"CA at surface 1"| Film1
    Film1 -->|"Molecular Diffusion"| Flux2
    Flux2 -->|"Driven by gradient"| Membrane
    Membrane -->|"Membrane Permeation"| Flux3
    Flux3 -->|"CA at surface 2"| Film2
    Film2 -->|"Molecular Diffusion"| Flux4
    Flux4 -->|"CA2 uptake"| Bulk2
    Flux4 -->|"Cellular binding"| Cell
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Fick's First Law of Diffusion. State the units of diffusion coefficient and explain its physical significance in biological mass transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a species A in a binary mixture is directly proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient:

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

**Units (1 Mark):**
The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ has units of **m²/s** (or cm²/s in CGS). It represents how rapidly a species spreads through a medium.

**Physical Significance in Biology (1 Mark):**
In biological systems, $D_{AB}$ determines the rate at which **O₂**, **CO₂**, **glucose**, and **drug molecules** traverse membranes and tissues. For example, the high diffusivity of O₂ in water ($D \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s) enables rapid gas exchange in alveoli, while the much lower diffusivity of glucose in tissue ($D \approx 6 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²/s) is a rate-limiting step in cellular metabolism.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is the Sherwood number? How is it analogous to the Nusselt number in heat transfer?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The **Sherwood number (Sh)** is a dimensionless group that represents the ratio of convective mass transfer to diffusive mass transfer:

$$Sh = \frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$$

Where $k_c$ is the mass transfer coefficient [m/s], $L$ is the characteristic length [m], and $D_{AB}$ is the diffusion coefficient [m²/s].

**Analogy to Nusselt Number (1.5 Marks):**
Analogous to $Nu = \frac{hL}{k}$ in heat transfer, the Sherwood number uses $D_{AB}$ in place of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$. Just as **Nu** characterizes the enhancement of heat transfer due to convection over pure conduction, **Sh** characterizes the enhancement of mass transfer due to convection over pure diffusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Mass Transfer Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting the negative sign in Fick's First Law (flux must be positive when concentration decreases in the direction of flow). **−1 mark penalty.**
> 2. **Unit Inconsistencies:** Mixing cm and m in diffusion coefficient problems. Always convert to SI (m²/s) before calculation. **−1 mark penalty.**
> 3. **Boundary Condition Misapplication:** Students often swap $C_{A1}$ and $C_{A2}$ in the integrated flux equation, leading to negative flux values. **−2 marks penalty.**
> 4. **Confusing Permeability with Diffusivity:** $P_M = D_{AB} K / L$ is a composite property, NOT equivalent to $D_{AB}$. Examiners will not award marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 5. **Skipping the Sherwood Number Derivation:** In 14-mark problems, students must show the dimensionless analysis and identify the relevant empirical correlation. Marks are awarded for **methodology**, not just the final answer.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, Q3(a-b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A biological membrane of thickness 0.5 mm separates two aqueous compartments. The diffusion coefficient of urea across the membrane is $D_{AB} = 4.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, and the partition coefficient is K = 0.8. The concentration of urea in compartment 1 is 50 mol/m³ and in compartment 2 is 10 mol/m³. Calculate:**
**(i) The membrane permeability (2 marks)**
**(ii) The steady-state molar flux of urea through the membrane (3 marks)**
**(iii) The amount of urea transported per hour through 1 cm² of membrane area (2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Membrane Permeability (2 Marks):**

Using the permeability equation:

$$P_M = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot K}{L}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_M = \frac{(4.5 \times 10^{-10})(0.8)}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P_M = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-10}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{P_M = 7.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Molar Flux (3 Marks):**

Applying Fick's First Law with permeability:

$$J_A = \frac{P_M (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{1}$$

$$J_A = P_M (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$

$$J_A = (7.2 \times 10^{-7})(50 - 10)$$

$$J_A = (7.2 \times 10^{-7})(40)$$

$$\boxed{J_A = 2.88 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

**(iii) Amount Transported per Hour (2 Marks):**

Flux per unit area = $J_A = 2.88 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/(m²·s)

For area A = 1 cm² = $10^{-4}$ m² and time t = 1 hr = 3600 s:

$$n_A = J_A \cdot A \cdot t$$

$$n_A = (2.88 \times 10^{-5})(10^{-4})(3600)$$

$$n_A = 2.88 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.36$$

$$\boxed{n_A = 1.037 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Define the mass transfer coefficient $k_c$. Derive its relationship with the diffusion coefficient and the stagnant film thickness. State the significance of the Sherwood number in correlating mass transfer data for biological systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ [m/s] is defined as the proportionality constant between the molar flux and the concentration driving force across a fluid interface:

$$N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

Where $C_{A,s}$ is the surface concentration and $C_{A,\infty}$ is the bulk concentration.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
Consider a stagnant film of thickness $\delta_M$ adjacent to a surface. Within the film, transport is purely diffusive (no convection), and Fick's First Law applies:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Integrating across the film from $x=0$ (surface) to $x=\delta_M$ (bulk edge):

$$\int_0^{\delta_M} N_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A,s}}^{C_{A,\infty}} dC_A$$

$$N_A \cdot \delta_M = -D_{AB}(C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s})$$

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_M}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

Comparing with the film theory form $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$, we get:

$$\boxed{k_c = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_M}}$$

**Significance of Sherwood Number (2 Marks):**
The Sherwood number $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ enables the development of **dimensionless correlations** (analogous to Nusselt number correlations) that can be applied across different biological geometries (capillaries, alveoli, hollow fibers). For example, the Leveque correlation for mass transfer in a tube is:

$$Sh = 1.62 \left(Re \cdot Sc \cdot \frac{d}{L}\right)^{1/3}$$

This allows engineers to predict $k_c$ in **hemodialyzers**, **oxygenators**, and **bioreactors** without expensive experimentation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, Q4(a-b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive Fick's Second Law of diffusion from Fick's First Law. A pharmaceutical tablet releases drug by diffusion into surrounding tissue. The drug concentration in the tablet is $C_0 = 200$ mol/m³, and the initial tissue concentration is zero. The diffusion coefficient in tissue is $D = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s. The tissue slab thickness is 1 mm. Using the appropriate error-function solution, find the concentration at the midplane (x = 0.5 mm) after 1 hour.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Fick's Second Law (3 Marks):**
Consider a control volume of infinitesimal thickness $dx$ and unit area. The accumulation rate of species A is:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx = J_A(x) - J_A(x+dx)$$

Using Taylor expansion:

$$J_A(x+dx) = J_A(x) + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}$$

Substituting Fick's First Law $J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\right)$$

For constant $D_{AB}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

**Solution to Diffusion Problem (4 Marks):**
For diffusion from a constant surface concentration into a semi-infinite medium, the solution is:

$$\frac{C_A(x,t) - C_0}{C_i - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

With $C_i = 200$ mol/m³ (initial surface concentration) and far-field $C_\infty = 0$:

$$C_A(x,t) = C_i \left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$$

Calculating the argument:

$$2\sqrt{Dt} = 2\sqrt{(5 \times 10^{-11})(3600)}$$

$$2\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-7}} = 2 \times 4.243 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$2\sqrt{Dt} = 8.485 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.8485 \text{ mm}$$

At $x = 0.5$ mm:

$$\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{0.5}{0.8485} = 0.5893$$

From standard error function tables: $\text{erf}(0.5893) \approx 0.5940$

$$C_A(0.5 \text{ mm}, 1 \text{ hr}) = 200 \times (1 - 0.5940)$$

$$\boxed{C_A \approx 81.2 \text{ mol/m}^3}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of convective mass transfer. Distinguish between the mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ and the mass transfer coefficient $k_y$ (mole fraction basis). For a binary gas mixture of O₂ and N₂ flowing past a tissue surface, where the mole fraction of O₂ at the surface is $y_{A,s} = 0.20$ and in the bulk is $y_{A,\infty} = 0.10$, with $k_y = 0.05$ mol/(m²·s), calculate the molar flux of O₂.**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Convective Mass Transfer (3 Marks):**
Convective mass transfer occurs when bulk fluid motion (caused by pressure gradients, external pumping, or natural convection) carries chemical species along with the flow. Unlike pure diffusion, convective transport is driven by the velocity field of the fluid and is typically orders of magnitude faster than diffusion alone. The combined flux equation for species A in a binary system is:

$$N_A = -c D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx} + y_A (N_A + N_B)$$

**Distinction Between $k_c$ and $k_y$ (2 Marks):**
- **$k_c$** is defined using concentration driving force: $N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$
- **$k_y$** is defined using mole fraction driving force: $N_A = k_y (y_{A,s} - y_{A,\infty})$

The two are related by the total molar concentration: $k_c = k_y \cdot c$ where $c = P/RT$ for an ideal gas.

**Calculation of Molar Flux (2 Marks):**

Given $k_y = 0.05$ mol/(m²·s):

$$N_A = k_y (y_{A,s} - y_{A,\infty})$$

$$N_A = 0.05 \times (0.20 - 0.10)$$

$$N_A = 0.05 \times 0.10$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Mass Transfer:**

- ✅ **Fick's First Law:** $J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$ — Always remember the **negative sign** indicates flow from high to low concentration.
- ✅ **Fick's Second Law:** $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ — Used for **time-dependent** (transient) problems.
- ✅ **Diffusion Coefficient Units:** Always **m²/s** in SI. Typical biological values: O₂ in water ≈ $2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s; glucose in tissue ≈ $6 \times 10^{-6}$ cm²/s.
- ✅ **Steady-State vs Unsteady-State:** If concentration **does not change with time** at any location → steady-state. Otherwise → unsteady-state.
- ✅ **Mass Transfer Coefficient $k_c$:** Has units of **[m/s]**; represents the **ease** of mass transfer at an interface.
- ✅ **Sherwood Number $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$:** Dimensionless number; high Sh = convection dominates; low Sh = diffusion dominates.
- ✅ **Membrane Permeability $P_M = D_{AB} K / L$:** A composite property combining diffusivity, partition coefficient, and membrane thickness.
- ✅ **Film Theory:** $k_c = D_{AB} / \delta_M$ — Provides the link between microscopic diffusion and macroscopic flux.
- ✅ **Error Function Solutions:** Used for semi-infinite media problems in transient diffusion. Remember the standard form:
$$C_A(x,t) = C_s \cdot \text{erfc}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$
- ✅ **Biological Applications:** O₂/CO₂ exchange in lungs, drug delivery through skin/membranes, nutrient absorption in intestines, hemodialysis for kidney failure, tissue engineering scaffolds.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Expect at least **one 3-mark question** on definitions and **one 14-mark problem** combining steady-state diffusion with permeability or Sherwood number analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Exam Day:**
> Always draw a clear diagram showing concentration profiles before solving. Label all boundary conditions explicitly. Show units at every step of the calculation. Examiners award **methodology marks** generously even when numerical answers are slightly off — so write out the complete solution path.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Momentum Transfer. Shell Mass Balance - Boundary Conditions - Diffusion Through Stagnant Gas - Diffusion with Heterogeneous and Homogeneous Chemical Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** and Momentum Transfer. Shell Mass Balance - Boundary Conditions - Diffusion Through Stagnant Gas - Diffusion with Heterogeneous and Homogeneous Chemical Reaction # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Momentum Transfer — The Molecular Drag Mechanism **Momentum Transfer** is the transport of molecular momentum between adjacent fluid layers or between a fluid and a solid surface, governed by the **Newtonian constitutive equation** for viscosity. $$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{du_x}{dy}$$ where: - $\tau_{yx}$ = flux of x-momentum in the y-direction (N/m²·s) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or kg/m·s) - $du_x/dy$ = velocity gradient (s⁻¹) > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that momentum flows from the region of high velocity to the region of low velocity — analogous to heat flowing from hot to cold.
