Viscosity ($\mu$) is the **momentum diffusivity analog** — it represents how "easily" momentum diffuses through a fluid. **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a crowd of people walking at different speeds in parallel lanes at a metro station. The faster lane "drags" the slower lane forward while the slower lane "retards" the faster lane. This mutual dragging at the molecular level is exactly what momentum transfer does — it represents the internal friction of a fluid. --- ## 1.2 Shell Mass Balance — The Foundation of Species Conservation A **Shell Mass Balance** is a macroscopic/differential mass balance applied to a thin shell (control volume of infinitesimal thickness) within a system. It is the starting point for deriving the **General Differential Mass Balance Equation**. For species A in a control volume: $$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{V}\rho_A \, dV = -\int_{S}\vec{n}\cdot\vec{N}_A \, dS + \int_{V}r_A \, dV$$ In differential (point) form (assuming constant $\rho$): $$\frac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} + \left(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla c_A\right) = D_{AB}\nabla^2 c_A + R_A$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The shell mass balance is the **FIRST STEP** in deriving ALL diffusion equations. KTU examiners specifically test whether students can set up the shell balance correctly — the sign convention and boundary terms are frequently asked. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a thin slice of bread in a loaf. Mass enters one face, leaves the other, and may be generated or consumed within. A shell balance treats this slice as a microscopic "bread slice" of the system and applies conservation. --- ## 1.3 Boundary Conditions — The Mathematical Constraints Boundary conditions are the **physical and mathematical constraints** imposed on the concentration profile $c_A(z)$ at the physical limits of the system (typically at $z=0$ and $z=L$). The three classical boundary conditions are: 1. **Specified Surface Concentration (Dirichlet):** $c_A \big\vert_{z=0} = c_{A0}$ 2. **Specified Surface Flux (Neumann):** $-D_{AB}\frac{dc_A}{dz}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = N_{A0}$ 3. **Continuity of Flux at Interface:** $N_{A1}\big\vert_{z=z_i} = N_{A2}\big\vert_{z=z_i}$ > [!NOTE] > **Heterogeneous Reaction at Surface:** When a heterogeneous reaction $A \rightarrow Products$ occurs at a catalytic surface, the boundary condition becomes: > > $$-D_{AB}\frac{dc_A}{dz}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = k_s'' c_A\big\vert_{z=0}$$ > > where $k_s''$ is the surface reaction rate constant (m/s). --- ## 1.4 Diffusion Through a Stagnant Gas Film (Stefan Diffusion) When gas **B is stagnant** and only **A diffuses** through it (e.g., water vapor evaporating into still air), the flux equation is the famous **Stefan Equation**: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB}\,c_{tot}}{z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constraint:** $N_B = 0$ (B does not move in bulk; counter-diffusion is zero in lab frame). **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a closed jar with a small vial of perfume (A) at the bottom and stationary air (B). The perfume rises by diffusion, but the air molecules — being "stuck" — only swap places through molecular collisions, not bulk motion. The flux equation above captures this precisely. --- ## 1.5 Diffusion with Chemical Reaction Two fundamentally different cases: ### (a) Homogeneous Reaction — reaction occurs throughout the fluid $$A \rightarrow Products \quad \text{at rate} \; -r_A = k c_A$$ The reaction term $R_A = -k c_A$ enters **inside the differential equation**. ### (b) Heterogeneous Reaction — reaction occurs only at a surface $$A \rightarrow Products \quad \text{at rate} \; -r_A'' = k_s'' c_{A,surface}$$ The reaction term enters **as a boundary condition**, NOT inside the differential equation. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently put $-k c_A$ inside the differential equation for heterogeneous reactions. **Remember:** heterogeneous = boundary condition; homogeneous = source term inside the equation. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: Setup of the Shell Balance - Choose a geometry (planar, cylindrical, or spherical). - Define a thin shell of thickness $\Delta z$ at position $z$. - Identify the fluxes entering and leaving the shell. ### Step 2: Conservation Statement At steady state: **Rate In − Rate Out + Rate of Generation = 0** ### Step 3: Express Fluxes Using Constitutive Laws - Diffusion flux: $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ - Convection flux: $c_A v_z$ ### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions Determine $c_A(z)$ uniquely. ### Step 5: Evaluate Flux of Interest Differentiate the concentration profile to get $N_A$. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet | **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Governing Equation** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newtonian Momentum Flux | $\tau_{yx} = -\mu\,(du_x/dy)$ | Pa or N/m² | | 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | | 3 | General Differential Mass Balance | $\partial c_A/\partial t + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla c_A) = D_{AB}\nabla^2 c_A + R_A$ | mol/m³·s | | 4 | Steady-State 1D Diffusion (No Reaction) | $0 = D_{AB}\,(d^2c_A/dz^2)$ | — | | 5 | Fick's First Law (Dilute) | $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\,(dc_A/dz)$ | mol/m²·s | | 6 | Diffusion Through Stagnant B (Stefan) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,c_{tot}}{z}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$ | mol/m²·s | | 7 | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion | $N_A = (D_{AB}/L)(c_{A1}-c_{A2})$ | mol/m²·s | | 8 | Diffusion with Homogeneous 1st Order Rx | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,c_{A1}}{L}\,\dfrac{\phi}{\tanh\phi}$ | mol/m²·s | | 9 | Thiele Modulus | $\phi = L\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Heterogeneous Rx BC | $-D_{AB}(dc_A/dz)\big\vert_{z=0} = k_s'' c_A\big\vert_{z=0}$ | mol/m²·s | | 11 | Catalytic Wall Effectiveness | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh\phi}{\phi}$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Molar Flux Total | $N_A = -c_{tot}D_{AB}(dy_A/dz) + y_A(N_A+N_B)$ | mol/m²·s | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Biomedical Engineering:** Oxygen diffusion through alveolar membranes (stagnant gas analogy) - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Drug release from polymer matrices (heterogeneous reaction boundary) - **Food Processing:** Moisture loss during drying (Stefan diffusion) - **Chemical Reactors:** Catalyst effectiveness (homogeneous reaction model) - **Bioartificial Organs:** Momentum transfer in blood through hollow fibers --- ## 2.4 The Physical Meaning of Each Term | **Term** | **Meaning** | **Engineering Significance** | |---|---|---| | $D_{AB}\nabla^2 c_A$ | Molecular diffusion of A | Determines transport without bulk motion | | $\vec{v}\cdot\nabla c_A$ | Convective transport | Dominant in flow reactors | | $R_A$ | Generation/consumption | Critical in bioreactors | | $k_s''$ | Surface reactivity | Determines catalyst efficiency | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Diffusion Through Stagnant Gas B ### Setup Consider gas A diffusing through stagnant gas B between two planes at $z=z_1$ and $z=z_2$. The total pressure and temperature are constant. ### Starting Point — Shell Mass Balance At steady state, for the thin shell between $z$ and $z+\Delta z$: $$0 = \left.N_{A,z}\right|_{z} - \left.N_{A,z}\right|_{z+\Delta z} + 0$$ Dividing by $\Delta z$ and taking the limit: $$\frac{dN_{A,z}}{dz} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{A,z} = \text{constant}$$ ### Apply Constitutive Flux Expression For a binary system at constant $T$ and $P$: $$N_A = -c_{tot}D_{AB}\frac{dy_A}{dz} + y_A(N_A + N_B)$$ Since **B is stagnant** in the lab frame: $N_B = 0$. $$\therefore N_A = -c_{tot}D_{AB}\frac{dy_A}{dz} + y_A N_A$$ ### Rearrange $$N_A(1 - y_A) = -c_{tot}D_{AB}\frac{dy_A}{dz}$$ $$\frac{dy_A}{1-y_A} = -\frac{N_A}{c_{tot}D_{AB}}\,dz$$ ### Integration Integrating from $z_1$ (where $y_A = y_{A1}$) to $z_2$ (where $y_A = y_{A2}$): $$\int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \frac{dy_A}{1-y_A} = -\frac{N_A}{c_{tot}D_{AB}}\int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz$$ $$\left.-\ln(1-y_A)\right|_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} = -\frac{N_A}{c_{tot}D_{AB}}(z_2-z_1)$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right) = -\frac{N_A(z_2-z_1)}{c_{tot}D_{AB}}$$ ### Final Stefan Equation $$\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{c_{tot}\,D_{AB}}{z_2-z_1}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)\,}$$ Equivalently, using $c_{tot} = P/(RT)$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,(z_2-z_1)}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Limiting Case Check:** When $y_{A1}, y_{A2} \ll 1$ (dilute limit), $\ln(1-y_A) \approx -y_A$, and the equation reduces to Fick's first law: > $N_A \approx (D_{AB}/L)(c_{A1}-c_{A2})$ ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Diffusion with Heterogeneous Chemical Reaction (Catalytic Wall) ### Setup A fluid containing species A flows past a catalytic wall at $z=0$. A first-order reaction $A \rightarrow B$ occurs at the wall. The concentration far from the wall is $c_{A\infty}$. ### Differential Equation (No Reaction in Bulk) At steady state, for stagnant film approximation: $$\frac{d^2 c_A}{dz^2} = 0$$ ### First Integration $$\frac{dc_A}{dz} = C_1$$ ### Boundary Condition 1 (Far from Wall) As $z \to \infty$: $c_A = c_{A\infty}$ $$\frac{dc_A}{dz} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = 0$$ Wait — this implies $c_A$ is constant. Instead, let's use the proper **film theory** approach. ### Proper Film Theory Boundary Condition at $z=0$ At the catalytic wall, the diffusive flux equals the surface reaction rate: $$-D_{AB}\frac{dc_A}{dz}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = k_s''\,c_A\big\vert_{z=0}$$ ### Boundary Condition at $z = \delta$ (Edge of Film) $$c_A\big\vert_{z=\delta} = c_{A\infty}$$ ### Solve the ODE Integrating $d^2c_A/dz^2 = 0$ with BCs: $$c_A(z) = c_{A\infty} - (c_{A\infty} - c_{As})\frac{z}{\delta}$$ where $c_{As} = c_A\big\vert_{z=0}$. ### Apply Catalytic Wall BC $$\frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}(c_{A\infty} - c_{As}) = k_s'' c_{As}$$ $$c_{As} = \frac{c_{A\infty}}{1 + k_s''\delta/D_{AB}}$$ ### Final Flux to the Wall $$\boxed{\,N_A\big\vert_{z=0} = \frac{k_s''\,c_{A\infty}}{1 + k_s''\delta/D_{AB}}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Limiting Cases:** > - Slow reaction ($k_s'' \to 0$): $N_A \to 0$ ✓ > - Fast reaction ($k_s'' \to \infty$): $N_A \to D_{AB}\,c_{A\infty}/\delta$ (diffusion-controlled) ✓ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Diffusion with Homogeneous Reaction ### Setup Species A undergoes first-order decay $A \rightarrow P$ throughout the bulk. Steady state. ### Modified Differential Equation $$D_{AB}\frac{d^2 c_A}{dz^2} - k\,c_A = 0$$ Rearranged: $$\frac{d^2 c_A}{dz^2} = \frac{k}{D_{AB}}\,c_A$$ ### Substitute Thiele Modulus Let $\phi^2 = k/D_{AB}$ (units of 1/length²): $$\frac{d^2 c_A}{dz^2} = \phi^2 c_A$$ ### General Solution $$c_A(z) = C_1 e^{\phi z} + C_2 e^{-\phi z}$$ ### Apply BCs - At $z=L$: $c_A = c_{AL}$ - At $z=0$: $c_A = c_{A0}$ Solving with hyperbolic functions: $$c_A(z) = c_{A0}\,\frac{\sinh\phi(L-z)}{\sinh\phi L} + c_{AL}\,\frac{\sinh\phi z}{\sinh\phi L}$$ ### Flux at $z=0$ $$N_A\big\vert_{z=0} = -D_{AB}\frac{dc_A}{dz}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = \frac{D_{AB}\,\phi}{\sinh\phi L}\!\left[c_{A0}\cosh\phi L - c_{AL}\right]$$ $$\boxed{\,N_A\big\vert_{z=0} = \frac{D_{AB}\,\phi}{\tanh\phi L}\!\left[c_{A0} - \frac{c_{AL}}{\cosh\phi L}\right]\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Catalyst Effectiveness:** $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh\phi}{\phi}$ — this is the ratio of actual reaction rate to rate if all fluid were at $c_{A0}$. KTU frequently asks for the effectiveness factor. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Oxygen (A) diffuses through stagnant carbon dioxide (B) in a tube of length $z_2 - z_1 = 0.1$ m. Given $T = 298$ K, $P = 101325$ Pa, $D_{AB} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $y_{A1} = 0.2$, $y_{A2} = 0.05$. Find $N_A$. ### Step 1: Compute $c_{tot}$ $$c_{tot} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.89 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$ ### Step 2: Apply Stefan Equation $$N_A = \frac{(1.8\times 10^{-5})(40.89)}{0.1}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-0.05}{1-0.2}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Evaluate Logarithm $$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.95}{0.80}\right) = \ln(1.1875) = 0.1719$$ ### Step 4: Final Answer $$N_A = \frac{7.36\times 10^{-4}}{0.1}\,(0.1719) = 1.265\times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Shell Mass Balance Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Transport Phenomena Problem]) --> B1[Identify Geometry] B1 --> B2[Define Shell of dz] B2 --> C1[Apply Conservation] C1 --> C2[Choose Constitutive Law] C2 --> D1{Diffusion Type?} D1 --> D1a[Equimolar
Counter] D1 --> D1b[Stagnant Gas B
Stefan Diffusion] D1 --> D1c[Homogeneous Rx
Bulk Reaction] D1 --> D1d[Heterogeneous Rx
Surface Reaction] D1a --> E1[Linear cA profile] D1b --> E2[Logarithmic Profile] D1c --> E3[Hyperbolic Sine] D1d --> E4[Linear + BC k_s] E1 --> F1[Compute NA at Boundary] E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 E4 --> F1 F1 --> G1([Final NA Value]) ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Diffusion Problem Solver ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT[Inputs: Geometry, T, P, DAB, yA] --> SHELL[Shell Mass Balance] SHELL --> BC[Boundary Conditions] BC --> ODE[Differential Equation] ODE --> SOLVE[Integration] SOLVE --> PROFILE[Concentration Profile cA z] PROFILE --> FLUX[Differentiate to Get NA] FLUX --> OUTPUT[Final Molar Flux NA] STYLE_ODE[Homogeneous Source] -.-> ODE STYLE_BC[Surface Reaction Term] -.-> BC ``` ## 4.3 Diagram — Diffusion with Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous Reaction ```mermaid flowchart TD P1([Bulk Fluid Phase]) --> P2{Type of Reaction} P2 --> Q1[Homogeneous A to Products] P2 --> Q2[Heterogeneous at Wall] Q1 --> R1[Rate eq in ODE
d2cA/dz2 = k cA / DAB] R1 --> S1[Thiele Modulus phi] S1 --> T1[Catalyst Effectiveness eta] Q2 --> R2[BC at z=0
DAB dcA/dz = ks cAs] R2 --> S2[Surface Rate Constant] S2 --> T2[Wall Flux NA0] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Boundary Condition Types ```mermaid flowchart TD BCSTART([Boundary Condition Selection]) --> BC1[Dirichlet Type 1] BCSTART --> BC2[Neumann Type 2] BCSTART --> BC3[Robin Type 3] BC1 --> BC1D[cA at z0 is Specified] BC2 --> BC2D[Flux at z0 is Specified] BC3 --> BC3D[Combined Flux and Concentration] BC3D --> BC3EX[Heterogeneous Reaction Example] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. What is the physical significance of the negative sign?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ between fluid layers is proportional to the velocity gradient: $$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{du_x}{dy}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that momentum is transported in the direction of **decreasing velocity** — analogous to heat flow from hot to cold regions. Here, $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (Pa·s). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is meant by diffusion through a stagnant gas? Write the Stefan equation and explain its significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** When component **B is stationary** in the lab frame ($N_B = 0$) and only **A diffuses** through it, the phenomenon is called **diffusion through a stagnant gas** (or **Stefan diffusion**). The governing equation is: $$N_A = \frac{c_{tot}\,D_{AB}}{z_2-z_1}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$$ **Significance:** It describes processes like evaporation of liquids into still air, moisture migration in food drying, and oxygen transport in lungs — all common biological engineering problems. --- ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the shell mass balance equation for species A diffusing through stagnant gas B. State the assumptions clearly.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state conditions 2. Constant temperature and pressure 3. One-dimensional diffusion in $z$-direction 4. Ideal gas behavior 5. $N_B = 0$ (B is stagnant) **Derivation Steps:** Step 1: Consider a thin shell between $z$ and $z+\Delta z$ with cross-section $S$. Step 2: Mass balance on species A (steady state): $$\left.N_{A,z}\right|_{z} \cdot S - \left.N_{A,z}\right|_{z+\Delta z} \cdot S = 0$$ Step 3: Divide by $S\Delta z$ and take limit $\Delta z \to 0$: $$\frac{dN_{A,z}}{dz} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = \text{constant}$$ Step 4: Express flux using constitutive law: $$N_A = -c_{tot}D_{AB}\frac{dy_A}{dz} + y_A(N_A + N_B)$$ Step 5: With $N_B = 0$: $$N_A(1 - y_A) = -c_{tot}D_{AB}\frac{dy_A}{dz}$$ Step 6: Separate and integrate from $z_1$ to $z_2$: $$\int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}}\frac{dy_A}{1-y_A} = -\frac{N_A}{c_{tot}D_{AB}}\int_{z_1}^{z_2}dz$$ Step 7: Final Stefan equation: $$N_A = \frac{c_{tot}D_{AB}}{z_2-z_1}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$$ (Each step carries 1 mark — full marks for complete derivation) #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Ammonia (A) is absorbed from a gas mixture into water. The gas contains 40% NH₃ and 60% N₂ (B is stagnant). The diffusion path is 2 mm. At 298 K and 1 atm, $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. At the gas-liquid interface $y_{A2} = 0.05$. Calculate $N_A$.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** Given: - $y_{A1} = 0.40$, $y_{A2} = 0.05$ - $L = z_2 - z_1 = 0.002$ m - $T = 298$ K, $P = 101325$ Pa - $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s **Step 1:** Calculate total molar concentration: $$c_{tot} = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{(8.314)(298)} = 40.89 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$ **Step 2:** Apply Stefan equation: $$N_A = \frac{(40.89)(2.5\times 10^{-5})}{0.002}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1-0.05}{1-0.40}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Compute the logarithm: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.95}{0.60}\right) = \ln(1.5833) = 0.4595$$ **Step 4:** Substitute: $$N_A = \frac{(40.89)(2.5\times 10^{-5})}{0.002}\,(0.4595)$$ **Step 5:** Final result: $$N_A = 0.5111 \times 0.4595 = 0.2349 \; \text{mol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$ $\boxed{N_A \approx 0.235 \; \text{mol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}}$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the concentration profile for steady-state diffusion of A through a stagnant gas B with a first-order homogeneous chemical reaction $A \rightarrow P$.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Differential Equation:** $$D_{AB}\frac{d^2 c_A}{dz^2} - k\,c_A = 0$$ **Step 1:** Define Thiele modulus $\phi^2 = k/D_{AB}$: $$\frac{d^2 c_A}{dz^2} = \phi^2 c_A$$ **Step 2:** General solution: $$c_A(z) = C_1 e^{\phi z} + C_2 e^{-\phi z}$$ **Step 3:** Rewrite using hyperbolic functions: $$c_A(z) = A_1 \cosh\phi z + A_2 \sinh\phi z$$ **Step 4:** Apply BCs: - At $z = L$ (centerline, symmetric): $\dfrac{dc_A}{dz}\bigg\vert_{z=L} = 0$ - At $z = 0$ (wall): $c_A = c_{A0}$ **Step 5:** From symmetry BC: $$A_1 \phi\sinh\phi L + A_2 \phi\cosh\phi L = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_2 = -A_1\tanh\phi L$$ **Step 6:** From wall BC: $c_{A0} = A_1$ (since $\cosh 0 = 1$) **Step 7:** Final profile: $$\boxed{\,c_A(z) = c_{A0}\,\frac{\cosh\phi(L-z)}{\cosh\phi L}\,}$$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Derive the expression for catalyst effectiveness factor $\eta$ using the Thiele modulus.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Actual rate of reaction per unit area = flux at wall: $$r_{actual} = -D_{AB}\frac{dc_A}{dz}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = -D_{AB}\,c_{A0}\phi\,\frac{-\sinh\phi L}{\cosh\phi L} = D_{AB}\,c_{A0}\,\phi\tanh\phi L$$ **Step 2:** Rate if no diffusion resistance (all at $c_{A0}$): $$r_{ideal} = k\,c_{A0}\,L = D_{AB}\phi^2\,c_{A0}\,L$$ **Step 3:** Effectiveness factor: $$\eta = \frac{r_{actual}}{r_{ideal}} = \frac{D_{AB}\,c_{A0}\,\phi\tanh\phi L}{D_{AB}\,\phi^2\,c_{A0}\,L} = \frac{\tanh\phi L}{\phi L}$$ **Step 4:** Final expression: $$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{\tanh\phi}{\phi}\,}$$ where $\phi = L\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$ **Limiting Cases:** - $\phi \to 0$ (slow reaction): $\eta \to 1$ (reaction-controlled) - $\phi \to \infty$ (fast reaction): $\eta \to 1/\phi$ (diffusion-controlled) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:** > > 1. **Sign Error in Stefan Equation** — Forgetting the negative sign convention in $J_A^* = -D_{AB}(dc_A/dz)$. Always carry the minus carefully. > > 2. **Wrong Domain for Heterogeneous Reaction** — Putting the reaction term inside the ODE (that is for homogeneous reactions only). Heterogeneous = **boundary condition**. > > 3. **Forgetting Units** — $N_A$ must be in **mol/m²·s**, $D_{AB}$ in **m²/s**, $c_{tot}$ in **mol/m³**. KTU deducts ½ mark per unit error. > > 4. **Misapplying $N_B = 0$** — Stagnant gas condition applies **only when B does not move** in the lab frame. If B is consumed/produced, the equation changes. > > 5. **Not Stating Assumptions** — Always list assumptions explicitly (steady state, constant T & P, 1D, ideal gas). Examiners allocate **1 mark** just for assumptions. > > 6. **Thiele Modulus Confusion** — $\phi = L\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$ for slab; for cylinder/sphere the form differs. Read the geometry carefully. > > 7. **Skipping Limiting Case Verification** — Always verify your final equation in the dilute limit to confirm it reduces to Fick's law. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary:** - **Momentum Transfer:** Governed by $\tau = -\mu(du/dy)$; viscosity is the momentum analog of thermal diffusivity. - **Shell Mass Balance:** Foundation of all diffusion derivations — write it BEFORE attempting any complex problem. - **Boundary Conditions:** - Dirichlet: $c_A$ specified - Neumann: flux specified - Robin/Catalytic: $-D_{AB}(dc_A/dz) = k_s'' c_A$ - **Stefan Diffusion (Stagnant B):** $$N_A = \frac{c_{tot}D_{AB}}{L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$$ Applies when $N_B = 0$. - **Homogeneous Reaction:** - Equation: $D_{AB}(d^2c_A/dz^2) = k\,c_A$ - Solution: hyperbolic sine/cosine - Effectiveness: $\eta = \tanh\phi/\phi$ - **Heterogeneous Reaction:** - NO modification to bulk ODE - Reaction enters as BC at $z=0$ (or $z=L$) - Wall flux: $N_A = D_{AB}(c_{A\infty}-c_{As})/\delta = k_s'' c_{As}$ - **Thiele Modulus:** $\phi = L\sqrt{k/D_{AB}}$ — ratio of reaction to diffusion rates. - **Dilute Limit Check:** Always verify that complex equations reduce to Fick's first law in the dilute limit ($y_A \ll 1$). - **Units:** $N_A$ in mol/m²·s; $D_{AB}$ in m²/s; $c$ in mol/m³; $k_s''$ in m/s. - **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Questions typically ask for derivation (7 marks) + numerical application (7 marks). - **Memory Aid:** "**H**eterogeneous = **H**appens at **H**ard wall (boundary); **H**omogeneous = **H**appens throughout fluid."

topics.title: Equation of Continuity for Binary Mixtures in Rectangular Coordinates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation of Continuity for Binary Mixtures in Rectangular Coordinates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Binary Mixture Continuity Equation — Core Definition**
> The **Equation of Continuity for a Binary Mixture** is a partial differential equation (PDE) that expresses the **conservation of mass** of each chemical species (A and B) in a flowing two-component system. In **rectangular (Cartesian) coordinates**, it is written as the species-mass balance for component A and component B independently, accounting for accumulation, convective transport, and **diffusive mass flux** $\vec{j}_A, \vec{j}_B$ relative to the mass-average velocity $\vec{v}$.

The most general statement is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial t} + (\nabla \cdot \rho_i \vec{v}) = -(\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_i) + r_i \qquad (i = A,B)$$

where $\rho_i$ is the **mass concentration** of species $i$ (kg·m⁻³), $\vec{v}$ is the **mass-average velocity** of the mixture, $\vec{j}_i$ is the **diffusive mass flux** of $i$ relative to $\vec{v}$, and $r_i$ is the **rate of mass production** of $i$ per unit volume by homogeneous chemical reaction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The "Two-Color Water Pipe" Analogy**
> Imagine a transparent rectangular pipe carrying a mixture of **red ink (species A)** and **clear water (species B)**. At every cross-section, you can independently count red and clear molecules. The continuity equation says:
> - **What accumulates** in any small control volume = **What flows in by bulk motion** (convection) − **What flows out** + **What diffuses across the boundary** ± **What is generated by reaction**.
> - For binary systems, the red and clear fluids share the same bulk velocity $\vec{v}$, but they **diffuse relative to each other** (a phenomenon called *molecular diffusion*) — much like sugar dissolving from a still bottom of a tea cup even when the bulk fluid is at rest.
> - The equation in rectangular coordinates simply **projects** these balances onto the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes of the laboratory frame.

### 1.3 Key Quantities (Standard Metrics)

- **Total density:** $\rho = \rho_A + \rho_B$ (kg·m⁻³)
- **Mass fraction of A:** $w_A = \rho_A/\rho$ (dimensionless)
- **Mass-average velocity:** $\vec{v} = (\rho_A \vec{v}_A + \rho_B \vec{v}_B)/\rho$ (m·s⁻¹)
- **Diffusive mass flux of A:** $\vec{j}_A = \rho_A(\vec{v}_A - \vec{v})$ (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- **Binary diffusivity:** $D_{AB}$ (m²·s⁻¹) — typically **$\mathbf{10^{-9}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-5}}$** m²/s for liquid and gas biological systems.
- **Constraint:** $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$ (the diffusive fluxes of A and B are equal and opposite in a binary system).

### 1.4 Scope Within the KTU Module

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Context**
> This topic is a foundational PDE in **Module 3 (Mass Transfer)**. The rectangular-coordinate form is the easiest to visualize and forms the basis for the **shell-balance method** used to derive working equations for falling films, tube flow, and absorption equipment in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-Wise Logical Build-Up of the Equation

1. **Step 1 — Start from the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) applied to species mass.**
   For a fixed control volume $V$ bounded by surface $S$, the mass of species A is $m_A = \int_V \rho_A \, dV$.

2. **Step 2 — RTT yields the integral continuity equation.**
   $$\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho_A \, dV = -\oint_S \rho_A (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS - \oint_S (\vec{j}_A \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS + \int_V r_A \, dV$$
   where the **first surface integral** is the bulk convective outflow, the **second** is the diffusive outflow, and the **volume integral** accounts for homogeneous reaction.

3. **Step 3 — Apply the Divergence Theorem** to convert surface integrals into volume integrals:
   $$\oint_S (\vec{\Phi} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{\Phi}) \, dV$$

4. **Step 4 — Combine all terms under a single volume integral**, then since $V$ is arbitrary, the integrand itself must vanish → obtain the **differential equation of continuity**.

5. **Step 5 — Project $\nabla$ onto Cartesian axes** $(\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k})$ to obtain the **rectangular-coordinate form**:
   $$\nabla \cdot \vec{\Phi} = \frac{\partial \Phi_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \Phi_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \Phi_z}{\partial z}$$

6. **Step 6 — Substitute Fick's First Law for binary diffusion** $\vec{j}_A = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla w_A$ (assuming constant $D_{AB}$) to obtain the **closed-form convection–diffusion equation** for species A.

7. **Step 7 — For the total mixture**, sum the two species equations. The reaction terms cancel ($r_A + r_B = 0$) and the diffusive fluxes cancel ($\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$), giving the **classical total continuity equation** with no diffusion term.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **All high-yield equations for Module 3 — rectangular coordinates.**

| # | Equation (Rectangular Coordinates) | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|-----------------------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$ | **Total mass** continuity (incompressible limit: $\nabla \cdot \vec{v}=0$) | Derivation starting point |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho_A v_x)}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho_A v_y)}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial(\rho_A v_z)}{\partial z} = -\left[\dfrac{\partial j_{Ax}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial j_{Ay}}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial j_{Az}}{\partial z}\right] + r_A$ | **Species A** continuity (general) | Shell-balance method |
| 3 | $j_{Ax} = -\rho D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial w_A}{\partial x}$, $\ j_{Ay} = -\rho D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial w_A}{\partial y}$, $\ j_{Az} = -\rho D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial w_A}{\partial z}$ | **Fick's First Law** (components) | Substitute into Eq. 2 |
| 4 | $\rho\dfrac{Dw_A}{Dt} = \rho D_{AB}\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial z^2}\right) + r_A$ | **Convection–diffusion** for $w_A$ (no reaction: $r_A=0$) | Steady-state bio-diffusion |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} + v_x\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial x} + v_y\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial y} + v_z\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial z^2}\right)$ | **Constant $\rho$ & $D_{AB}$** form in molar conc. $c_A$ | Most exam-friendly form |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial z^2}\right)$ | **Fick's Second Law** (no convection, no reaction) | Diffusion in stagnant media (e.g., tissue) |

Where the **substantial (material) derivative** is:
$$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] **Biological-Systems Applications**
> - **Oxygen transport** from alveolar air into pulmonary capillary blood (lung modeling).
> - **Drug delivery** through polymeric membranes and hydrogel patches.
> - **Glucose diffusion** across the endothelial cell layer in tissue engineering scaffolds.
> - **Carbon-dioxide removal** in blood oxygenators (membrane contactors).
> - **Nutrient uptake** in bio-film reactors used in wastewater treatment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Total Mass Continuity in Rectangular Coordinates

We begin with the **integral form** of conservation of total mass for a control volume $V$ bounded by closed surface $S$:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV = -\oint_S \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS$$

Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the right-hand side:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV = -\int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

Move the time derivative inside the integral (since $V$ is fixed):

$$\int_V \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV = -\int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

Bring both integrals under one common integrand:

$$\int_V \left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})\right] dV = 0$$

Since $V$ is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish identically:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

Now expand $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})$ using the **product rule** in Cartesian coordinates:

$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}$$

Hence, the **rectangular-coordinate form** of the total continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0 \qquad \text{(Total mass, rect. coords.)}$$

For an **incompressible binary mixture** ($\rho = $ constant), this reduces to the familiar divergence-free condition:

$$\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Species Continuity Equation for Component A in a Binary Mixture

Apply the **Reynolds Transport Theorem** to the mass of species A:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho_A \, dV = -\oint_S \rho_A (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS \;-\; \oint_S \vec{j}_A \cdot \hat{n} \, dS \;+\; \int_V r_A \, dV$$

Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to both surface integrals:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho_A \, dV = -\int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho_A \vec{v}) \, dV \;-\; \int_V \nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A \, dV \;+\; \int_V r_A \, dV$$

Move the time derivative inside (fixed $V$):

$$\int_V \frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} \, dV = -\int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho_A \vec{v}) \, dV \;-\; \int_V \nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A \, dV \;+\; \int_V r_A \, dV$$

Combine into a single volume integral:

$$\int_V \left[\frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho_A \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A - r_A \right] dV = 0$$

Since $V$ is arbitrary:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho_A \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A = r_A$$

Write out the divergence operator in rectangular coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho_A v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho_A v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho_A v_z)}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial j_{Ax}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial j_{Ay}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial j_{Az}}{\partial z} = r_A$$

This is the **full rectangular-coordinate form** of the species continuity equation for species A.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Conservation Check (sanity test)**
> Summing the A and B equations gives the total-continuity equation with **no diffusion term** and **no reaction term**, exactly as required by Galilean invariance.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Closed Form in Mass Fraction $w_A$

Substitute $\rho_A = w_A \rho$ into the species equation:

$$\frac{\partial (w_A \rho)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (w_A \rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (w_A \rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (w_A \rho v_z)}{\partial z} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A + r_A$$

Apply the product rule to the convective terms, e.g.:

$$\frac{\partial (w_A \rho v_x)}{\partial x} = \rho v_x \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial x} + w_A \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x}$$

Similarly for $y$ and $z$. Combining all terms:

$$\rho \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial t} + w_A \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho v_x \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial x} + w_A \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \rho v_y \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial y} + w_A \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \rho v_z \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial z} + w_A \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A + r_A$$

Group the $w_A$-coefficient terms using the total continuity equation $\partial \rho/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$:

$$w_A \left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z}\right] = 0$$

Thus these terms vanish identically, leaving:

$$\rho \left[\frac{\partial w_A}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial z}\right] = -\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A + r_A$$

Recognize the **substantial derivative** $D w_A / Dt$ on the left:

$$\rho \frac{D w_A}{Dt} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A + r_A$$

Now invoke **Fick's First Law of Binary Diffusion** (Cartesian components):

$$j_{Ax} = -\rho D_{AB} \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial x}, \quad j_{Ay} = -\rho D_{AB} \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial y}, \quad j_{Az} = -\rho D_{AB} \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial z}$$

Compute the divergence (assuming $D_{AB} =$ constant and $\rho =$ constant):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A = -\rho D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial z^2}\right)$$

Substitute back:

$$\rho \frac{D w_A}{Dt} = \rho D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial z^2}\right) + r_A$$

Divide by $\rho$:

$$\frac{\partial w_A}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial z^2}\right) + \frac{r_A}{\rho}$$

This is the **closed-form, rectangular-coordinate equation of continuity for species A in a binary mixture** — the most general expression KTU examiners expect.

> [!NOTE] **Special cases for the KTU exam**
> - **Steady state, no reaction, no convection:** reduces to the **Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 w_A = 0$.
> - **Transient, no reaction, no convection:** reduces to **Fick's Second Law** (Eq. 6 of the cheat sheet).
> - **Steady state, with reaction, no convection:** gives the **Poisson-type** form $\nabla^2 c_A = -r_A/D_{AB}$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A slab of bio-tissue of thickness $L = 2$ mm separates two reservoirs of oxygen (species A) at concentrations $c_{A,1} = 0.20$ mol·m⁻³ and $c_{A,2} = 0.05$ mol·m⁻³. The diffusivity of O₂ in tissue is $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²·s⁻¹. Assuming steady 1-D diffusion in the $x$-direction only, with no bulk flow and no reaction, find the steady-state concentration profile $c_A(x)$ and the molar flux $N_{Ax}$.

**Governing equation:**

$$\frac{d^2 c_A}{dx^2} = 0$$

**Integrate twice:**

$$\frac{d c_A}{dx} = C_1, \qquad c_A(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

**Apply boundary conditions:**

- $c_A(0) = 0.20$ mol·m⁻³  $\Rightarrow C_2 = 0.20$
- $c_A(L) = 0.05$ mol·m⁻³  $\Rightarrow C_1 L + 0.20 = 0.05 \Rightarrow C_1 = -0.075$ mol·m⁻⁴

**Concentration profile:**

$$c_A(x) = 0.20 - 0.075\, x \quad \text{(with $x$ in metres)}$$

**Molar flux (Fick's Law, 1-D, steady):**

$$N_{Ax} = -D_{AB} \frac{d c_A}{dx} = -(1.5 \times 10^{-9})(-0.075)$$

$$N_{Ax} = 1.125 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**
> The negative concentration gradient drives oxygen from the high-concentration reservoir ($x=0$, blood side) into the low-concentration side ($x=L$, cell interior), which is the physical direction of physiological oxygen transport.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Control Volume V bounded by surface S] --> B[Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem to species mass]
    B --> C[Obtain integral continuity for species A]
    C --> D[Apply Divergence Theorem to surface integrals]
    D --> E[Combine into single volume integral]
    E --> F{V is arbitrary?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Integrand vanishes identically]
    G --> H[Write divergence in rectangular coordinates]
    H --> I[Species A continuity in x, y, z]
    I --> J[Substitute Fick's First Law components]
    J --> K[Use constant rho and D_AB assumption]
    K --> L[Closed-form convection diffusion PDE in wA]
    L --> M{Special case selector}
    M -- No flow, no reaction --> N[Fick's Second Law]
    M -- Steady, no reaction --> O[Laplace equation]
    M -- Steady, with reaction --> P[Poisson equation]
    M -- Full convection-diffusion --> Q[Apply IC and BC to solve]
    Q --> R[KTU Part A or Part B Answer]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Rectangular Control Volume with Mass Fluxes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CV[Rectangular Control Volume dx dy dz]
        direction TB
        NAx_in[rhoA vx at x, y, z] -->|Bulk convection in| CVnode((CV interior))
        NAx_out[rhoA vx at x+dx, y, z] -->|Bulk convection out| CVnode
        jAx_in[jAx at x, y, z] -->|Diffusion in| CVnode
        jAx_out[jAx at x+dx, y, z] -->|Diffusion out| CVnode
        CVnode -->|Source rA dx dy dz| CVnode
    end
    CVnode --> S[Sum: accumulation = net in - net out + generation]
    S --> PDE[Differential species continuity PDE]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Binary Mixture Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Species A, density rhoA, velocity vA] -->|mass-average velocity v| Mix[Binary Mixture, density rho = rhoA + rhoB]
    B[Species B, density rhoB, velocity vB] -->|mass-average velocity v| Mix
    Mix -->|Total mass continuity, no diffusion term| TOT[Total continuity equation]
    Mix -->|Species A: Fick diffusion jA| SA[Species A continuity PDE]
    Mix -->|Species B: jB = -jA| SB[Species B continuity PDE]
    SA --> ICBC[Apply initial and boundary conditions]
    SB --> ICBC
    ICBC --> SOL[Solve for wA, wB in rectangular coordinates]
```

> [!NOTE] **How to read these diagrams in an exam**
> - Diagram 4.1 maps the **logical derivation pipeline** (use it as a checklist when writing a 14-mark answer).
> - Diagram 4.2 is the **physical picture** of the shell balance — examiners love a labelled sketch before the math.
> - Diagram 4.3 shows the **coupling constraint** $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$, which is a frequent KTU sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the equation of continuity for a binary mixture in rectangular coordinates, clearly defining each term. Mention the condition under which the diffusion terms vanish from the species continuity equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equation of continuity for species A in a binary mixture in rectangular coordinates is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho_A v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho_A v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho_A v_z)}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial j_{Ax}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial j_{Ay}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial j_{Az}}{\partial z} = r_A$$

where $\rho_A$ is the mass concentration of A (kg·m⁻³), $v_i$ are components of the mass-average velocity (m·s⁻¹), $j_{Ai}$ are components of the diffusive mass flux of A (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹), and $r_A$ is the volumetric rate of generation of A (kg·m⁻³·s⁻¹).

The **diffusion terms vanish** when summing the two species equations to obtain total mass continuity, since $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$ and $r_A + r_B = 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*For a binary mixture, what is the relationship between the diffusive mass fluxes of the two species? Justify using the definition of the mass-average velocity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The relationship is $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$.

Justification: By definition, the mass-average velocity is $\vec{v} = (\rho_A \vec{v}_A + \rho_B \vec{v}_B)/\rho$, and the diffusive flux of species A is $\vec{j}_A = \rho_A(\vec{v}_A - \vec{v})$. A similar definition holds for B. Adding the two flux definitions and using $\rho_A + \rho_B = \rho$ and $\vec{v}_A w_A + \vec{v}_B w_B = \vec{v}$ gives $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$. Physically, this means in a binary system the two species diffuse in **opposite directions** at **equal molar-equivalent rates** relative to the centre-of-mass frame. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Starting from the Reynolds Transport Theorem, derive the equation of continuity for **total mass** in rectangular coordinates. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a steady, one-dimensional diffusion of species A through a stagnant film of B (no bulk flow, no homogeneous reaction), simplify the species continuity equation and integrate it for the boundary conditions $w_A = w_{A,1}$ at $x = 0$ and $w_A = w_{A,2}$ at $x = L$. Express the steady-state mass flux of A. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total mass continuity (7 marks):**

1. Start with RTT for total mass in a fixed CV $V$ bounded by surface $S$:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_V \rho \, dV = -\oint_S \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS$$

2. Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to the surface integral:

$$\oint_S \rho (\vec{v} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS = \int_V \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

3. Hence:

$$\int_V \left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v})\right] dV = 0$$

4. For an arbitrary $V$, the integrand vanishes, giving the **differential form**:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

5. Expanding the divergence in rectangular coordinates:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) no mass sources/sinks in CV, (ii) CV is fixed in space, (iii) velocity field is differentiable.

**Part (b) — Steady, 1-D, stagnant film diffusion (7 marks):**

1. With $v_x = v_y = v_z = 0$ (stagnant), steady-state, $r_A = 0$, the species equation reduces to:

$$\frac{d^2 w_A}{dx^2} = 0$$

2. **Integrate twice:**

$$\frac{d w_A}{dx} = C_1, \qquad w_A(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

3. **Apply BCs:**

- $w_A(0) = w_{A,1} \Rightarrow C_2 = w_{A,1}$
- $w_A(L) = w_{A,2} \Rightarrow C_1 = (w_{A,2} - w_{A,1})/L$

4. **Concentration profile:**

$$w_A(x) = w_{A,1} + \frac{w_{A,2} - w_{A,1}}{L} x$$

5. **Diffusive mass flux from Fick's law:**

$$j_{Ax} = -\rho D_{AB} \frac{d w_A}{dx} = -\rho D_{AB} \frac{w_{A,2} - w_{A,1}}{L} = \frac{\rho D_{AB}(w_{A,1} - w_{A,2})}{L}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A)**
> - **Common mark-loss #1:** Forgetting to take the divergence OUT of the volume integral before invoking "arbitrary $V$" — the integrand argument is mandatory.
> - **Common mark-loss #2:** Not stating that $V$ is *fixed* in space (otherwise Leibniz's rule has extra terms).
> - **Common mark-loss #3:** Forgetting the assumption of differentiability of the velocity field.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the general species continuity equation, derive the **convection–diffusion equation** for the mass fraction $w_A$ in a binary mixture in rectangular coordinates. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin film of biological fluid of thickness $\delta = 1$ mm flows down a vertical surface with a fully-developed velocity profile $v_z(y) = v_{\max}\bigl[1 - (y/\delta)^2\bigr]$ where $v_{\max} = 0.01$ m·s⁻¹. A dissolved gas (species A) diffuses into the film from the free surface $y = 0$ at concentration $c_{A,s} = 1.0$ mol·m⁻³. The gas concentration at the wall $y = \delta$ is essentially zero. The diffusivity is $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²·s⁻¹. Set up the **steady-state** species continuity equation and identify the dimensionless group that compares convection to diffusion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of the convection–diffusion equation (7 marks):**

1. Begin with the general species continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho_A \vec{v}) + \nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A = r_A$$

2. Substitute $\rho_A = w_A \rho$ and expand the convective divergence term-by-term in rectangular coordinates. After applying the product rule and using the **total mass continuity** to eliminate the $w_A$-coefficient terms, the convective part reduces to $\rho D w_A / Dt$.

3. Substitute **Fick's First Law** components $j_{Ai} = -\rho D_{AB} \partial w_A/\partial x_i$ (assuming constant $\rho$ and $D_{AB}$):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{j}_A = -\rho D_{AB} \nabla^2 w_A$$

4. The final closed-form equation is:

$$\rho \frac{D w_A}{Dt} = \rho D_{AB} \nabla^2 w_A + r_A$$

Expanding $D/Dt$ in rectangular coordinates:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial w_A}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial w_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\left(\frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 w_A}{\partial z^2}\right) + \frac{r_A}{\rho}}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) binary system, (ii) constant $\rho$ and $D_{AB}$, (iii) Fick's law valid, (iv) ideal mixing, (v) $r_A$ may be present (often set to zero in exam problems).

**Part (b) — Falling-film mass-transfer setup (7 marks):**

1. **Assumptions:** steady state ($\partial/\partial t = 0$), fully developed velocity (no $z$-gradient of $v_z$), neglect stream-wise diffusion in $z$, $r_A = 0$, constant $\rho$ and $D_{AB}$.

2. **Specialise** the species continuity to a 1-D-in-$y$ form:

$$v_z(y) \frac{\partial c_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 c_A}{\partial y^2}$$

3. **Boundary conditions:**

- $c_A(y=0) = c_{A,s} = 1.0$ mol·m⁻³
- $c_A(y=\delta) = 0$ mol·m⁻³
- $c_A(z=0) = 0$ (clean inlet)

4. **Nondimensionalise** using $Y = y/\delta$, $Z = z/L$, $C = c_A/c_{A,s}$, $V = v_z/v_{\max}$:

$$V(Y)\, \frac{\partial C}{\partial Z} = \frac{1}{\text{Pe}_\delta} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial Y^2}$$

where the **Peclet number for mass transfer** is:

$$\text{Pe}_\delta = \frac{v_{\max} \delta^2}{L \, D_{AB}}$$

This dimensionless group compares **convective transport in the $z$-direction** to **diffusive transport in the $y$-direction**.

5. **Order-of-magnitude estimate** with $L = 0.1$ m, $\delta = 10^{-3}$ m, $v_{\max} = 0.01$ m·s⁻¹, $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²·s⁻¹:

$$\text{Pe}_\delta = \frac{(0.01)(10^{-3})^2}{(0.1)(2 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-10}} = 50$$

A Peclet number of order unity-to-100 indicates **convection and diffusion are comparable** — neither can be neglected a priori.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B)**
> - **Common mark-loss #1:** Writing $D w_A/Dt$ in the final answer without expanding it into its $\partial/\partial t + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla$ components in rectangular form. Examiners **demand** the explicit $x$, $y$, $z$ expansion for full marks.
> - **Common mark-loss #2:** Forgetting to set $r_A = 0$ in part (b) before writing the simplified PDE. The no-reaction assumption must be **declared**, not assumed.
> - **Common mark-loss #3:** Identifying the Peclet number as $\text{Pe} = vL/D$ in $y$ alone — the correct definition uses **two different characteristic lengths** ($L$ and $\delta$) because the convection and diffusion are along different axes.
> - **Common mark-loss #4:** Confusing **molar** and **mass** fluxes. Use $N_A$ for molar (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) and $j_A$ or $n_A$ for mass (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **The two species equations** for a binary mixture are independent only in appearance — they are coupled through the **constraint** $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$ and the **total mass continuity**.
- **Diffusion appears ONLY** in the species equation, never in the total continuity equation.
- **Homogeneous reaction** ($r_A \neq 0$) **survives** in the species equation but **vanishes** from the total continuity because $r_A + r_B = 0$.
- **Rectangular-coordinate form** simply means expanding the $\nabla$ operator as $(\partial/\partial x, \partial/\partial y, \partial/\partial z)$.
- **For constant $\rho$ and $D_{AB}$** (the standard exam case), the closed-form equation in $w_A$ or $c_A$ is:

$$\frac{\partial c_A}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial c_A}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial c_A}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial c_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB} \nabla^2 c_A$$

- **Fick's First Law** in rectangular coordinates (binary): $j_{Ax} = -\rho D_{AB} \partial w_A / \partial x$, etc.
- **Boundary conditions** for binary mass-transfer problems typically take one of three forms: prescribed concentration (Dirichlet), prescribed flux (Neumann), or continuous-flux/concentration at an interface.
- **The Peclet number for mass transfer**, $\text{Pe}_L = vL/D_{AB}$, is the **mass-transfer analogue** of the Reynolds number — high $\text{Pe}$ means convection dominates.
- **Diffusivities in biological systems:** $D_{AB} \approx 10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²·s⁻¹ for liquids; $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ m²·s⁻¹ for gases at body temperature.
- **Always verify** the no-net-mass-flux condition $\vec{j}_A + \vec{j}_B = 0$ before substituting Fick's law — it is a free assumption only in **binary** systems.
- **Physical units check** (KTU favourite): $\rho$ [kg·m⁻³] × $v$ [m·s⁻¹] × area [m²] = [kg·s⁻¹], matching the time-derivative integral.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Transfer Coefficients and Theories of Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mass Transfer Coefficients and Theories of Mass Transfer ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Mass Transfer Coefficient — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_c$)** is a proportionality constant that relates the molar flux of a species undergoing mass transfer to a driving force, typically expressed as a concentration difference between the bulk phase and the interface (or between the bulk of two adjacent phases). The general flux equation is mathematically expressed as: $$N_A = k_c \cdot (C_{A,b} - C_{A,i})$$ Where: - $N_A$ = Molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s) - $k_c$ = Mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $C_{A,b}$ = Bulk concentration of A in the phase (kmol/m³) - $C_{A,i}$ = Concentration of A at the interface (kmol/m³) > [!IMPORTANT] > The mass transfer coefficient is **NOT** a property of the solute — it is a function of the hydrodynamic conditions, geometry of the system, physical properties of the fluid, and the diffusion characteristics of the solute. ### 1.2 Types of Mass Transfer Coefficients | Notation | Symbol | Driving Force | Units | |----------|--------|---------------|-------| | Concentration-based | $k_c$ | $\Delta C$ | m/s | | Mole fraction-based | $k_y$ or $k_x$ | $\Delta y$ or $\Delta x$ | kmol/m²·s | | Partial pressure-based | $k_G$ or $k_p$ | $\Delta p$ | kmol/m²·s·Pa | | Overall | $K_G$, $K_L$, $K_y$ | Bulk-to-bulk | varies | ### 1.3 Theories of Mass Transfer — The "How" of Mass Transport The **theories of mass transfer** are mathematical models that attempt to describe the *mechanism* by which a solute moves from the bulk of one phase to the bulk of another across a phase boundary. There are four classical theories: 1. **Whitman's Two-Film Theory (1923)** — Steady-state, stagnant films at the interface 2. **Higbie's Penetration Theory (1935)** — Transient, unsteady diffusion into a fluid element 3. **Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory (1951)** — Random surface replacement by bulk fluid 4. **Boundary Layer Theory** — Analogous to momentum/heat transfer boundary layers ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: The "Sponge and Bucket" Picture > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — Sugar Dissolving in a Stirred Cup of Water:** > > Imagine dropping a sugar cube into a still glass of water. The water molecules directly touching the sugar dissolve it almost instantly (this is the **interface**). However, far from the cube, the water remains unsaturated (this is the **bulk**). Between the interface and the bulk lies a thin region where the sugar concentration transitions from "saturated" to "low" — this is the **mass transfer boundary layer (or film)**. > > The **mass transfer coefficient** is essentially a measure of how *efficiently* this layer delivers the dissolved sugar into the bulk. Stir the water (higher turbulence) and the layer thins — $k_c$ increases. Don't stir — the layer thickens, and $k_c$ drops. This is the central idea behind the four theories. ### 1.5 Biological Engineering Relevance In biological systems, mass transfer is critical for: - **Oxygen transfer** from air to blood in lungs (alveolar-capillary exchange) - **Drug diffusion** across cell membranes - **Nutrient uptake** in bioreactors - **CO₂ removal** from fermenters - **Hemodialysis** in artificial kidneys > [!NOTE] > In **Module 4**, the emphasis is on the *mechanistic* models used to predict mass transfer rates in such systems, especially across gas-liquid and liquid-liquid interfaces encountered in bioprocessing equipment. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Whitman's Two-Film Theory (1923) — The Stagnant Film Model #### Core Assumptions - Two thin, stagnant films exist on either side of the interface - Mass transfer occurs by **molecular diffusion** (steady-state) through these films - The interface itself offers **no resistance** to transfer - Equilibrium exists **at the interface** between the two phases - Bulk phases are perfectly mixed (uniform concentration) #### Why It Works The theory simplifies the complex, turbulent near-interface region into a defined, predictable region where Fick's law can be applied. The **rate-limiting step** is diffusion through these films. #### The Two Resistances in Series For gas-liquid transfer of species A: $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m \cdot k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$ Where: - $k_G$ = Individual gas-phase mass transfer coefficient (kmol/m²·s·Pa) - $k_L$ = Individual liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $K_G$ = Overall gas-phase coefficient (kmol/m²·s·Pa) - $K_L$ = Overall liquid-phase coefficient (m/s) - $m$ = Slope of equilibrium line (dimensionless, $dy/dx$ at interface) #### Flux Expressions For the gas film: $$N_A = k_G (p_{A,b} - p_{A,i}) = k_G (p_A - p_{A,i})$$ For the liquid film: $$N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_A)$$ ### 2.2 Higbie's Penetration Theory (1935) — The Unsteady-State Model #### Core Concept A fluid element from the bulk moves to the interface, stays for a short, finite **contact time** $t_c$, during which unsteady-state diffusion occurs into the element. After $t_c$, it is replaced by a fresh element. #### Why It's More Realistic The two-film theory assumes *steady-state* diffusion — but in practice, eddies constantly bring fresh fluid to the interface. The penetration theory accounts for this by assuming **transient diffusion** during the contact time. #### Key Result Higbie solved Fick's second law for a semi-infinite medium with constant surface concentration: $$k_L = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}}$$ Where: - $D_{AB}$ = Diffusivity of A in B (m²/s) - $t_c$ = Contact time of fluid element at interface (s) - $k_L$ = Liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s) > [!IMPORTANT] > Higbie's theory predicts $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$, in contrast to the two-film theory which predicts $k_L \propto D_{AB}$. Experimental evidence (especially for gas absorption in packed columns) generally supports the square-root dependence. ### 2.3 Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory (1951) — The Stochastic Model #### Core Concept The surface is continuously being replaced by fresh fluid elements from the bulk at random times, characterized by a **surface renewal rate** $s$ (s⁻¹). The probability that an element stays at the surface for time between $t$ and $t+dt$ is: $$\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-s \cdot t}$$ #### Why It's More General Unlike Higbie's fixed contact time, Danckwerts allows for a **distribution** of surface ages, making the model stochastic and more realistic for turbulent systems. #### Key Result $$k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$ Where: - $s$ = Surface renewal rate (s⁻¹) — physically the fractional rate of surface replacement - $D_{AB}$ = Diffusivity (m²/s) > [!TIP] > When $s = 4/(\pi \cdot t_c)$, Danckwerts' result **reduces to Higbie's**. The theories are thus consistent at the limit of uniform surface age. ### 2.4 Boundary Layer Theory (Analogous to Heat/Momentum Transfer) By analogy with the Prandtl boundary layer in momentum transfer and the thermal boundary layer in heat transfer, a **concentration boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_c$ is defined where 99% of the concentration change occurs. The mass transfer coefficient is related to the boundary layer thickness as: $$k_c = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_c}$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Table 2.1: High-Yield Formulas for Mass Transfer Theories** | **Theory** | **$k_c$ Expression** | **Dependence on $D_{AB}$** | **Key Parameter** | |------------|----------------------|----------------------------|-------------------| | Two-Film (Whitman) | $k_c = D_{AB} / \delta$ | $k_c \propto D_{AB}^1$ | Film thickness $\delta$ | | Penetration (Higbie) | $k_c = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_c)}$ | $k_c \propto D_{AB}^{1/2}$ | Contact time $t_c$ | | Surface Renewal (Danckwerts) | $k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ | $k_c \propto D_{AB}^{1/2}$ | Renewal rate $s$ | | Boundary Layer | $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta_c$ | $k_c \propto D_{AB}^1$ | BL thickness $\delta_c$ | | **Two-Resistance Concept** | **Formula** | |---------------------------|-------------| | Overall $K_G$ | $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ | | Overall $K_L$ | $1/K_L = 1/(m k_G) + 1/k_L$ | | Fraction resistance in gas film | $(1/k_G) / (1/K_G)$ | | Fraction resistance in liquid film | $(m/k_L) / (1/K_G)$ | | **Useful Correlations** | **Formula** | |------------------------|-------------| | Schmidt number | $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$ | | Sherwood number | $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ | | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ | | General correlation (analogous to heat transfer) | $Sh = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility | **Application** | **Theory Best Suited** | **Reason** | |-----------------|------------------------|------------| | Gas absorption in packed columns | Two-film | Steady operation, large interface | | Spray towers, bubble columns | Penetration/Surface Renewal | Frequent surface renewal by droplets/bubbles | | Liquid-liquid extraction in agitated tanks | Surface Renewal | Turbulent, stochastic surface replacement | | Oxygen transfer in cell culture bioreactors | Surface Renewal / Penetration | Short contact times, turbulent sparging | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I: Mass Transfer Coefficient from Two-Film Theory **Starting Point:** Fick's First Law for steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion through a stagnant film of thickness $\delta$: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Step 1:** Apply the boundary conditions: - At $z = 0$ (interface): $C_A = C_{A,i}$ - At $z = \delta$ (bulk edge of film): $C_A = C_{A,b}$ **Step 2:** Integrate the equation: $$ \begin{aligned} N_A \int_{0}^{\delta} dz &= -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A,i}}^{C_{A,b}} dC_A \\ N_A \cdot \delta &= -D_{AB} (C_{A,b} - C_{A,i}) \\ N_A \cdot \delta &= D_{AB} (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $N_A$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$$ **Step 4:** Compare with the definition $N_A = k_c (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$: $$\boxed{k_c = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The mass transfer coefficient is directly proportional to the diffusivity and inversely proportional to the film thickness. Higher turbulence → thinner film → larger $k_c$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II: Higbie's Penetration Theory **Starting Point:** Fick's Second Law for unsteady-state, one-dimensional diffusion: $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$ **Initial and Boundary Conditions:** - $C_A(z, 0) = C_{A,b}$ for all $z > 0$ (bulk concentration initially) - $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,i}$ for $t > 0$ (interface concentration constant) - $C_A(z \to \infty, t) = C_{A,b}$ (semi-infinite medium) **Step 1:** Define a dimensionless variable to simplify: $$\eta = \frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot t}}$$ **Step 2:** The standard solution to the diffusion equation with these conditions is: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C_A - C_{A,b}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}} &= \text{erfc}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot t}}\right) \\ \frac{C_A - C_{A,b}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}} &= 1 - \text{erf}(\eta) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the flux at the interface ($z = 0$) using Fick's First Law evaluated at $t = t_c$: $$N_A(t_c) = -D_{AB} \left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} \right|_{z=0, \, t=t_c}$$ **Step 4:** Differentiate the solution w.r.t. $z$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} &= (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \cdot \frac{d}{dz}\left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)\right] \\ &= -(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{4 D_{AB} t}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Evaluate at $z = 0$, $t = t_c$: $$\left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} \right|_{z=0} = -\frac{(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t_c}}$$ **Step 6:** Substitute into the flux expression: $$ \begin{aligned} N_A &= -D_{AB} \cdot \left[-\frac{(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t_c}}\right] \\ N_A &= (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7:** Compare with $N_A = k_c (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$: $$\boxed{k_c = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}} \approx 1.13 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{t_c}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical value of the constant:** $2/\sqrt{\pi} = 1.128$. This is the key coefficient for the penetration theory. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III: Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory **Starting Point:** Average flux over a stochastic surface age distribution: $$\bar{N_A} = \int_0^\infty N_A(t) \cdot \phi(t) \, dt$$ **Step 1:** Use the time-dependent flux from the penetration result: $$N_A(t) = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the renewal age distribution $\phi(t) = s \cdot e^{-st}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{N_A} &= (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \int_0^\infty \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}} \cdot s \cdot e^{-st} \, dt \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Use the standard integral result $\int_0^\infty t^{-1/2} e^{-st} dt = \sqrt{\pi/s}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{N_A} &= (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{s}} \\ \bar{N_A} &= (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \cdot \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Identify the mass transfer coefficient: $$\boxed{k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}}$$ > [!TIP] > The **surface renewal rate $s$** can be estimated from hydrodynamic data. For a stirred tank, $s$ scales with the impeller speed $N$ roughly as $s \propto N^{1.5}$ to $N^2$, depending on geometry. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example (Numerical) **Problem:** Oxygen is absorbed from air into water in a wetted-wall column. The contact time of water at the air-water interface is estimated to be $t_c = 0.1$ s, and the diffusivity of O₂ in water at 25°C is $D_{AB} = 2.1 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Estimate $k_L$ using: 1. Two-film theory with film thickness $\delta = 0.05$ mm 2. Higbie's penetration theory 3. Danckwerts' theory with $s = 10$ s⁻¹ **Solution:** **Method 1 — Two-Film Theory:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_L &= \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-9}}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}} \\ k_L &= \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\ k_L &= 4.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Method 2 — Higbie's Penetration Theory:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_L &= 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}} \\ &= 2 \sqrt{\frac{2.1 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times 0.1}} \\ &= 2 \sqrt{6.685 \times 10^{-9}} \\ &= 2 \times 8.176 \times 10^{-5} \\ k_L &= 1.635 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Method 3 — Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_L &= \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s} = \sqrt{2.1 \times 10^{-9} \times 10} \\ &= \sqrt{2.1 \times 10^{-8}} \\ k_L &= 1.449 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison:** All three methods give values within the same order of magnitude ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ m/s). The penetration and surface renewal theories give very similar results, as expected. The two-film theory result is sensitive to the assumed film thickness, which is often the *weakest* input. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Theory | **Operating Condition** | **Dominant Mechanism** | **Best Theory** | **Reason** | |--------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|------------| | Packed absorption column, low flow rates | Steady molecular diffusion | Two-Film | Long contact time, quasi-steady | | Spray tower, fast droplets | Transient diffusion in droplet | Penetration | Known short contact time | | Stirred fermenter, turbulent sparging | Stochastic surface exposure | Surface Renewal | Random bubble-fluid interaction | | Falling film, laminar flow | Well-defined concentration profile | Boundary Layer | Predictable $\delta_c$ from hydrodynamics | | Biological membrane transport | Permeation + diffusion | Two-Film analog | Steady-state, well-defined barrier | ### 3.6 Master Equation: Linking the Theories > [!IMPORTANT] > The four theories can be **unified** by recognizing they all predict $k_c \propto D_{AB}^n$ with $n = 1$ (film, BL) or $n = 1/2$ (penetration, renewal), and the proportionality constant encodes the characteristic timescale or length of the contact event. A general form often written is: $$k_c = a \cdot D_{AB}^n \cdot \text{(geometry / hydrodynamics)}$$ Where $a$ and $n$ depend on the chosen model. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Mass Transfer Across a Gas-Liquid Interface (Two-Film Concept) ```mermaid graph LR A[Bulk Gas Phase
