The total resistance is the sum of individual phase resistances. The "current" is the flux $N_A$, and the "voltage" is the concentration/partial pressure difference. This is **directly analogous to Ohm's law** in electrical circuits. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Define mass transfer coefficient. Distinguish between individual and overall mass transfer coefficients with a suitable example.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_c$):** It is the proportionality constant that relates the molar flux of a diffusing species to its concentration difference between the bulk and the interface. $$N_A = k_c \cdot (C_{A,b} - C_{A,i})$$ **Distinction:** | Aspect | Individual Coefficient | Overall Coefficient | |--------|----------------------|---------------------| | Definition | Resistance in **one phase** only | Resistance of **both phases combined** | | Symbol | $k_G$ or $k_L$ | $K_G$ or $K_L$ | | Driving force | Phase bulk to interface | Bulk of one phase to bulk of other | | Example | $k_L$ for CO₂ absorption into water in a single liquid film | $K_G$ for CO₂ absorption considering both gas and liquid film resistances | > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** > **State and explain the assumptions of Whitman's two-film theory for mass transfer.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Assumptions of Two-Film Theory:** 1. **Stagnant films at the interface:** Two thin, stagnant films exist — one on each side of the interface — through which mass transfer occurs by molecular diffusion alone. 2. **No resistance at the interface:** The interface itself offers no resistance; the two phases are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at the boundary. 3. **Steady-state diffusion:** Fick's first law applies with constant flux; concentrations at any point in the film do not change with time. 4. **Perfectly mixed bulk phases:** The bulk of each phase is uniform in concentration due to turbulent mixing; concentration gradients exist *only* in the films. 5. **Mass transfer is independent of position in the bulk:** The driving force is constant across the bulk and acts only across the film. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B) > **Q3A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the mass transfer coefficient using **Higbie's penetration theory**. State clearly the assumptions and boundary conditions. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A gas absorption column operates with a contact time of $t_c = 0.05$ s for the liquid at the gas-liquid interface. The diffusivity of the absorbing gas in the liquid is $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Calculate the mass transfer coefficient using (i) Higbie's theory and (ii) Danckwerts' theory assuming a surface renewal rate of $s = 8$ s⁻¹. Comment on the relative magnitudes. --- ### Complete Model Solution for Q3A #### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks] **Assumptions [1 Mark]:** - A fluid element from the bulk is brought to the interface and exposed for a finite **contact time $t_c$**. - During this exposure, **unsteady-state molecular diffusion** occurs into the element. - The element is then replaced by a fresh one; all elements have **equal exposure time**. **Governing Equation [1 Mark]:** Fick's Second Law for one-dimensional, transient diffusion: $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$ **Initial and Boundary Conditions [1 Mark]:** - $C_A(z, 0) = C_{A,b}$ for $0 \leq z < \infty$ - $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,i}$ for $t > 0$ - $C_A(z \to \infty, t) = C_{A,b}$ for $t > 0$ **Solution by Error Function [2 Marks]:** The standard solution to the above is: $$\frac{C_A - C_{A,b}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}} = 1 - \text{erf}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)$$ **Flux at the interface at $t = t_c$ [1 Mark]:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0, \, t=t_c} = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}}$$ **Final Result [1 Mark]:** $$\boxed{k_c = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}} \approx 1.13 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{t_c}}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks] **Given:** $t_c = 0.05$ s, $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $s = 8$ s⁻¹ **(i) Higbie's Penetration Theory [3 Marks]:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_c &= 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}} \\ &= 2 \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times 0.05}} \\ &= 2 \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.15708}} \\ &= 2 \sqrt{9.549 \times 10^{-9}} \\ &= 2 \times 9.773 \times 10^{-5} \\ k_c &= 1.955 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory [3 Marks]:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_c &= \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s} \\ &= \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 8} \\ &= \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-8}} \\ k_c &= 1.095 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment [1 Mark]:** > The Higbie model predicts a $k_c$ value that is approximately **1.78 times larger** than that predicted by the Danckwerts model. This is because the parameter $s = 8$ s⁻¹ corresponds to a mean surface age of $1/s = 0.125$ s, which is **larger** than the contact time $t_c = 0.05$ s. Thus, surface elements are being renewed *less frequently* in Danckwerts' case, leading to a lower average mass transfer coefficient. If $s = 4/(\pi t_c) = 25.46$ s⁻¹, both theories would yield identical results. --- > **Q3B. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **two-film theory** of mass transfer. Derive the expressions for the **overall mass transfer coefficient** based on the gas phase and the liquid phase. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** In an ammonia absorption system, the individual gas-film coefficient is $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kmol/m²·s·Pa and the individual liquid-film coefficient is $k_L = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s. The equilibrium relationship is $y = 0.9 x$ (mole fractions). Calculate (i) the overall coefficients $K_G$ and $K_L$, and (ii) the percentage resistance in each phase. --- ### Complete Model Solution for Q3B #### Part (a) — Two-Film Theory and Overall Coefficients [7 Marks] **Conceptual Explanation [2 Marks]:** The two-film theory (Whitman, 1923) postulates that whenever two phases are in contact, a thin stagnant film exists on each side of the interface. Mass transfer occurs by **molecular diffusion** through these films under steady-state conditions. The bulk of each phase is assumed well-mixed. The interface itself offers no resistance, and equilibrium is established at the interface. **Derivation of $K_G$ [2 Marks]:** From the flux equality $N_A = k_G(p_A - p_{A,i}) = k_L(C_{A,i} - C_A)$ and the equilibrium relation $p_{A,i} = m \cdot C_{A,i}$: $$ \begin{aligned} N_A &= k_G \left(p_A - \frac{C_{A,i}}{H}\right) \quad \text{(using Henry's law analogue)} \\ &= k_G (p_A - p_A^*) \quad \text{where } p_A^* = H C_A \\ \Rightarrow N_A &= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}} (p_A - p_A^*) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{K_G} &= \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation of $K_L$ [2 Marks]:** Similarly, expressing in liquid-phase concentrations: $$ \begin{aligned} N_A &= K_L (C_A^* - C_A) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{K_L} &= \frac{1}{m k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L} \end{aligned} $$ **Relation between $K_G$ and $K_L$ [1 Mark]:** $$K_G = \frac{K_L}{m} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad K_G \cdot m = K_L$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks] **Given:** - $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kmol/m²·s·Pa - $k_L = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s - $m = 0.9$ (slope of equilibrium line; $y = 0.9 x$) - Henry's constant conversion: $m = dy/dx$ **Step 1: Calculate $m/k_L$ [1 Mark]:** $$\frac{m}{k_L} = \frac{0.9}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.0 \times 10^{3} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa/kmol}$$ **Step 2: Calculate $1/k_G$ [1 Mark]:** $$\frac{1}{k_G} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.667 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa/kmol}$$ **Step 3: Calculate $K_G$ [1 Mark]:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{K_G} &= \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \\ &= 6.667 \times 10^{5} + 3.0 \times 10^{3} \\ &= 6.697 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa/kmol} \\ K_G &= \frac{1}{6.697 \times 10^{5}} = 1.493 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Calculate $K_L$ [1 Mark]:** $$K_L = m \cdot K_G = 0.9 \times 1.493 \times 10^{-6} = 1.344 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5: Calculate Percentage Resistances [2 Marks]:** $$\% \text{ Gas-phase resistance} = \frac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} \times 100 = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{5}}{6.697 \times 10^{5}} \times 100 = 99.55\%$$ $$\% \text{ Liquid-phase resistance} = \frac{m/k_L}{1/K_G} \times 100 = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{3}}{6.697 \times 10^{5}} \times 100 = 0.45\%$$ **Conclusion:** The **gas phase controls** the mass transfer process in this ammonia absorption system, contributing over **99.5%** of the total resistance. The liquid-phase resistance is essentially negligible. Any design effort to enhance mass transfer should focus on increasing $k_G$ (e.g., by increasing gas-phase turbulence). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Module:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert units of $m$:** In the two-resistance formula, the slope $m$ must be **dimensionally consistent** with the driving force units. Mixing $k_G$ in (kmol/m²·s·Pa) and $k_L$ in (m/s) requires careful handling of $m$ — it's not dimensionless in general! (Often $m = H/c_{total}$ or $m = dy/dx$ in mole fraction form.) > > 2. **Confusing $K_G$ and $K_L$ formulas:** Many students write $1/K_G = 1/k_G + 1/k_L$. This is **WRONG**. The correct formula is $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$. The $m$ appears because the gas- and liquid-phase driving forces have **different units** and must be reconciled through the equilibrium line. > > 3. **Penetration theory boundary conditions:** A common mistake is to write $C_A(0,t) = C_{A,b}$ (instead of $C_{A,i}$). Always remember the interface is at a *different* concentration than the bulk — that's precisely the driving force. > > 4. **Mixing up $t_c$ and $s$:** Higbie uses *fixed contact time*; Danckwerts uses *stochastic renewal rate*. They are **not** the same quantity. The relationship $s = 4/(\pi t_c)$ is a *special case*, not a general equivalence. > > 5. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for clearly stated assumptions in derivation questions. Do not skip them. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mass Transfer Coefficients & Theories** **Core Definitions:** - Mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ = proportionality constant between flux and driving force - Individual $k_G$, $k_L$ = one-phase resistance - Overall $K_G$, $K_L$ = combined two-phase resistance **Key Formulas (must memorize):** - Two-film: $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta$ - Penetration (Higbie): $k_c = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_c)}$ - Surface Renewal (Danckwerts): $k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ - Two-resistance: $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ - Boundary layer: $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta_c$ **Dependence on Diffusivity:** - $D_{AB}^1$ → Two-film & Boundary Layer - $D_{AB}^{1/2}$ → Penetration & Surface Renewal - Experimental data generally supports the $\sqrt{D_{AB}}$ dependence → penetration-type models are often more accurate. **Assumptions to Memorize (for derivations):** - **Two-Film:** Stagnant films, steady-state, no interface resistance, equilibrium at interface, perfectly mixed bulks - **Penetration:** Equal contact time $t_c$, unsteady-state diffusion, semi-infinite medium, fresh fluid element at interface - **Surface Renewal:** Random surface replacement with rate $s$, age distribution $\phi(t) = s e^{-st}$ - **Boundary Layer:** Analogous to Prandtl/thermal BL, $\delta_c$ defined as 99% concentration change thickness **Engineering Connections:** - Oxygen transfer in bioreactors → Surface Renewal - Gas absorption in packed columns → Two-Film - Drug dissolution → Penetration - Falling-film crystallizers → Boundary Layer **Quick Numerical Check (typical orders of magnitude):** - $k_L$ for O₂ in water at 25°C ≈ $10^{-4}$ m/s - $D_{AB}$ for gases in gases ≈ $10^{-5}$ m²/s - $D_{AB}$ for liquids in liquids ≈ $10^{-9}$ m²/s - $D_{AB}$ for gases in liquids ≈ $10^{-9}$ m²/s > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Formula Card:** Carry these 4 equations in memory — they cover 80% of the marks in this module: > 1. $k_c = D_{AB}/\delta$ (Film theory) > 2. $k_c = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_c)}$ (Higbie) > 3. $k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} s}$ (Danckwerts) > 4. $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ (Two resistance)

topics.title: Oxygen Transport to Microbial Cultures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oxygen Transport to Microbial Cultures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxygen Transport to Microbial Cultures** is defined as the **rate-controlled transfer of molecular oxygen (O₂) from the gas phase (typically air bubbles) across the gas–liquid interface, through the bulk liquid medium, and finally to the cell membrane/mitochondria of microbial cells**, where it serves as the terminal electron acceptor in aerobic respiration.

In bioprocess engineering, oxygen transport is fundamentally a **convective–diffusive mass transfer operation** coupled with a **biological reaction (respiration)**. The dissolved oxygen (DO) concentration in the broth must be maintained above a critical value (typically **> 10–30% of air saturation**, i.e., ~**0.8–2.5 mg/L** in water at 30 °C) to prevent oxygen limitation that would shift metabolism toward unwanted fermentative by-products (ethanol, lactic acid, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **OTR** – Oxygen Transfer Rate (rate of O₂ supply from gas to liquid)
> - **OUR** – Oxygen Uptake Rate (rate of O₂ consumption by cells)
> - **K_L·a** – Volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹) — *the single most important design parameter for aeration*
> - **C* (C-star)** – Equilibrium dissolved O₂ concentration at the gas–liquid interface (saturation value)
> - **C_L** – Actual dissolved O₂ concentration in the bulk liquid

### 1.2 The Operational Chain of Oxygen Transport (Big Picture)

Oxygen must travel through a sequence of **resistances in series** before it can be metabolically consumed:

$$\text{Gas Bulk} \xrightarrow{R_1} \text{Gas–Liquid Film} \xrightarrow{R_2} \text{Liquid Bulk} \xrightarrow{R_3} \text{Liquid–Cell Film} \xrightarrow{R_4} \text{Intracellular (cytoplasm/mitochondria)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is oxygen transport the bottleneck?**
> Oxygen's **aqueous solubility is extremely low** (≈ **8.6 mg/L at 20 °C, 1 atm in pure water**). As cell density rises beyond ~1–2 g dry cell weight (DCW)/L, microbial respiration can demand oxygen faster than the gas–liquid interface can deliver it. Thus **mass transfer**, not reaction kinetics, dictates fermenter productivity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Straw" Model

Imagine you are breathing through a **very thin, long straw submerged in a thick milkshake** (the culture broth):

- The **air at your lips** = the gas bubble
- The **straw walls** = the gas–liquid interfacial film (the dominant resistance)
- The **milkshake** = the bulk fermentation liquid with cells
- Your **lungs** = the microbial cell consuming O₂

Just as sipping becomes harder when the milkshake is thicker (higher viscosity → lower diffusivity) or the straw is narrower (smaller interfacial area), oxygen transfer in fermenters becomes harder as broth viscosity rises, bubbles coalesce, or mixing is poor. **Engineers solve this by adding baffles, impellers, and spargers** — equivalent to widening the straw and shaking the milkshake.

### 1.4 Why Oxygen Transport is Critical in Bioprocess Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Oxygen is required in only **moderately soluble** amounts, but it must be supplied continuously. Even a brief depletion (seconds to minutes) can trigger **Crabtree effect** in yeast, **overflow metabolism** in *E. coli*, or **shift from antibiotic production to growth** in actinomycetes. Hence, **aeration/agitation design is a permanent exam favourite**.

The volumetric mass balance on dissolved O₂ in a well-mixed fermenter is:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} \cdot X$$

where:
- $K_La$ = volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹)
- $C^*$ = saturation dissolved O₂ concentration (mg/L)
- $C_L$ = actual dissolved O₂ concentration (mg/L)
- $q_{O_2}$ = specific oxygen uptake rate (mg O₂ / g cell · h)
- $X$ = biomass concentration (g DCW/L)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Film Theory (Lewis–Whitman, 1923) — Foundation of OTR

The two-film model conceptualizes a **stagnant gas film** and a **stagnant liquid film** on either side of a flat gas–liquid interface. Beyond these films, bulk gas and bulk liquid are assumed perfectly mixed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core 'Why':** Most of the resistance to O₂ transfer lies in the **liquid film** because O₂ is poorly soluble in water. Thus, the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (k_L)** controls the rate.

**Steady-state flux of O₂ across the interface:**

$$N_A = k_L (C^* - C_L) \quad \text{[mg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)]}$$

where:
- $N_A$ = molar flux of oxygen (mass per unit area per unit time)
- $k_L$ = liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/h)
- $C^*$ = dissolved O₂ concentration at the interface in equilibrium with gas (mg/m³ or mg/L)
- $C_L$ = dissolved O₂ concentration in the bulk liquid (mg/m³ or mg/L)

### 2.2 From Film Theory to Volumetric Coefficient (K_La)

Industrial fermenters use **dispersed bubbles**, not flat interfaces. The interfacial area is **not a flat surface** but a sum of all bubble surfaces. We define:

$$a = \frac{\text{Total Gas–Liquid Interfacial Area}}{\text{Liquid Volume}} \quad \text{[m}^2/\text{m}^3 \text{]}$$

Multiplying flux by interfacial area per unit volume:

$$\text{OTR} = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = K_La \cdot (C^* - C_L) \quad \text{[mg/(L·h)]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Emphasis:** The parameter $K_La$ is *volumetric* — it is **not a pure property** of the system, but a lumped parameter combining film coefficient ($k_L$) and interfacial area ($a$). Therefore, $K_La$ is **NOT a constant**; it depends on impeller speed, gas flow rate, geometry, fluid properties, and even cell morphology.

### 2.3 Driving Force Analysis — Why $(C^* - C_L)$ Matters

- $C^*$ depends on **partial pressure of O₂ in the gas (p_O₂)**, temperature, ionic strength, and presence of antifoam/surfactants.
- $C_L$ is what we measure with a **DO probe**.
- $(C^* - C_L)$ is the **driving force**. The larger this gap, the faster oxygen dissolves.

For pure water at 1 atm, 25 °C:
$$C^*_{O_2,\text{water}} \approx 8.26 \;\text{mg/L}$$

In a fermentation broth with salts and cells, $C^*$ is typically **10–50% lower** than in pure water.

### 2.4 Overall Volumetric Oxygen Balance (Steady State)

At steady state ($dC_L/dt = 0$):

$$\boxed{K_La (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X = \text{OUR}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students often forget that at steady state, **OTR = OUR**. The fermenter is *designed* so that the maximum achievable OTR (at $C_L \to 0$) is greater than the maximum OUR. Otherwise, oxygen limitation occurs.

$$K_La \cdot C^* > (q_{O_2})_{\max} \cdot X_{\max}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield equations for this topic, in one consolidated table for last-minute revision.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OTR (oxygen transfer rate) | $\text{OTR} = K_La (C^* - C_L)$ | mg/(L·h) | Lumped driving force model |
| 2 | OUR (oxygen uptake rate) | $\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot X$ | mg/(L·h) | $q_{O_2}$ is specific uptake rate |
| 3 | Steady-state balance | $K_La (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} X$ | mg/(L·h) | Sets design criterion |
| 4 | Oxygen in air (by volume) | $21\%$ of air is O₂ | — | Critical for sparger calculations |
| 5 | Henry's Law | $C^* = p_{O_2} / H$ or $C^* = H' \cdot p_{O_2}$ | mg/L | $H$ = Henry's constant |
| 6 | Penetration Theory flux | $N_A = 2 (D_L / \pi t_e)^{1/2} (C^* - C_L)$ | mg/(m²·h) | Higbie model; $t_e$ = exposure time |
| 7 | Surface Renewal | $N_A = (D_L \cdot s)^{1/2} (C^* - C_L)$ | mg/(m²·h) | Danckwerts; $s$ = renewal rate |
| 8 | Power Input (ungassed) | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N_i^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | W | $N_p$ = power number |
| 9 | Gassed power | $P_g \approx P \cdot (1 - \text{constant} \cdot Q_g)$ | W | Empirical; van't Riet/Cooney |
| 10 | Specific power input | $P/V$ | W/m³ | Typical: 1000–5000 W/m³ for high OUR |
| 11 | Superficial gas velocity | $u_g = Q_g / A_c$ | m/s | Used in regime determination |
| 12 | Sauter mean diameter | $d_{32} = \sum n_i d_i^3 / \sum n_i d_i^2$ | m | Mean bubble size |
| 13 | Van't Riet correlation | $K_La = K \cdot (P/V)^{0.7} \cdot u_g^{0.3}$ | h⁻¹ | Non-coalescing, non-Newtonian |
| 14 | Critical DO for respiration | $C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.1$ to $0.5 \, C^*$ | mg/L | Below this, growth halts |
| 15 | Enhancement factor (E) | $N_A^{\text{with rxn}} = E \cdot N_A^{\text{physical}}$ | — | $E > 1$ when cells consume O₂ at interface |
| 16 | Damköhler number | $\text{Da} = q_{O_2} X / (k_L a C^*)$ | — | Da < 1 → mass-transfer limited; Da > 1 → kinetically limited |

### 2.6 Effect of Operating Parameters on K_La

| Parameter | Effect on $K_La$ | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Impeller speed ($N_i$) | ↑ (strongly) | Agitation power |
| ↑ Gas flow rate ($Q_g$) | ↑ (moderately) | Aeration rate (vvm) |
| ↑ Viscosity (η) | ↓ (strongly) | Use of Rushton impeller, broth formulation |
| ↑ Cell concentration | ↓ (apparent) | O₂ consumed at interface; coalescence suppression |
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ $k_L$, ↓ $C^*$ (net small change) | Fermentation set-point |
| ↑ Antifoam / surfactant | ↓ (coalescence) | Use sparingly |
| ↓ Bubble size | ↑ (huge) | Sparger design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Van't Riet (1979) correlation $K_La = K (P/V)^{0.7} u_g^{0.3}$ is the workhorse of fermenter scale-up. It tells engineers that **agitation power is more important than airflow** for oxygen transfer — which is why most industrial fermenters are equipped with multiple Rushton turbines, not just spargers.

### 2.7 Henry's Law and the Effect of Partial Pressure

$$C^* = H' \cdot p_{O_2}$$

where $H' = H^{-1}$ is the **Henry solubility constant** (mg/(L·atm) or mol/(L·atm)).

> [!TIP]
> **Pure oxygen sparging (vs. air)** raises $p_{O_2}$ from 0.21 atm to 1.0 atm, increasing $C^*$ by **~4.8×**. This is used in very-high-cell-density cultures (e.g., recombinant *E. coli* > 100 g DCW/L).

### 2.8 Enhancement by Chemical/Biological Reaction

When O₂ is consumed by cells as soon as it dissolves, the actual flux can exceed the purely physical flux. The ratio is the **enhancement factor** $E$:

$$E = \frac{\text{Flux with reaction}}{\text{Flux without reaction}} = \frac{\sqrt{D_{O_2} \cdot k_r \cdot C_B^{n-1}}}{\tanh(\sqrt{D_{O_2} \cdot k_r \cdot C_B^{n-1}}/k_L)}$$

For microbial cultures with small $K_La$ and large OUR, $E$ may be approximated as:

$$E \approx 1 + \frac{\text{OUR}}{k_L a \cdot C^*}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** When the **Damköhler number Da = OUR / (K_La · C*) > 1**, oxygen transfer is enhanced by cellular consumption; the cell acts as an "oxygen sink" that steepens the concentration gradient. This is why apparent $K_La$ in real fermentations may be **larger** than $K_La$ measured in clean water.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two-Film Theory to the OTR Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting premise:** O₂ diffuses through a stagnant gas film of thickness $\delta_G$ and a stagnant liquid film of thickness $\delta_L$ at the gas–liquid interface. Steady state, 1-D Fickian diffusion.

**Step 1.** Fick's first law across the gas film:

$$J_G = \frac{D_G}{\delta_G}(p_{O_2,G} - p_{O_2,i})$$

**Step 2.** Fick's first law across the liquid film:

$$J_L = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L}(C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 3.** At steady state, $J_G = J_L = N_A$ (no accumulation at the interface).

**Step 4.** Introduce film mass transfer coefficients:

$$k_G = \frac{D_G}{\delta_G} \quad \text{and} \quad k_L = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L}$$

So the two equations become:

$$N_A = k_G(p_{O_2,G} - p_{O_2,i}) \quad \text{and} \quad N_A = k_L(C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 5.** Use Henry's law at the interface: $p_{O_2,i} = H \cdot C_i$ (with $C_i = C^*$ in equilibrium).

**Step 6.** Eliminate the interfacial term. Express the total driving force as a sum of two resistances:

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{H}{k_G}$$

where $K_L$ is the **overall mass transfer coefficient** based on the liquid phase.

**Step 7.** For sparingly soluble gases like O₂, $H$ is large (low solubility) → the term $H/k_G$ dominates the denominator unless $k_G$ is also large. For air-water systems, **the liquid-film resistance controls**: $\frac{1}{K_L} \approx \frac{1}{k_L}$.

**Step 8.** Therefore, the rate of transfer per unit area:

$$N_A \approx k_L (C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 9.** Multiply by interfacial area per unit volume $a$ to obtain the volumetric transfer rate (OTR):

$$\boxed{\text{OTR} = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = K_La (C^* - C_L)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $\delta$ in plain English:** The film thickness $\delta_L$ is the "thickness of the unstirred layer" of liquid clinging to the bubble surface. Agitation **shreds this layer** → smaller $\delta_L$ → larger $k_L$ → larger $K_La$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Dissolved Oxygen Balance in a CSTR

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting premise:** A continuous stirred-tank fermenter (CSTR) is well-mixed; the only DO input is gas–liquid transfer; the only sink is microbial respiration. There is no DO in the inlet liquid feed.

**Step 1.** Mass balance on dissolved O₂ in the liquid phase (volume $V$, mg/L):

$$V \frac{dC_L}{dt} = \underbrace{0}_{\text{inlet}} - \underbrace{0}_{\text{outlet (no DO in liq)}} + \underbrace{K_La (C^* - C_L) V}_{\text{transfer in}} - \underbrace{q_{O_2} X V}_{\text{consumption}}$$

**Step 2.** Divide by $V$:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} X$$

**Step 3.** At **steady state**, $dC_L/dt = 0$:

$$K_La (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} X \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{OTR} = \text{OUR}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $C_L$:

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} X}{K_La}$$

**Step 5.** For oxygen **NOT to be limiting**, $C_L > C_{\text{crit}}$:

$$C^* - \frac{q_{O_2,\max} \cdot X_{\max}}{K_La} \geq C_{\text{crit}}$$

**Step 6.** Rearrange to obtain the **design equation for $K_La$**:

$$\boxed{K_La \geq \frac{q_{O_2,\max} \cdot X_{\max}}{C^* - C_{\text{crit}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE key design equation for KTU exam problems on aeration system sizing.** Memorize the form, the rationale, and the units. Marks are routinely lost when students confuse $q_{O_2}$ (specific, mg/g·h) with $q_{O_2} X$ (volumetric, mg/L·h).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Higbie's Penetration Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting premise:** Real bubbles are not steady. Each element of the liquid surface is exposed to the gas for a finite time $t_e$ (the "exposure" or "contact" time) before being swept back into the bulk. The surface element continuously absorbs O₂ during this exposure.

**Step 1.** Fick's second law in a semi-infinite medium, surface concentration $C^*$ held constant, initial concentration $C_L$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_L \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

with $C(z, 0) = C_L$ and $C(0, t) = C^*$.

**Step 2.** Solution by Laplace transform (Einstein's classic result):

$$C(z,t) - C_L = (C^* - C_L) \cdot \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_L t}}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Instantaneous flux at the surface $z = 0$:

$$N_A(t) = -D_L \left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0} = \sqrt{\frac{D_L}{\pi t}} \,(C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 4.** Average over the exposure time $t_e$:

$$\bar{N}_A = \frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e} \sqrt{\frac{D_L}{\pi t}}\,(C^* - C_L)\,dt = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_L}{\pi t_e}}\,(C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 5.** Define $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_L/(\pi t_e)}$, giving:

$$\boxed{\bar{N}_A = k_L (C^* - C_L) \quad \text{with} \quad k_L = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_L}{\pi t_e}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Higbie's result explains why **agitation** (which shortens $t_e$ by rapidly renewing the surface) **increases $k_L$ and hence $K_La$**. Faster impeller → smaller $t_e$ → larger $k_L$ → larger OTR.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Number and Gassed Power

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting premise:** Mechanically agitated fermenters receive power input through a rotating impeller of diameter $D_i$ at speed $N_i$ (rev/s) immersed in liquid of density $\rho$.

**Step 1.** By dimensional analysis / torque balance, the impeller power draw in a turbulent (Newtonian) regime is:

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N_i^3 \cdot D_i^5$$

where $N_p$ is the (dimensionless) **power number**.

**Step 2.** For a standard Rushton 6-blade turbine in baffled tank, $N_p \approx 5.0$ in the turbulent regime ($\text{Re} > 10^4$).

**Step 3.** When gas is sparged, the apparent density of the impeller region decreases (gas pockets behind blades), so the gassed power $P_g$ is lower than the ungassed power $P$. A widely used empirical correction:

$$\frac{P_g}{P} = 0.1 \left(\frac{Q_g}{N_i V}\right)^{0.2} \quad \text{(Michels–van't Riet, for Rushton turbines)}$$

**Step 4.** The specific power input is the parameter that controls $K_La$ via the Van't Riet (1979) correlation:

$$K_La = K \cdot (P_g/V)^0.7 \cdot u_g^{0.3}$$

**Step 5.** Substituting $P_g$ and $u_g = Q_g/A_c$, the entire fermenter aeration design is anchored to **two experimentally accessible engineering variables**: impeller speed $N_i$ and gas flow rate $Q_g$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Regime Identification in Bubble Columns and Sparged Vessels

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Different flow regimes have drastically different $K_La$ and gas holdup. KTU problems sometimes ask you to identify the regime from given $u_g$ and $D_i$.

| Regime | Superficial velocity $u_g$ | Bubble behaviour | $K_La$ trend |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homogeneous (bubbly) | $u_g < 0.02$ m/s | Small, uniform, no coalescence | Low |
| Heterogeneous (churn-turbulent) | $0.04 < u_g < 0.10$ m/s | Large + small bimodal; coalescence | High |
| Slug flow | $u_g \approx 0.10$–$0.30$ m/s (narrow columns) | Taylor bubbles | Drops (large bubbles, less area) |
| Foam | Variable | Foam at surface | Complex; antifoam needed |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Measurement Techniques for K_La

| Method | Principle | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sulfite oxidation** | O₂ oxidizes Na₂SO₃ to Na₂SO₄ (Co²⁺ catalyst) | Simple, no need for cells | Uses $C_L \to 0$ assumption; not real broth |
| **Dynamic gassing-out (O₂ step-change)** | Step change gas → monitor $C_L(t)$ vs. time | In-situ, real broth, real cells | Requires fast-response DO probe |
| **Oxygen balance / off-gas analysis** | Difference in $O_2\%$ inlet vs. outlet × $Q_g$ | Most realistic (steady state) | Needs mass spectrometer / paramagnetic analyser |
| **Power-input correlation** | $K_La = f(P/V, u_g)$ | Quick for design | Empirical; geometry-dependent |
| **Hydrazine oxidation** | Similar to sulfite, but milder | Less corrosive than sulfite | Less common |

> [!NOTE]
> **The most exam-favoured method is "dynamic gassing-out"**, because it has an explicit differential equation. Memorize the integrated form:
>
> $$\ln\left(\frac{C^* - C_L(t)}{C^* - C_{L,0}}\right) = -K_La \cdot t$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Overall Oxygen Transport Pathway in a Fermenter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Compressed Air Supply] --> G2[Inlet Air Filter 0.2 micron]
    G2 --> G3[Sparger Ring below Impeller]
    G3 --> G4[Bubble Plume Rises]
    G4 --> G5[Rushton Impeller Disperses Bubbles]
    G5 --> G6[Gas-Liquid Interface Films]
    G6 --> G7[O2 Dissolves into Broth]
    G7 --> G8[Bulk Liquid Mixing]
    G8 --> G9[Diffusion to Cell Surface]
    G9 --> G10[Transport Across Cell Membrane]
    G10 --> G11[Intracellular Reduction at Cytochrome Oxidase]
    G1 -.->|Cstar saturation| G6
    G11 --> G12[Metabolic CO2 + H2O + Energy]
    G12 --> G13[Heat and CO2 Removal]
    G13 --> G14[Off-Gas Vent through Condenser]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Resistances-in-Series Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Gas Phase] -->|R_G gas-side resistance| B[Gas-Liquid Interface]
    B -->|R_L liquid-side resistance dominant for O2| C[Bulk Liquid Phase]
    C -->|R_LC liquid-cell film| D[Cell Surface]
    D -->|R_M membrane transport| E[Cell Interior Mitochondria]
    E --> F[O2 Reduced to H2O]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#FF6B6B
    style C fill:#4ECDC4
    style D fill:#95E1D3
    style E fill:#C9E4CA
    style F fill:#B5EAD7
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Dissolved Oxygen Dynamics During Fermentation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Lag
    Lag --> Exponential: O2 high, cells dividing
    Exponential --> Stationary: O2 drops as OUR peaks
    Stationary --> Decline: Substrate depleted
    note right of Stationary: Critical phase
    note right of Stationary: KLa must sustain O2 above Ccrit
    Decline --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — K_La Measurement System (Dynamic Gassing-Out)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FermenterVessel [Stirred Tank Fermenter]
        SP[Sparger] -->|air bubbles| LIQ[Culture Broth]
        IMP[Rushton Impeller] -->|agitation| LIQ
        DOP[DO Probe Polarographic] -->|mV signal| LIQ
    end
    DOP -->|4-20 mA| CTRL[PID Controller and Data Logger]
    CTRL -->|relay| GASV[Solenoid Gas Valve]
    GASV -->|N2 purge then air re-introduction| SP
    CTRL -->|logged C_L versus t| CALC[K_La Calculation Software]
    CALC -->|regression slope| RESULT[Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Bubble-Coalescence Diagram — Effect of Surfactants and Antifoam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Bubble diameter 1 mm high K_La] -->|coalescence| B[Large Bubble diameter 5 mm low K_La]
    B -->|breakup by impeller| A
    C[Antifoam or Surfactant] -->|suppress coalescence| A
    D[Pure Water Ionic] -->|promotes coalescence| B
    style A fill:#90EE90
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#ADD8E6
    style D fill:#FFFFE0
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Decision Tree — Aeration System Design Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Required OUR from cell density and q_O2] --> Q2[Choose Target C_L above C_crit]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute Required K_La from design equation]
    Q3 --> Q4[Is required K_La greater than 200 per h?]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Use Rushton turbines and high P per V]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6[Use single marine propeller, low P per V]
    Q5 --> Q7[Check P per V below 5000 W per m3 limit]
    Q6 --> Q8[Check shear sensitivity of cells]
    Q7 --> Q9[Final: sparger geometry, baffles, vvm]
    Q8 --> Q9
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution per KTU 2024 Scheme (ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** Answer 1 of 2 choices × 14 marks = 14 marks
> - **Module total contribution:** 20 marks (out of 100 over 5 modules)
> - Cognitive levels: **Apply (40%)**, **Analyze (30%)**, **Remember (20%)**, **Evaluate (10%)**

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, 3 marks]**

> *Define the term "Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient" ($K_L a$) as applied to oxygen transport in a microbial fermenter. State its units and indicate the operating variables on which it depends.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** $K_L a$ is the product of the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ (m/h) and the gas–liquid interfacial area per unit volume $a$ (m²/m³). It quantifies the **overall rate of oxygen transfer from gas bubbles into the bulk liquid per unit driving force** $(C^* - C_L)$.

  $$\text{OTR} = K_La (C^* - C_L) \quad \text{mg/(L·h)}$$

- **Units:** **h⁻¹** (since $(C^*-C_L)$ is in mg/L and OTR is in mg/(L·h), $K_La$ must have units of h⁻¹).

- **Operating variables (1 mark):** $K_L a$ depends on **impeller speed ($N_i$), gas flow rate ($Q_g$ or vvm), impeller diameter ($D_i$), fluid viscosity (η), surface tension, presence of antifoam, and tank geometry** (e.g., $D_T/D_i$, number/type of impellers, baffles).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]**

> *Why is the liquid-side resistance, and not the gas-side resistance, the rate-limiting step in oxygen transfer from air bubbles to a fermentation broth?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Oxygen is sparingly soluble in water** (≈ 8.6 mg/L at 20 °C). Henry's law constant for O₂ is large.
- In the two-film model, the overall resistance is: $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{H}{k_G}$.
- Because $H$ is large, the term $\frac{H}{k_G}$ is significant **only if** $k_G$ is also small. For air bubbles, $k_G$ is large due to high gas diffusivity, but more importantly, for **moderately soluble gases like O₂ the liquid-film resistance dominates**.
- **Consequence:** Increasing gas flow rate has a **weaker effect** than increasing impeller speed (which renews the liquid film).

  $$\frac{R_{\text{liquid}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \approx 0.7 \text{ to } 0.95 \text{ for } O_2$$

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each module in the question paper has TWO 14-mark questions (Q9A and Q9B). The student answers ONE. Each 14-mark question is split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 14 marks]**

> **(a)** *Derive the steady-state mass balance for dissolved oxygen in a well-mixed aerobic fermenter operated as a CSTR. Clearly show all terms, units, and assumptions. From the balance, obtain the design equation that sets the minimum $K_L a$ required to avoid oxygen limitation. State each variable.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *In a 10 m³ working-volume stirred-tank fermenter producing recombinant *E. coli*, the maximum biomass is $X_{\max} = 30$ g DCW/L and the specific oxygen uptake rate peaks at $q_{O_2,\max} = 0.4$ mg O₂/(g cell·h). The broth is aerated with air (21% O₂) and the saturation DO at operating conditions is $C^* = 7.0$ mg/L. Critical DO for this strain is $C_{\text{crit}} = 1.5$ mg/L. Calculate (i) the maximum OUR, (ii) the minimum $K_L a$ required, and (iii) the corresponding impeller speed if the correlation $K_L a = 2.6 \times 10^{-3} \, (P/V)^{0.7} u_g^{0.3}$ applies with $P/V$ in W/m³ and $u_g$ in m/s. Take $u_g = 0.005$ m/s.* **(7 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1. General mass balance (moles/mass) on dissolved O₂ in liquid of volume $V$:

$$V \frac{dC_L}{dt} = (\text{in by flow}) - (\text{out by flow}) + (\text{in by gas-liquid transfer}) - (\text{consumption})$$

Step 2. Apply CSTR assumptions: inlet DO ≈ 0 (no DO in liquid feed) and outlet DO = $C_L$ (well-mixed). Then flow in = flow out = 0 contribution to net balance.

Step 3. Substitute gas–liquid transfer and biological consumption:

$$V \frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) V - q_{O_2} X V$$

Step 4. Divide by $V$:

$$\frac{dC_L}{dt} = K_La (C^* - C_L) - q_{O_2} X$$

Step 5. At steady state, $dC_L/dt = 0$:

$$K_La (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} X \quad \text{(OTR = OUR)}$$

Step 6. Solve for $C_L$:

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} X}{K_La}$$

Step 7. To avoid oxygen limitation: $C_L \geq C_{\text{crit}}$:

$$C^* - \frac{q_{O_2,\max} X_{\max}}{K_La} \geq C_{\text{crit}}$$

Step 8. **Rearrange to obtain the design equation for $K_L a$:**

$$\boxed{K_La \geq \frac{q_{O_2,\max} \, X_{\max}}{C^* - C_{\text{crit}}}}$$

**Variable definitions (must be in your answer):**
- $K_L a$ — volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹)
- $C^*$ — saturation dissolved O₂ concentration (mg/L)
- $C_L$ — actual bulk DO (mg/L)
- $C_{\text{crit}}$ — minimum DO for unrestricted respiration (mg/L)
- $q_{O_2}$ — specific O₂ uptake rate (mg O₂/g DCW·h)
- $X$ — biomass concentration (g DCW/L)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Maximum OUR:**

$$\text{OUR}_{\max} = q_{O_2,\max} \times X_{\max} = 0.4 \, \frac{\text{mg O}_2}{\text{g·h}} \times 30 \, \frac{\text{g}}{\text{L}} = 12 \, \frac{\text{mg O}_2}{\text{L·h}}$$

**(ii) Minimum $K_La$:**

$$K_La \geq \frac{\text{OUR}_{\max}}{C^* - C_{\text{crit}}} = \frac{12 \, \text{mg/(L·h)}}{7.0 - 1.5 \, \text{mg/L}} = \frac{12}{5.5} = 2.18 \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Impeller speed calculation:**

Given correlation: $K_La = 2.6 \times 10^{-3} (P/V)^{0.7} u_g^{0.3}$

Solve for $P/V$:

$$P/V = \left(\frac{K_La}{2.6 \times 10^{-3} \, u_g^{0.3}}\right)^{1/0.7}$$

Substitute values:

$$P/V = \left(\frac{2.18}{2.6 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.005)^{0.3}}\right)^{1/0.7}$$

Compute $u_g^{0.3}$:

$$u_g^{0.3} = (0.005)^{0.3} = 0.224$$

Denominator:

$$2.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.224 = 5.82 \times 10^{-4}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{2.18}{5.82 \times 10^{-4}} = 3745.7$$

Raise to power $1/0.7 = 1.4286$:

$$P/V = (3745.7)^{1.4286} \approx 44{,}500 \, \text{W/m}^3 \quad \text{(impractical)}$$

**Engineering recommendation:** $P/V$ of 44.5 kW/m³ exceeds practical limits (typically ≤ 5 kW/m³). The student should propose **pure oxygen sparging** or **higher $u_g$** to bring $P/V$ into a feasible range.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b-iii):** When a calculated $P/V$ is unrealistic (e.g., > 10 kW/m³), **do not just stop**. State the conclusion that **either (a) pure O₂ is required, (b) the design is infeasible, or (c) cell density target must be lowered**, and recompute with the corrected assumption. Full marks only with such critical commentary.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 14 marks]**

> **(a)** *Explain the **two-film theory** and **Higbie's penetration theory** for gas–liquid mass transfer. Compare their expressions for the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ and discuss which is more realistic for a turbulent agitated fermenter.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A bubble column fermenter (no mechanical agitation, only gas sparging) is used for a yeast fermentation. The vessel has $D_T = 0.5$ m, height $H = 2$ m, gas flow $Q_g = 0.005$ m³/s, and operates at $u_g = 0.025$ m/s. The OUR is constant at 8 mg O₂/(L·h) and $C^* = 6.5$ mg/L. (i) Calculate the gas holdup $\varepsilon_g$ using $\varepsilon_g = 0.6 \, u_g^{0.7}$, (ii) calculate the minimum $K_La$ to maintain $C_L \geq 1.0$ mg/L, and (iii) discuss whether increasing $u_g$ to 0.05 m/s alone is sufficient to keep $C_L$ above critical.* **(7 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Two-film theory** (Whitman, 1923): assumes stagnant gas and liquid films of thickness $\delta_G$ and $\delta_L$ at the interface; bulk phases are perfectly mixed. Steady Fickian diffusion:

$$k_L^{\text{2-film}} = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = k_L (C^* - C_L)$$

**Higbie's penetration theory** (1935): assumes each liquid surface element is exposed to the gas for a finite contact time $t_e$, after which it is replaced by a fresh element from the bulk. The surface "penetrates" (absorbs gas) during this exposure:

$$k_L^{\text{Higbie}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_L}{\pi t_e}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_L}{\pi t_e}}(C^* - C_L)$$

**Comparison (must include a table or clear comparison in your answer):**

| Feature | Two-Film | Higbie |
|---|---|---|
| Surface behaviour | Stagnant, steady | Periodic renewal |
| $k_L \propto$ | $D_L / \delta_L$ | $\sqrt{D_L / t_e}$ |
| Predicts effect of agitation? | No | Yes (via $t_e$) |
| Realism for turbulent fermenter | Limited | Better |
| Physical meaning of $\delta_L$ | Unstirred layer | Exposure time $t_e$ |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Higbie's penetration theory is **more realistic** for a turbulent fermenter because it predicts the experimentally observed dependence of $k_L$ on **agitation speed** (higher $N_i$ reduces $t_e$, increasing $k_L$), whereas the two-film theory does not. The Danckwerts surface renewal model is a further refinement.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Gas holdup:**

$$\varepsilon_g = 0.6 \times (0.025)^{0.7} = 0.6 \times 0.0714 = 0.0428 \;\;(\text{or } 4.28\%)$$

**(ii) Minimum $K_La$:**

$$C_L \geq C_{\text{crit}} = 1.0 \text{ mg/L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_La \geq \frac{\text{OUR}}{C^* - C_{\text{crit}}}$$

$$K_La \geq \frac{8.0}{6.5 - 1.0} = \frac{8.0}{5.5} = 1.45 \;\; \text{h}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Effect of doubling $u_g$:**

Recompute $\varepsilon_g$ at $u_g = 0.05$ m/s:

$$\varepsilon_g^{\text{new}} = 0.6 \times (0.05)^{0.7} = 0.6 \times 0.1225 = 0.0735 \;\;(\text{or } 7.35\%)$$

Since for a bubble column, $K_La \propto u_g^{0.7}$ to $u_g^{1.0}$ empirically, doubling $u_g$ increases $K_La$ by approximately $2^{0.7} = 1.62\times$ (if exponent 0.7 holds) or up to $2\times$.

So:

$$K_La^{\text{new}} \approx 1.45 \times 1.62 = 2.35 \;\; \text{h}^{-1} \;\; (\text{optimistic})$$

New $C_L$:

$$C_L = C^* - \frac{\text{OUR}}{K_La^{\text{new}}} = 6.5 - \frac{8.0}{2.35} = 6.5 - 3.40 = 3.10 \;\; \text{mg/L}$$

Since 3.10 mg/L > 1.0 mg/L, **yes, doubling $u_g$ alone is sufficient** to keep $C_L$ above the critical value, **provided the exponent on $u_g$ is ≥ 0.7**. In coalescing broths the exponent can be lower, so engineers typically add a **mechanical agitator** to be safe.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b-iii):** A common error is to assume $K_La$ scales **linearly** with $u_g$ (i.e., doubling $u_g$ doubles $K_La$). The accepted exponents range from **0.3 (Van't Riet, non-coalescing)** to **0.7 (Akita-Yoshida, bubble columns)** to **1.0 (linear)**. Always **state which correlation** you are using. Marks lost for vague handwaving.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision. Print this and keep it on your desk.**

- ✅ **OTR** = $K_La (C^* - C_L)$  (units: mg/(L·h))  — the **design equation** everyone forgets the plus/minus sign on.
- ✅ **OUR** = $q_{O_2} \cdot X$  — specific rate times biomass.
- ✅ At steady state: **OTR = OUR** — must be able to draw and label this on a fermenter schematic.
- ✅ **Design equation for $K_La$:** $K_La \geq \dfrac{q_{O_2,\max} X_{\max}}{C^* - C_{\text{crit}}}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- ✅ Liquid-film resistance controls because **O₂ is sparingly soluble in water** ($C^* \approx 7$–9 mg/L).
- ✅ **Two-film** $k_L = D_L/\delta_L$ (no agitation effect); **Higbie** $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_L/(\pi t_e)}$ (agitation reduces $t_e$, raises $k_L$).
- ✅ **$K_La$ is NOT a fluid property**; it is a **lumped process parameter** that depends on $N_i$, $Q_g$, geometry, viscosity.
- ✅ **Agitation power > gas flow** for raising $K_La$ (Van't Riet exponent 0.7 vs. 0.3).
- ✅ Typical target: $C_L > 30\%$ of $C^*$, with $C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.1$–$0.5 \, C^*$.
- ✅ **Dynamic gassing-out** measurement: $\ln[(C^*-C_L)/(C^*-C_{L,0})] = -K_La \, t$ — slope gives $K_La$.
- ✅ **Sulfite oxidation** measures $K_La$ in absence of cells (chemical surrogate); assumes $C_L \approx 0$.
- ✅ **Enhancement factor $E > 1$** when microbial consumption steepens gradient at the bubble surface.
- ✅ **Damköhler number** $\text{Da} = \text{OUR}/(K_La \, C^*)$ — if Da > 1, transfer-limited; if Da < 1, kinetically limited.
- ✅ Pure O₂ sparging raises $C^*$ by **~4.8×** over air.
- ✅ **Rushton turbine** is the workhorse impeller; **marine propeller** is used for shear-sensitive cultures.
- ✅ **Power number** $N_p \approx 5$ for Rushton 6-blade in turbulent baffled tank: $P = N_p \rho N_i^3 D_i^5$.
- ✅ **Gassed power** is **less than** ungassed power due to gas pocketing behind blades — this is why the gassed/ungassed power correction matters for design.
- ✅ Bubble coalescence (suppressed by salts, increased by antifoam) **reduces $a$ and $K_La$** — has a major effect in viscous broths.
- ✅ **Common exam pitfall:** confusing $k_L$ (m/h) with $K_La$ (h⁻¹) — they differ by the interfacial area $a$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Final Tip:** When a problem gives you a target $K_La$, **always check whether it is feasible** for the proposed scale. If it requires $P/V > 10\,\text{kW/m}^3$, comment on the impracticality and propose alternatives (pure O₂, taller column, cell-density reduction). KTU examiners reward **engineering judgment**, not just arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Transport in Porous Tissues
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mass Transport in Porous Tissues

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Transport in Porous Tissues** is the quantitative study of the movement of chemical species (solutes, gases, nutrients, drugs, and metabolic waste) through the interstitial spaces of biological tissues, which are treated as **biphasic or multiphasic porous media** comprising a solid extracellular matrix (ECM), an interstitial fluid phase, and often a cellular phase.

From a continuum-mechanics perspective, a porous biological tissue is modeled as a **homogenized continuum** where the discrete microscopic architecture (collagen fibers, proteoglycan gel, capillary network, cells) is replaced by effective macroscopic transport coefficients. The transport of species $\,i\,$ is governed by the **species conservation (continuity) equation** coupled with the **Navier–Stokes/Darcy equations** for momentum conservation in the interstitial fluid.

The governing mass-balance equation in a porous tissue is:

$$\frac{\partial (\varepsilon \, c_i)}{\partial t} \;+\; \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{N}_i) \;=\; R_i$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the **tissue porosity (void fraction)**, $c_i$ is the molar concentration of species $i$ in the interstitial fluid, $\mathbf{N}_i$ is the total molar flux vector, and $R_i$ is the net volumetric rate of generation/consumption per unit total tissue volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Porosity ($\varepsilon$)** of typical biological tissues:
> - Cartilage (articular): $\varepsilon \approx 0.75 - 0.85$
> - Liver parenchyma: $\varepsilon \approx 0.15 - 0.25$
> - Brain grey matter: $\varepsilon \approx 0.20$
> - Spongy (cancellous) bone: $\varepsilon \approx 0.50 - 0.90$
> - Lung parenchyma: $\varepsilon \approx 0.70 - 0.80$

The **total molar flux** $\mathbf{N}_i$ has two principal contributions — **diffusive** and **convective**:

$$\mathbf{N}_i \;=\; \underbrace{-D_{\text{eff},i} \, \nabla c_i}_{\text{Effective Diffusion}} \;-\; \underbrace{\frac{z_i \, F \, D_{\text{eff},i}}{RT} \, c_i \, \nabla \Phi}_{\text{Migration (electrical)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mathbf{v} \, c_i}_{\text{Convection}}$$

where $D_{\text{eff},i}$ is the **effective diffusion coefficient** of species $i$ in the porous tissue, $z_i$ is the valence, $F$ is **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C mol⁻¹**, $R$ is the **universal gas constant = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**, $T$ is the absolute temperature in **K (Kelvin)**, $\Phi$ is the local electrical potential, and $\mathbf{v}$ is the **interstitial (seepage) fluid velocity** vector.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **dense, wet sponge** — the sponge rubber itself is the *solid matrix* (collagen, proteoglycans, cell walls), and the tiny water-filled holes between rubber strands are the *porous interstitial space*. When you drip colored dye onto one side of the sponge, the dye slowly *diffuses* through the twisted channels, while water being squeezed through (e.g., by pressure from your hand) carries the dye by *convection*.

Biological tissues behave in **exactly the same way**:

| Engineering System | Biological Tissue Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Catalyst pellet in reactor | Liver lobule / cell aggregate |
| Packed chromatographic column | Cartilage / intervertebral disc |
| Sand filter bed | Bone tissue / vascularized organ |
| Hydrogel wound dressing | Extracellular matrix (ECM) |

**The key insight:** because the molecules cannot travel in straight lines — they must *weave* around fibers, cells, and macromolecules — diffusion in tissue is **slower** than in free water. This slowdown is quantified by the **tortuosity factor $\tau$**, which is always $\tau \geq 1$. A typical value for soft tissues is $\tau \approx 1.3 - 2.5$.

> [!TIP]
> **Tortuosity Visualized:** If a molecule wants to travel a straight distance $L$ from point A to point B, but the porous matrix forces it to follow a winding path of actual length $L_{\text{eff}} > L$, then the **tortuosity** is defined as $\tau = (L_{\text{eff}}/L)^2$ for diffusion problems. Higher $\tau$ ⇒ slower transport.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Porous Tissue (Three-Phase Idealization)

A biological tissue is idealized as a **three-phase continuum**:

1. **Solid Phase ($s$):** ECM scaffold (collagen, elastin, proteoglycans, hydroxyapatite in bone) + cells. Volume fraction $= 1 - \varepsilon$.
2. **Liquid Phase ($\ell$):** Interstitial fluid (water + dissolved ions, plasma filtrate, lymph). Volume fraction $= \varepsilon$.
3. **Optional Gas Phase ($g$):** Found in lung alveoli, intestinal lumen, etc.

For **tissue engineering scaffolds** (e.g., PLA, collagen sponges, hydrogels), the **solid volume fraction** $\phi_s = 1 - \varepsilon$ is a critical design parameter governing both mechanical stiffness and mass-transport rate.

### 2.2 The Three Coupled Phenomena

The transport in a porous biological medium is governed by **three coupled field equations** that must be solved simultaneously:

#### (A) Mass Conservation (Species Balance)

For a single dilute solute undergoing diffusion + convection + first-order consumption (e.g., oxygen uptake by cells):

$$\varepsilon \, \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} \;=\; \nabla \cdot \big( D_{\text{eff}} \, \nabla c \big) \;-\; \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla c \;-\; k \, c$$

where $k$ (units: s⁻¹) is the **first-order volumetric reaction rate constant** (e.g., oxygen consumption by mitochondria). The factor $\varepsilon$ on the LHS arises because concentration $c$ is defined *per unit volume of interstitial fluid*, but accumulation occurs *per unit total tissue volume*.

#### (B) Momentum Conservation — Darcy's Law in Biological Tissues

For low Reynolds-number flow (creeping flow) through the tortuous interstitial space:

$$\mathbf{v} \;=\; -\frac{\kappa}{\mu} \, \nabla P$$

where:
- $\mathbf{v}$ = superficial (Darcy / seepage) velocity, m s⁻¹
- $\kappa$ = **permeability** of the tissue, m²
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of interstitial fluid, Pa·s
- $\nabla P$ = pressure gradient driving filtration, Pa m⁻¹

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical biological permeabilities:**
> - Cartilage: $\kappa \approx 10^{-15} - 10^{-16}$ m²
> - Liver: $\kappa \approx 10^{-13} - 10^{-14}$ m²
> - Brain (grey matter): $\kappa \approx 10^{-16}$ m²
> - Tumor interstitium: $\kappa \approx 10^{-14} - 10^{-13}$ m² (leakier than normal tissue due to EPR effect)

#### (C) Continuity (Incompressibility)

For an incompressible interstitial fluid in a non-deforming tissue:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} \;=\; 0$$

In a **deformable porous tissue** (e.g., swelling cartilage, growing tumor), this becomes:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} \;=\; \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial t}$$

---

### 2.3 Effective Diffusivity — The Central Transport Property

The **effective diffusion coefficient** $D_{\text{eff}}$ relates the macroscopic flux to the macroscopic concentration gradient in a porous tissue. The two most widely used constitutive relations are:

#### (i) Mackie–Meares / Maxwell Model (Solid-Matrix Obstruction Only)

For tissues where the solid phase is the dominant obstruction:

$$D_{\text{eff}} \;=\; D_0 \, \varepsilon^2 \;=\; D_0 \, \frac{\varepsilon^2}{(1-\varepsilon)^2 \cdot \tau^2}$$

where $D_0$ is the free-solution (bulk-water) diffusion coefficient.

#### (ii) Combined Porosity–Tortuosity Model (Most Common)

$$\boxed{\;D_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{D_0 \, \varepsilon}{\tau}\;}$$

This is the **canonical KTU-board-expected formula** for effective tissue diffusivity. For uncharged small molecules in soft tissue, a useful empirical approximation is:

$$D_{\text{eff}} \;\approx\; D_0 \, \varepsilon^{1.5}$$

---

### 2.4 Peclet Number — Diffusive vs. Convective Dominance

The relative importance of convection to diffusion is captured by the **Peclet number**:

$$\text{Pe} \;=\; \frac{v \, L}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$

| Regime | Condition | Physical Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diffusion-dominated | $\text{Pe} \ll 1$ | Slow flow / small pores | Drug release into static tissue |
| Mixed | $\text{Pe} \approx 1$ | Both equally important | Interstitial drug infusion (Convection-Enhanced Delivery) |
| Convection-dominated | $\text{Pe} \gg 1$ | Bulk flow sweeps solute | Liver sinusoid perfusion |

---

### 2.5 The Kedem–Katchalsky Equations (Membrane Form)

For thin porous tissue layers treated as **membranes**, the **Kedem–Katchalsky (KK) formalism** gives the volumetric flux $J_v$ and solute flux $J_s$:

$$J_v \;=\; L_p \, (\Delta P \;-\; \sigma \, \Delta \Pi)$$

$$J_s \;=\; P \, \Delta c \;+\; (1 - \sigma) \, \bar{c} \, J_v$$

where:
- $L_p$ = hydraulic conductivity, m s⁻¹ Pa⁻¹
- $\sigma$ = **Staverman reflection coefficient** ($0 \leq \sigma \leq 1$)
- $\Delta \Pi$ = osmotic pressure difference, Pa
- $P$ = solute permeability, m s⁻¹
- $\bar{c}$ = mean solute concentration

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU End-Semester Examinations.**

| # | Equation / Law | Mathematical Form | Key Parameters & Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Diffusivity | $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \varepsilon / \tau$ | $D_0$ [m²/s], $\varepsilon$ [-], $\tau$ [-] | Diffusion in porous tissue |
| 2 | Darcy's Law (tissue) | $\mathbf{v} = -(\kappa/\mu)\nabla P$ | $\kappa$ [m²], $\mu$ [Pa·s] | Interstitial flow |
| 3 | Convection–Diffusion | $\varepsilon \partial_t c = \nabla \cdot (D_{\text{eff}}\nabla c) - \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla c - kc$ | $c$ [mol/m³], $k$ [s⁻¹] | Coupled transport + reaction |
| 4 | Peclet Number | $\text{Pe} = vL / D_{\text{eff}}$ | dimensionless | Identify dominant mode |
| 5 | Damköhler Number | $\text{Da} = kL^2 / D_{\text{eff}}$ | dimensionless | Reaction vs. diffusion |
| 6 | Kedem–Katchalsky Volume flux | $J_v = L_p(\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \Pi)$ | $L_p$ [m/(s·Pa)] | Membrane (tissue slab) |
| 7 | Kedem–Katchalsky Solute flux | $J_s = P \Delta c + (1-\sigma)\bar{c} J_v$ | $P$ [m/s] | Coupled solute–solvent |
| 8 | Hydraulic Permeability (Kozeny–Carman) | $\kappa = \dfrac{\varepsilon^3}{K\, S^2\, (1-\varepsilon)^2}$ | $K \approx 5$ (K-C constant), $S$ = surface area/volume [m⁻¹] | Tissue scaffold design |
| 9 | Nernst–Planck Flux | $\mathbf{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i - \dfrac{z_i F D_i}{RT} c_i \nabla \Phi + \mathbf{v} c_i$ | $\Phi$ [V] | Charged species in tissue |
| 10 | Steady-state 1-D tissue slab | $D_{\text{eff}} d^2 c/dx^2 - v\, dc/dx - kc = 0$ | governing ODE | Oxygen/drug through slab |
| 11 | Krogh Cylinder (ext.) | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \pi L (R^2 - r_c^2) \, q_{O_2}$ | $q_{O_2}$ [mol/(m³·s)] | Capillary–tissue O₂ |
| 12 | Thiele Modulus | $\phi = L \sqrt{k / D_{\text{eff}}}$ | dimensionless | Reaction–diffusion in tissue |

---

### 2.7 Engineering & Biomedical Utility

The mass-transport framework for porous tissues is the **design backbone** of:

- **Convection-Enhanced Delivery (CED):** Direct intraparenchymal drug infusion for brain tumors (e.g., Gliadel®, Topotecan CED) — relies on $\mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla c$ term.
- **Tissue-Engineered Scaffolds:** Designing pore size, porosity, and permeability of PLA/PLGA/collagen sponges to match oxygen/nutrient diffusion limits (≈ 200 µm before cell death).
- **Transdermal Drug Patches:** Modeling skin (stratum corneum) as a porous/membrane barrier.
- **Artificial Kidney / Dialysis Membranes:** Hollow-fiber mass exchange.
- **Tumor Pharmacokinetics:** EPR effect, interstitial hypertension.
- **Wound Healing & Burn Modeling:** Growth factor diffusion through hydrogel dressings.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Diffusivity from Volume-Averaging

**Goal:** Starting from Fick's law at the pore scale, derive the macroscopic $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \varepsilon / \tau$ relation used for tissue transport.

#### Step 1 — Pore-scale Fick's Law

Within a single pore (interstitial channel), Fick's law for binary diffusion holds:

$$\mathbf{j}_A^{\text{pore}} \;=\; -D_0 \, \nabla c_A$$

where $D_0$ is the molecular diffusivity in free water at the local tissue temperature.

#### Step 2 — Volume-Averaging Operator

Define the **intrinsic phase average** of a quantity $\psi$ over the liquid phase ($\ell$):

$$\langle \psi \rangle^{\ell} \;=\; \frac{1}{V_\ell} \int_{V_\ell} \psi \, dV$$

The **superficial (tissue-averaged)** concentration is then:

$$\bar{c}_A \;=\; \varepsilon \, \langle c_A \rangle^{\ell}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Volume-Averaging Theorem (Slattery–Whitaker)

For a steady, non-reacting solute with negligible surface reaction on the solid matrix, the Whitaker averaging theorem applied to the pore-scale diffusion equation yields:

$$\nabla \cdot \langle \mathbf{j}_A \rangle \;=\; \nabla \cdot \big( -D_0 \, \langle \nabla c_A \rangle^{\ell} \big) \;+\; \text{closure terms}$$

#### Step 4 — Closure Approximation (Long-Wavelength Limit)

When the macroscopic length scale $L$ is much greater than the pore scale $\ell_p$, the deviation between pore-scale and averaged gradients is isotropic and characterized by the **tortuosity tensor** $\boldsymbol{\tau}$. For isotropic tissue:

$$\langle \nabla c_A \rangle^{\ell} \;=\; \tau \, \nabla \bar{c}_A$$

#### Step 5 — Define Macroscopic Flux

The macroscopic molar flux per unit total tissue area (which is what experiments measure) is:

$$\mathbf{N}_A^{\text{macro}} \;=\; \varepsilon \, \langle \mathbf{j}_A^{\text{pore}} \rangle \;=\; -\varepsilon \, D_0 \, \langle \nabla c_A \rangle^{\ell}$$

#### Step 6 — Combine

Substituting Step 4 into Step 5:

$$\mathbf{N}_A^{\text{macro}} \;=\; -\varepsilon \, D_0 \, \tau \, \nabla \bar{c}_A$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{N}_A^{\text{macro}} \;=\; -\underbrace{\dfrac{D_0 \, \varepsilon}{\tau}}_{D_{\text{eff}}} \, \nabla \bar{c}_A\;}$$

This rigorously justifies the **canonical effective-diffusivity relation** $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \varepsilon / \tau$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State 1-D Convection–Diffusion with Reaction in a Tissue Slab

**Physical Setup (very common in KTU problems):**

A tissue slab of thickness $L$ separates a source compartment (blood, reservoir) at $x = 0$ with concentration $c = c_s$ from a sink compartment (capillary bed, lymph) at $x = L$ with $c = 0$. Interstitial fluid flows through the slab with superficial velocity $v$ in the $+x$ direction. The solute is consumed by first-order kinetics (rate $k c$).

#### Governing ODE

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{eff}} \, \frac{d^2 c}{dx^2} \;-\; v \, \frac{dc}{dx} \;-\; k \, c \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

with boundary conditions:
$$c(0) \;=\; c_s \qquad\qquad c(L) \;=\; 0$$

#### Step 1 — Define the Thiele Modulus and a Combined Parameter

$$\phi^2 \;=\; \frac{k L^2}{D_{\text{eff}}} \qquad\qquad \lambda \;=\; \frac{v L}{2 D_{\text{eff}}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Pe}}{2}$$

The governing ODE becomes (after dividing by $D_{\text{eff}}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 c}{dx^2} \;-\; \frac{2\lambda}{L} \, \frac{dc}{dx} \;-\; \frac{\phi^2}{L^2} \, c \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Change of Variable

Let $\eta = x / L$, so $\eta \in [0, 1]$. The ODE becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 c}{d\eta^2} \;-\; 2\lambda \, \frac{dc}{d\eta} \;-\; \phi^2 \, c \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3 — Solve the Characteristic Equation

Assume $c = e^{m\eta}$. Substituting yields the auxiliary equation:

$$m^2 - 2\lambda m - \phi^2 \;=\; 0$$

Solving with the quadratic formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
m \;=\; \frac{2\lambda \pm \sqrt{4\lambda^2 + 4\phi^2}}{2} \;=\; \lambda \pm \sqrt{\lambda^2 + \phi^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Define $\gamma = \sqrt{\lambda^2 + \phi^2}$ for brevity. Then $m_1 = \lambda + \gamma > 0$ and $m_2 = \lambda - \gamma < 0$.

#### Step 4 — General Solution

$$c(\eta) \;=\; A \, e^{(\lambda + \gamma)\eta} \;+\; B \, e^{(\lambda - \gamma)\eta}$$

#### Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions

**BC 1:** $c(0) = c_s \Rightarrow A + B = c_s$

**BC 2:** $c(1) = 0 \Rightarrow A \, e^{\lambda + \gamma} + B \, e^{\lambda - \gamma} = 0$

From BC 2:

$$B \;=\; -A \, e^{(\lambda + \gamma) - (\lambda - \gamma)} \;=\; -A \, e^{2\gamma}$$

Substituting into BC 1:

$$A - A \, e^{2\gamma} \;=\; c_s \;\Rightarrow\; A \;=\; \frac{c_s}{1 - e^{2\gamma}}$$

And:

$$B \;=\; -\frac{c_s \, e^{2\gamma}}{1 - e^{2\gamma}} \;=\; \frac{c_s \, e^{2\gamma}}{e^{2\gamma} - 1}$$

#### Step 6 — Final Closed-Form Solution

$$\boxed{\;c(\eta) \;=\; \frac{c_s}{e^{2\gamma} - 1} \Big[ e^{2\gamma} \, e^{(\lambda - \gamma)\eta} \;-\; e^{(\lambda + \gamma)\eta} \Big] \;=\; c_s \, \frac{e^{(\lambda - \gamma)\eta} - e^{(\lambda + \gamma)\eta - 2\gamma}}{1 - e^{-2\gamma}}\;}$$

For the simpler **diffusion-only + reaction case** ($\lambda = 0$, so $\gamma = \phi$):

$$c(x) \;=\; c_s \, \frac{\sinh[\phi (1 - x/L)]}{\sinh(\phi)}$$

**Limiting behavior check:**
- If $k \to 0$ (no reaction, $\phi \to 0$): the hyperbolic sine ratio reduces to the linear profile $c(x) = c_s (1 - x/L)$ ✓
- If $v \to 0$ (no convection, $\lambda \to 0$): recovers pure diffusion + reaction ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Kedem–Katchalsky Solute Flux from Thermodynamic Irreversibility

The KK equations are the *Onsager reciprocal* limit of porous-membrane transport. The dissipation function per unit membrane area for a membrane separating two bulk phases is:

$$\Phi \;=\; J_v \, \Delta P \;+\; J_s \, \Delta \mu_s$$

where $\Delta \mu_s \approx (RT/c) \, \Delta c$ for dilute solutes. Onsager's linear phenomenological laws:

$$J_v \;=\; L_{11} \, \Delta P \;+\; L_{12} \, \Delta \mu_s$$

$$J_s \;=\; L_{21} \, \Delta P \;+\; L_{22} \, \Delta \mu_s$$

The **reciprocal relation** $L_{12} = L_{21}$ (Onsager) and the definition of the **reflection coefficient** $\sigma = -L_{12}/(c L_{11})$ give, after eliminating $L_{ij}$ in favor of $L_p$ and $P$:

$$\begin{aligned}
J_v \;=\; L_p \big( \Delta P - \sigma \Delta \Pi \big) \\
J_s \;=\; P \, \Delta c + (1-\sigma) \, \bar{c} \, J_v
\end{aligned}$$

with $\Delta \Pi = RT \, \Delta c$ for an ideal dilute solute. This completes the derivation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases Summary Table

| Physical Regime | Conditions | Effective Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure diffusion, no reaction | $v = 0$, $k = 0$ | $D_{\text{eff}} d^2 c/dx^2 = 0$ | Linear concentration profile |
| Diffusion + first-order reaction | $v = 0$, $k \neq 0$ | $D_{\text{eff}} d^2 c/dx^2 - kc = 0$ | Hyperbolic sine profile; cell death if $\phi > 5$ |
| Convection + diffusion, no reaction | $v \neq 0$, $k = 0$ | $D_{\text{eff}} d^2 c/dx^2 - v\, dc/dx = 0$ | Exponential approach; convective wash-out |
| Full convection–diffusion–reaction | All non-zero | Full eqn from §3.2 | Most general tissue transport |
| Reaction-limited | $\phi \ll 1$ | First-order kinetics dominate | Drug metabolized everywhere uniformly |
| Diffusion-limited | $\phi \gg 1$ | Sharp reaction front | Drug depleted near source only |
| Convection-limited | $\text{Pe} \gg 1$ | Bulk sweep | Drug reaches deep tissue (CED) |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** Oxygen diffuses through a 500 µm-thick slab of cartilage (porosity $\varepsilon = 0.80$, tortuosity $\tau = 1.6$). The free-water diffusivity of O₂ at 37 °C is $D_0 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. The O₂ is consumed by chondrocytes with first-order rate constant $k = 0.4$ s⁻¹. The slab surface concentration is $c_s = 0.20$ mol/m³ and the sink side is $c(L) = 0.02$ mol/m³. Compute (a) the effective diffusivity, (b) the Thiele modulus, (c) the minimum concentration inside the slab.

#### (a) Effective Diffusivity

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{D_0 \, \varepsilon}{\tau} \;=\; \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-9})(0.80)}{1.6} \;=\; 1.25 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (b) Thiele Modulus

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi \;=\; L \, \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{\text{eff}}}} \;=\; (500 \times 10^{-6}) \, \sqrt{\frac{0.4}{1.25 \times 10^{-9}}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\frac{0.4}{1.25 \times 10^{-9}}} \;=\; \sqrt{3.2 \times 10^{8}} \;=\; 1.789 \times 10^{4} \;\; \text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi \;=\; (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.789 \times 10^{4}) \;=\; 8.944
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\phi \gg 1$, the system is **diffusion-limited** — O₂ will be depleted well before reaching $x = L$.

#### (c) Minimum Concentration

Solving the ODE for $\lambda = 0$, $\gamma = \phi \approx 8.944$:

$$c(x) \;=\; c_s \, \frac{\sinh[\phi(1 - x/L)]}{\sinh(\phi)} \;+\; c(L) \, \frac{\sinh(\phi x/L)}{\sinh(\phi)}$$

With $c_s = 0.20$ mol/m³, $c(L) = 0.02$ mol/m³, $\phi = 8.944$, $\sinh(8.944) \approx 3908$:

At $x = L/2$, $\sinh[\phi(1 - 0.5)] = \sinh(4.472) \approx 43.7$ and $\sinh(\phi/2) = \sinh(4.472) \approx 43.7$:

$$\begin{aligned}
c(L/2) \;\approx\; (0.20)\frac{43.7}{3908} + (0.02)\frac{43.7}{3908} \;\approx\; 0.00246 \;\; \text{mol/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

This is **below the hypoxic threshold** ($\approx 0.04$ mol/m³ for chondrocytes), confirming the well-known experimental observation that chondrocytes more than 200–300 µm from a synovial fluid interface experience hypoxia — motivating the development of porous scaffolds for cartilage tissue engineering.

---

### 3.6 The Krogh Cylinder Extension for Porous Tissue

The classical **Krogh cylinder model** treats one capillary surrounded by a tissue cylinder of outer radius $R$ and length $L$. The oxygen balance for steady state with uniform consumption rate $q_{O_2}$ (mol m⁻³ s⁻¹) in the tissue ring ($r_c \leq r \leq R$) gives:

$$D_{\text{eff}} \, \frac{1}{r} \, \frac{d}{dr}\!\left( r \, \frac{dp_{O_2}}{dr} \right) \;=\; q_{O_2}$$

Integrating twice with the boundary conditions $p_{O_2}(r_c) = p_{c}$ (capillary wall partial pressure) and $d p_{O_2}/dr \big\vert_{r=R} = 0$ (no flux at the cylinder edge):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{O_2}(r) \;=\; p_c \;-\; \frac{q_{O_2}}{4 D_{\text{eff}}} \, \Big[ r^2 - r_c^2 - 2R^2 \ln(r/r_c) \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

The **critical radius** $R_{\text{crit}}$ (where $p_{O_2}$ first drops to zero) is found by setting $p_{O_2}(R_{\text{crit}}) = 0$ — this defines the **maximum inter-capillary distance** the tissue can sustain without anoxic necrosis (typically ≈ 200 µm).

For diagrams that the Mermaid engine cannot render effectively, the following block-level functional map describes the **Krogh cylinder unit cell** used to model capillary–tissue oxygen transport:

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|              TISSUE CYLINDER (Outer Radius = R)            |
|                                                            |
|        c(r,t) = oxygen concentration in tissue             |
|                                                            |
|              r_c (capillary) ----- R (outer)               |
|                |                                          |
```
