```
|        |               |                                  |
|        |  (Tissue)     |                                  |
|        |   ring with   |   q_O2 = constant consumption   |
|        |   cellular    |                                  |
|        |   uptake      |                                  |
|        |_______________|                                  |
|                                                            |
|        p_c (capillary wall O2 partial pressure)           |
|        d p_O2/dr = 0 at r = R (symmetry BC)               |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Mapping logic for the student:**

- The **capillary** is the inner source.
- The **tissue ring** is the porous medium (modeled with $D_{\text{eff}}$).
- Cells distributed in the ring consume oxygen at rate $q_{O_2}$.
- The governing ODE in cylindrical coordinates is:
$$\dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\Big(r\dfrac{dp_{O_2}}{dr}\Big) \;=\; q_{O_2}$$
- Solve with two BCs to get the radial oxygen profile; identify the **critical radius** $R_{\text{crit}}$ where $p_{O_2}$ vanishes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define **effective diffusion coefficient** for a solute in a porous biological tissue. State its dependence on tissue porosity and tortuosity.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **effective diffusion coefficient** $D_{\text{eff}}$ of a solute in a porous biological tissue is the macroscopic transport coefficient that, when used in Fick's law with the tissue-averaged concentration gradient, predicts the actual solute flux per unit total tissue area.
>
> It accounts for two effects: (i) only a fraction $\varepsilon$ (the porosity) of the tissue cross-section is available for solute transport, and (ii) the solute must follow tortuous paths around the solid matrix, characterized by the **tortuosity $\tau \geq 1$**.
>
> $$\boxed{\;D_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{D_0 \, \varepsilon}{\tau}\;}$$
>
> where $D_0$ is the free-water diffusivity of the solute at body temperature.
>
> **Marks breakdown:** Definition 1 m, role of $\varepsilon$ & $\tau$ 1 m, formula box 1 m.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
What is the **Peclet number** in the context of mass transport in porous tissues? What does $\text{Pe} \ll 1$ signify physically?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Peclet number is the dimensionless ratio of convective to diffusive transport rates:
>
> $$\text{Pe} \;=\; \dfrac{v \, L}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$
>
> where $v$ is the interstitial (Darcy) velocity, $L$ is a characteristic tissue length (e.g., slab thickness), and $D_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective tissue diffusivity.
>
> **$\text{Pe} \ll 1$ signifies a diffusion-dominated regime** — molecular diffusion through the interstitial fluid is much faster than bulk convective transport. Examples include passive drug release into static tissue and oxygen transport in cartilage under no-load conditions.
>
> **Marks breakdown:** Formula 1 m, physical meaning of ratio 1 m, example 1 m.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper, Module 4, Choice-1]*

**(a)** Derive the steady-state governing equation for one-dimensional mass transport of a solute through a porous tissue slab of thickness $L$, incorporating (i) effective diffusion, (ii) first-order homogeneous consumption, and (iii) bulk interstitial convection. Clearly state the boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the special case of diffusion only (no convection) with no reaction ($v = 0$, $k = 0$), determine the concentration profile, the molar flux at the slab mid-plane, and the total mass leaving the slab per unit area per unit time. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

Apply a differential mass balance on a thin slice of the porous slab between $x$ and $x + dx$. The slice has unit cross-sectional area (per unit total tissue area).

*Rate of accumulation* $= \varepsilon \, dx \, \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial t}$

*Net diffusive inflow* $= \big[N_{A,\text{diff}}(x) - N_{A,\text{diff}}(x+dx)\big] = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\big(D_{\text{eff}} \, \partial c/\partial x\big)\, dx$

*Net convective inflow* $= -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}(v c) \, dx$

*Consumption* $= -k \, c \, dx$

Setting accumulation = inflow + generation (with consumption as negative generation) and dividing by $dx$:

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon \, \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial t} \;=\; \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left( D_{\text{eff}} \, \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial x} \right) \;-\; v \, \dfrac{\partial c}{\partial x} \;-\; k \, c\;}$$

For a homogeneous tissue ($D_{\text{eff}}$, $v$, $k$ constant), the steady-state 1-D form is:

$$D_{\text{eff}} \, \dfrac{d^2 c}{dx^2} \;-\; v \, \dfrac{dc}{dx} \;-\; k \, c \;=\; 0$$

Boundary conditions (Dirichlet at both faces):
$$c(0) \;=\; c_s \qquad\qquad c(L) \;=\; c_L$$

**(7 Marks breakdown:** Differential mass balance 2 m, full PDE 2 m, steady-state ODE 1 m, BCs 1 m, boxed final equation 1 m.)

---

**Part (b) — Diffusion-only limit (7 Marks)**

Set $v = 0$ and $k = 0$ in the governing ODE:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{eff}} \, \dfrac{d^2 c}{dx^2} \;=\; 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \dfrac{d^2 c}{dx^2} \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

Integrate twice:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dc}{dx} \;=\; C_1 \qquad\qquad c(x) \;=\; C_1 x + C_2
\end{aligned}$$

Apply BCs: $c(0) = c_s \Rightarrow C_2 = c_s$ and $c(L) = c_L \Rightarrow C_1 = (c_L - c_s)/L$.

$$\boxed{\;c(x) \;=\; c_s \;+\; (c_L - c_s) \, \dfrac{x}{L}\;}$$

This is the **linear Fickian profile**, valid when the tissue is inert and there is no interstitial flow.

The molar flux (Fick's law with tissue-level diffusion):

$$N_A(x) \;=\; -D_{\text{eff}} \, \dfrac{dc}{dx} \;=\; -D_{\text{eff}} \cdot \dfrac{c_L - c_s}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}(c_s - c_L)}{L}$$

The flux is **independent of $x$** (as required for steady, non-reacting transport). At the slab mid-plane $x = L/2$, the flux is identical:

$$\boxed{\;N_A(L/2) \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}(c_s - c_L)}{L}\;}$$

Total mass leaving per unit area per unit time (outflow at $x = L$):

$$\begin{aligned}
J \;=\; N_A(L) \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{eff}}(c_s - c_L)}{L} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^2\,\text{s}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 Marks breakdown:** Governing ODE 1 m, double integration 2 m, apply BCs 1 m, boxed profile 1 m, flux evaluation 2 m.)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper, Module 4, Choice-2]*

**(a)** State and explain **Kedem–Katchalsky equations** for transport through a porous tissue membrane. Define all terms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A drug of molecular weight 250 g/mol diffuses across a 1.5 mm-thick porous tissue layer (porosity $\varepsilon = 0.70$, tortuosity $\tau = 1.5$, free-water diffusivity $D_0 = 5.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s). The upstream concentration is $c_1 = 10$ mol/m³ and downstream is $c_2 = 2$ mol/m³. Compute (i) the effective diffusivity, (ii) the steady-state diffusive flux per unit tissue area, (iii) the total mass transported in 6 hours across 1 cm² of tissue. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Kedem–Katchalsky Equations (7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> The Kedem–Katchalsky (KK) equations are the **irreversible-thermodynamics** description of coupled solvent (water) and solute transport across a porous biological membrane — derived from Onsager's linear phenomenological laws with the reciprocal relation $L_{12} = L_{21}$.

The two KK equations are:

**(i) Volume flux (solvent transport):**

$$J_v \;=\; L_p \, \big( \Delta P \;-\; \sigma \, \Delta \Pi \big)$$

**(ii) Solute flux:**

$$J_s \;=\; P \, \Delta c \;+\; (1 - \sigma) \, \bar{c} \, J_v$$

where:
- $J_v$ = volumetric flux of solvent across membrane [m³ / (m²·s)] = m/s
- $J_s$ = molar flux of solute across membrane [mol / (m²·s)]
- $L_p$ = **hydraulic conductivity** of the membrane [m / (s·Pa)]
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ = transmembrane hydrostatic pressure difference [Pa]
- $\Delta \Pi = RT(c_1 - c2)$ = osmotic pressure difference for dilute ideal solute [Pa]
- $\sigma$ = **Staverman reflection coefficient**, $0 \leq \sigma \leq 1$ (0 = membrane ignores solute; 1 = perfect semipermeable)
- $P$ = **solute permeability** of the membrane [m/s]
- $\Delta c = c_1 - c_2$ = concentration difference [mol/m³]
- $\bar{c} = (c_1 + c_2)/2$ = mean concentration [mol/m³]

**Physical meaning:** the solvent flow $J_v$ is driven by the *net* pressure (hydrostatic minus osmotic, weighted by $\sigma$). The solute flow $J_s$ has two parts: a pure diffusive/convective permeability term $P \Delta c$ and a **solvent-drag** term $(1-\sigma)\bar{c} J_v$ that couples solute to solvent flow.

**(7 Marks breakdown:** Statement of both eqns 2 m, definition of all terms 4 m, physical interpretation 1 m.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**(i) Effective diffusivity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{D_0 \, \varepsilon}{\tau} \;=\; \dfrac{(5.5 \times 10^{-10})(0.70)}{1.5} \;=\; 2.567 \times 10^{-10} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Steady-state diffusive flux:**

$$\begin{aligned}
J_s \;=\; D_{\text{eff}} \, \dfrac{c_1 - c_2}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{(2.567 \times 10^{-10})(10 - 2)}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
J_s \;=\; \dfrac{2.054 \times 10^{-9}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.369 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \dfrac{\text{mol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Total mass transported in 6 hours across 1 cm²:**

Convert area: $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
Convert time: $t = 6 \text{ h} = 21600$ s
Molar mass: $M = 250$ g/mol

Total moles transported:

$$\begin{aligned}
n \;=\; J_s \, A \, t \;=\; (1.369 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^{-4})(21600) \;=\; 2.957 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

Total mass transported:

$$\begin{aligned}
m \;=\; n \, M \;=\; (2.957 \times 10^{-6})(250) \;=\; 7.39 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{g} \;=\; 0.739 \;\; \text{mg}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;m \;\approx\; 0.74 \;\; \text{mg in 6 h}\;}$$

**(7 Marks breakdown:** (i) 2 m, (ii) 3 m, (iii) 2 m.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the porosity–tortuosity correction.** Many students plug in $D_0$ directly into Fick's law instead of $D_{\text{eff}}$. This causes a systematic **over-estimation** of flux by a factor of 2–3×. Marks lost: 1–2 per part.
> 2. **Sign convention errors in Kedem–Katchalsky.** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ where 1 = upstream (high pressure). Students often write $\Delta P$ with the wrong sign convention for osmotic pressure.
> 3. **Confusing Darcy velocity with interstitial (pore) velocity.** The **superficial velocity** $v = Q/A_{\text{total}}$ is smaller than the **average pore velocity** $v_{\text{pore}} = v/\varepsilon$ by the factor of porosity. Be explicit which one your equation uses.
> 4. **Dropping the $\varepsilon$ on the LHS of the continuity equation.** The accumulation term is $\varepsilon \, \partial c/\partial t$, not $\partial c/\partial t$, because $c$ is per volume of fluid but accumulation is per total tissue volume.
> 5. **Mixing units of $D$ (cm²/s) and $L$ (mm).** Always convert to SI (m²/s and m) before computing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Effective diffusivity formula** — master $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \, \varepsilon / \tau$ and know typical tissue values.
- ✅ **Darcy's Law in tissue** — $\mathbf{v} = -(\kappa/\mu)\nabla P$; remember that biological permeabilities span $10^{-16}$ to $10^{-13}$ m².
- ✅ **Convection–diffusion–reaction equation** — full 3-term PDE; recognize all three dimensionless groups: Pe, Da, and $\phi$.
- ✅ **Kedem–Katchalsky equations** — both flux laws; define $L_p$, $\sigma$, $P$; know the **reflection coefficient** meaning.
- ✅ **Thiele modulus** $\phi = L\sqrt{k/D_{\text{eff}}}$ — high $\phi$ means diffusion-limited, sharp reaction front.
- ✅ **Peclet number** $\text{Pe} = vL/D_{\text{eff}}$ — distinguishes diffusion-dominated vs. convection-dominated regimes.
- ✅ **Krogh cylinder extension** — radial diffusion–consumption; find $R_{\text{crit}}$ (≈ 200 µm in vivo).
- ✅ **Tortuosity** $\tau = (L_{\text{eff}}/L)^2 \geq 1$ — captures the winding-path slowdown in porous tissue.
- ✅ **Porosity of tissues** — bone ~0.5–0.9, liver ~0.2, brain ~0.2, cartilage ~0.8; commit typical ranges to memory.
- ✅ **Volume averaging** — the rigorous way to obtain $D_{\text{eff}}$ from pore-scale physics (Whitaker).
- ✅ **Engineering applications** — CED, tissue scaffolds, transdermal patches, dialysis, tumor pharmacokinetics.
- ✅ **Units discipline** — always SI; check $D$ in m²/s, $L$ in m, $c$ in mol/m³, $v$ in m/s.
- ✅ **Limiting cases** — when $v = 0$, $k = 0$: linear profile. When $\phi \to 0$: flat profile. When $\phi \to \infty$: exponential decay.
- ✅ **Examination strategy** — define every symbol, box the final equation, show every algebraic step; examiners award process marks generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport of Gases Between Blood and Tissues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transport of Gases Between Blood and Tissues # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Transport of gases between blood and tissues** is a steady-state, passive mass transfer process governed by molecular diffusion, in which respiratory gases — primarily **oxygen (O₂)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — move across a series of biological barriers driven by **partial pressure (concentration) gradients**. The pathway consists of four sequential resistive layers: 1. The **erythrocyte (RBC) interior** and its membrane, 2. The **plasma** layer within the capillary, 3. The **capillary endothelium and basement membrane**, 4. The **interstitial fluid (ISF)** and the **tissue cell membrane** into the cell cytoplasm/mitochondria. The governing principle is **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**, applied in series across each layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transport is a *dissolved-phase* transport. Gases do NOT travel as free gas bubbles in healthy tissue — they are carried in **molecularly dissolved form** according to **Henry's Law**. The chemical binding to hemoglobin (O₂–Hb) and the bicarbonate buffer (CO₂–HCO₃⁻) are treated as equilibrium boundary conditions at the RBC interface. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Crowded Bus Stop":** > Imagine people (gas molecules) wanting to leave a bus (capillary carrying blood) and walk into a shopping mall (tissue). The bus has a narrow door (capillary wall) — this is one **resistance**. They then have to walk across a wide open plaza (interstitial fluid) — another **resistance**. Finally, they must pass through a turnstile at the mall entrance (cell membrane) — yet another **resistance**. Each "obstacle" slows them down. The total slowness is the **sum of all the individual slow-downs (resistances in series)**. The bigger the difference between how crowded the bus is (high gas concentration/pressure in blood) and how empty the mall is (low gas concentration/pressure in tissue), the faster the people move. This pressure difference is the **driving force**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Diffusivity of O₂ in tissue | $D_{O_2}$ | **2.0 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s** | | Diffusivity of CO₂ in tissue | $D_{CO_2}$ | **2.0 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s** | | Solubility of O₂ in water/tissue (α) | $\alpha_{O_2}$ | **1.3 × 10⁻⁶ mol/(L·mmHg)** | | Solubility of CO₂ in water/tissue (α) | $\alpha_{CO_2}$ | **3.3 × 10⁻⁵ mol/(L·mmHg)** | | Krogh diffusion coefficient (O₂) | $K_{O_2} = D_{O_2} \cdot \alpha_{O_2}$ | **≈ 1.7 × 10⁻⁵ mL O₂/(cm·s·mmHg)** | | Krogh diffusion coefficient (CO₂) | $K_{CO_2} = D_{CO_2} \cdot \alpha_{CO_2}$ | **≈ 1.7 × 10⁻⁴ mL CO₂/(cm·s·mmHg)** | | Arterial PO₂ | $P_aO_2$ | **≈ 95 mmHg** | | Venous PO₂ | $P_vO_2$ | **≈ 40 mmHg** | | Arterial PCO₂ | $P_aCO_2$ | **≈ 40 mmHg** | | Venous PCO₂ | $P_vCO_2$ | **≈ 46 mmHg** | | Tissue PO₂ (mitochondrial) | $P_{t}O_2$ | **≈ 5–20 mmHg** | | Typical capillary diameter | $d_c$ | **5–10 µm** | | Typical intercapillary distance | $L$ | **20–100 µm** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework ### Step 1 — The Driving Force - Gases always diffuse **DOWN** their **partial pressure (concentration) gradient** — from high to low. - For **O₂**: Blood $P_{O_2}$ (~95 mmHg arterial, 40 mmHg venous) → Tissue $P_{O_2}$ (~5–20 mmHg). Net direction: **Blood → Tissue**. - For **CO₂**: Tissue $P_{CO_2}$ (~46–50 mmHg) → Blood $P_{CO_2}$ (~40 mmHg arterial). Net direction: **Tissue → Blood**. ### Step 2 — Henry's Law (Linking Pressure to Dissolved Concentration) - A gas dissolved in a liquid has concentration proportional to its partial pressure above the liquid. - $C = \alpha \cdot P$ - $C$ = dissolved concentration [mol/L], $P$ = partial pressure [mmHg], $\alpha$ = solubility coefficient [(mol/L)/mmHg]. - **CO₂ is ~24× more soluble in water/plasma than O₂** — this is why CO₂ transport is highly efficient even with a small pressure gradient. ### Step 3 — Fick's First Law of Diffusion - The molar flux $J$ [mol/(cm²·s)] of gas through a thin layer is: - $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ - $D$ = molecular diffusivity [cm²/s]. ### Step 4 — Combining Fick's + Henry's → Krogh's Law - Substituting $C = \alpha P$: - $J = -D \cdot \alpha \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx} = -K \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ - $K = D \cdot \alpha$ is the **Krogh Diffusion Coefficient** [mL gas/(cm·s·mmHg)] — a single lumped parameter that captures both diffusivity and solubility. ### Step 5 — Resistances in Series (Capillary → Tissue Pathway) - The total resistance to gas transfer is the **sum** of individual layer resistances, analogous to electrical resistors in series: - $R_{total} = R_{cap\,wall} + R_{interstitial} + R_{cell\,membrane} + R_{intracellular}$ - For a flat slab of thickness $L$ of tissue: $R_{tissue} = \dfrac{L}{K}$. - The **net flux** = $\dfrac{\Delta P}{R_{total}}$. ### Step 6 — The Krogh Cylinder Model (Geometric Idealization) - Each capillary supplies a cylindrical volume of tissue around it. - The cylinder has radius $r_t$ (tissue radius) and the capillary has radius $r_c$. - $\dfrac{r_t}{r_c}$ is the **supply ratio** (typically 5–10). - Tissue O₂ consumption rate $q_{O_2}$ [mL O₂/(mL tissue·s)] is assumed uniform. - Solving the cylindrical diffusion equation yields the **critical radius** beyond which the tissue becomes anoxic. ### Step 7 — Why the Series Resistance Model is Biologically Important - Different tissues (muscle, brain, fat) have different barrier thicknesses and different $K$ values. - The brain is exquisitely sensitive to O₂ supply failure (stroke, ischemia) because neurons cannot tolerate low ATP. - Skeletal muscle has high tolerance because of myoglobin O₂ storage and lower metabolic rate. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = \alpha P$ | Henry's Law (dissolved concentration) | mol/L | | 2 | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law | mol/(cm²·s) | | 3 | $J = -K \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | Krogh's Law (lumped) | mL/(cm²·s) | | 4 | $K = D \alpha$ | Krogh diffusion coefficient | mL/(cm·s·mmHg) | | 5 | $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{K_i}$ | Series resistance | (cm·s·mmHg)/mL | | 6 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_{total}}$ | Net driving-force/Resistance form | mL/(cm²·s) | | 7 | $r_{c,\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 K (P_{cap} - P_{min})}{q_{O_2}}}$ | Krogh cylinder critical radius | cm | | 8 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = q_{O_2} \cdot V_{tissue}$ | O₂ consumption rate | mL O₂/s | > [!NOTE] > **In all KTU 2024 numerical problems**, students must: > 1. Always quote the **driving force** ($\Delta P$) explicitly first. > 2. Always state the **Krogh coefficient** for the gas in question. > 3. Always include **units at every step** — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Artificial Lung / ECMO Design**: Engineers designing membrane oxygenators use the *exact same* Fick + Henry framework to choose hollow-fiber polymer membranes, gas-permeability, and surface area. - **Tissue Engineering Scaffolds**: Designing oxygen-permeable bioreactor scaffolds for cell culture uses the Krogh cylinder model to avoid necrotic cores in 3D-printed tissue constructs. - **Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy**: Predicting how deep O₂ can penetrate ischemic tissue uses the same diffusion equations with elevated $P_{O_2}$ as the driving force. - **CO₂ Removal in Diving/Submarines**: Closed-circuit rebreathers use Henry + Fick to scrub CO₂ from exhaled gas. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Fick's Law to Krogh's Law We start with Fick's First Law applied across a thin tissue slab of thickness $L$: $$ J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx} $$ **Step 1 — Apply Henry's Law substitution** ($C = \alpha P$) using the chain rule: $$ \frac{dC}{dx} = \alpha \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} $$ *Reason*: $\alpha$ is constant at a fixed temperature (Henry's constant is temperature-dependent only, not position-dependent). So differentiating $C = \alpha P$ w.r.t. $x$ gives the product $\alpha \cdot dP/dx$. **Step 2 — Substitute back into Fick's Law:** $$ J = -D \cdot \alpha \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} $$ **Step 3 — Define the Krogh diffusion coefficient** $K \equiv D \alpha$: $$ J = -K \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate across a slab of thickness $L$** with constant $J$ and $K$ (steady state, homogeneous tissue): $$ \int_0^L J\,dx = -\int_{P_{cap}}^{P_{tissue}} K\,dP $$ $$ J \cdot L = -K \cdot (P_{tissue} - P_{cap}) = K \cdot (P_{cap} - P_{tissue}) $$ **Step 5 — Solve for flux $J$:** $$ J = \frac{K \cdot (P_{cap} - P_{tissue})}{L} $$ *Engineering interpretation*: The molar flux of O₂ through tissue is directly proportional to the **driving pressure difference** and inversely proportional to the **tissue thickness**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resistances in Series Consider three layers between capillary blood and the mitochondrion: - Capillary wall: thickness $\delta_w$, Krogh coefficient $K_w$ - Interstitial fluid: thickness $\delta_i$, Krogh coefficient $K_i$ - Cell membrane + cytoplasm: thickness $\delta_c$, Krogh coefficient $K_c$ **Step 1 — Write the steady-state flux in each layer** (all equal because mass conservation): $$ J = \frac{K_w (P_b - P_w)}{\delta_w} = \frac{K_i (P_w - P_i)}{\delta_i} = \frac{K_c (P_i - P_{mit})}{\delta_c} $$ **Step 2 — Define individual resistances** $R_j = \dfrac{\delta_j}{K_j}$: $$ J \cdot R_w = P_b - P_w \quad ; \quad J \cdot R_i = P_w - P_i \quad ; \quad J \cdot R_c = P_i - P_{mit} $$ **Step 3 — Add all three pressure drops** (telescoping sum): $$ J \cdot (R_w + R_i + R_c) = (P_b - P_w) + (P_w - P_i) + (P_i - P_{mit}) = P_b - P_{mit} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for net flux:** $$ J = \frac{P_b - P_{mit}}{R_w + R_i + R_c} = \frac{\Delta P_{total}}{R_{total}} $$ *Engineering interpretation*: **Total resistance is the sum of individual resistances in series** — exactly like electrical resistors. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Krogh Cylinder Critical Radius Consider a single capillary of radius $r_c$ supplying a tissue cylinder of outer radius $r_t$ (Krogh, 1919). The tissue consumes O₂ at volumetric rate $q_{O_2}$ [mL O₂/(mL tissue·s)], assumed uniform. **Step 1 — Set up cylindrical mass balance** at radius $r$ from capillary axis: For steady state, the flux entering the cylindrical shell of radius $r$ equals flux leaving at $r + dr$ plus the consumption in the shell of volume $2\pi r L\,dr$: $$ \frac{d}{dr}(2\pi r L \cdot J) - 2\pi r L \cdot q_{O_2} = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Use Krogh's Law for the radial flux:** $$ J = K \frac{dP}{dr} $$ Substituting: $$ \frac{d}{dr}\left(2\pi r L K \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = 2\pi r L q_{O_2} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify** (K and L constant): $$ \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = \frac{q_{O_2}}{K} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate once** w.r.t. $r$: $$ r \frac{dP}{dr} = \frac{q_{O_2} r^2}{2K} + C_1 $$ $$ \frac{dP}{dr} = \frac{q_{O_2} r}{2K} + \frac{C_1}{r} $$ **Step 5 — Apply boundary condition 1: finite $dP/dr$ at $r = 0$** (axis of symmetry) → $C_1 = 0$. **Step 6 — Integrate again:** $$ P(r) = \frac{q_{O_2} r^2}{4K} + C_2 $$ **Step 7 — Apply boundary condition 2: at the capillary wall** ($r = r_c$), $P = P_{cap}$: $$ C_2 = P_{cap} - \frac{q_{O_2} r_c^2}{4K} $$ **Step 8 — Final $P(r)$ profile:** $$ P(r) = P_{cap} - \frac{q_{O_2}}{4K}\left(r_c^2 - r^2\right) $$ **Step 9 — Apply boundary condition 3: at the tissue-cylinder edge** ($r = r_t$), $P = P_{min}$ (anoxic threshold, e.g., 1 mmHg): $$ P_{min} = P_{cap} - \frac{q_{O_2}}{4K}(r_t^2 - r_c^2) $$ **Step 10 — Solve for the maximum tolerable supply radius** $r_t$: $$ r_t^2 = r_c^2 + \frac{4K(P_{cap} - P_{min})}{q_{O_2}} $$ **Step 11 — Approximation** ($r_t \gg r_c$, so $r_c^2$ is negligible): $$ r_{t,\max} \approx \sqrt{\frac{4K(P_{cap} - P_{min})}{q_{O_2}}} $$ *Engineering interpretation*: This is the **maximum distance** a tissue cell can be from a capillary before it becomes anoxic. Active muscle has high $q_{O_2}$, so $r_{t,\max}$ is small (dense capillary network). Cartilage has very low $q_{O_2}$, so capillaries are sparse. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — O₂ vs. CO₂ Transport | Property | O₂ Transport | CO₂ Transport | Ratio (CO₂/O₂) | |---|---|---|---| | Diffusivity $D$ [cm²/s] | 2.0 × 10⁻⁵ | 2.0 × 10⁻⁵ | **1** | | Solubility $\alpha$ | 1.3 × 10⁻⁶ mol/(L·mmHg) | 3.3 × 10⁻⁵ mol/(L·mmHg) | **~25** | | Krogh coefficient $K$ | 2.6 × 10⁻¹¹ | 6.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ | **~25** | | Blood–tissue $\Delta P$ | ~55 mmHg | ~6 mmHg | **~0.1** | | Net transfer rate per area | Moderate | Fast (high $K$ × $\Delta P$) | **CO₂ ~20× faster** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "CO₂ is faster than O₂" result is fundamental** — even with a 10× smaller pressure gradient, CO₂ crosses tissue 20× faster because of its ~25× greater solubility. This is why CO₂ retention (hypercapnia) is rare in healthy lungs but O₂ diffusion limitation (hypoxia) is common in disease (pulmonary fibrosis, pneumonia). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Blood–Tissue Gas Transport ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[ARTERIAL BLOOD
PO2 = 95 mmHg
PCO2 = 40 mmHg] --> B1[CAPILLARY LUMEN
RBC membrane + Plasma] B1 --> B2[CAPILLARY WALL
Endothelium + Basement membrane
Resistance Rw] B2 --> B3[INTERSTITIAL FLUID
ISF space
Resistance Ri] B3 --> B4[CELL MEMBRANE
Lipid bilayer
Resistance Rcm] B4 --> B5[CELL CYTOPLASM
Intracellular diffusion
Resistance Rcy] B5 --> B6[MITOCHONDRION
PO2 = 5 to 20 mmHg
PCO2 = 46 to 50 mmHg] A2[VENOUS BLOOD LEAVES
PO2 = 40 mmHg
PCO2 = 46 mmHg] <- B1 B1 --> A2 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Driving Force and Resistance Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DRV[DRIVING FORCE] D1[Delta P Blood minus Tissue] end subgraph RES[SERIES RESISTANCES] R1[R_capillary_wall] R2[R_interstitial] R3[R_cell_membrane] R4[R_cytoplasm] end subgraph OUT[NET FLUX] F1[J = Delta P / R_total] end D1 --> F1 R1 --> F1 R2 --> F1 R3 --> F1 R4 --> F1 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Krogh Cylinder Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TD C1[CENTRAL CAPILLARY
Radius rc
Pcap = 40 to 95 mmHg] --> C2[TISSUE CYLINDER
Outer radius rt
Tissue PO2 falls radially] C2 --> C3[CYTOCHROME OXIDASE
Mitochondrial O2 sink
PO2 = 1 to 5 mmHg] C2 -. Critical anoxic radius .-> C4[ANOXIC THRESHOLD
Pmin = 1 mmHg
Necrosis beyond this radius] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Partial Pressure Profile from Capillary to Mitochondrion ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Arterial Blood
PO2 95] --> P2[Capillary Wall
PO2 90] P2 --> P3[Interstitial
PO2 60] P3 --> P4[Cell Membrane
PO2 40] P4 --> P5[Cell Cytoplasm
PO2 25] P5 --> P6[Mitochondrion
PO2 5] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define the Krogh diffusion coefficient. Why is it used in biological transport analysis instead of using diffusivity and solubility separately?** **Model Answer:** The **Krogh diffusion coefficient** is defined as the product of the molecular diffusivity $D$ [cm²/s] and the solubility coefficient $\alpha$ [(mol/L)/mmHg] of a gas in a given biological medium: $$ K = D \cdot \alpha $$ It has units of [mL gas / (cm·s·mmHg)]. **Why it is used:** 1. When Fick's First Law ($J = -D\,dC/dx$) is combined with Henry's Law ($C = \alpha P$), the result is $J = -K\,dP/dx$ — a single parameter $K$ captures the combined effect of **how fast** the gas diffuses AND **how much** dissolves. 2. This simplifies flux calculations by removing the need to track two independent physical properties. 3. It is the experimentally measured lumped parameter in tissue-physiology studies, making it directly useful in clinical engineering design (e.g., membrane oxygenators). --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: List the four sequential resistances encountered by O₂ as it diffuses from capillary blood to the mitochondrion. State which resistance is normally the rate-limiting one in healthy tissue.** **Model Answer:** The four sequential resistances are: 1. **$R_{cap\,wall}$** — Resistance of the capillary endothelium and its basement membrane. 2. **$R_{interstitial}$** — Resistance of the interstitial fluid (ISF) layer. 3. **$R_{cell\,membrane}$** — Resistance of the parenchymal cell plasma membrane. 4. **$R_{cytoplasm}$** — Resistance of intracellular diffusion through the cytoplasm to the mitochondrion. **Rate-limiting resistance (healthy tissue):** The **interstitial fluid resistance $R_{interstitial}$** is typically the largest and therefore the rate-limiting resistance, because the ISF has a low effective solubility for O₂ and a relatively long diffusion distance (10–50 µm). The capillary wall and cell membrane are thin and offer low resistance in healthy tissue. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) Derive an expression for the steady-state flux of O₂ through a homogeneous tissue slab of thickness $L$ from a capillary at partial pressure $P_{cap}$ to a tissue at partial pressure $P_{tiss}$. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state (flux constant in time). 2. One-dimensional diffusion (x-direction only). 3. Homogeneous tissue (constant $D$, $\alpha$). 4. No chemical reaction in the slab (only physical transport). 5. Constant temperature (so $\alpha$ and $D$ are constant). **Derivation:** Start with Fick's First Law: $$ J = -D\,\frac{dC}{dx} $$ Apply Henry's Law $C = \alpha P$: $$ \frac{dC}{dx} = \alpha\,\frac{dP}{dx} $$ Substituting: $$ J = -D\,\alpha\,\frac{dP}{dx} = -K\,\frac{dP}{dx} $$ Rearranging: $$ J\,dx = -K\,dP $$ Integrate from $x = 0$ (capillary, $P = P_{cap}$) to $x = L$ (tissue, $P = P_{tiss}$): $$ \int_0^L J\,dx = -K\int_{P_{cap}}^{P_{tiss}} dP $$ $$ J \cdot L = -K(P_{tiss} - P_{cap}) = K(P_{cap} - P_{tiss}) $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{J = \frac{K(P_{cap} - P_{tiss})}{L}} $$ **Numerical example (KTU-style):** Given $K_{O_2} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ mL O₂/(cm·s·mmHg), $P_{cap} = 95$ mmHg, $P_{tiss} = 20$ mmHg, $L = 50$ µm = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ cm: $$ J = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-5} \times (95 - 20)}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 75}{5 \times 10^{-3}} $$ $$ J = 2.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mL O₂ / (cm²·s)} $$ --- **(b) Using the resistance-in-series analogy, show that the total mass-transfer resistance from blood to mitochondrion is the sum of individual layer resistances. Compute the net O₂ flux for a tissue having three layers with the following data: (7 Marks)** | Layer | Thickness $\delta$ [µm] | Krogh coefficient $K$ [mL O₂/(cm·s·mmHg)] | |---|---|---| | Capillary wall | 0.5 | $2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ | | Interstitial fluid | 30 | $1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ | | Cell cytoplasm | 10 | $1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ | **Blood $P_{O_2} = 90$ mmHg, Mitochondrial $P_{O_2} = 5$ mmHg.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert thicknesses to cm:** - $\delta_1 = 0.5\,\mu m = 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm - $\delta_2 = 30\,\mu m = 30 \times 10^{-4} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ cm - $\delta_3 = 10\,\mu m = 10 \times 10^{-4} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ cm **Step 2 — Compute individual resistances** $R_i = \delta_i / K_i$: $$ R_1 = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.5 \text{ (cm·s·mmHg)/mL} $$ $$ R_2 = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 200 \text{ (cm·s·mmHg)/mL} $$ $$ R_3 = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.7 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 58.82 \text{ (cm·s·mmHg)/mL} $$ **Step 3 — Sum the resistances:** $$ R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 2.5 + 200 + 58.82 = 261.32 \text{ (cm·s·mmHg)/mL} $$ **Step 4 — Net driving force:** $$ \Delta P = 90 - 5 = 85 \text{ mmHg} $$ **Step 5 — Net flux:** $$ J = \frac{\Delta P}{R_{total}} = \frac{85}{261.32} $$ $$ \boxed{J \approx 0.325 \text{ mL O₂ / (cm²·s)}} $$ **Observation for KTU exam:** Note that $R_2$ (interstitial) dominates — ~76% of total resistance. This is the rate-limiting step. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) Explain the Krogh cylinder model for capillary–tissue O₂ transfer. Derive the expression for the partial pressure distribution $P(r)$ in the tissue cylinder. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Explanation:** The Krogh cylinder model (August Krogh, 1919) idealizes the capillary network as a set of identical, parallel, non-branching cylinders, each surrounded by a concentric tissue cylinder that the capillary exclusively supplies. It assumes: - Uniform O₂ consumption rate $q_{O_2}$ per unit tissue volume. - Steady-state radial diffusion only. - Cylindrical symmetry. - No axial variation along the capillary (or treats it as a small element). **Derivation of $P(r)$:** **Step 1 — Mass balance on a cylindrical shell** of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, length $L$: O₂ entering at $r$ – O₂ leaving at $r + dr$ = O₂ consumed in shell: $$ 2\pi r L J(r) - 2\pi(r+dr) L J(r+dr) = 2\pi r L\,dr \cdot q_{O_2} $$ **Step 2 — Take the limit $dr \to 0$:** $$ -\frac{d}{dr}(2\pi r L J) = 2\pi r L q_{O_2} $$ **Step 3 — Use Krogh's Law** $J = -K\,dP/dr$ (note: in radial coordinates the sign convention is $J = -K\,dP/dr$ for inward flux, and we work with magnitudes): $$ -\frac{d}{dr}\left(2\pi r L K \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = 2\pi r L q_{O_2} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel $2\pi L$:** $$ -\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = \frac{q_{O_2}}{K} $$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $-1$ and integrate** w.r.t. $r$: $$ \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dP}{dr}\right) = -\frac{q_{O_2} r}{K} $$ $$ r \frac{dP}{dr} = -\frac{q_{O_2} r^2}{2K} + A $$ **Step 6 — Boundary condition:** At $r = 0$ (axis), $dP/dr$ must be finite, hence $A = 0$. $$ \frac{dP}{dr} = -\frac{q_{O_2} r}{2K} $$ **Step 7 — Integrate again:** $$ P(r) = -\frac{q_{O_2} r^2}{4K} + B $$ **Step 8 — Apply boundary condition** at $r = r_c$ (capillary wall), $P = P_{cap}$: $$ B = P_{cap} + \frac{q_{O_2} r_c^2}{4K} $$ **Step 9 — Final radial pressure profile:** $$ \boxed{P(r) = P_{cap} - \frac{q_{O_2}}{4K}(r^2 - r_c^2)} $$ *This shows PO₂ falls parabolically with radial distance, and is highest at the capillary wall.* --- **(b) A skeletal muscle has the following data. Compute the maximum tissue cylinder radius $r_{t,\max}$ that just avoids anoxic core formation. (7 Marks)** - Krogh coefficient $K_{O_2} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ mL O₂/(cm·s·mmHg) - O₂ consumption rate $q_{O_2} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ mL O₂/(mL tissue·s) - Capillary radius $r_c = 4$ µm - Capillary PO₂ $P_{cap} = 40$ mmHg - Critical (anoxic) tissue PO₂ $P_{min} = 1$ mmHg **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Use the boundary condition** at $r = r_{t,\max}$, $P = P_{min}$: $$ P_{min} = P_{cap} - \frac{q_{O_2}}{4K}(r_{t,\max}^2 - r_c^2) $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange:** $$ r_{t,\max}^2 - r_c^2 = \frac{4K(P_{cap} - P_{min})}{q_{O_2}} $$ **Step 3 — Convert units to be consistent:** Use cm throughout. - $r_c = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ cm - $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-5}$ - $P_{cap} - P_{min} = 40 - 1 = 39$ mmHg - $q_{O_2} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ **Step 4 — Compute the numerator:** $$ 4K(P_{cap} - P_{min}) = 4 \times 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 39 $$ $$ = 6.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 39 = 2.652 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 5 — Divide by $q_{O_2}$:** $$ \frac{2.652 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.304 \text{ cm}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Add $r_c^2$** (negligible: $r_c^2 = 16 \times 10^{-8}$ cm² $\approx 0$): $$ r_{t,\max}^2 \approx 5.304 \text{ cm}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Take the square root:** $$ \boxed{r_{t,\max} \approx 2.30 \text{ cm}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks on numerical questions. Always convert µm → cm before substituting. > 2. **Forgetting the $r_c^2$ correction** loses 1 mark; for $r_t \gg r_c$ it is often negligible, but you MUST state the assumption explicitly. > 3. **Sign convention error** in the derivation of $P(r)$ — the negative sign in $J = -K dP/dr$ is often flipped, leading to a physically meaningless *increasing* $P(r)$. Write out the integration limits every time. > 4. **Confusing $D$, $\alpha$, and $K$** in numerical substitutions — examiners specifically test whether you know whether the formula needs $D$, $\alpha$, or $K$. > 5. **Not stating assumptions** before the derivation loses 1–2 marks (KTU 2024 emphasises outcome-based marking — assumptions carry marks). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, Gas Transport Between Blood and Tissues** - ☐ **Fick's First Law** is the foundation: $J = -D\,dC/dx$. - ☐ **Henry's Law** is the bridge: $C = \alpha P$, so $J = -K\,dP/dr$ with $K = D\alpha$. - ☐ **Krogh coefficient $K$** = $D \cdot \alpha$ — always use this lumped parameter in tissue problems. - ☐ **CO₂ is ~25× more soluble** than O₂ → CO₂ transfers ~20× faster despite smaller $\Delta P$. - ☐ **Series resistance model** is the KTU-favoured framework: $R_{total} = \sum \delta_i / K_i$. - ☐ **Interstitial fluid is normally the rate-limiting barrier** in healthy tissue. - ☐ **Krogh cylinder model** assumes: uniform $q_{O_2}$, cylindrical symmetry, steady state, no axial gradient. - ☐ **Final radial profile**: $P(r) = P_{cap} - \dfrac{q_{O_2}}{4K}(r^2 - r_c^2)$ — parabolic fall. - ☐ **Critical radius formula**: $r_{t,\max} \approx \sqrt{4K(P_{cap} - P_{min})/q_{O_2}}$. - ☐ **Always convert units** to cm and mmHg before substituting; µL ≠ mL, s ≠ min. - ☐ **State assumptions** explicitly before every derivation (steady state, 1D, homogeneous, isothermal). - ☐ **Blood gases**: Arterial PO₂ ≈ 95, PCO₂ ≈ 40; Venous PO₂ ≈ 40, PCO₂ ≈ 46; Tissue PO₂ ≈ 5–20 mmHg. - ☐ **Engineering applications**: membrane oxygenators, ECMO, hyperbaric O₂, tissue engineering scaffolds, submarine CO₂ scrubbers.

topics.title: Oxygen-Haemoglobin Binding Kinetics and Equilibria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oxygen-Haemoglobin Binding Kinetics and Equilibria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxygen–Haemoglobin (O₂–Hb) Binding Kinetics and Equilibria** is the quantitative study of the reversible, cooperative binding of molecular oxygen (O₂) to the four haem (heme) prosthetic groups of the tetrameric haemoglobin (Hb) molecule, encompassing the **thermodynamic equilibrium** (characterized by the oxygen dissociation curve and the Hill equation) and the **kinetic rate processes** (forward association rate constant `k_on` and reverse dissociation rate constant `k_off`) that govern oxygen uptake in the pulmonary capillaries and release in the systemic tissue capillaries.

Mathematically, the overall reversible binding reaction is:

$$\text{Hb} + 4\,\text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{Hb(O}_2)_4$$

However, this reaction proceeds through four sequential elementary steps (Adair's intermediate scheme), each with its own microscopic binding constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Biological Insight:** Unlike myoglobin (a monomer with a single haem), haemoglobin is a **tetramer** (α₂β₂) that exhibits **cooperative, allosteric** behaviour. The binding of the first O₂ molecule makes the binding of subsequent O₂ molecules progressively easier (positive cooperativity), producing the characteristic **sigmoidal** oxygen dissociation curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Four-Handled Bucket":**
>
> Imagine haemoglobin as a bucket with **4 handles** (the 4 haem groups). When the bucket is **empty**, the handles are stiff and far apart — hard to grab. Once you load the first handle (bind the first O₂), the bucket shape changes — the iron atom (Fe²⁺) moves into the plane of the porphyrin ring, dragging the F-helix with it. This subtle conformational shift **loosens the remaining handles**, making them progressively easier to grab. By the time you load the 3rd and 4th O₂, the handles practically jump onto your hands. This is **positive cooperativity**.
>
> Conversely, when one O₂ is released in oxygen-starved tissue, the remaining O₂ molecules let go **even faster** — like a "chain-release" effect. This sigmoidal "all-or-none" behaviour is what makes haemoglobin an **efficient oxygen transporter**, capable of both loading heavily in the lungs and unloading heavily in the tissues.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Physiological Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants are essential for KTU numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of Hb (tetramer) | $M_{\text{Hb}}$ | **64,500 Da** (≈ 64.5 kDa) |
| Hill coefficient (max) | $n_H$ | **2.7 – 3.0** |
| Bohr coefficient (H⁺ effect) | $\Delta \log P_{50} / \Delta \text{pH}$ | **−0.48 to −0.50** |
| Standard P₅₀ (adult Hb, pH 7.4, 37 °C) | $P_{50}$ | **26.6 mmHg** (≈ 3.5 kPa) |
| O₂ carrying capacity of blood | $C_{\max}$ | **1.34 mL O₂ per g Hb** |
| Normal blood [Hb] | $[\text{Hb}]_{\text{blood}}$ | **15 g/dL** (men); 13 g/dL (women) |
| Intracellular [2,3-BPG] in RBC | $[\text{2,3-BPG}]$ | **~5 mM** |
| Total blood O₂ content (arterial) | $C_a\text{O}_2$ | **~20 mL O₂/dL blood** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The "**1.34 mL O₂ per gram Hb**" (Hüfner's constant) is the theoretical maximum O₂ binding capacity when every haem site is saturated. In practice, only about **1.30–1.34 mL/g** is achieved due to small amounts of methaemoglobin (Fe³⁺) and carboxyhaemoglobin (HbCO).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adair Four-Step Sequential Binding Scheme

Haemoglobin binds O₂ in **four sequential elementary steps**, each characterized by an **Adair intrinsic binding constant** $K_i$:

$$\text{Hb} + \text{O}_2 \;\overset{K_1}{\rightleftharpoons}\; \text{HbO}_2$$

$$\text{HbO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \;\overset{K_2}{\rightleftharpoons}\; \text{Hb(O}_2)_2$$

$$\text{Hb(O}_2)_2 + \text{O}_2 \;\overset{K_3}{\rightleftharpoons}\; \text{Hb(O}_2)_3$$

$$\text{Hb(O}_2)_3 + \text{O}_2 \;\overset{K_4}{\rightleftharpoons}\; \text{Hb(O}_2)_4$$

The **fractional saturation** $Y$ (also called $\theta$ or $S$) — the fraction of haem sites occupied by O₂ — is given by the **Adair equation**:

$$Y = \frac{\text{[bound O}_2\text{ sites]}}{\text{total available sites}} = \frac{K_1 p + 2 K_1 K_2 p^2 + 3 K_1 K_2 K_3 p^3 + 4 K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 p^4}{4\left(1 + K_1 p + K_1 K_2 p^2 + K_1 K_2 K_3 p^3 + K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 p^4\right)}$$

where $p = P_{\text{O}_2}$ is the **partial pressure of oxygen**.

For **cooperative** haemoglobin, the empirically determined Adair constants satisfy:
$$K_1 < K_2 < K_3 < K_4$$
This progressive increase in binding affinity with each successive O₂ loaded is the **mathematical signature of positive cooperativity**.

## 2.2 The Hill Equation (Empirical Cooperative Model)

The Adair equation, while exact, is cumbersome. **Archibald Hill (1910)** proposed a simplified empirical model assuming **infinite cooperativity** (i.e., Hb exists only in two states: fully unbound Hb or fully bound Hb(O₂)₄):

$$\boxed{\,Y = \frac{(P_{\text{O}_2} / P_{50})^{\,n_H}}{1 + (P_{\text{O}_2} / P_{50})^{\,n_H}}\,}$$

Rearranged into the **linear Hill form** (used to extract $n_H$ experimentally from binding data):

$$\boxed{\,\log\!\left(\frac{Y}{1-Y}\right) = n_H \log(P_{\text{O}_2}) - n_H \log(P_{50})\,}$$

- $n_H = 1$ → **no cooperativity** (rectangular hyperbola, e.g., myoglobin)
- $n_H > 1$ → **positive cooperativity** (sigmoidal; e.g., Hb, $n_H \approx 2.7$)
- $n_H < 1$ → **negative cooperativity**

## 2.3 The Bohr Effect (pH-Modulated Binding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Christian Bohr (1904)** discovered that increasing [H⁺] (lowering pH) and increasing $P_{\text{CO}_2}$ **decreases** Hb's affinity for O₂, shifting the dissociation curve to the **right**. This is physiologically essential — actively metabolizing tissues (which are acidic and CO₂-rich) extract more O₂ from the same blood.

The shift is quantified by the **Bohr coefficient** $\phi$:

$$\boxed{\,\phi = \frac{\Delta \log P_{50}}{\Delta \text{pH}} \approx -0.48\,}$$

A negative $\phi$ means that as pH decreases (more acidic), $P_{50}$ increases (lower affinity, more O₂ release).

## 2.4 Allosteric Regulation by 2,3-Bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2,3-BPG** is a small, highly negatively charged organic phosphate present in red blood cells at ~5 mM. It binds in the **central cavity** of deoxy-Hb (T-state) between the two β-chains, forming salt bridges with positively charged residues (His₂, Lys₄₃, etc.). This **stabilizes the T-state**, thereby **decreasing** O₂ affinity (shifts curve to the right, ↑ P₅₀).

Clinical/physiological relevance:
- **High-altitude acclimatization:** ↑ RBC [2,3-BPG] → ↑ P₅₀ → better tissue O₂ unloading.
- **Fetal Hb (HbF):** Has γ-chains instead of β-chains → binds 2,3-BPG poorly → **higher O₂ affinity** (P₅₀ ≈ 19 mmHg) → facilitates O₂ transfer from maternal to fetal blood across the placenta.

## 2.5 Carbon Monoxide (CO) Binding — A Toxicological Concern

CO binds Hb at the same Fe²⁺ haem site as O₂, but with an affinity **~200–250× higher**:

$$\frac{K_{\text{CO}}}{K_{\text{O}_2}} \approx 200 \text{ to } 250$$

CO binding also induces an **allosteric shift** in the remaining subunits, increasing their O₂ affinity (left-shift), which **impairs O₂ release** at tissues — a dual mechanism that makes CO poisoning especially dangerous.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formula Card for KTU 2024 ESE & Internal Assessments**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Hb} + 4\text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{Hb(O}_2)_4$ | Overall reversible binding | — |
| 2 | $K_d = \dfrac{k_{\text{off}}}{k_{\text{on}}}$ | Microscopic dissociation constant | mmHg (or M) |
| 3 | $Y = \dfrac{(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}{1+(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}$ | Hill equation — fractional saturation | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{1-Y}\right) = n_H \log P_{\text{O}_2} - n_H \log P_{50}$ | Linear Hill plot | — |
| 5 | $P_{50}$ | Partial pressure of O₂ at 50 % saturation (standard affinity index) | mmHg or kPa |
| 6 | $\phi = \dfrac{\Delta \log P_{50}}{\Delta \text{pH}} \approx -0.48$ | Bohr coefficient | unitless |
| 7 | $[\text{O}_2]_{\text{blood}} = 1.34\,[\text{Hb}]\,Y + \alpha\,P_{\text{O}_2}$ | Total blood O₂ content (Hüfner + dissolved) | mL O₂ / dL blood |
| 8 | $\alpha_{\text{O}_2} = 0.0031$ mL O₂ / (dL · mmHg) | O₂ solubility in plasma at 37 °C | mL O₂·dL⁻¹·mmHg⁻¹ |
| 9 | $[\text{HbO}_2] = [\text{Hb}]_t \cdot Y$ | Mass balance for oxyhaemoglobin | g/dL |
| 10 | $\tau_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{off}}}$ | Half-time of O₂ release from a site | seconds |
| 11 | $k_{\text{on}} \approx 3 \times 10^{7}$ M⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Forward (association) rate constant for Hb–O₂ | M⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 12 | $k_{\text{off}} \approx 15$–$30$ s⁻¹ | Reverse (dissociation) rate constant | s⁻¹ |
| 13 | $M_{\text{Hb}} = 64{,}500$ Da | Molecular weight of tetrameric Hb | Da |
| 14 | Hill coefficient: $n_H \in [2.7, 3.0]$ | Index of cooperativity for adult Hb | unitless |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering / Medical Utility

- **Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO):** Designing oxygenator membranes requires understanding of Hb-O₂ equilibrium and the kinetics of O₂ transfer across the gas-exchange surface.
- **Blood Substitutes and Haemoglobin-Based Oxygen Carriers (HBOCs):** Engineered Hb variants with modified P₅₀ values are designed to optimize tissue O₂ delivery.
- **Pulse Oximetry:** Operates on the differential light absorption of HbO₂ and deoxy-Hb at 660 nm and 940 nm — the same equilibrium physics underlies the device.
- **High-Altitude Medicine and Sports Physiology:** Acclimatization-induced 2,3-BPG shifts directly govern O₂ delivery.
- **Anaesthesiology:** Inspired O₂ fractions (FiO₂), shunt fractions, and V/Q mismatch calculations all rely on the O₂-Hb dissociation curve.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Hill Equation from the Adair Scheme (Under Infinite Cooperativity Limit)

### Objective
Starting from the rigorous Adair four-step scheme, derive the simplified Hill equation used for routine analysis of the O₂-Hb dissociation curve.

### Step 1 — Set up the binding polynomial
The Adair fractional saturation is defined as the **average number of bound O₂ per Hb** divided by the total number of binding sites (which is 4):

$$Y = \frac{\text{average bound O}_2 \text{ per Hb}}{4}$$

Let $p = P_{\text{O}_2}$. Define the **binding polynomial** (also called the partition function) $Z$:

$$Z = 1 + K_1 p + K_1 K_2 p^2 + K_1 K_2 K_3 p^3 + K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 p^4$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate the binding polynomial
The average number of bound O₂ per Hb is the **fractional derivative** of $\ln Z$ with respect to $\ln p$:

$$\langle n \rangle = \frac{d \ln Z}{d \ln p} = \frac{p}{Z}\frac{dZ}{dp}$$

Computing the derivative term by term:

$$\frac{dZ}{dp} = K_1 + 2K_1 K_2 p + 3K_1 K_2 K_3 p^2 + 4K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 p^3$$

### Step 3 — Combine into the Adair saturation equation
Dividing by $Z$ and normalizing by 4:

$$Y = \frac{K_1 p + 2K_1 K_2 p^2 + 3K_1 K_2 K_3 p^3 + 4K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 p^4}{4\,Z}$$

This is the **exact** four-step Adair expression. It is algebraically correct but rarely used directly in KTU problems due to its complexity.

### Step 4 — Apply the infinite-cooperativity (Hill) limit
Hill's key assumption: **all intermediate species are negligible**, and Hb exists only in two states — fully free (Hb) or fully ligated (Hb(O₂)₄). This means $K_1 \approx K_2 \approx K_3 \approx K_4 \equiv K$, so:

$$K_1 K_2 K_3 K_4 = K^4$$

The Adair equation collapses to:

$$Y = \frac{4 K^4 p^4}{4(1 + K^4 p^4)} = \frac{K^4 p^4}{1 + K^4 p^4}$$

### Step 5 — Rewrite in terms of $P_{50}$
By definition, when $P_{\text{O}_2} = P_{50}$, we have $Y = 1/2$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{K^4 P_{50}^4}{1 + K^4 P_{50}^4} \;\Longrightarrow\; K^4 P_{50}^4 = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; K P_{50} = 1$$

Substituting $K = 1/P_{50}$ back into Step 4:

$$\boxed{\,Y = \frac{(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^4}{1 + (P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^4}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Generalize to an arbitrary Hill coefficient $n_H$
Empirically, $n_H = 4$ overestimates the cooperativity of real Hb. Replacing the integer 4 with the empirical constant $n_H$ (typically 2.7–3.0) yields the **operational Hill equation** used in clinical and engineering practice:

$$\boxed{\,Y = \frac{(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}{1 + (P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bohr Effect — Quantitative pH-Modulated $P_{50}$ Shift

### Objective
Derive the quantitative relationship between blood pH and $P_{50}$, and compute the expected $P_{50}$ at a non-standard pH.

### Step 1 — Define the Bohr coefficient
$$\phi = \frac{\Delta \log_{10} P_{50}}{\Delta \text{pH}}$$

A typical experimental value is $\phi \approx -0.48$ for adult human Hb in physiological conditions.

### Step 2 — Express as a differential
$$d(\log_{10} P_{50}) = \phi \cdot d(\text{pH})$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from standard conditions $(P_{50,0}, \text{pH}_0)$ to a new state $(P_{50}, \text{pH})$
$$\int_{P_{50,0}}^{P_{50}} d(\log_{10} P_{50}) = \phi \int_{\text{pH}_0}^{\text{pH}} d(\text{pH})$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{50}}{P_{50,0}}\right) = \phi \cdot (\text{pH} - \text{pH}_0)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the new $P_{50}$
$$\boxed{\,P_{50} = P_{50,0} \cdot 10^{\,\phi \cdot (\text{pH} - \text{pH}_0)}\,}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)
**Given:** $P_{50,0} = 26.6$ mmHg at pH₀ = 7.40. Compute $P_{50}$ at pH = 7.20 (mild tissue acidosis).

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{50} &= 26.6 \times 10^{(-0.48)\times(7.20 - 7.40)} \\
       &= 26.6 \times 10^{(-0.48)\times(-0.20)} \\
       &= 26.6 \times 10^{0.096} \\
       &= 26.6 \times 1.247 \\
       &\approx 33.18 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** At the lower pH of actively metabolizing tissue, the P₅₀ has **risen from 26.6 to 33.2 mmHg**, indicating a **decrease in O₂ affinity** and therefore **greater O₂ release** to the tissues. This is the essence of the Bohr effect.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Blood Oxygen Content

### Objective
Derive an expression for the total O₂ content of whole blood, including both haem-bound and physically dissolved contributions.

### Step 1 — Identify the two contributions
- **Haem-bound O₂** (vast majority, ~98.5 %): $C_{\text{HbO}_2} = 1.34\,[\text{Hb}]\,Y$ in mL O₂/dL blood
- **Physically dissolved O₂** (small, ~1.5 %): $C_{\text{dissolved}} = \alpha\,P_{\text{O}_2}$ in mL O₂/dL blood

where $\alpha = 0.0031$ mL O₂·dL⁻¹·mmHg⁻¹ (Bunsen solubility coefficient for O₂ in plasma at 37 °C).

### Step 2 — Sum the contributions
$$\boxed{\,C_{\text{total}} = 1.34\,[\text{Hb}]\,Y + 0.0031\,P_{\text{O}_2}\quad [\text{mL O}_2/\text{dL blood}]\,}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Y from the Hill equation
Insert the Hill expression for $Y$:

$$C_{\text{total}} = 1.34\,[\text{Hb}]\cdot\frac{(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}{1+(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}} + 0.0031\,P_{\text{O}_2}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** $[\text{Hb}] = 15$ g/dL, $P_{50} = 26.6$ mmHg, $n_H = 2.8$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 100$ mmHg (arterial blood). Compute $C_{\text{total}}$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H} &= (100/26.6)^{2.8} = (3.759)^{2.8} \\
&\approx 56.81 \\
Y &= \frac{56.81}{1 + 56.81} = \frac{56.81}{57.81} \approx 0.9827 \\
C_{\text{HbO}_2} &= 1.34 \times 15 \times 0.9827 \approx 19.76 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL} \\
C_{\text{dissolved}} &= 0.0031 \times 100 = 0.31 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL} \\
C_{\text{total}} &\approx 20.07 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL blood}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the **standard textbook value of ~20 mL O₂/dL** for normal arterial blood.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: O₂–Hb Kinetics — Time Constants and Half-Lives

### Objective
Relate the kinetic rate constants $k_{\text{on}}$ and $k_{\text{off}}$ to the physiological time available for O₂ exchange in the pulmonary capillary.

### Step 1 — Write the first-order dissociation rate
For a single bound haem site:
$$\frac{d[\text{HbO}_2]}{dt} = -k_{\text{off}}[\text{HbO}_2]$$

### Step 2 — Solve the first-order ODE
Integrating with initial condition $[\text{HbO}_2](0) = [\text{HbO}_2]_0$:

$$[\text{HbO}_2](t) = [\text{HbO}_2]_0 \, e^{-k_{\text{off}}\,t}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the half-life
Set $[\text{HbO}_2](\tau_{1/2}) = [\text{HbO}_2]_0/2$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k_{\text{off}}\,\tau_{1/2}} \;\Longrightarrow\; \tau_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{off}}}$$

### Step 4 — Plug in the physiological $k_{\text{off}}$ for Hb–O₂
Using $k_{\text{off}} \approx 20$ s⁻¹ (typical):

$$\tau_{1/2} \approx \frac{0.693}{20} \approx 0.035 \text{ s} = 35 \text{ ms}$$

**Physiological Insight:** A red blood cell traverses a pulmonary capillary in approximately **0.75 seconds**. With a half-life of O₂ release of only ~35 ms, **complete O₂ loading** at the lung (and complete unloading at the tissue) is kinetically feasible. The cooperative shift (R vs T state) accelerates this further by orders of magnitude.

### Step 5 — Compute the effective on-rate
The equilibrium dissociation constant $K_d$ is:

$$K_d = \frac{k_{\text{off}}}{k_{\text{on}}}$$

Using $K_d \approx 26.6$ mmHg (converted to M via Henry's law: 1 mmHg ≈ 1.34 × 10⁻⁶ M O₂ in plasma at 37 °C, so $K_d \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-5}$ M):

$$k_{\text{on}} = \frac{k_{\text{off}}}{K_d} = \frac{20}{3.6 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $k_{\text{on}}$ values:** Literature reports for the on-rate vary considerably depending on whether diffusion-limited corrections are applied. Reported values range from ~3 × 10⁷ M⁻¹s⁻¹ (diffusion-limited, theoretical) to ~10⁵–10⁶ M⁻¹s⁻¹ (experimentally observed in red-cell suspensions). The KTU board typically accepts values in the order of **10⁵–10⁷ M⁻¹s⁻¹** depending on the assumed $K_d$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Key O₂ Carriers

| Property | Myoglobin (Mb) | Adult Hb (HbA) | Fetal Hb (HbF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structure | Monomer (1 haem) | Tetramer α₂β₂ (4 haems) | Tetramer α₂γ₂ (4 haems) |
| Subunit cooperativity | None (non-cooperative) | Strong (cooperative) | Strong (cooperative) |
| Hill coefficient $n_H$ | **1.0** | **2.7 – 3.0** | **~2.5** |
| Standard $P_{50}$ | **~2.8 mmHg** (very high affinity) | **~26.6 mmHg** | **~19 mmHg** (higher affinity) |
| O₂ binding curve shape | Rectangular hyperbola | Sigmoidal | Sigmoidal |
| 2,3-BPG binding | N/A (monomer) | Strong | Weak (γ-chains lack His₂) |
| Primary function | O₂ storage in muscle | O₂ transport in blood | O₂ transport in fetus (placental transfer) |
| Bohr effect | None | Strong | Moderate |
