# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Q: Define the term "cooperativity" in the context of oxygen–haemoglobin binding. How is it quantified experimentally? Mention the typical value for adult haemoglobin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Cooperativity refers to the phenomenon in which the binding of a ligand (O₂) at one site of a multi-subunit protein (Hb) alters the binding affinity of the remaining sites for the same ligand. In **positive cooperativity**, binding at one site *increases* the affinity of the remaining sites, producing a **sigmoidal** (S-shaped) binding curve.
- **Quantification (1 mark):** Cooperativity is quantified by the **Hill coefficient** $n_H$, obtained from the slope of the linear Hill plot $\log(Y/(1-Y))$ vs $\log P_{\text{O}_2}$. $n_H = 1$ implies no cooperativity; $n_H > 1$ implies positive cooperativity.
- **Typical value (1 mark):** For adult human haemoglobin, $n_H \approx \mathbf{2.7 \text{ to } 3.0}$ at standard physiological conditions (pH 7.4, 37 °C, 5 mM 2,3-BPG).

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Q: State the Bohr effect and explain its physiological significance in oxygen transport.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** The Bohr effect is the phenomenon by which an **increase in [H⁺] (decrease in pH)** and an **increase in $P_{\text{CO}_2}$** cause a **decrease in haemoglobin's affinity for O₂**, shifting the O₂-Hb dissociation curve to the **right** (i.e., increasing $P_{50}$).
- **Quantitative measure (1 mark):** The **Bohr coefficient** $\phi = \Delta \log P_{50}/\Delta \text{pH} \approx -0.48$ for adult Hb.
- **Physiological significance (1 mark):** Actively metabolizing tissues are **acidic and CO₂-rich**. The Bohr effect causes Hb to **release more O₂** precisely where it is needed most, thereby enhancing tissue oxygenation. Conversely, in the well-ventilated lungs, the lower $P_{\text{CO}_2}$ and higher pH **enhance O₂ loading** onto Hb.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting units in $K_d$:** $K_d$ for O₂-Hb is conventionally expressed in **mmHg** (not in molarity) when using the Hill equation. The unit confusion between molar $K_d$ and partial-pressure $P_{50}$ is the single most common error in KTU board scripts, costing 1–2 marks.
> - **Miscounting the Bohr sign:** A *decrease* in pH should *increase* $P_{50}$ (right-shift). Students often report a *decrease* in $P_{50}$ — wrong by sign. The Bohr coefficient is **negative** by definition.
> - **Hill plot linearization:** When asked to extract $n_H$ from data, students forget to take the logarithm of *both* axes. The plot is $\log(Y/(1-Y))$ vs $\log P_{\text{O}_2}$, with slope $= n_H$.
> - **Algebra of $k_{\text{on}}$ calculation:** Confusing $K_d = k_{\text{off}}/k_{\text{on}}$ with the inverse. Memorize: **"$K_d$ = off over on"**.
> - **Total O₂ content:** Forgetting the dissolved-O₂ term ($0.0031\,P_{\text{O}_2}$) in $C_{\text{total}}$ loses 1 mark.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A. (a)** State and explain the **Hill equation** for oxygen–haemoglobin binding. Sketch and describe the **O₂–Hb dissociation curve** clearly marking the values of $P_{\text{O}_2}$ corresponding to 25 %, 50 %, and 97 % saturation. Comment on the physiological significance of the **sigmoidal shape** of this curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blood sample has $[\text{Hb}] = 14 \text{ g/dL}$, $P_{50} = 27$ mmHg, and Hill coefficient $n_H = 2.8$. Calculate:
- (i) The fractional saturation $Y$ at $P_{\text{O}_2} = 90$ mmHg.
- (ii) The total O₂ content of the blood (in mL O₂/dL), including both haem-bound and physically dissolved fractions.
- (iii) The O₂ content if the same blood equilibrated with $P_{\text{O}_2} = 40$ mmHg. Comment on the **arteriovenous O₂ difference**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Hill equation and dissociation curve (7 marks):**

The Hill equation relates the **fractional saturation** $Y$ of haemoglobin to the **partial pressure** $P_{\text{O}_2}$ of dissolved oxygen:

$$Y = \frac{(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}{1 + (P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}$$

where $P_{50}$ is the $P_{\text{O}_2}$ at which haemoglobin is 50 % saturated, and $n_H$ is the **Hill coefficient** (≈ 2.8 for adult Hb).

**Key values on the standard curve (pH 7.4, 37 °C):**
- $Y = 0.25$ at $P_{\text{O}_2} \approx 15$ mmHg
- $Y = 0.50$ at $P_{\text{O}_2} = P_{50} \approx 26.6$ mmHg
- $Y = 0.97$ at $P_{\text{O}_2} \approx 100$ mmHg (typical alveolar/arterial)

**Physiological significance of the sigmoidal shape:**
- **Flat upper plateau** ($P_{\text{O}_2} > 60$ mmHg) ensures that even if alveolar $P_{\text{O}_2}$ drops moderately (e.g., at altitude or in mild lung disease), Hb remains nearly fully saturated — a **safety margin**.
- **Steep middle region** (around 20–50 mmHg) means that a small drop in tissue $P_{\text{O}_2}$ causes a **large release** of O₂ from Hb — efficient unloading where it is needed.
- **Flat lower tail** at very low $P_{\text{O}_2}$ ensures Hb does not release *all* its O₂, retaining a small reserve for extreme hypoxia.

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Fractional saturation at $P_{\text{O}_2} = 90$ mmHg:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H} &= (90/27)^{2.8} = (3.333)^{2.8} \\
&\approx 47.0 \\
Y_{90} &= \frac{47.0}{1 + 47.0} = \frac{47.0}{48.0} \approx 0.9792
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii) — Total O₂ content at $P_{\text{O}_2} = 90$ mmHg:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{HbO}_2} &= 1.34 \times 14 \times 0.9792 \approx 18.37 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL} \\
C_{\text{dissolved}} &= 0.0031 \times 90 = 0.279 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL} \\
C_{\text{total, art}} &\approx 18.37 + 0.28 \approx 18.65 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii) — O₂ content at $P_{\text{O}_2} = 40$ mmHg (mixed venous):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H} &= (40/27)^{2.8} = (1.4815)^{2.8} \\
&\approx 3.69 \\
Y_{40} &= \frac{3.69}{1 + 3.69} = \frac{3.69}{4.69} \approx 0.7868 \\
C_{\text{HbO}_2} &= 1.34 \times 14 \times 0.7868 \approx 14.76 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL} \\
C_{\text{dissolved}} &= 0.0031 \times 40 = 0.124 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL} \\
C_{\text{total, ven}} &\approx 14.76 + 0.12 \approx 14.88 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL}
\end{aligned}$$

**Arteriovenous O₂ difference:**

$$\Delta C_{\text{O}_2} = 18.65 - 14.88 = 3.77 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL blood}$$

**Comment:** This represents the **oxygen delivered to the tissues per 100 mL of blood** (~3.8 mL O₂/dL), which falls within the normal physiological range (4–5 mL O₂/dL at rest; can rise to 15+ mL/dL during strenuous exercise).

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**B. (a)** With the help of a **schematic sketch**, describe the **two-state (MWC) allosteric model** of haemoglobin. Explain how the equilibrium between the **T (tense)** and **R (relaxed)** states gives rise to the **sigmoidal** O₂ binding curve. Discuss the roles of **2,3-BPG**, **H⁺**, and **CO₂** as **allosteric effectors**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient has a haemoglobin variant with a **right-shifted** dissociation curve. The standard $P_{50}$ of normal Hb at pH 7.4 is 26.6 mmHg, and the patient's effective $P_{50}$ is measured to be **36 mmHg** under the same pH.
- (i) Calculate the **Bohr coefficient** if the same patient's $P_{50}$ changes to **28 mmHg** when the blood pH rises to 7.50 (with all other conditions unchanged).
- (ii) If the Bohr coefficient of normal Hb is $\phi = -0.48$, what does the more positive (or less negative) Bohr coefficient in this patient suggest about the variant's sensitivity to pH? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) MWC Allosteric Model (7 marks):**

The **Monod–Wyman–Changeux (MWC) model** (1965) proposes that haemoglobin exists as an equilibrium between **two conformational states**:

- **T (tense) state:** deoxy conformation; **low O₂ affinity**; constrained by salt bridges; central cavity open; binds 2,3-BPG.
- **R (relaxed) state:** oxy conformation; **high O₂ affinity**; salt bridges broken; central cavity closed; 2,3-BPG released.

**Key propositions:**
1. All four subunits switch **concertedly** (no stable half-liganded intermediates in the strict MWC model).
2. The T ↔ R equilibrium constant $L_0 = [\text{T}]/[\text{R}] \gg 1$ in the absence of O₂.
3. O₂ binds to **both** states, but with much higher affinity to R (ratio $c \ll 1$).
4. Binding of successive O₂ progressively shifts the equilibrium toward R — this is the molecular origin of **cooperativity**.

**Origin of the sigmoidal curve:**
- At **low $P_{\text{O}_2}$**: most Hb is in the T state (low affinity) — the curve rises slowly.
- At **intermediate $P_{\text{O}_2}$**: as O₂ binds, the T/R balance shifts toward R, and the effective affinity increases — the curve **steepens**.
- At **high $P_{\text{O}_2}$**: most Hb is in the R state (high affinity) — the curve flattens into the **upper plateau**.

**Allosteric effectors:**
- **2,3-BPG:** Binds in the central cavity of the **T state** (between the two β-chains) via salt bridges with His₂β, Lys₂β, His₄₃β. Stabilizes T → **decreases** O₂ affinity (right shift, ↑ P₅₀).
- **H⁺ (Bohr effect):** Binds to specific amino acid residues (e.g., His₁₄₆ β, N-terminal α-NH₂) more strongly in the T state → stabilizes T → **decreases** O₂ affinity in acidic environments.
- **CO₂:** Forms **carbamates** with N-terminal α-NH₂ groups: $-\text{NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons -\text{NH}-\text{COO}^- + \text{H}^+$. Stabilizes T → **decreases** O₂ affinity (right shift). CO₂ is also transported as **bicarbonate** via carbonic anhydrase in RBCs.

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Calculate the Bohr coefficient:**

The Bohr coefficient is defined as:

$$\phi = \frac{\Delta \log_{10} P_{50}}{\Delta \text{pH}} = \frac{\log_{10}(P_{50,2}/P_{50,1})}{\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1}$$

With state 1 (pH₁ = 7.40, $P_{50,1} = 36$ mmHg) and state 2 (pH₂ = 7.50, $P_{50,2} = 28$ mmHg):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \log_{10} P_{50} &= \log_{10}(28) - \log_{10}(36) \\
&= 1.4472 - 1.5563 \\
&= -0.1091 \\
\Delta \text{pH} &= 7.50 - 7.40 = +0.10 \\
\phi &= \frac{-0.1091}{+0.10} = -1.091
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii) — Interpretation:**

The patient's Bohr coefficient is $\phi \approx -1.09$, which is **more negative** (more than 2× larger in magnitude) than the normal value of $\phi = -0.48$. This means that **small changes in pH cause a much larger shift in $P_{50}$** for this variant. In other words, **the variant haemoglobin is more sensitive to pH** than normal adult Hb.

Physiologically, this **enhanced Bohr effect** could be beneficial: in active tissue (lower pH), the variant would release O₂ even more readily than normal Hb — potentially advantageous for O₂ delivery. However, in the lungs, the higher pH would also bind O₂ more strongly, which could be compensatory. The clinical impact depends on the specific amino-acid substitution and its location in the protein.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision Card — Print and Carry**

### ✅ Must-Memorize Constants
- [x] $P_{50}$ (adult Hb, std conditions) = **26.6 mmHg** (≈ 3.5 kPa)
- [x] Hill coefficient (adult Hb) = **2.7–3.0**
- [x] Bohr coefficient (adult Hb) = **−0.48**
- [x] Hüfner's constant = **1.34 mL O₂ per g Hb**
- [x] O₂ solubility in plasma, 37 °C: $\alpha = 0.0031$ mL O₂/(dL·mmHg)
- [x] Molecular weight of Hb (tetramer) = **64,500 Da**
- [x] CO affinity vs O₂ = **~200–250×**
- [x] Myoglobin $P_{50}$ = **~2.8 mmHg** (much higher affinity than Hb)
- [x] Fetal Hb $P_{50}$ = **~19 mmHg** (higher affinity than adult Hb)

### ✅ Must-Know Equations
- [x] **Hill equation:** $Y = \dfrac{(P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}{1 + (P_{\text{O}_2}/P_{50})^{n_H}}$
- [x] **Linear Hill form:** $\log[Y/(1-Y)] = n_H \log P_{\text{O}_2} - n_H \log P_{50}$
- [x] **Bohr shift:** $P_{50} = P_{50,0} \cdot 10^{\,\phi \cdot (\text{pH} - \text{pH}_0)}$
- [x] **Total blood O₂ content:** $C_{\text{total}} = 1.34\,[\text{Hb}]\,Y + 0.0031\,P_{\text{O}_2}$
- [x] **Kinetic half-life:** $\tau_{1/2} = \ln 2 \,/\, k_{\text{off}}$
- [x] **Equilibrium relation:** $K_d = k_{\text{off}}/k_{\text{on}}$

### ✅ Must-Recognize Concepts
- [x] **Positive cooperativity** → sigmoidal curve, $n_H > 1$
- [x] **Allosteric T ↔ R transition** → molecular basis of cooperativity
- [x] **Bohr effect** → ↓ pH ⇒ ↓ O₂ affinity (right shift)
- [x] **Haldane effect** → ↑ O₂ binding promotes CO₂ release in lungs
- [x] **2,3-BPG** → binds T-state, stabilizes it, right-shifts curve
- [x] **Fetal Hb** → γ-chains bind 2,3-BPG poorly → high O₂ affinity
- [x] **CO poisoning** → binds Fe²⁺ haem, blocks O₂ site, also left-shifts remaining sites

### ✅ High-Yield Diagrams to Draw in the Exam
- [x] **O₂-Hb dissociation curve** — with marks at $Y = 0.25, 0.50, 0.97$
- [x] **Effectors causing right-shift and left-shift** — labelled arrows
- [x] **T → R allosteric transition** — with 2,3-BPG in T cavity
- [x] **Bohr effect** — schematic of pH change along the capillary

### ✅ Examiner's Top Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- [x] Don't confuse $P_{50}$ (in mmHg) with molar $K_d$ (in M) — keep them separate
- [x] Don't forget the sign in the Bohr coefficient — it's **negative**
- [x] Don't omit the **dissolved O₂ term** in total blood O₂ content
- [x] Don't confuse **Bohr effect** (O₂ affinity vs pH) with **Haldane effect** (CO₂ carrying vs O₂ saturation) — they are reciprocal phenomena

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxygen Transport in Lung Capillaries and to Tissues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Oxygen Transport in Lung Capillaries and to Tissues # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Oxygen Transport** is the biophysical and biochemical process by which molecular oxygen (O₂) is conveyed from the external environment (alveolar air) to the mitochondria of peripheral tissue cells, where it serves as the terminal electron acceptor in oxidative phosphorylation. In vertebrates, this transport occurs via two coupled mechanisms: **(a) convective bulk transport** through the cardiovascular system, and **(b) diffusive transport** across the alveolar-capillary membrane and the tissue-capillary interface, governed by partial pressure gradients described by Fick's First Law of Diffusion. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBBT635)**, oxygen transport is treated as a classical **mass transfer problem** coupled with **reaction kinetics** (hemoglobin binding). The pathway can be reduced to four resistances in series: 1. Alveolar membrane resistance 2. Plasma resistance 3. Erythrocyte membrane resistance 4. Tissue (interstitial + intracellular) resistance ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "City Water Supply Network"** > Think of oxygen as **water** flowing through a city's distribution network. The **alveoli** are the **water treatment plant** (source), the **pulmonary vein → heart → arteries** are the **main pipelines** (bulk transport), the **capillaries** are the **local distribution pipes** (diffusion zone), and the **tissue mitochondria** are the **houses** (consumers). > - **Pressure gradient** (P_alveolus → P_tissue) = water pressure drop from reservoir to tap. > - **Hemoglobin (Hb)** = **water tanker trucks** that dramatically increase carrying capacity (from ~3 mL/L of plain plasma to ~200 mL/L of blood). > - **Bohr effect** = trucks that **drop their load faster** when local pH drops (more delivery to active muscles). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants are **mandatory** for KTU numericals and must be memorized: | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Standard atmospheric pressure | P_atm | **101.325 kPa** | kPa | | Alveolar partial pressure of O₂ | P_AO₂ | **~13.3 kPa (100 mmHg)** | kPa (mmHg) | | Arterial partial pressure of O₂ | P_aO₂ | **~12.7 kPa (95 mmHg)** | kPa (mmHg) | | Venous partial pressure of O₂ | P_vO₂ | **~5.3 kPa (40 mmHg)** | kPa (mmHg) | | Tissue mitochondrial PO₂ | P_mito | **~0.13–1.3 kPa (1–10 mmHg)** | kPa (mmHg) | | O₂ solubility coefficient in plasma (37°C) | α_O₂ | **1.226 × 10⁻⁵ mL O₂ / (mL·mmHg)** | mL·mL⁻¹·mmHg⁻¹ | | O₂ binding capacity of Hb | (Hüfner's constant) | **1.34 mL O₂ / g Hb** | mL·g⁻¹ | | Normal Hb concentration | [Hb] | **15 g/dL (women: 14, men: 16)** | g·dL⁻¹ | | Cardiac output (resting) | Q | **5 L/min** | L·min⁻¹ | | O₂ diffusion coefficient in tissue | D_O₂ | **~2.0 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s** | m²·s⁻¹ | | Krogh's diffusion constant (muscle) | K_O₂ | **1.5 × 10⁻⁸ mL O₂ / (cm·s·mmHg)** | varied | > [!NOTE] > **Hüfner's constant (1.34 mL/g)** is a KTU examiner favorite. Always use this value, NOT 1.39, unless specifically stated (1.39 represents the theoretical maximum considering only the four binding sites). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Oxygen Transport Pathway – A Stepwise Mass Transfer Model The complete oxygen journey can be broken into **three sequential resistances in series**: ### Step 1: Convection in Pulmonary Ventilation Fresh air (P_IO₂ ≈ 21.2 kPa / 160 mmHg at sea level) enters the alveoli and is humidified and diluted by residual FRC (Functional Residual Capacity). The **alveolar gas equation** gives the resultant alveolar PO₂: $$P_{AO_2} = P_{IO_2} - \frac{P_{ACO_2}}{R}$$ where R is the **respiratory quotient** (~0.8 at rest). Typical P_AO₂ ≈ 100 mmHg. ### Step 2: Diffusion Across the Alveolar-Capillary Membrane O₂ moves down its partial pressure gradient from alveolar gas (P_AO₂ ≈ 100 mmHg) to capillary plasma (initial P_pO₂ ≈ 40 mmHg in venous blood). The driving force is **ΔP = 60 mmHg** across a membrane of thickness **L ≈ 0.5 µm**. ### Step 3: Reaction with Hemoglobin Inside the Erythrocyte O₂ binds reversibly to the four Fe²⁺ sites of hemoglobin following the **Adair equation** (often simplified to the Hill equation with n ≈ 2.7). This reaction is **fast** (t_½ ≈ 10 ms) and acts as a "**sink**" that maintains the gradient by continuously removing free O₂ from the plasma. ### Step 4: Convective Transport via Systemic Circulation Hb-bound O₂ (~98.5%) plus dissolved O₂ (~1.5%) is pumped at **Q = 5 L/min** to the tissues. ### Step 5: Diffusion from Systemic Capillaries to Mitochondria At the tissue end, O₂ unloads from Hb and diffuses across the **capillary wall → interstitium → cell membrane → cytoplasm → mitochondrial inner membrane**, driven by ΔP from ~95 mmHg to <5 mmHg. ## 2.2 The Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve (OHDC) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **sigmoidal OHDC** is non-linear because of **cooperative binding** (positive homotropic allostery). Binding of O₂ to one Hb subunit increases the affinity of the remaining subunits. This is mathematically captured by the **Hill equation**: $$\text{Saturation } S = \frac{(P_{O_2})^n}{(P_{50})^n + (P_{O_2})^n}$$ where: - **P₅₀ ≈ 26.6 mmHg** (PO₂ at which Hb is 50% saturated — a KTU key value) - **n ≈ 2.7** (Hill coefficient, indicating cooperativity; n=1 would be non-cooperative/Michaelis-Menten) - **S = 1.0 at P_AO₂ ≈ 100 mmHg** (near full arterial saturation) ## 2.3 The Bohr Effect > [!NOTE] > **Bohr Effect (1904):** Increased [H⁺] (lower pH) and increased P_CO₂ **shift the OHDC to the RIGHT**, decreasing Hb's affinity for O₂, thus **promoting O₂ unloading** in metabolically active tissues. Quantitatively, the **Bohr coefficient** is defined as: $$\text{Bohr coefficient} = \frac{\Delta \log P_{50}}{\Delta \text{pH}} \approx -0.48$$ A negative coefficient means that a *decrease* in pH (i.e., increase in [H⁺]) results in an *increase* in P₅₀, hence rightward shift. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table is your **one-page cheat sheet** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) on this topic. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Description / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Henry's Law** (dissolved O₂) | $C_{O_2} = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$ | mL O₂ per mL blood per mmHg; gives the small physically-dissolved fraction. | | 2 | **Oxygen Content Equation** | $C_{O_2} = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S + \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$ | Total O₂ carried per 100 mL blood (mL O₂/dL). **The most-tested KTU equation.** | | 3 | **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** | $J_{O_2} = -D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ | Flux of O₂ in mL/(cm²·s); applies to all membrane crossings. | | 4 | **Fick's Law (pressure form)** | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{L}$ | Volume rate of O₂ diffusion; for tissue, A = capillary surface area. | | 5 | **Krogh Cylinder Model** | $P(r) = P_{cap} - \frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{4 K_{O_2}} \left( R^2 \ln \frac{r}{R_{cap}} + \frac{R_{cap}^2 - r^2}{2} \right)$ | Radial PO₂ distribution in tissue cylinder around a capillary. | | 6 | **Fick Principle (whole body)** | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = Q \cdot (C_{a,O_2} - C_{v,O_2})$ | Cardiac output times arteriovenous O₂ content difference. | | 7 | **Alveolar Gas Equation** | $P_{AO_2} = F_{IO_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{aCO_2}}{R}$ | Determines driving pressure for diffusion. | | 8 | **Hill Equation** | $S = \dfrac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}$ | Sigmoidal Hb saturation curve. | | 9 | **O₂ Delivery (DO₂)** | $DO_2 = Q \cdot C_{a,O_2}$ | Total O₂ delivered to tissues per minute. | | 10 | **O₂ Extraction Ratio (OER)** | $OER = \dfrac{C_{a,O_2} - C_{v,O_2}}{C_{a,O_2}}$ | Fraction of delivered O₂ actually consumed; ~0.25 at rest. | | 11 | **2,3-BPG Effect** | $\uparrow$ 2,3-BPG $\Rightarrow \uparrow P_{50} \Rightarrow$ right shift | Altitude acclimatization mechanism. | | 12 | **Fåhræus-Lindqvist Effect** | $\mu_{app}$ decreases as tube diameter $d \to 300$ µm | Relevant for capillary flow resistance. | **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations form the mathematical foundation of **extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) design**, **artificial lung/oxygenator devices**, **hyperbaric oxygen therapy chambers**, and **pulse oximeter calibration algorithms** — all areas where bioprocess and biomedical engineers actively contribute. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Every algebraic transition below is **explicitly written out**. Multi-line derivations use `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` for crisp formatting, in line with KTU's "step marking" valuation scheme (each valid transition = 1 mark). ## 3.1 Derivation: The Oxygen Content Equation **Starting Point:** The total oxygen content of blood, $C_{O_2}$, is the sum of (i) oxygen **chemically bound** to hemoglobin and (ii) oxygen **physically dissolved** in plasma, both expressed in mL O₂ per 100 mL of blood. ### Step 1: Define the Bound Component The maximum O₂ binding capacity of Hb is given by **Hüfner's constant**, $k_H = 1.34$ mL O₂ per gram of Hb. The actual amount bound depends on the **fractional saturation**, $S \in [0, 1]$: $$\text{Bound O}_2 \text{ per dL} = k_H \cdot [Hb] \cdot S$$ where $[Hb]$ is the hemoglobin concentration in **g/dL**. ### Step 2: Define the Dissolved Component By **Henry's Law**, the dissolved O₂ concentration is proportional to the local partial pressure: $$C_{dissolved} = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$$ with $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.003$ mL O₂ per 100 mL blood per mmHg (the standard textbook value used in KTU problems). ### Step 3: Superposition (Linearity) Because binding and dissolution are independent physical processes operating in parallel on the same fluid: $$\begin{aligned} C_{O_2} &= C_{bound} + C_{dissolved} \\[4pt] C_{O_2} &= (1.34 \text{ mL/g}) \cdot [Hb] \cdot S \; + \; (0.003 \text{ mL/100mL·mmHg}) \cdot P_{O_2} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **canonical KTU oxygen content equation** used in ~80% of numericals. ## 3.2 Derivation: Fick's Law Applied to the Alveolar-Capillary Membrane **Starting Point:** Consider a planar membrane of thickness $L$ separating alveolar gas (PO₂ = P_A) from capillary plasma (PO₂ = P_C). O₂ flux $J$ is the volume of O₂ crossing unit area per unit time. ### Step 1: State Fick's First Law in Concentration Form $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ For a **linear concentration gradient** across a thin membrane (valid when reaction is negligible compared to diffusion, which holds for the thin alveolar wall), $dC/dx \approx \Delta C / L$: $$J = -D \cdot \frac{C_C - C_A}{L} = D \cdot \frac{C_A - C_C}{L}$$ ### Step 2: Convert to Partial Pressure Form (Henry's Law) Using $C = \alpha \cdot P$, we substitute: $$\begin{aligned} J &= D \cdot \frac{\alpha \cdot P_A - \alpha \cdot P_C}{L} \\[4pt] J &= \frac{D \cdot \alpha}{L} \cdot (P_A - P_C) \\[4pt] J &= K \cdot (P_A - P_C) \end{aligned}$$ where $K = D \cdot \alpha / L$ is the **membrane permeability** (units of mL O₂ per cm² per s per mmHg). This is the **diffusing capacity**, often written $D_L$ or $D_M$. ### Step 3: Total Volume Transfer Rate For a membrane of total area $A$, the total O₂ transfer rate is: $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot \alpha}{L} \cdot (P_A - P_C) = D_L \cdot (P_A - P_C)$$ where $D_L \approx 21$ mL O₂/(min·mmHg) at rest for healthy adult lungs. This is the **diffusing capacity of the lung for O₂**. ## 3.3 Derivation: Total O₂ Delivery (DO₂) to Tissues **Starting Point:** Apply the principle of mass conservation to the systemic circulation. ### Step 1: Total O₂ Leaving the Left Heart per Minute $$DO_2 = Q \cdot C_{a,O_2}$$ where $Q$ is cardiac output (L/min) and $C_{a,O_2}$ is the arterial O₂ content (mL O₂/dL). ### Step 2: Substitute the Content Equation $$\begin{aligned} DO_2 &= Q \cdot \left[ 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_a + 0.003 \cdot P_{a,O_2} \right] \\[4pt] DO_2 &= Q \cdot 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_a + Q \cdot 0.003 \cdot P_{a,O_2} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Numerical Example (Standard KTU-Style) For $Q = 5$ L/min, $[Hb] = 15$ g/dL, $S_a = 0.97$, $P_{a,O_2} = 95$ mmHg: $$\begin{aligned} DO_2 &= 5 \cdot 1.34 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.97 + 5 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 95 \\[4pt] DO_2 &= 97.51 + 1.425 \\[4pt] DO_2 &= 98.93 \text{ mL O₂/min} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > The dissolved fraction contributes only **~1.4%** of total delivery. This is the **key insight** that justifies the engineering relevance of hemoglobin-mimetic research (e.g., perfluorocarbons, Hb-based oxygen carriers). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Conditions Used in KTU Numericals | Region | P_O₂ (mmHg) | S (%) | C_O₂ (mL/dL) | Comments | |---|---|---|---|---| | Inspired air (trachea) | 150 | 0 | 0 | Humidified | | Alveolar gas | 100 | 0 | 0 | After gas exchange | | Arterial blood (end-pulm. cap.) | 95 | 97 | 19.5 | Near equilibrium with alveolus | | Mixed venous blood (entry to pul. cap.) | 40 | 75 | 15.1 | Returning from tissues | | End-capillary (systemic) | 40 | 75 | 15.1 | After tissue extraction | | Tissue interstitium | 20–40 | — | — | Variable with activity | | Intracellular (cytoplasm) | 5–20 | — | — | Buffer zone | | Mitochondrial matrix | 1–10 | — | — | Site of consumption | > [!TIP] > **Fick's Principle Numerical Trick:** KTU examiners often give you DO₂ and ask for venous content. Use $\dot{V}_{O_2} = Q \cdot (C_a - C_v) \Rightarrow C_v = C_a - \dot{V}_{O_2}/Q$. At rest, $\dot{V}_{O_2} \approx 250$ mL/min. ## 3.5 The Krogh Cylinder Model — Tissue Diffusion **Starting Point (Krogh, 1919):** A single tissue capillary is modeled as a cylinder of radius $R_{cap}$ supplying a surrounding tissue cylinder of outer radius $R$. O₂ is consumed uniformly in the tissue at rate $\dot{q}_{O_2}$ (mL O₂ per mL tissue per s). ### Step 1: Set Up the Steady-State Diffusion Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates For purely radial diffusion (axial symmetry, no longitudinal gradient within tissue), the Laplacian reduces to: $$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dP}{dr} \right) = -\frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{K_{O_2}}$$ where $K_{O_2} = D \cdot \alpha$ is the **Krogh diffusion constant** (mL O₂ per cm per s per mmHg). ### Step 2: Integrate Once $$r \frac{dP}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{2K_{O_2}} r^2 + C_1$$ $$\frac{dP}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{2K_{O_2}} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$ Apply **boundary condition 1**: symmetry at $r = 0$ requires $dP/dr = 0$ (finite, regular solution), so $C_1 = 0$. ### Step 3: Integrate Again $$P(r) = -\frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{4K_{O_2}} r^2 + C_2$$ Apply **boundary condition 2**: at $r = R_{cap}$, $P = P_{cap}$ (capillary wall PO₂): $$C_2 = P_{cap} + \frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{4K_{O_2}} R_{cap}^2$$ ### Step 4: Final Krogh Equation $$\boxed{\,P(r) = P_{cap} - \frac{\dot{q}_{O_2}}{4K_{O_2}} \left( R^2 \ln \frac{R}{R_{cap}} - \frac{r^2 - R_{cap}^2}{2} \right)\,}$$ This gives the **radial PO₂ profile** in tissue and predicts a "**lethal corner**" at $r = R$, where PO₂ is minimum and is most vulnerable to hypoxia. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: O₂ Transport Cascade The following Mermaid block renders the complete oxygen transport pathway as a top-down cascade, including all key resistances and pressures. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ATMOSPHERIC AIR
PiO2 = 160 mmHg] --> B[UPPER AIRWAY
Humidification and Warming] B --> C[ALVEOLAR GAS
PAO2 = 100 mmHg] C -->|Resistance 1: Alveolar Membrane
L = 0.5 micrometer| D[PULMONARY CAPILLARY PLASMA
PCO2 initial = 40 mmHg] D -->|Resistance 2: Erythrocyte Membrane| E[ERYTHROCYTE INTERIOR
Reaction with HbO2] E --> F[OXYHEMOGLOBIN
SaO2 = 97 percent] F -->|Convective transport Q = 5 L/min| G[SYSTEMIC ARTERIES
PaO2 = 95 mmHg] G --> H[ARTERIOLES] H --> I[SYSTEMIC CAPILLARIES
Initial capillary PO2 = 95 mmHg] I -->|Resistance 3: Capillary Wall| J[INTERSTITIUM
PiO2 = 30 mmHg] J -->|Resistance 4: Cell Membrane| K[CELL CYTOPLASM
PCO2 = 10 mmHg] K -->|Resistance 5: Mito Membrane| L[MITOCHONDRIA
Pmito = 5 mmHg] L --> M[OXIDATIVE PHOSPHORYLATION
ATP synthesis site] style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064 style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style I fill:#ffecb3,stroke:#e65100 style L fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style M fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Resistance-in-Series Analogy This block diagram mirrors the **electrical circuit analogy** (O₂ partial pressure = voltage, O₂ flux = current, diffusing capacity = 1/resistance), which KTU examiners frequently use in problems. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Source: Alveolar PO2
100 mmHg] --> R1[Resistance R1
Alveolar Membrane
0.5 micrometer thick] R1 --> N1[Node: Capillary Plasma
PO2 = 95 mmHg] N1 --> R2[Resistance R2
Erythrocyte Membrane and Plasma] R2 --> N2[Node: Hb Binding Site
O2 consumed by reaction] N2 --> R3[Resistance R3
Cardiovascular Convection
Q = 5 L/min] R3 --> N3[Node: Arterial Blood
PaO2 = 95 mmHg] N3 --> R4[Resistance R4
Systemic Capillary Wall] R4 --> N4[Node: Interstitium
PiO2 = 30 mmHg] N4 --> R5[Resistance R5
Cell Membrane and Cytoplasm] R5 --> N5[Node: Mitochondria
Pmito = 5 mmHg] N5 --> P2[O2 Consumed
Rate = 250 mL per min] style P1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825 style P2 fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#c62828 style N2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style N5 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#6a1b9a ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map: Factors Affecting O₂ Transport ```mermaid mindmap root((OXYGEN TRANSPORT FACTORS)) HEMOGLOBIN Concentration Saturation percent Cooperativity Hill n 2,3-BPG level PARTIAL PRESSURE GRADIENT Alveolar PO2 Venous PO2 Tissue PO2 GAS EXCHANGE Membrane thickness Surface area Diffusion coefficient D BLOOD FLOW Cardiac output Capillary density Fahraeus Lindqvist effect ENVIRONMENTAL Altitude Temperature CO2 level DISEASE STATES Anemia Emphysema Pulmonary fibrosis CO poisoning ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State and explain Henry's Law in the context of oxygen transport in blood.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Henry's Law states that at constant temperature, the amount of a gas dissolved in a given volume of liquid is **directly proportional** to the partial pressure of that gas in equilibrium with the liquid. Mathematically: $$C_{O_2} = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$$ In blood at 37°C, $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.003$ mL O₂ per 100 mL blood per mmHg. Thus, at $P_{a,O_2} = 95$ mmHg, the physically dissolved O₂ is only **0.285 mL/dL** — a tiny fraction compared to Hb-bound O₂ (~19.5 mL/dL). This tiny dissolved fraction is, however, **crucial** because it is the **only O₂ that creates the partial pressure gradient** for diffusion; the bound fraction is "hidden" from the diffusion equation until it dissociates. **(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for numerical example, 1 for physiological significance)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Hüfner's constant. What is its significance in determining oxygen carrying capacity?** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Hüfner's constant** ($k_H$) is defined as the **maximum volume of oxygen (in mL at STP) that can combine with 1 gram of hemoglobin**. Its standard value is **1.34 mL O₂/g Hb** (some older texts use 1.39). It is used in the oxygen content equation: $$C_{O_2} = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S + 0.003 \cdot P_{O_2}$$ For a healthy adult with $[Hb] = 15$ g/dL and 100% saturation, the Hb-bound O₂ capacity = $1.34 \times 15 = 20.1$ mL O₂/dL of blood. This value sets the **upper limit of oxygen carrying capacity** and is reduced in **anemia** (low [Hb]) or **CO poisoning** (reduced S due to competitive binding at Hb sites — CO has ~240× higher affinity than O₂). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the **oxygen content equation** for blood and explain the physical significance of each term. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A patient has the following arterial blood gas values: $P_{a,O_2} = 80$ mmHg, $S_a = 0.94$, $[Hb] = 12$ g/dL. If the cardiac output is 4.5 L/min, calculate: (i) the arterial O₂ content, and (ii) the total O₂ delivery to tissues per minute. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Identify Components:** Total O₂ content has two parallel contributions. $$\text{Bound O}_2 + \text{Dissolved O}_2$$ **Step 2 — Bound Component Derivation:** Hüfner's constant = 1.34 mL/g. $$\text{Bound O}_2 = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S$$ **Step 3 — Dissolved Component Derivation:** From Henry's Law: $$\text{Dissolved O}_2 = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2} = 0.003 \cdot P_{O_2}$$ **Step 4 — Superposition:** $$\begin{aligned} C_{O_2} &= 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S + 0.003 \cdot P_{O_2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Physical Significance (key for full marks):** - **Term 1 (1.34·[Hb]·S):** Represents **chemically bound** O₂ (~98.5% of total). Depends on Hb concentration and saturation; not affected by moderate changes in PO₂ (flat upper part of OHDC). - **Term 2 (0.003·PO₂):** Represents **physically dissolved** O₂ (~1.5%). Linear in PO₂; small but **diffusion-driving**. - **Clinical use:** Differentiates **hypoxemic hypoxia** (low PO₂) from **anemic hypoxia** (low [Hb]) from **stagnant hypoxia** (low Q) from **histotoxic hypoxia** (poisoned mitochondria). **(7 marks: 1 setup + 2 bound + 2 dissolved + 2 significance)** #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** $P_{a,O_2} = 80$ mmHg, $S_a = 0.94$, $[Hb] = 12$ g/dL, $Q = 4.5$ L/min. **(i) Arterial O₂ Content:** $$\begin{aligned} C_{a,O_2} &= 1.34 \cdot 12 \cdot 0.94 + 0.003 \cdot 80 \\[4pt] &= 15.12 + 0.24 \\[4pt] &= 15.36 \text{ mL O}_2 \text{/dL blood} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) O₂ Delivery to Tissues:** $$\begin{aligned} DO_2 &= Q \cdot C_{a,O_2} = 4.5 \text{ L/min} \cdot 15.36 \text{ mL/dL} \\[4pt] &= 4.5 \cdot 153.6 \text{ mL/min} \quad \text{(since 1 L = 10 dL)} \\[4pt] &= 691.2 \text{ mL O}_2 \text{/min} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** Normal DO₂ at rest is ~1000 mL/min. This patient's DO₂ is reduced (691.2 mL/min) primarily due to **anemia** (low [Hb] = 12). At rest, this may be compensated by increased extraction; under exercise stress, tissue hypoxia may result. **(7 marks: 2 setup + 2 part (i) + 2 part (ii) + 1 interpretation)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve (OHDC)**. Discuss the significance of its **sigmoidal shape** and the **Bohr effect**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the **Krogh cylinder equation** for oxygen diffusion in tissue, clearly stating the boundary conditions. Hence, explain what is meant by the **"lethal corner"** in tissue oxygenation. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a) **Sketch description (KTU expects you to describe since drawing is hard in text):** The OHDC plots **% Saturation of Hb (S)** on the y-axis (0–100%) vs **PO₂ (mmHg)** on the x-axis (0–100 mmHg). Key landmarks: - P₅₀ = **26.6 mmHg** (50% saturation) - S = 97% at $P_{a,O_2} = 95$ mmHg (arterial) - S = 75% at $P_{v,O_2} = 40$ mmHg (venous) - The curve is **sigmoidal (S-shaped)**, NOT hyperbolic. **Sigmoidal Shape Significance — Cooperativity:** The Hill equation is: $$S = \frac{P^n}{P_{50}^n + P^n}, \quad n \approx 2.7$$ The exponent n > 1 implies **positive cooperativity** (Adair's model: binding of O₂ to one Hb tetramer subunit increases affinity of the others via T→R conformational shift). Physiological consequences: - **Flat upper plateau (PO₂ > 60 mmHg):** Provides a **safety margin** — even if alveolar PO₂ drops (e.g., mild altitude), Hb remains nearly fully saturated. - **Steep middle region (10–60 mmHg):** Allows **efficient unloading** in tissues, where small drops in local PO₂ release disproportionately large amounts of O₂. **Bohr Effect:** - Definition: $\uparrow$ [H⁺] or $\uparrow$ P_CO₂ or $\uparrow$ temperature **shifts the OHDC to the right** (increases P₅₀, decreases Hb affinity for O₂). - Mechanism: H⁺ binds to histidine residues of Hb, stabilizing the T (tense/deoxy) state. - Physiological benefit: In actively metabolizing tissues (e.g., exercising muscle), lactic acid and CO₂ production lower pH locally, **enhancing O₂ release exactly where it is needed most**. - Bohr coefficient ≈ -0.48 (negative: pH decrease → P₅₀ increase). **(7 marks: 2 sketch/landmarks + 2 cooperativity + 2 Bohr + 1 clinical link)** #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 — Setup:** A tissue capillary is modeled as a cylinder of radius $R_{cap}$ surrounded by a tissue cylinder of outer radius $R$. O₂ is consumed uniformly at rate $\dot{q}$ per unit tissue volume per unit time. Steady-state, purely radial diffusion. **Step 2 — Governing Equation (Cylindrical Coordinates, Fick's 2nd Law at steady state = 0):** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dP}{dr} \right) = -\frac{\dot{q}}{K} \end{aligned}$$ where $K = D \cdot \alpha$ is the Krogh diffusion constant. **Step 3 — First Integration:** $$r \frac{dP}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q}}{2K} r^2 + C_1$$ **Step 4 — Apply BC 1: $dP/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$ (symmetry).** This forces $C_1 = 0$. **Step 5 — Second Integration:** $$P(r) = -\frac{\dot{q}}{4K} r^2 + C_2$$ **Step 6 — Apply BC 2: $P = P_{cap}$ at $r = R_{cap}$:** $$\begin{aligned} C_2 &= P_{cap} + \frac{\dot{q}}{4K} R_{cap}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Final Krogh Equation:** $$\begin{aligned} P(r) = P_{cap} - \frac{\dot{q}}{4K} \left( R^2 \ln \frac{R}{R_{cap}} - \frac{r^2 - R_{cap}^2}{2} \right) \end{aligned}$$ **"Lethal Corner" Concept:** At $r = R$ (the outer edge of the tissue cylinder, midway between two adjacent capillaries), the PO₂ is **minimum**. The term $R^2 \ln(R/R_{cap})$ is large and dominates. If this PO₂ falls below the **critical mitochondrial PO₂ (~1–3 mmHg)**, oxidative phosphorylation fails and the cell dies. This explains: - Why **capillary density** must increase in high-metabolism tissues (heart, brain). - Why **intercapillary distance** is critical in **diabetic microangiopathy** and **hypertensive vascular disease**. - Why athletes develop **increased capillary density** (training adaptation). **(7 marks: 2 setup + 1 PDE + 1 first integration + 1 BC1 + 1 second integration + 1 BC2 + 1 lethal corner + 1 clinical)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting the dissolved fraction:** In oxygen content problems, ~30% of students compute only $1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S$ and miss the $0.003 \cdot P_{O_2}$ term. KTU awards **dedicated marks** for the dissolved component — never skip it, especially in **hyperbaric oxygen** problems where it dominates. > > 2. **Mixing up units:** $\alpha_{O_2}$ has two common values: > - **0.003 mL O₂ / (100 mL blood · mmHg)** ← Use in content equation > - **1.226 × 10⁻⁵ mL O₂ / (mL blood · mmHg)** ← Use in Fick's law > Using the wrong one is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > > 3. **Hill coefficient misinterpretation:** A common trap question asks, "If n = 1, what does it imply?" Answer: **No cooperativity** (Michaelis-Menten hyperbolic shape). Do NOT write "non-cooperative binding is impossible in Hb." > > 4. **Direction of shift in Bohr effect:** "Right shift = decreased affinity = increased P₅₀ = better unloading in tissues." KTU examiners specifically test the **direction**; confusing it costs 1 full mark. > > 5. **Krogh equation sign error:** The equation gives PO₂ as a *decreasing* function of $r$ (from capillary to outer tissue). The negative sign is critical and often dropped, leading to physically impossible "PO₂ increasing outward" results. > > 6. **Fick's Principle inverse problem:** When asked for $C_v$, rearrange correctly: $C_v = C_a - \dot{V}_{O_2}/Q$. Students frequently invert and write $C_v = \dot{V}_{O_2}/Q - C_a$ (which gives negative values). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize These Before the Exam!** - [x] **Two pathways of O₂ transport:** Hb-bound (~98.5%) + physically dissolved (~1.5%). - [x] **Memorize the 4 magic numbers:** $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.003$, $k_H = 1.34$, $P_{50} = 26.6$ mmHg, $n \approx 2.7$. - [x] **OHDC is sigmoidal** because of **cooperative binding** (Hill equation, n > 1). - [x] **Right shift = ↑ P₅₀ = ↓ affinity = ↑ unloading.** Caused by ↑ H⁺, ↑ CO₂, ↑ temperature, ↑ 2,3-BPG. - [x] **Left shift = ↑ affinity = ↓ unloading.** Caused by ↓ temperature, ↓ P_CO₂, fetal Hb, CO, methemoglobin. - [x] **Fick's Law in pressure form:** $\dot{V}_{O_2} = (A \cdot D \cdot \alpha / L) \cdot \Delta P = D_L \cdot \Delta P$. - [x] **Lung diffusing capacity** $D_L \approx 21$ mL O₂/(min·mmHg) at rest. - [x] **Krogh cylinder** predicts the **"lethal corner"** at the outer tissue radius $R$ between capillaries. - [x] **Fick's Principle (whole body):** $\dot{V}_{O_2} = Q \cdot (C_a - C_v)$. At rest: Q = 5 L/min, $\dot{V}_{O_2} \approx 250$ mL/min, OER ≈ 0.25. - [x] **Normal arterial blood:** $P_{a,O_2} = 95$ mmHg, $S_a = 0.97$, $C_{a,O_2} \approx 20$ mL/dL. - [x] **CO binds Hb ~240× more avidly than O₂** — competitive inhibition; pulse oximeter **cannot** distinguish HbCO from HbO₂. - [x] **Altitude acclimatization:** ↑ 2,3-BPG → right shift → better unloading despite low $P_{a,O_2}$. - [x] **Engineering relevance:** ECMO, hyperbaric O₂ therapy, blood substitutes (perfluorocarbons, Hb-based oxygen carriers), pulse oximetry, anesthesia machines. - [x] **Bohr coefficient** $\approx -0.48$ (negative sign is critical). - [x] **Diffusion-limited vs perfusion-limited gases:** O₂ is **perfusion-limited** normally (Hb reaction keeps gradient); it becomes **diffusion-limited** during heavy exercise at high altitude. > **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **OHDC** in any question involving shift, P₅₀, or Bohr effect. KTU examiners give **"process marks"** for a correctly labeled diagram even if the math is slightly off.

topics.title: Shell Mass Balance and Boundary Conditions for Transport of O2 and CO2 in the Lungs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT635 - TRANSPORT PROCESSES IN BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shell Mass Balance and Boundary Conditions for Transport of O2 and CO2 in the Lungs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shell Mass Balance (Definition):** A shell mass balance is a differential mass conservation technique in which a thin, hypothetical control volume (a *shell*) of thickness $\Delta r$ is drawn within a transport geometry (sphere, cylinder, slab). A balance is written across the shell in the limit $\Delta r \to 0$, yielding an **Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)** or **Partial Differential Equation (PDE)** for the species concentration profile.

In the context of the lungs, the **alveolus** is idealized as a thin-walled **gas-filled sphere** surrounded by a dense **capillary network** (modeled as a cylinder). The shell mass balance framework is applied to:

- **Alveolar gas phase (O₂ in, CO₂ out)**
- **Alveolar-capillary membrane (tissue layer)**
- **Blood plasma within the capillary (O₂ uptake, CO₂ release)**

The driving force for transport is the **partial pressure gradient** of O₂ and CO₂ between alveolar gas and pulmonary capillary blood.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hot Air Balloon Through a Tunnel"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a row of people (gas molecules) standing inside a long tunnel (capillary). At one end, a fan (heart/ventilation) pushes fresh air (O₂-rich blood enters via pulmonary artery). Along the tunnel, passengers exit through the walls (O₂ diffuses into alveolus) while new passengers enter from the other side (CO₂ enters the blood). A **shell balance** is like counting how many people are in a small segment of the tunnel at any instant — what comes in, what goes out, what leaks through the walls, and what accumulates.

For gas exchange in the lungs:
- **Input** (axial convection): blood enters pulmonary capillary with venous O₂ content.
- **Output** (axial convection + radial diffusion): O₂ leaves across alveolar wall.
- **Source/Sink** (chemical reaction): O₂ binds hemoglobin ($Hb + O_2 \rightleftharpoons HbO_2$).
- **Accumulation** = 0 at steady state.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (Adult Human) |
|---|---|
| Alveolar radius ($R_a$) | **~150 μm** |
| Capillary radius ($R_c$) | **~4 μm** |
| Alveolar wall thickness | **~0.5 μm** |
| Pulmonary capillary length | **~10⁻³ m (≈ 1 mm)** |
| Alveolar partial pressure O₂ ($P_{AO_2}$) | **~104 mmHg** |
| Venous blood PO₂ | **~40 mmHg** |
| Alveolar partial pressure CO₂ ($P_{ACO_2}$) | **~40 mmHg** |
| Venous blood PCO₂ | **~45 mmHg** |
| Diffusivity of O₂ in tissue | **~2 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s** |
| Diffusivity of CO₂ in tissue | **~1.8 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shell Mass Balance — Logical Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4-Step Universal Shell Mass Balance Procedure:**
> 1. **Define geometry** — slab, cylinder, or sphere (lungs use sphere for alveolus, cylinder for capillary).
> 2. **Draw a thin shell** of thickness $\Delta r$ at radius $r$ within the geometry.
> 3. **Apply conservation**: (Rate In) − (Rate Out) + (Generation) = (Accumulation).
> 4. **Take the limit** $\Delta r \to 0$ to obtain the differential equation.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Rate In (at $r$)**: molar flux $N_r \cdot A_r$ where $A_r$ is the area of the inner shell surface.
- **Rate Out (at $r + \Delta r$)**: $N_{r+\Delta r} \cdot A_{r+\Delta r}$ where $N$ is the total molar flux (convection + diffusion).
- **Generation rate ($G$)**: rate of appearance of species per unit volume (e.g., O₂ production/consumption by reaction).
- **Accumulation**: $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \cdot \Delta V$.

At **steady state** (the lung operates nearly at steady state during quiet breathing): accumulation = 0.

## 2.2 Geometry-Specific Shell Areas and Volumes

| Geometry | Surface Area $A$ | Volume $V$ | Shell Volume $\Delta V$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sphere (alveolus) | $4\pi r^2$ | $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | $4\pi r^2 \Delta r$ |
| Cylinder (capillary) | $2\pi r L$ | $\pi r^2 L$ | $2\pi r L \Delta r$ |

## 2.3 Fick's Law of Diffusion (Radial Component)

For a binary gas mixture (O₂–N₂ in alveolus, or O₂–tissue in membrane):

$$N_{A,r} = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r} + x_A (N_{A,r} + N_{B,r})$$

For **dilute systems** (gas-phase pulmonary diffusion in tissue is treated as a dilute solute in tissue matrix), Fick's law simplifies to:

$$N_{A,r} = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}$$

## 2.4 Henry's Law at the Alveolar Membrane

Equilibrium of O₂ between alveolar gas and pulmonary capillary blood:

$$C_A = \frac{P_A}{H_A}$$

where $H_A$ is the Henry's law constant. This provides the **boundary condition** linking partial pressure in the alveolus to dissolved concentration in blood plasma.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Applicable Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2 N_{A,r}\right) = 0$ | Steady-state spherical shell balance | Alveolus (radial) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r N_{A,r}\right) = 0$ | Steady-state cylindrical shell balance | Capillary (radial) |
| 3 | $N_{A,r} = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}$ | Fick's law (dilute) | All regions |
| 4 | $C_A = \dfrac{P_A}{H_A}$ | Henry's law equilibrium | Gas–liquid interface |
| 5 | $C_A(r) = C_{A,s}\dfrac{R}{r}$ | Steady-state solution in sphere | Alveolar gas |
| 6 | $C_A(r) = C_{A,1} + (C_{A,2}-C_{A,1})\dfrac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | Steady-state solution in cylinder | Capillary tissue |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = 4\pi R^2 N_{A,r}\big\vert_{r=R}$ | Total molar transfer rate | Alveolus surface |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + v_z \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = D_{AB}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial r}\right)\right] - R_A$ | Convection–diffusion–reaction in capillary | Pulmonary capillary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe symbols used inside table cells** — absolute values are written out or using $\vert x \vert$ form to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This mathematical framework is the **backbone of:**
- **Mechanical ventilators** and artificial lung (ECMO) design.
- **Drug delivery via inhalation** (aerosol particle deposition in alveoli).
- **High-altitude / hyperbaric medicine** (predicting O₂ uptake limits).
- **Toxicology modeling** (CO, HCN uptake rates in the lung).
- **COVID-19 ARDS analysis** (diffusion impairment quantification).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spherical Shell Balance in the Alveolus

### Setup
Let $A = O_2$ (or $CO_2$), diffusing radially through a spherical alveolar gas phase of outer radius $R_a$.

### Step 1: Draw the Shell
A spherical shell at radius $r$ with thickness $\Delta r$ has:

- **Inner surface area:** $A_r = 4\pi r^2$
- **Outer surface area:** $A_{r+\Delta r} = 4\pi (r+\Delta r)^2$
- **Shell volume:** $\Delta V = 4\pi r^2 \Delta r$ (to first order)

### Step 2: Apply Mass Balance at Steady State

$$\left. N_{A,r} \cdot 4\pi r^2 \right|_{r} \;-\; \left. N_{A,r} \cdot 4\pi r^2 \right|_{r+\Delta r} \;+\; 0 \;=\; 0$$

(The generation term is zero because no chemical reaction occurs in the gas phase.)

### Step 3: Expand the Outer Term Using Taylor Series

$$N_{A,r}(r+\Delta r) = N_{A,r}(r) + \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr}\Delta r + \mathcal{O}(\Delta r^2)$$

Substituting:

$$4\pi r^2 N_{A,r}(r) \;-\; 4\pi (r+\Delta r)^2 \left[N_{A,r}(r) + \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr}\Delta r\right] = 0$$

### Step 4: Expand $(r+\Delta r)^2$

$$(r+\Delta r)^2 = r^2 + 2r\Delta r + \Delta r^2$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Collect Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
4\pi r^2 N_{A,r} \;-\; 4\pi (r^2 + 2r\Delta r + \Delta r^2)\left(N_{A,r} + \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr}\Delta r\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4\pi r^2 N_{A,r} \;-\; 4\pi \Big[ &r^2 N_{A,r} + r^2 \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr}\Delta r \\
&+ 2r\Delta r \, N_{A,r} + 2r\Delta r \, \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr}\Delta r \\
&+ \Delta r^2 N_{A,r} + \Delta r^2 \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr}\Delta r \Big] &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Cancel the $4\pi r^2 N_{A,r}$ Terms and Divide by $4\pi \Delta r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
- r^2 \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr} - 2r \, N_{A,r} - 2r\Delta r \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr} - \Delta r \, N_{A,r} - \Delta r^2 \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Take the Limit $\Delta r \to 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{\Delta r \to 0} \left[-r^2 \frac{dN_{A,r}}{dr} - 2r \, N_{A,r}\right] &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Final Differential Equation — Spherical

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 N_{A,r}\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical spherical shell mass balance equation**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylindrical Shell Balance in the Capillary Wall

### Setup
A pulmonary capillary modeled as a hollow cylinder of inner radius $R_1$ and outer radius $R_2$. The shell is at radius $r$ with thickness $\Delta r$.

### Shell Properties

- **Lateral surface area:** $A_r = 2\pi r L$
- **Volume:** $\Delta V = 2\pi r L \Delta r$

### Mass Balance at Steady State

$$\left. N_{A,r} \cdot 2\pi r L \right|_{r} - \left. N_{A,r} \cdot 2\pi r L \right|_{r+\Delta r} = 0$$

### Expand and Take $\Delta r \to 0$

Following identical Taylor expansion as Section 3.1 but with $A_r = 2\pi r L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dr}\left(r N_{A,r}\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical cylindrical shell mass balance equation**.

## 3.3 Solution of Spherical Equation for O₂ Concentration Profile

Substitute Fick's law $N_{A,r} = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dr}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \left[-D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right]\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $D_{AB}$ = constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-D_{AB}\,\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating once:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 \frac{dC_A}{dr} &= K_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_A}{dr} &= \frac{K_1}{r^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating again:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A(r) &= -\frac{K_1}{r} + K_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply boundary conditions:
- At $r = R$: $C_A = C_{A,R}$ (concentration at alveolar wall)
- At $r = R_i$ (inner radius, capillary entrance): $C_A = C_{A,R_i}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= \frac{C_{A,R} - C_{A,R_i}}{\dfrac{1}{R_i} - \dfrac{1}{R}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final concentration profile in spherical shell:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A(r) &= C_{A,R} + (C_{A,R_i} - C_{A,R}) \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{r} - \dfrac{1}{R}}{\dfrac{1}{R_i} - \dfrac{1}{R}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Solution of Cylindrical Equation for CO₂ Concentration Profile

Substitute Fick's law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dr}\left(-r D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrate once:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r \frac{dC_A}{dr} &= K_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Separate and integrate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_A}{dr} &= \frac{K_1}{r} \\
C_A(r) &= K_1 \ln r + K_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply boundary conditions $C_A(R_1) = C_{A,1}$ and $C_A(R_2) = C_{A,2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= \frac{C_{A,2} - C_{A,1}}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final cylindrical profile:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A(r) &= C_{A,1} + (C_{A,2} - C_{A,1})\, \frac{\ln(r/R_1)}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logarithmic concentration profile** in cylindrical geometry vs **inverse-r profile** in spherical geometry — a critical distinction tested in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Boundary Conditions for O₂ Transport in the Lungs

| Location | Boundary Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $r = R_a$ (alveolar wall, gas side) | $C_{O_2} = \dfrac{P_{AO_2}}{H_{O_2}}$ | Henry's law equilibrium |
| $r = R_c$ (capillary lumen) | $C_{O_2}(z=0) = C_{v,O_2}$ | Venous blood entry |
| $z = L$ (capillary exit) | $C_{O_2}(z=L) = C_{a,O_2}$ | Arterialized blood |
| $r = 0$ (centerline) | $\dfrac{\partial C_{O_2}}{\partial r} = 0$ | Symmetry (no flux) |

## 3.6 Boundary Conditions for CO₂ Transport

| Location | Boundary Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $r = R_a$ (alveolar wall) | $C_{CO_2} = \dfrac{P_{ACO_2}}{H_{CO_2}}$ | Henry's law |
| $r = R_c$ | $C_{CO_2}(z=0) = C_{v,CO_2}$ | Venous CO₂ content (~ 52 mL/dL blood) |
| $r = 0$ | $\dfrac{\partial C_{CO_2}}{\partial r} = 0$ | Symmetry |
| Capillary wall outer surface | $-D\,\dfrac{\partial C_{CO_2}}{\partial r}\bigg\vert_{r=R_c} = k_L(C_{CO_2,b} - C_{CO_2}^*)$ | Mass transfer coefficient model |
